title: Turkey Meatloaf
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (160C fan) or gas mark 4.
Warm the olive oil in a large frying pan and cook the onion for 8-10 minutes until softened.
Stir in the garlic, Worcestershire sauce and 2 teaspoons of the tomato purée until combined, then set the mixture aside to cool.
In a large bowl combine the turkey mince, egg, breadcrumbs and the cooled onion mixture, season well, then mix thoroughly.
Shape the mixture into a rectangular loaf and place it in a large roasting tin.
Spread 2 tablespoons of the barbecue sauce over the top and bake for 30 minutes.
Meanwhile, drain one can of the cannellini beans only, then tip both cans into a bowl.
Stir in the remaining barbecue sauce and tomato purée, season, and set aside.
After the meatloaf's initial bake, scatter the beans around it in the tin and bake for a further 15 minutes until the meatloaf is cooked through and the beans are piping hot.
Scatter over the parsley and serve the meatloaf in slices.

title: Chicken & mushroom Hotpot
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan / gas 6).
Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat.
Add the chopped onion and cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the mushrooms and cook until the onion and mushrooms are almost tender.
Stir in the flour to make a roux, crumbling in the stock cube if using.
Cook the roux over low heat for 2 minutes, stirring constantly, to cook out the raw flour taste.
Take the pan off the heat and slowly stir in the stock, or 500ml water if you used a stock cube.
Season with pepper, a pinch of nutmeg and a pinch of mustard powder.
Return the pan to medium heat and bring to a gentle boil, stirring until thickened.
Reduce to a very low heat and stir in the cooked chicken and vegetables.
Grease an ovenproof pie dish with a little butter and pour in the chicken and mushroom filling.
Arrange the potato slices over the top, overlapping slightly like a pie crust.
Brush the potatoes with a little melted butter and bake for about 35 minutes until golden and the potatoes are cooked through.

title: Pork Cassoulet
instructions: Preheat the oven to 140C/120C fan/gas 1.
Set a large ovenproof pan with a tight lid over high heat and melt the goose fat.
Add the diced pork and sear for a few minutes, turning to colour all sides.
Turn the heat down low, add the sliced onion, whole garlic cloves, sliced carrot and fennel seeds, and cook gently until the veg softens.
Pour in the red wine vinegar, scraping up any browned bits from the base of the pan.
Add the stock, tomato purée and half the rosemary and parsley.
Bring to the boil, then simmer for 10 minutes.
Season, cover with the lid and transfer to the oven for 2 hours, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the haricot beans when there's 30 minutes of cooking time left.
Remove the lid for the final hour of oven time.
Take the pan out of the oven and heat the grill.
Scatter over the remaining herbs and breadcrumbs, drizzle with a little oil, and grill for 5-10 minutes until the top is golden.
Serve with crusty bread and green vegetables on the side.

title: Spinach & Ricotta Cannelloni
instructions: First get the tomato sauce going: heat the oil in a large pan and fry the garlic for a minute.
Stir in the sugar, vinegar, chopped tomatoes and a little seasoning, then let it simmer for 20 minutes, stirring now and then, until it thickens up.
Stir the basil through the sauce and spread it across two or more shallow ovenproof dishes; set aside.
Beat the mascarpone with the milk until smooth and season it — this is your second sauce.
Wilt the spinach by pouring a kettle of boiling water over it in a colander, working in batches if needed.
Once it's cool enough to handle, squeeze out all the excess water and roughly chop the spinach.
Mix the spinach in a large bowl with 100g of the Parmesan and the ricotta, then season well with salt, pepper and nutmeg.
Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6.
Pipe the spinach and ricotta filling into the cannelloni tubes using a piping bag or a food bag with the corner snipped off.
Lay the filled tubes side by side over the tomato sauce, then spoon the mascarpone sauce on top.
Scatter over the remaining Parmesan and the mozzarella.
Bake for 30-35 minutes until golden and bubbling.
Let it stand for 5 minutes out of the oven before serving.

title: Chicken Couscous

title: Tuna Nicoise
instructions: Heat your oven to 200°C (180°C fan / gas 6).
Toss the potatoes with 2 teaspoons of the olive oil and season, spread them on a large baking tray, and roast for 20 minutes, stirring halfway, until crisp and cooked through.
Meanwhile, put the eggs in a small pan of water, bring to the boil, then simmer for 8-10 minutes to your preferred doneness.

In a large salad bowl, whisk together the remaining olive oil, red wine vinegar, capers and chopped tomatoes.
Season, then add the onion, spinach, tuna and roasted potatoes, tossing gently to combine.
Top the salad with the halved eggs and serve straight away.

title: Chocolate Avocado Mousse
instructions: Toss the banana, avocado, cacao powder, honey, lemon juice, vanilla, water and a pinch of salt into your food processor.
Blend until silky smooth, tasting as you go — nudge the ratio of avocado to banana if one starts to dominate, since sizes vary so much.

Spoon the mousse into little cups or shot glasses.

title: Pancakes
instructions: Tip the flour, eggs, milk, 1 tbsp of oil and a pinch of salt into a bowl or large jug, then whisk everything together until you have a smooth, lump-free batter.
If you have time, let the batter rest for 30 minutes; otherwise get straight on with cooking.
Warm a medium frying pan or crêpe pan over medium heat and wipe it lightly with oiled kitchen paper.
Once hot, cook each pancake for about 1 minute per side until golden, keeping finished ones warm in a low oven while you work through the batter.
Serve with lemon wedges and sugar, or whatever filling you fancy.
Once cooled, pancakes can be layered with baking parchment between them, wrapped in cling film, and frozen for up to 2 months.

title: Banana Pancakes
instructions: Mash the banana in a bowl with a fork until it's a thick purée.
Stir in the eggs, baking powder and vanilla extract until you have a smooth batter.
Heat a large non-stick frying pan over medium heat and brush with half the oil.
Spoon in two pancakes from half the batter and cook 1-2 minutes per side, then transfer to a plate.
Repeat with the remaining oil and batter to cook the last two pancakes.
Top the pancakes with pecans and raspberries before serving.

ingredients.note: '1 large' banana estimated at typical large-banana weight
'2 medium' eggs estimated at ~50g each
'pinch' estimated as ~2g
'sprinkling' estimated as ~2g
1 tsp oil used to grease pan; absorbed into pancakes rather than drained, so counted as eaten

title: Choc Chip Pecan Pie
instructions: Make the pastry: tip the flour, butter, cream cheese and icing sugar into a food processor with 1/4 tsp salt and blend until it looks like breadcrumbs. With the blade running, drizzle in 2-3 tsp cold water until the mixture clumps together. Bring together into a ball, pat into a disc, wrap and chill at least 20 mins.
Heat oven to 200C/180C fan. Roll the pastry to the thickness of a coin and line a deep 23cm fluted tart tin, pressing into the corners with no gaps. Leave 1cm overhanging and keep the scraps.

Line the case with scrunched parchment and baking beans. Blind-bake 15-20 mins until the sides are set, then remove the beans and parchment and bake 5 mins more until golden. Trim the pastry flush with the tin and patch any cracks with the scraps.
In a pan, melt the butter, maple syrup, both sugars and 1/4 tsp salt, stirring until smooth. Take off the heat and cool 10 mins. Reduce oven to 160C/140C fan.
Beat the eggs, then mix in the syrup mixture, vanilla and pecans until well combined.
Pour half the filling into the case, scatter over half the chocolate chips, then add the rest of the filling and chips. Bake on the middle shelf 50-55 mins until set.
Cool, then chill at least 2 hrs before serving.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp icing sugar inferred as ~8g
200ml converted to ~268g at ~1.34 g/ml
4 eggs inferred at ~50g each
1 tsp inferred as ~4g

title: Pumpkin Pie
instructions: Boil the pumpkin in a covered saucepan of water for about 15 minutes until fork-tender, then drain and let it cool.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4 and roll the pastry out on a lightly floured surface to line a 22cm loose-bottomed tart tin.
Chill the lined tin for 15 minutes, then blind-bake with parchment and baking beans for 15 minutes.
Remove the beans and paper and bake for another 10 minutes until the base turns pale golden and biscuity, then let it cool slightly.
Turn the oven up to 220C/200C fan/gas 7.
Push the cooled pumpkin through a sieve into a large bowl to make a smooth purée.
In a separate bowl, mix the sugar, salt, nutmeg and half the cinnamon.
Stir in the beaten eggs, melted butter and milk, then combine everything with the pumpkin purée.
Pour the filling into the baked tart shell and bake for 10 minutes, then drop the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4 and continue baking for 35-40 minutes until just set.
Cool the pie completely, then remove it from the tin.
Mix the remaining cinnamon with the icing sugar and dust it over the top before serving chilled.

converted from ½ tsp using standard tsp-to-gram estimate
converted from ½ tsp using standard tsp-to-gram estimate
converted from 1 tsp using standard tsp-to-gram estimate; split between filling and topping per instructions
2 beaten eggs estimated at ~50g each
melted before mixing into filling per instructions, but stays in the served pie so not a frying medium
converted from 1 tbsp using standard tbsp-to-gram estimate

title: New York cheesecake
instructions: Set an oven shelf in the middle position and preheat to 160°C fan (180°C conventional, gas 4).
Line the base of a 23cm springform cake tin with parchment paper.
For the crust, melt the butter in a saucepan.
Stir in the biscuit crumbs and sugar until the mixture is evenly moistened.
Press the crumb mixture into the base of the tin and bake for 10 minutes, then cool on a wire rack while you make the filling.
Raise the oven temperature to 200°C fan (240°C conventional, gas 9).
In a stand mixer fitted with the paddle attachment, beat the cream cheese on medium-low speed until creamy, about 2 minutes.
With the mixer on low, gradually add the sugar, then the flour and a pinch of salt, scraping down the bowl and paddle twice.
Switch to the whisk attachment and mix in the vanilla, lemon zest and lemon juice.
Whisk in the eggs and yolk one at a time, scraping down the bowl and whisk at least twice.
Stir the first carton of sour cream until smooth, measure out about three-quarters of it, and blend it into the batter on low speed without over-beating; reserve the rest.
Brush the sides of the springform tin with melted butter and set it on a baking sheet.
Pour the filling into the tin, smoothing the top and popping any lumps with a knife.
Bake for 10 minutes, then reduce the oven to 90°C fan (110°C conventional, gas ¼) and bake a further 25 minutes until the filling has just a slight wobble.
Turn off the oven and leave the door open for a creamier centre, or closed for a drier one, then let the cheesecake cool inside for 2 hours.
Combine the reserved sour cream with the second carton, sugar and lemon juice to make the topping, then spread it over the cheesecake right to the edges.
Cover loosely with foil and refrigerate for at least 8 hours or overnight.
Run a round-bladed knife around the edge of the tin, release the springform, slide the cheesecake onto a plate, and peel away the parchment from underneath.

title: Key Lime Pie
instructions: Heat your oven to 160C/fan 140C/gas 3.
Blitz the digestive biscuits to fine crumbs in a food processor, or bash them in a bag with a rolling pin.
Stir the crumbs into the melted butter until well combined.
Press the mixture into the base and up the sides of a 22cm loose-based tart tin.
Bake for 10 minutes, then remove and let it cool.
Whisk the egg yolks in a large bowl with electric beaters for a minute.
Add the condensed milk and whisk for 3 minutes.
Add the lime zest and juice and whisk for another 3 minutes.
Pour the filling into the cooled biscuit base.
Bake for a further 15 minutes.
Cool, then chill for at least 3 hours, or overnight if you can wait.
Carefully remove the pie from the tin and set it on a serving plate.
Softly whip the cream with the icing sugar.
Dollop or pipe the cream over the top of the pie.
Finish with a scattering of extra lime zest before serving.

ingredients.note: 3 egg yolks estimated at ~17g each; not weighed in original
4 limes estimated at ~67g each for zest and juice; not weighed in original

garnish zest 'to serve', quantity not specified; estimated small amount

title: Chocolate Raspberry Brownies
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan/gas 4) and line a 20 x 30cm baking tray with baking parchment.
Melt the dark chocolate, milk chocolate, butter and sugar together gently in a pan over low heat, stirring occasionally with a wooden spoon, then take off the heat.
Beat the eggs in one at a time into the melted chocolate mixture.
Sift the flour and cocoa over the mixture and stir through.
Fold in half the raspberries, scrape the batter into the lined tray, then scatter the remaining raspberries over the top.
Bake on the middle shelf for 30 minutes, or 5 minutes longer if you prefer a firmer texture.
Cool completely before slicing into squares.
Store in an airtight container for up to 3 days.

ingredients.note: 4 large eggs estimated at 50g each = 200g

title: Peanut Butter Cheesecake
instructions: Oil a 20cm round loose-bottomed cake tin and line it with cling film, smoothing it out as much as possible.
Melt the butter in a pan.
Crush the peanut cookies in a bag with a rolling pin, then stir them into the melted butter until well coated.
Press the biscuit mixture firmly into the base of the tin and chill.
Soak the gelatine leaves in water while you make the filling.
Tip the ricotta into a bowl and beat in the peanut butter and golden syrup.
Blitz the mixture with a stick blender until smooth, if you like.
Squeeze the soaked gelatine dry and put it into a pan with the milk, heating gently until dissolved.
Beat the gelatine-milk mixture into the peanut filling, then pour it onto the biscuit base.
Chill until set.
To freeze, leave the cheesecake in the tin, cover the surface with cling film once solid, then wrap the tin in cling film and foil.
To defrost, thaw in the fridge overnight.
To serve, carefully remove the cheesecake from the tin.
Whisk the double cream with the light brown soft sugar until it holds its shape, then spread over the top of the cheesecake and scatter with crushed peanut brittle.

ingredients.note: grams inferred from leaf count (5 leaves ≈ 2g each)

no amount given in original; estimated 50g for garnish

title: Peach & Blueberry Grunt
instructions: Heat your oven to 190C/170C fan/gas 5 and butter a wide shallow ovenproof dish.
Blend the cornflour with the orange zest and juice, then tip into a large pan with the caster sugar.
Halve, stone and slice the peaches, add to the pan, and bring slowly to the boil, stirring gently until the sauce turns shiny and thickened, about 3-4 minutes.
Take off the heat, stir in the blueberries, and tip the mixture into the prepared dish.
Tip the self-raising flour into a mixing bowl and rub in the 50g butter until it looks like fine breadcrumbs.
Stir in half the muscovado sugar; mix the remaining sugar with the cinnamon and set it aside for the topping.
Add the milk to the dry ingredients and mix to a soft dough.
Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface, knead briefly, then roll into an oblong roughly 16 x 24cm.
Brush the dough with melted butter and sprinkle evenly with the spicy sugar mix.
Roll up from one long side and cut into 12 slices.
Arrange the slices around the top of the dish, leaving the centre uncovered.
Bake for 20-25 minutes until the topping is crisp and golden, then serve warm.

ingredients.note: 1 tsp converted to grams using standard density (~3g/tsp)
juice of 2 oranges estimated at ~60ml each (~120g)
zest of 1 orange estimated at ~6g

title: Vegetarian Casserole
instructions: Heat the oil in a large, heavy-based pan and cook the onion gently for 5-10 minutes until softened.
Stir in the garlic, spices, dried thyme, carrots, celery and peppers, and cook for 5 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, stock, courgettes and fresh thyme, then simmer for 20-25 minutes.
Remove the thyme sprigs, stir in the lentils, and bring back to a simmer.
Serve with wild and white basmati rice, mash or quinoa.

ingredients.note: No yield stated in recipe; inferred servings=5 from total food mass (~2.5kg, ~500g/serving casserole portion). Oil amount '1' assumed ~1 tbsp (14g).

title: Mushroom & Chestnut Rotolo
instructions: Soak the dried mushrooms in 350ml boiling water and set them aside.
Blitz three-quarters of the chestnuts with 150ml water until smooth and creamy; roughly chop the rest.
Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a large non-stick frying pan and fry the shallots with a pinch of salt until soft.
Add the garlic, chopped chestnuts and rosemary, and fry for 2 minutes more.
Add the wild mushrooms, another 2 tbsp oil and some seasoning, and cook for 3 minutes until they start to soften.
Drain the soaked mushrooms, keeping the liquid, chop them roughly, and stir them into the pan with the soy sauce; fry for 2 minutes more.
Whisk together the wine, reserved mushroom liquid and chestnut cream to make a sauce, then season it.
Stir half the sauce into the mushroom mixture and cook for 1 minute until glossy; discard the rosemary sprigs and set the filling aside.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4.
Blanch the lasagne sheets in boiling salted water for 2 minutes until pliable, then plunge them into iced water.
Separate the sheets carefully and lay them on a clean tea towel.
Spread a spoonful of the mushroom sauce over the bottom two-thirds of each sheet, then roll up from the shorter end.
Cut each roll in half and arrange the pieces cut-side up in a serving dish.
Use up any leftover sauce by pushing it into the exposed ends of the rolls.
Pour the remaining sauce over the pasta and bake for 10 minutes, until a skewer meets no resistance.
Toss the breadcrumbs with the last 2 tbsp olive oil, sage leaves and seasoning.
Scatter the crumb mixture over the rotolo and bake for another 10 minutes until golden and the sage is crisp.
Rest for 10 minutes so the pasta soaks up the sauce, then drizzle with a little truffle oil if you like before serving.

title: Matar Paneer
instructions: Get the oil hot and shimmering in a frying pan over high heat.
Drop in the paneer, then dial the heat back a touch.
Fry until the edges start to brown, then flip and brown all sides — it colours fast, so stay with it.
Lift the paneer out and drain it on kitchen paper.
Tip the ginger, cumin, turmeric, ground coriander and chilli into the same pan and fry for 1 minute.
Add the tomatoes, mashing them with the back of a spoon, and simmer for 5 minutes until the sauce turns fragrant.
Splash in a little water if it looks too thick.
Season well.
Stir in the peas and simmer for a further 2 minutes.
Fold the paneer back in and scatter over the garam masala.
Divide between two bowls, top with coriander leaves, and serve with naan bread, roti or rice.

title: Squash linguine
instructions: Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan / gas 6).
Scatter the squash and garlic on a baking tray and drizzle with the olive oil.
Roast for 35-40 minutes until soft, then season.
Cook the linguine according to the pack instructions.
Drain the pasta, reserving the cooking water.
Blitz the roasted squash with 400ml of the reserved pasta water using a stick blender until smooth.
Heat a little oil in a frying pan and fry the sage until crisp, then drain it on kitchen paper.
Tip the pasta and the squash sauce into the pan and warm through.
Scatter with the crispy sage to serve.

title: Vegetarian Chilli
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan / gas 6).
Tip the roasted vegetables into a casserole dish and roast for 15 minutes.
Stir in the kidney beans and chopped tomatoes, season, and cook for a further 10-15 minutes until piping hot.
Microwave the mixed grain pouch on high for 1 minute and serve alongside the chilli.

title: Kidney Bean Curry
instructions: Warm the vegetable oil in a large frying pan over low-medium heat.
Stir in the onion with a pinch of salt and cook slowly until softened and just starting to colour.
Add the garlic, ginger and coriander stalks, and cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Sprinkle in the cumin, paprika and garam masala, and cook for 1 minute until everything smells aromatic.
Pour in the chopped tomatoes and kidney beans with their liquid, then bring to the boil.
Reduce the heat and simmer for 15 minutes until the curry turns thick.
Season to taste and serve with basmati rice and the coriander leaves.

title: Tahini Lentils
instructions: Whisk the tahini with the lemon zest and juice and 50ml cold water in a jug until it forms a runny dressing; season to taste and set aside.
Heat the olive oil in a wok or large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Add the chopped red onion with a pinch of salt and fry for 2 minutes until it starts to soften and colour.
Stir in the garlic, yellow pepper, green beans and courgette and fry for 5 minutes, stirring frequently.
Tip in the kale, lentils and the tahini dressing.
Keep everything on the heat for a couple of minutes, stirring until the kale wilts and everything is coated in the creamy dressing.

title: Chickpea Fajitas
instructions: Heat your oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6 and line a baking tray with foil.
Drain the chickpeas, pat them dry, and tip them onto the prepared tray.
Toss the chickpeas with the olive oil and paprika to coat evenly.
Roast the chickpeas for 20-25 minutes until browned and crisp, giving the tray a shake halfway through.
While the chickpeas roast, put the tomatoes and red onion in a small bowl with the red wine vinegar and set aside to pickle.
Mash the avocado in another bowl with a fork, leaving some chunky bits.
Stir the lime juice into the avocado and season well to make a chunky guacamole.
Mix the sour cream with the harissa spice and set aside until serving.
Heat a griddle pan until nearly smoking and char the tortillas one at a time on both sides until hot with griddle lines.

Lay everything out on the table and build the fajitas: spread a little harissa cream over each tortilla, then top with roasted chickpeas, guacamole, pickled tomato and onion, and coriander if you like.
Serve with lime wedges for squeezing over.

title: Yaki Udon
instructions: Bring a large pan of water to the boil.
Add 250ml cold water along with the udon noodles — this slows the cooking so the thick noodles cook through the middle.
Cook fresh or frozen noodles for about 2 minutes until al dente, or dried noodles for 5-6 minutes.
Drain the noodles and leave them in the colander.
Heat 1 tablespoon of the sesame oil in a wok or large pan.
Add the sliced onion and cabbage, and sauté for about 5 minutes until softened.
Stir in the mushrooms and some of the spring onions, and cook for another minute.
Pour in the remaining sesame oil along with the drained noodles.
Add the mirin, soy sauce, sugar and Worcestershire sauce to make the sauce.
Keep stir-frying everything together until sticky and piping hot.
Scatter over the remaining spring onions and serve.

title: Spanish Tortilla
instructions: Warm a large non-stick frying pan over low heat and sweat the sliced onion in the olive oil and butter until soft and translucent, about 15 minutes.
Add the potatoes, cover the pan, and let them cook gently for 15-20 minutes, stirring now and then so they fry evenly without browning.
Once the potatoes are tender and the onion glossy, crush 2 garlic cloves into the pan and stir through.
Pour in the beaten eggs, cover again, and let the tortilla set slowly for about 20 minutes until the base and edges are golden and the middle is just set but still soft.
Slide the tortilla onto a plate, invert it with a second plate, and return it to the pan to finish cooking the other side.
Transfer to a serving plate and scatter with chopped parsley; serve warm or at room temperature.
For the accompaniment, warm slices of baguette, prick them with a fork, rub with the remaining garlic, top with grated tomato, and finish with sea salt and a drizzle of olive oil.

title: Beef Dumpling Stew
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F/Gas 4).
Heat the olive oil and butter in an ovenproof casserole, then brown the beef on all sides.
Sprinkle over the flour and cook for 2-3 minutes, stirring to coat the meat.
Add the garlic, onions, celery, carrots, leek and swede, and fry for 1-2 minutes.
Pour in the red wine and beef stock, add the bay leaves and thyme, then stir in the Worcestershire sauce and balsamic vinegar to taste.
Season generously with salt and freshly ground black pepper.
Cover with a lid, transfer to the oven and cook for about two hours, until the beef is tender.
Meanwhile, sift the flour, baking powder and salt into a bowl for the dumplings.
Mix in the suet and just enough water to form a thick dough.
With floured hands, roll spoonfuls of the dough into small balls.
After two hours, uncover the stew, sit the dumplings on top, then cover and return to the oven for a further 20 minutes until the dumplings are swollen and tender (leave uncovered for a golden top).
To serve, spoon mashed potato onto four plates, top with the stew and dumplings, and scatter over the chopped parsley.

title: Beef and Mustard Pie
instructions: Preheat the oven to 150C/300F/Gas 2.
Toss the beef with the flour, salt and black pepper in a bowl until well coated.
Heat a large casserole until hot and add half of the rapeseed oil.
Add enough beef to just cover the bottom of the pan and fry until browned on all sides, then set aside.
Repeat with the remaining oil and beef.
Return all the beef to the casserole and pour in the red wine, letting it bubble until reduced by half.
Add the beef stock, onion, carrots, thyme and mustard, then season well with salt and pepper.
Cover with a lid and transfer to the oven to cook for two hours.
Remove from the oven, taste and adjust the seasoning, then discard the thyme sprigs and let the filling cool.
Once cool, preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6.
Spoon the beef filling into a pie dish and brush the rim with beaten egg yolk.
Lay the puff pastry over the top and brush it with more beaten egg yolk.
Trim the excess pastry, crimp the edges to seal, then bake for 30 minutes until the pastry is golden and cooked through.
Meanwhile, bring a pan of salted water to the boil and cook the green beans for 4-5 minutes until just tender.
Drain the beans and toss with the butter, then season with black pepper.
Serve a generous spoonful of pie on each plate alongside the buttered green beans.

title: Chicken Ham and Leek Pie
instructions: Heat the chicken stock in a lidded saucepan, add the chicken breasts, bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and poach for 10 minutes.
Lift the chicken out with tongs onto a plate, then pour the poaching liquid into a jug and set aside.
Melt 25g of the butter in a large heavy-based saucepan over low heat, add the leeks, and fry gently for 2 minutes until just softened.
Stir in the garlic and cook for a further minute.
Add the remaining butter, and once melted stir in the flour, cooking for 30 seconds while stirring constantly.
Gradually whisk in the milk a little at a time, then slowly add 250ml of the reserved stock and the wine if using, stirring until smooth and slightly thickened.
Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 3 minutes.
Season with salt and pepper, remove from the heat, and stir in the cream.
Pour the sauce into a bowl, press cling film onto the surface to stop a skin forming, and leave to cool.
Preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6 and put a baking tray in to heat.
For the pastry, pulse the flour and butter in a food processor until it resembles fine breadcrumbs, then with the motor running add the beaten egg and water and blend until it forms a ball.
Set aside 250g of the pastry for the lid.
Roll out the remaining pastry on a floured surface to about 5mm thick, large enough to line the pie dish, lower it in, and press firmly up the sides, leaving the excess overhanging.
Cut the poached chicken into 3cm pieces and stir it into the cooled sauce along with the ham and leeks.
Pour the filling into the pastry case, brush the rim with beaten egg, and roll out the reserved pastry for the lid.
Cover the pie with the lid, press the edges together to seal, and trim off the excess pastry.
Cut a small steam hole in the centre, brush the top with beaten egg, and bake on the hot tray in the centre of the oven for 35-40 minutes until golden-brown and piping hot inside.

ingredients.note: only ~250ml of the 450ml poaching stock is incorporated into the sauce; remainder used for poaching and largely discarded

title: Minced Beef Pie
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6.
Heat the oil in a deep frying pan and fry the beef mince for 4-5 minutes, breaking it up with a wooden spoon as it browns.
Add the onion and cook for 2-3 minutes.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2-3 more minutes.
Stir in the flour and cook for a further minute.
Add the mushrooms, the beef stock, and a couple of dashes of Worcestershire sauce.
Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat, cover the pan, and simmer for 20 minutes.
Set aside to cool, then turn the meat mixture into a one litre pie dish.
Roll out the pastry on a floured surface until slightly larger than the pie dish.
Drape the pastry over the dish, press firmly onto the edges, then trim and flute the edges.
Cut leaf shapes from the pastry trimmings, decorate the top of the pie, and stick them on with the beaten egg yolk.
Cut three or four slits in the pastry to let steam escape, then brush the pie with the remaining egg yolk.
Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until golden-brown.
Slice into wedges to serve.

ingredients.note: 2 tbsp estimated at 14g each; oil is worked into the gravy filling (flour/stock added to same pan) so it stays in the served dish
no weight given for '1 chopped onion'; estimated 110g for a medium onion
1 tbsp estimated at 16g
1 1/2 tbsp estimated at 8g/tbsp

'dash' has no stated weight; estimated 5g
estimated 18g for one egg yolk, used as egg wash to glaze and stick decorations

title: Lamb and Potato pie
instructions: Dust the lamb shoulder pieces lightly with flour.
Heat a dash of vegetable oil in a large saucepan and fry the onion and lamb together until lightly browned, seasoning with salt and pepper.
Stir in the carrots, pour in the vegetable stock, and season to taste.
Bring to the boil, then cover and reduce to a simmer. Let it cook gently for at least an hour, until the lamb is tender — the longer, the better the flavour.
Preheat the oven to 180C/350F/Gas 4.
Drain the potato cubes and stir them into the lamb mixture.
Turn everything into a pie dish or casserole and top with the shortcrust pastry, cutting three slits in the top to let steam escape.
Brush the pastry with beaten egg and bake for about 40 minutes, until golden brown.
Serve hot.

ingredients.note: 1 tbls estimated as 8g
'Dash' of oil estimated at 15g; oil remains in the stew and is served, so not treated as a frying medium
1 sliced onion estimated at 110g
2 sliced carrots estimated at 60g each
'To glaze' estimated at 15g (roughly a quarter of one beaten egg)

title: Beef and Oyster pie
instructions: Season the beef cubes with salt and black pepper.
Heat oil in a frying pan and brown the beef in three batches over high heat, transferring each batch to a large flameproof casserole once coloured; add a splash more oil if the pan looks dry.
In the same pan, add more oil and cook the shallots for 4-5 minutes.
Add the garlic and fry for 30 seconds.
Stir in the bacon and fry until lightly browned.
Tip the shallot and bacon mixture into the casserole with the beef, then add the thyme and bay leaves.
Preheat the oven to 180C/350F/Gas 4.
Pour the stout into the frying pan, bring to the boil and scrape up any browned bits from the base.
Pour the stout over the beef, add the beef stock, cover the casserole and braise in the oven for 1½-2 hours, until the beef is tender and the sauce has reduced.
Skim off any surface fat, taste and adjust the seasoning, then stir in the cornflour paste.
Set the casserole on the hob and simmer for 1-2 minutes, stirring, until thickened, then leave to cool.
Increase the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6.
Make the pastry: put the flour and salt in a large bowl, grate in the butter in three batches and mix in, then stir in cold water gradually until the dough just comes together.
Knead the pastry lightly into a ball and set aside 250g for the lid.
Roll out the rest of the pastry and line the pie dish.
Pile in the beef filling, tucking the oysters in among it.
Brush the pastry edge with beaten egg.
Roll out the reserved pastry for the lid, lay it over the filling, press and trim the edges to seal.
Brush the top with beaten egg to glaze.
Bake for 25-30 minutes, until the pastry is golden-brown and the filling is bubbling.

ingredients.note: oil used both to sear the beef and to soften the shallots; pan is later deglazed with stout and poured into the stew, so all of it ends up in the dish
weight inferred: 3 shallots at ~30g each
weight inferred: 2 cloves at ~3g each

weight inferred: 2 dried bay leaves

title: Chicken Parmentier
instructions: Boil the potatoes in salted water until tender, then drain them well.
Push the potatoes through a ricer or mash thoroughly until smooth.
Stir the butter, cream and egg yolks into the mash, season, and set aside.
Melt the butter in a large pan.
Add the shallots, carrots and celery and fry gently until soft.
Add the garlic and cook briefly.
Pour in the wine and cook for a minute to burn off the alcohol.
Stir in the tomato purée, tinned tomatoes and chicken stock, and simmer for 10-15 minutes until thickened.
Fold in the shredded chicken, olives and parsley, then season to taste with salt and pepper.
Preheat the oven to 180C/160C Fan/Gas 4.
Spread the filling into a 20x30cm ovenproof dish and top with the mashed potato.
Grate the Gruyère over the top.
Bake for 30-35 minutes until piping hot and golden-brown on top.

ingredients.note: 5 tbsp estimated as 15ml each
2 yolks estimated at ~18g each
7 shallots estimated at ~25g each
3 chopped carrots estimated at ~60g each
2 sticks estimated at ~40g each
1 clove estimated at ~5g

2 tbsp chopped parsley estimated

title: McSinghs Scotch pie
instructions: Toast the cumin seeds in a dry, hot frying pan for a couple of minutes until fragrant, then tip them out and set aside.
Heat the oil in the same pan and fry the onion, garlic, chilli, pepper and a good pinch of salt for about eight minutes, until all the moisture has cooked away.
Take the pan off the heat and stir the toasted cumin seeds, nutmeg and ground coriander through the onion mixture, then leave it to cool.
In a large bowl, combine the lamb mince, pepper, fresh coriander and the cooled spiced onion mixture until evenly mixed. Cover and chill in the fridge.
Preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6 and generously grease a 20cm loose-bottomed or springform cake tin with lard.
Sift the flour and salt into a large bowl and make a well in the centre.
Heat the milk, lard and water gently in a saucepan; once the lard has melted, bring the liquid to the boil.
Pour the boiling liquid into the flour and stir with a wooden spoon until cool enough to handle, then bring the dough together into a ball.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface and knead briefly - it will be soft and moist.
Set aside a third of the pastry, then roll out the rest and line the greased tin, pressing it up the sides so it pokes just over the rim.
Pack the lamb filling into the pastry case bit by bit, shaping a slight peak as you reach the top.
Roll out the reserved pastry, lay it over the filling, pinch the edges to seal and trim off any excess.
Poke a hole in the centre of the lid and insert a small foil tube to let steam escape.
Brush the top with beaten egg yolk and bake for 30 minutes, with a tray on the shelf below to catch drips.
Lower the oven to 160C/325F/Gas 3 and continue baking for a further 1¼ hours until deep golden-brown.
Let the pie cool completely, then refrigerate for at least two hours, or overnight.
Run a knife around the edge, release from the tin and serve with chutneys, salads or pickles.

ingredients.note: toasted dry before use; 2 tsp ≈ 4g inferred conversion
oil sizzles aromatics that stay in the filling, so counted as eaten not a frying medium; 1 tbsp ≈ 14g inferred
2 onions, weight inferred at ~150g each
6 cloves, weight inferred at ~5g each
3 chillies, weight inferred at ~10g each
1 pepper, weight inferred at ~150g

½ tsp ≈ 3g inferred conversion

title: Steak and Kidney Pie
instructions: Crank the oven up to 220°C/425°F/Gas 7 so it's blazing hot for later.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large frying pan and brown the beef all over, working in batches if needed, then set it aside.
Brown the kidneys on both sides in the same pan.
Add the onions and cook for 3-4 minutes until softened.
Return the beef to the pan, sprinkle over the flour, and stir to coat the meat and onions.
Pour in the beef stock, stir well, and bring to the boil.
Turn the heat down and simmer uncovered for about 1½ hours, topping up with more stock if it reduces too much.
Take the pan off the heat, stir in salt, pepper, and a dash of Worcestershire sauce, then let the filling cool completely.
Spoon the cooled meat mixture into a pie dish.
Roll out the puff pastry to about 5mm thick, roughly 5cm larger all round than your pie dish.
Lift the pastry over the filling with a rolling pin, then trim and crimp the edges with your fingers and thumb.
Brush the top with the beaten egg mixture and bake for 30-40 minutes until golden, puffed, and irresistible.
Serve hot with creamy mash and steamed vegetables to soak up the gravy.

ingredients.note: No quantity stated; estimated ~15g used for the egg wash brushing.
No quantity stated; estimated ~15g used for the egg wash brushing.
2 tbsp converted to grams; oil stays in the same pan used to build the braise, so it remains in the served filling rather than being drained off.

title: Three Fish Pie
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6 (180C fan).
Boil the potatoes in salted water until completely tender.
Drain the potatoes well and mash them with butter and milk.
Season the mash with pepper, then taste and adjust with salt and more pepper as needed.
Melt the butter in a saucepan for the fish filling.
Add the leeks and stir over the heat, then cover and simmer gently for 10 minutes until soft.
Whisk the flour and wine together in a small bowl until smooth.
Add the milk to the leeks and bring to the boil.
Stir in the wine mixture and keep stirring briskly until thickened.
Season the sauce, then stir in the parsley and the salmon, haddock and smoked haddock.
Cook the fish mixture over the heat for two minutes, then spoon it into an ovenproof casserole.
Scatter the eggs over the fish mixture and let it cool until firm.
Spoon the mashed potato over the top and mark it with a fork.
Sprinkle the cheese over the potato.
Bake for 30-40 minutes until golden-brown on top and bubbling at the edges.

ingredients.note: 'Knob' of butter inferred as ~15g
'Dash' of milk inferred as ~15g
'2 sliced leeks' estimated at ~100g each = 200g total
'2 tbsp chopped parsley' estimated at ~4g per tbsp = 8g
6 whole eggs estimated at ~50g each (300g); fish pie convention implies hard-boiled eggs scattered in, though the given instructions don't detail the boiling step

title: Sticky Toffee Pudding
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180C (160C fan/Gas 4) and butter a wide, shallow ovenproof dish (about 1.7 litres).
Beat the butter, sugar, eggs, flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda and treacle together for about 30 seconds until combined.
Whisk in the milk gradually until the batter is smooth.
Pour the batter into the prepared dish and bake for 35–40 minutes, until well risen and springy in the centre.
For the toffee sauce, put the butter, sugar, treacle, cream and vanilla into a saucepan and stir over low heat until the sugar has dissolved and the butter has melted.
Bring the sauce to the boil and stir for a minute.
Pour half the sauce over the warm pudding and serve with the remaining sauce, cream or ice cream.

ingredients.note: 2 large eggs assumed ~50g each
1 tsp ≈4g
1 tsp ≈5g
3 tbsp ≈20g each
275ml milk ≈275g

1 tbsp ≈20g
300ml cream ≈300g
1 tsp ≈4g

title: Lancashire hotpot
instructions: Heat your oven to 160C (fan 140C, gas 3).
Melt some butter in a large shallow casserole and brown the lamb in batches, moving each batch to a plate as it colours.
Brown the kidneys in the same pan and set aside with the lamb.
Add a little more butter to the pan and fry the onions and carrots until golden.
Sprinkle in the flour and cook for a couple of minutes, stirring.
Shake in the Worcestershire sauce, then pour in the stock and bring to the boil.
Stir the browned lamb, kidneys and bay leaves back into the pan, then take off the heat.
Layer the sliced potatoes on top of the meat and drizzle with a little more butter.
Cover the casserole and bake for about 1½ hours, until the potatoes are tender.
Uncover, brush the potatoes with a little more butter, then raise the oven heat (or finish under the grill for 5-8 minutes) until the potatoes are browned on top.

ingredients.note: Used to brown meat and soften veg but the fat stays in the one-pot stew and is served, so counted as eaten, not a frying medium.

title: Bubble & Squeak
instructions: Melt the butter in a non-stick pan and let it get properly hot.
Toss in the bacon and let it start to brown.
Add the onion and garlic and cook them together until fragrant.
Stir in the sliced Brussels sprouts and let them pick up a little colour, about 5-6 minutes total for this stage.
Add the potato and work everything together in the pan.
Press the mixture down so it covers the base, let it catch and colour on the bottom, then flip and repeat on the other side.
Cut into wedges and serve.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp butter estimated as 14 g; stays in the dish so not counted as a frying medium
4 rashers estimated at 25 g each; no explicit weight given
1 medium onion estimated at 110 g
1 clove estimated at 5 g
20 sprouts estimated at ~15 g each; no explicit weight given

title: Spotted Dick
instructions: Sift the flour and salt into a mixing bowl.
Stir in the suet, currants, sugar, and the lemon and orange zest.
Pour in 150ml of milk and mix to a firm but moist dough, adding a splash more milk if it's too dry.
Shape the dough into a fat roll about 20cm long and set it on a large sheet of baking parchment.
Wrap it loosely, leaving room for the pudding to rise, and twist the ends like a cracker, tying with string.
Set a steamer over a large pan of boiling water, lower in the wrapped pudding, cover, and steam for 1½ hours, topping up the water as needed.
Lift the pudding out, let it cool slightly, then unwrap.
Slice and serve warm with custard.

ingredients.note: 'pinch' inferred as ~1g
'zest of 1 lemon' converted to ~5g estimate
'zest of 1 orange' converted to ~5g estimate
150ml converted 1:1 to grams
'to serve' with no quantity given; estimated 30g custard per serving across 6 servings

title: Kedgeree
instructions: Heat the oil in a large lidded pan and gently fry the onion for about 5 minutes until softened but not coloured.
Stir in the spices and a pinch of salt, and keep frying for about 3 minutes until fragrant and starting to brown.
Tip in the rice and stir well to coat it in the spiced onion mix.
Pour in 600ml water, stir, and bring to the boil.
Reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook for 10 minutes.
Take the pan off the heat and let it stand, still covered, for 10-15 minutes more without lifting the lid.
Meanwhile, put the smoked haddock and bay leaves in a frying pan, cover with the milk, and poach for about 10 minutes until the fish flakes easily.
Lift the haddock out of the milk, peel away the skin, and flake the flesh into thumb-sized pieces.
Put the eggs in a pan, cover with water, bring to the boil, then reduce to a simmer for 4½-5 minutes.
Plunge the eggs into cold water, then peel and cut into quarters.
Gently mix the flaked fish, egg quarters, parsley, coriander and cooked rice together in the pan.
Serve hot, sprinkled with a few extra herbs.

ingredients.note: 2 bay leaves; weight inferred (~0.5g each)
used as poaching liquid for the haddock; most is drained off with only residual moisture retained in the flaked fish, so full 300g may overstate what's actually eaten

"Handful" fresh coriander - weight inferred at ~10g

title: Eccles Cakes
instructions: Dice the butter and freeze it until rock hard.
Pulse the flour with half the frozen butter in a food processor until it looks like breadcrumbs.
Add the lemon juice and 100ml iced water, pulsing until a rough dough forms.
Tip in the remaining butter and pulse a few times only, so the dough stays heavily flecked with butter for flakiness.
Roll the dough on a floured surface into a neat 20 x 30cm rectangle.
Fold both ends into the middle, then fold in half.
Roll out again and repeat the fold three more times, resting the dough at least 15 minutes between each roll and fold.
Chill the finished pastry in the fridge for at least 30 minutes before using.
Melt the 25g butter in a large saucepan, then take it off the heat.
Stir in the currants, mixed peel, muscovado sugar, cinnamon, ginger, allspice and lemon zest until fully combined, then set the filling aside.
Roll the pastry out to just over the thickness of a £1 coin and cut 8 rounds about 12cm across, re-rolling trimmings as needed.
Spoon a heaped tablespoon of filling into the centre of each round.
Brush the edges with water, gather the pastry around the filling and pinch to seal.
Flip each parcel smooth-side up and pat into a neat round.
Flatten each with a rolling pin into an oval until the fruit just starts to show through, then place on a baking tray.
Cut two small slits in the top of each cake, brush generously with beaten egg, and sprinkle with sugar.
Heat the oven to 220C/200C fan/gas 8 and bake the cakes for 15-20 minutes until just past golden brown and sticky.
Cool on a rack and serve warm or cold with tea, or with a wedge of tangy British cheese like Lancashire or cheddar.

ingredients.note: Servings inferred from stated yield: 'cut out 8 rounds about 12cm across' → 8 cakes.
Inferred grams for 'juice of 1/2 lemon' ≈15g.
1 tsp ground spice ≈2.6g, inferred.
1 tsp ≈2g, inferred.
1 tsp ≈2g, inferred.

title: Summer Pudding
instructions: Wash the fruit and pat dry with kitchen paper, keeping the strawberries separate from the rest.
Put the caster sugar and 3 tbsp water in a large pan and heat gently, stirring a few times, until the sugar dissolves.
Bring to the boil for 1 minute, then tip in the blackberries, redcurrants and raspberries.
Cook over a low heat for 3 minutes, stirring 2-3 times, until the fruit is softened but mostly intact and surrounded by dark red juice.
Set a sieve over a bowl and tip in the fruit and juice to separate them.
Line a 1.25-litre pudding basin with two overlapping pieces of cling film, leaving about 15cm overhang.
Cut the crusts off the bread, then cut 4 slices in half at an angle to make lopsided rectangles.
Cut 2 slices into 4 triangles each and leave the final slice whole.
Dip the whole slice of bread briefly in the juice and press it into the base of the basin.
Dip the rectangular pieces one at a time and press them around the sides of the basin, fitting them snugly together.
Trim and dip any awkward last piece to fill gaps as needed.
Spoon the softened fruit into the bread-lined basin, tucking in the reserved strawberries as you go.
Dip the bread triangles in juice and lay them on top, trimming off any overhang.
Reserve the leftover juice for serving.
Fold the cling film over the top, loosely seal, then weight down with a plate and cans.
Chill for 6 hours or overnight.
To serve, unwrap the cling film, invert onto a serving plate, and serve with the reserved juice, extra berries and cream.

ingredients.note: kept raw and folded in with the cooked fruit, not simmered
gram weight inferred: assumed ~40g per slice for 7 slices of sandwich bread, no weight stated in text

title: Jam Roly-Poly
instructions: Set a deep roasting tin on the oven's bottom shelf with a free shelf right above it, then fill the tin two-thirds with boiling water and slide it back in.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4.
Layer a large sheet of greaseproof paper on top of foil, about 30 x 40cm, and butter the paper.
Pulse the butter, flour and vanilla seeds in a food processor until the butter disappears, then tip into a mixing bowl.
Stir in the suet, pour in the milk, and mix with a cutlery knife until you have a sticky dough, adding a splash more milk if needed.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface, pat it smooth, and roll into a roughly 25 x 25cm square.
Spread the jam over the dough, leaving one edge bare, then roll up from the opposite edge.
Pinch the jam-free edge to seal and pinch the ends closed.
Lift the roll onto the buttered paper join-side down, then wrap loosely in the paper and foil, sealing the edges without wrapping tightly so it can puff.
Set the wrapped pudding on the rack directly above the water-filled tin and bake for 1 hour.
Let the pudding rest for 5 minutes, then unwrap and slice thickly to serve with custard.

ingredients.note: '1 small vanilla' interpreted as a small vanilla pod (seeds scraped); weight inferred, not stated
150ml converted to grams assuming 1ml≈1g
listed 'to serve' with no quantity given; amount left unset as it's a garnish, not summed into the roly-poly itself

title: Blackberry Fool
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200C/180C fan/Gas 6 and line two large baking trays with parchment.
Scatter the hazelnuts on a tray and roast for 6-8 minutes until golden, watching closely so they don't burn.
Let the nuts cool on a board, then roughly chop them.
Beat the butter and caster sugar together until light and creamy.
Fold in the chopped nuts, lemon zest, flour and baking powder, mixing until it forms a dough ball (use your hands if needed).
Divide the dough into 24 pieces, roll into balls, and space them well apart on the trays.
Flatten each ball to about 1cm thick.
Bake one tray at a time for 12 minutes until pale golden - they'll firm up as they cool.
Cool the biscuits completely on the trays; store airtight and eat within five days.
Rinse the blackberries and put them in a non-stick saucepan with the caster sugar.
Stir in the lemon juice and heat gently for two minutes until the berries soften and release their juice.
Set aside 12 blackberries for decoration, then simmer the rest gently for 15 minutes until soft and squidgy.
Press the cooked berries through a sieve into a bowl to extract a smooth purée, discarding the seeds; you should get about 325ml.
Let the purée cool completely.
Whip the cream and yoghurt together until soft peaks form (don't overwhip - the fruit's acidity will thicken it further).
Taste the cooled purée and stir in extra sugar if needed.
Fold the purée into the whipped cream and yoghurt with just a couple of stirs to keep it marbled, not fully blended.
Spoon the fool into individual glass dishes or one large bowl.
Scatter mint leaves on top, decorate with the reserved blackberries, and add a sprinkle of sugar if you like.
Serve with the hazelnut biscuits.

ingredients.note: Amount not specified in original ('Grated Lemon'); estimated as zest of one lemon (~5g).
Converted from ½ tsp (~2g).

No stated yield in the recipe text; servings inferred as 6 based on total fool volume (~725ml purée+cream+yoghurt) portioned into individual glass dishes, cross-checked against typical dessert serving sizes.

Converted from 1 tbsp lemon juice (~15ml).
Converted from 300ml double cream using ~1:1 ml-to-g ratio.
Converted from 100ml yogurt using ~1:1 ml-to-g ratio.
Amount not specified in original ('Garnish with Mint'); estimated a small garnish quantity (~2g).

title: Treacle Tart
instructions: Rub the butter into the flour until it looks like fine breadcrumbs, using your fingertips or a food processor.
Mix in about three tablespoons of cold water to form a firm dough, then wrap and chill in the fridge for 20 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6 and put a heavy baking tray in to warm up.
Grease a deep 18cm loose-bottomed fluted flan tin with butter.
Cut off about 150g of the pastry and set it aside for the lattice top.
Roll out the remaining pastry thinly on a floured surface and line the flan tin with it.
Prick the base all over with a fork so it doesn't puff up while baking.
Roll out the reserved lattice pastry thinly on cling film, brush with egg wash, then chill it in the fridge without cutting it into strips yet.
Gently heat the golden syrup in a large pan, being careful not to let it boil.
Once melted, stir in the breadcrumbs, lemon juice and zest, adding a few more breadcrumbs if the mixture looks too runny.
Pour the syrup filling into the lined tin and level the surface.
Take the chilled lattice pastry and cut it into long strips about 1cm wide, longer than the tin's edges.
Brush the edge of the tart pastry with egg wash, then weave the strips over the filling in a lattice pattern, letting them overhang the edge.
Press down along the rim with your hands to trim off the excess pastry strips for a neat finish.
Bake on the hot tray for about 10 minutes until the pastry starts to colour, then lower the oven to 180C/350F/Gas 4, covering with foil if the lattice browns too fast.
Continue baking for a further 25-30 minutes until the pastry is golden and the filling is set.
Remove from the oven and let the tart firm up in the tin before serving warm or cold.

ingredients.note: Zest of 2 lemons; grams inferred at ~4g per lemon zest.
Used as an egg wash for the pastry lattice, not as a filling ingredient; weight inferred for 1 beaten egg.

title: Apple & Blackberry Crumble
instructions: Heat your oven to 190°C (170°C fan/gas 5).
Tip the flour and caster sugar into a large bowl, add the butter, and rub together with your fingertips until it looks like light breadcrumbs — don't overwork it or the crumble turns heavy.
Spread the crumble mix evenly over a baking sheet and bake for 15 minutes, or until lightly coloured.
Meanwhile, peel, core and dice the apples into 2cm pieces.
Melt the butter and demerara sugar together in a medium saucepan over medium heat, and cook for 3 minutes until it turns to a light caramel.
Stir in the apples and cook for 3 minutes more.
Add the blackberries and cinnamon, and cook for a further 3 minutes.
Cover, take off the heat, and let it sit for 2-3 minutes to finish cooking in the pan's warmth.
Spoon the warm fruit compote into an ovenproof gratin dish, scatter the baked crumble mix over the top, and reheat in the oven for 5-10 minutes.
Serve warm with a scoop of vanilla ice cream.

ingredients.note: converted from 1/4 tsp cinnamon (~1g)
no amount given for 'to serve' ice cream; inferred ~60g per serving for 4 servings

title: Battenberg Cake
instructions: Heat oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Line the base and sides of a 20cm square tin with baking parchment, crossing two 20cm strips over the base.
Make the almond sponge: beat butter, caster sugar, self-raising flour, ground almonds, baking powder, eggs, vanilla and almond extract in a large bowl with an electric whisk until smooth.
Scrape into the tin, spread to the corners and bake 25-30 mins, until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin 10 mins, then finish on a wire rack.
Make the pink sponge the same way but omit the almond extract and fold in pink food colouring. Bake and cool as before.
Heat the apricot jam until runny, then sieve. Trim the sponges square, then cut each into 4 slices the same width as the sponge height. Trim to equal lengths.
Roll one marzipan block on icing-sugar-dusted surface to just over 20cm wide and about 0.5cm thick. Brush with jam.

Lay a pink and an almond slice side by side on the marzipan, jam between them, leaving 4cm marzipan clear at the end. Brush jam on top and sandwich the remaining 2 slices on, alternating colours for a checkerboard.
Trim marzipan to length, lift and smooth over the cake, leaving a small fold along the bottom edge. Crimp the edges with fingers or a fork and mark the 10 slices.
Repeat to assemble the second Battenberg. Keep airtight or wrapped up to 3 days, or freeze up to a month.

ingredients.note: Instructions specify ground almonds; mapped to ground almonds key.
½ tsp inferred as ~2.5g.
3 medium eggs inferred as ~50g each = 150g.
½ tsp inferred as ~2.5g.
¼ tsp inferred as ~1.25g.

title: English Breakfast
instructions: Heat your flat grill plate over a low flame and brush it sparingly with olive oil.
Start the sausages first, letting them cook slowly for 15-20 minutes, turning occasionally, until golden; after the first 10 minutes, bump the heat up to medium.
Snip a few cuts into the bacon's fatty edge, then lay it on the grill and fry 2-4 minutes per side until as crisp as you like.

Brush the mushrooms clean, trim the stalks level, season with salt, pepper and a little olive oil, then grill stalk-side up for 1-2 minutes before flipping and cooking 3-4 minutes more without disturbing them.
Halve the tomatoes and cut out the green eye, season and oil them, then grill cut-side down for 2 minutes undisturbed, flip, season again and cook 2-3 minutes more until tender but holding shape.
Slice the black pudding into rounds, peel off the skin, and grill 1½-2 minutes per side until slightly crisp.
In a separate oiled pan over medium heat, fry the bread 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crisp, adding a knob of butter partway through if you want extra richness.
Crack the eggs into the same pan as the bread, baste with melted butter, and cook to your preferred doneness, then season and lift out with a fish slice.
Serve everything hot on warmed plates straight away with a good squeeze of ketchup or brown sauce.

ingredients.note: grams inferred: 2 sausages at ~50g each raw
grams inferred: 3 rashers at ~25g each raw
grams inferred: 2 mushrooms at ~50g each
grams inferred: 2 tomatoes at ~100g each
grams inferred: 1 slice at ~30g
grams inferred: 2 eggs at ~50g each
grams inferred: 1 slice at ~35g; no yield stated in recipe, servings inferred as 1 portion from listed quantities

title: Full English Breakfast
instructions: Warm the flat grill plate over low heat, using two rings if it spans that far, and brush it lightly with olive oil.
Lay the sausages on the grill first, letting them cook slowly for 15-20 minutes, turning now and then until golden all over; bump the heat to medium after the first 10 minutes.
Snip a few cuts into the bacon's fatty edge, then fry it straight on the grill for 2-4 minutes a side until it's as crisp as you like.

Brush the mushrooms clean, trim the stalks flush, season with salt and pepper, drizzle with olive oil, and grill stalk-side up for 1-2 minutes, then flip and cook 3-4 minutes more without fussing over them.

Halve the tomatoes and cut out the green eye, season and drizzle with oil, then grill cut-side down for 2 minutes undisturbed, flip, season again, and cook 2-3 minutes more until tender but holding shape.
Slice the black pudding into 3-4 rounds, peel off the skin, and grill for 1½-2 minutes per side until slightly crisp.
Fry the bread separately in a well-oiled pan over medium heat for 2-3 minutes a side until golden and crisp, adding a knob of butter partway through if you want extra richness.
Crack the eggs into the same pan as the bread, let them set for 30 seconds, then baste with a melting knob of butter until cooked to your liking, season, and lift out with a fish slice.
Plate everything hot together and serve at once with ketchup or brown sauce on the side.

ingredients.note: No yield stated; 4 sausages estimated at ~50g raw each.
4 rashers estimated at ~30g each raw.
4 mushrooms estimated at ~40g each (flat mushrooms typical for a full English).
3 tomatoes estimated at ~80g each.
2 black pudding pieces (before slicing into 3-4 rounds each) estimated at ~50g each.
2 eggs estimated at ~50g each, standard egg weight.
1 slice estimated at ~35g, standard bread slice weight.
100g stated explicitly in ingredient list; instructions never mention preparing or heating the beans.

title: Carrot Cake
instructions: Preheat the oven to 160C/325F/Gas 3, then grease and line a 26cm springform tin.
Combine all the cake ingredients except the carrots and walnuts in a bowl until smooth.
Fold in the grated carrots and walnuts.
Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and bake for 1 hour 15 minutes, until a skewer comes out clean.
Cool the cake in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn it out onto a rack to cool completely.
Beat the cream cheese, caster sugar and butter together until fluffy to make the icing.
Spread the icing over the cooled cake with a palette knife.

ingredients.note: 450ml converted to grams using oil density ~0.92g/ml
2 tsp converted to grams (~5g/tsp)

title: Chelsea Buns
instructions: Sift the flour and salt into a large bowl and make a well in the centre.
Add the yeast to the well.
Warm the milk and 40g butter in a saucepan until the butter melts and the mixture is lukewarm.
Pour the milk mixture and the egg into the flour and stir until a soft dough forms, adding a little extra flour if needed.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface and knead for five minutes until smooth and elastic.
Lightly oil a bowl with the vegetable oil, place the dough in it, turn to coat, cover with cling film and let rise in a warm spot for about an hour until doubled.
Grease a baking tray.
Knock back the risen dough and roll it out on a floured surface into a rectangle about 0.5cm thick.
Brush the rectangle all over with the melted butter, then scatter over the brown sugar, cinnamon and dried fruit.
Roll the dough up tightly into a cylinder and cut into ten slices about 4cm thick.
Arrange the slices on the greased baking sheet with a little space between each, cover with a tea towel and let rise for 30 minutes.
Preheat the oven to 190C/375F/Gas 5.
Bake the buns for 20-25 minutes until risen and golden-brown.
While the buns bake, heat the milk and caster sugar in a saucepan until boiling, then simmer for 2-3 minutes to make the glaze.
Remove the buns from the oven, brush with the glaze, and cool on a wire rack.

ingredients.note: 1 tsp converted to ~6g by standard volume-to-weight estimate
'1 Eggs' assumed to be one medium egg (~50g)
'Dash' estimated as ~5g; used only to grease the proofing bowl, not a frying/searing medium and not part of the served food
2 tsp converted to ~5g by standard volume-to-weight estimate
2 tbsp converted to ~30g by standard volume-to-weight estimate
2 tbsp converted to ~25g by standard volume-to-weight estimate

title: Dundee cake
instructions: Cover the almonds with boiling water in a small bowl, let them sit for 5 minutes, then drain and dry them.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/Gas Mark 4 and line a deep 20cm loose-based cake tin with baking parchment.
Beat the butter in a large bowl until soft, then beat in the muscovado sugar until light and fluffy.
Stir in the orange zest and apricot jam.
Sieve the flour and baking powder together.
Beat the eggs into the creamed butter mixture a little at a time, adding a spoonful of flour if it starts to curdle.
Fold in the remaining flour and ground almonds until well combined.
Mix in the milk, then gently fold in the dried fruit and glace cherries.
Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and level the top with the back of a spoon.
Arrange the whole almonds in neat circles over the top of the cake.
Bake for 45 minutes at the higher temperature.

Lower the oven to 160C/140C fan/Gas Mark 3 and continue baking for 60-80 minutes, checking from 50 minutes with a skewer that should come out with just a few crumbs attached, and checking every 10 minutes after that so the centre stays a little soft.
Once cooked, pull the cake briefly from the oven.
Warm the milk and caster sugar together in a small pan until the sugar dissolves, brush this glaze over the top of the cake, then return it to the oven for 2-3 minutes.
Cool the cake completely in the tin before turning out, wrapping in foil, and resting for at least 2 days before cutting.

ingredients.note: inferred: zest of 1 orange ≈6g
inferred: 3 tbsp ≈20g each
inferred: 1 tsp ≈4g
inferred: 3 large eggs ≈60g each
inferred: 2 tbsp ≈15g each
inferred: 1 tbsp ≈15g, used for glaze
inferred: 2 tsp ≈4g each, used for glaze

title: Madeira Cake
instructions: Heat the oven to 180C/350F/Gas 4.
Grease an 18cm round cake tin, line the base with greaseproof paper, and grease the paper too.
Cream the butter and sugar together until pale and fluffy.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, mixing well after each, adding a spoonful of the flour with the last egg to stop the batter curdling.
Sift in the remaining flour and fold gently, adding enough milk so the mixture falls slowly off the spoon.
Fold in the lemon zest.
Spoon the batter into the tin and level the top.
Bake in the middle of the oven for 30-40 minutes, until golden and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
Decorate with the candied peel before serving.

ingredients.note: 3 eggs estimated at 50g each
3 tbs milk estimated at 15g per tbs
zest of 1 lemon estimated at 5g
amount for glaze not specified in original; estimated a light garnish quantity of 20g

title: Rock Cakes
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180C/350F/Gas 4 and line a baking tray with parchment.
Mix the flour, sugar and baking powder in a bowl, then rub in the cubed butter until it looks like breadcrumbs.
Stir in the dried fruit.
In a separate bowl, beat the egg with the milk and vanilla extract.
Pour the egg mixture into the dry ingredients and stir with a spoon until it just comes together as a thick, lumpy dough, adding a splash more milk only if needed.
Spoon golfball-sized mounds of the mixture onto the prepared tray, leaving space as they spread and roughly double in size.
Bake for 15-20 minutes until golden-brown.
Let the rock cakes cool on the tray for a couple of minutes, then transfer to a wire rack to cool fully.

ingredients.note: 1 tsp estimated as 4g
rubbed into dough and baked into the cakes, not a frying medium
1 egg estimated as 50g
1 tbsp estimated as 15g
2 tsp estimated as 8g

title: Parkin Cake
instructions: Heat your oven to 160C/140C fan/gas 3.
Grease a deep 22cm/9in square cake tin and line it with baking parchment.
Beat the egg and milk together with a fork and set aside.
Gently melt the syrup, treacle, sugar and butter together in a large pan until the sugar has fully dissolved, then take the pan off the heat.
Mix the oatmeal, flour and ginger together, then stir this into the syrup mixture.
Stir in the egg and milk mixture until combined.
Pour the batter into the prepared tin.
Bake for 50 minutes to 1 hour, until the cake feels firm and a little crusty on top.
Cool the cake in the tin, then wrap it in fresh parchment and foil.
Keep for 3-5 days before eating if you can bear to wait — it gets softer and stickier the longer you leave it, up to 2 weeks.

ingredients.note: "1 large Egg" — estimated ~50g for a large egg
"4 tbs Milk" — estimated ~60g (1 tbsp ≈ 15g)
"1 tbs Ground Ginger" — estimated ~6g

title: French Onion Soup
instructions: Melt the butter with the olive oil in a large heavy-based pan.
Tip in the onions, cover with the lid, and let them fry gently for about 10 minutes until soft.

Sprinkle in the sugar and keep cooking, stirring frequently, for another 20 minutes until the onions turn deeply golden, soft, and full of flavour — watch closely near the end so they don't catch and burn.
Stir in the garlic for the last few minutes of the onions' cooking time.
Sprinkle over the flour and stir well to coat everything.
Turn up the heat and gradually pour in the wine, stirring constantly, then add the hot stock.
Cover and simmer for 15-20 minutes.
When ready to serve, heat the grill and toast the bread slices.
Ladle the soup into heatproof bowls, top each with a slice or two of toast, and pile on the Gruyère.
Grill until the cheese is melted and bubbling, or melt the cheese on the toasts separately under the grill before placing them on top of the soup.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp converted to ~14 g
1 tsp converted to ~4 g
4 cloves estimated at ~5 g each
2 tbsp converted to ~16 g
4 slices estimated at ~35 g each

title: Beef Bourguignon
instructions: Melt the goose fat in a large casserole over medium-high heat.
Season the beef shin and sear it in 2-3 batches until browned all over, adding more fat as needed; transfer each batch to a colander set over a bowl to catch the juices.
In the same pan, fry the bacon, shallots, mushrooms, garlic and bouquet garni until lightly coloured.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for a few minutes to enrich the base.
Return the beef and its drained juices to the pan and stir through.
Pour in the red wine and about 100ml water so the meat is mostly submerged, then bring to the boil, scraping up the browned bits from the bottom.
Heat the oven to 150C/fan 130C/gas 2.
Cover the stew with a foil cartouche and braise in the oven for 3 hours.
If the sauce looks thin, lift out the beef and veg, then reduce the sauce over high heat before returning everything to the pan.
For the celeriac mash, peel and cube the celeriac.
Heat the olive oil in a frying pan and fry the celeriac for 5 minutes until golden, seasoning with salt and pepper.
Add the rosemary, thyme, bay and cardamom, pour in 200ml water to nearly cover, then partially cover and simmer on low for 25-30 minutes.
Drain any remaining water, discard the herb sprigs, bay and cardamom, then crush the celeriac roughly with a masher.
Finish with a glug of olive oil and season to taste.
Spoon the beef bourguignon into bowls, top with the celeriac mash, and garnish with a bay leaf if you like.

title: Chocolate Souffle
instructions: Heat the oven to 220°C/fan 200°C/gas 7 and slide a baking tray onto the top shelf.
For the sauce, heat the cream and sugar together until boiling.
Take off the heat, stir in the chocolate and butter until melted and smooth, then keep it warm.
Brush six 150ml ramekins with melted butter, coat with caster sugar, and tip out the excess.
Melt the chocolate and cream together in a bowl set over simmering water, cool slightly, then beat in the egg yolks.
Whisk the egg whites until they hold their shape, then whisk in the sugar a tablespoon at a time until glossy.
Fold a spoonful of the whites into the chocolate mixture to loosen it, then gently fold in the rest.
Working quickly, fill the ramekins, wipe the rims clean and run a thumb around the edges.
Turn the oven down to 200°C/fan 180°C/gas 6, set the ramekins on the tray, and bake for 8-10 minutes until risen with a slight wobble.
Resist opening the oven door too early or the soufflés may collapse.
Once baked, dust the tops with icing sugar, scoop out a small hole, and pour in some of the hot chocolate sauce.
Replace the lids and serve immediately.

amount not specified ('drizzle'); inferred ~5 g for greasing ramekins

title: Flamiche
instructions: Sift the flour and salt into a food processor, add the butter and lard, then pulse until it looks like fine breadcrumbs.
Tip into a bowl, stir in the grated cheddar, then add just enough water to bring the dough together.
Knead briefly on a floured surface until smooth, then roll out thinly and line a 23cm x 4cm loose-bottomed fluted flan tin.
Prick the base all over with a fork and chill for 20 minutes.
Melt the 75g butter in a saucepan over low heat, add the leeks and salt, cover and cook for 10 minutes until soft.
Uncover, raise the heat and cook for 2 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the liquid has evaporated, then spoon onto a plate to cool.
Preheat the oven to 200°C/fan180°C/gas 6.
Line the pastry case with baking paper and baking beans (or rice) and blind bake for 15-20 minutes until the edges turn biscuit-coloured.
Remove the paper and beans, then return the case to the oven for 7-10 minutes until the base is crisp and lightly golden; set aside and lower the oven to 190°C/fan170°C/gas 5.
Whisk the crème fraîche with the whole egg, egg yolks and nutmeg in a bowl, then season.
Stir in the cooled cooked leeks, spoon the mixture into the tart case, and bake for 35-40 minutes until set and lightly golden.
Rest the tart for 10 minutes, then unmould and serve.

½ tsp salt inferred as ~3g

title: Duck Confit
instructions: Scatter half the sea salt, garlic and thyme over the base of a small shallow dish.
Lay the duck legs skin-side up on top, then scatter the remaining salt, garlic and thyme over them.
Cover and refrigerate overnight (or up to 2 days) to cure.
The next day, brush the salt off the duck legs.
Pour the white wine into a saucepan that fits the duck legs snugly in a single layer.
Place the duck legs skin-side down in the wine, cover with a lid, and set over medium heat.
Once the wine starts to bubble, turn the heat to the lowest setting and let it barely simmer for 2 hours, checking occasionally.
After 2 hours the duck should be submerged in its own fat and fork-tender; let it cool.
Eat the duck now, or pack it tightly into a container, cover with the rendered fat (leaving the liquid behind), and refrigerate up to a month or freeze up to 3 months.
Save the leftover liquid in the pan to use as a gravy; chill or freeze until needed.
To crisp the duck, heat the oven to 220C/fan 200C/gas 7.
Remove the duck legs from the fat and place skin-side down in an ovenproof frying pan.
Roast for 30-40 minutes, turning halfway, until browned and crisp.
Serve with the reheated gravy, a crisp salad and golden potatoes.

ingredients.note: 'Handful' amount inferred; ~30g estimate for curing salt, most is brushed off before cooking
4 leaves; ~0.5g each estimated, standard dried bay leaf weight
4 cloves; ~3g each estimated
'Handful' amount inferred; ~5g estimate
4 legs; ~350g each raw weight estimated (typical duck leg size); no stated servings, inferred 4 servings (one leg per person) based on 4 legs used

title: Ratatouille
instructions: Cut the aubergines in half lengthways, then cut each half lengthways again and slice across into 1.5cm chunks.
Trim the courgette ends and slice into 1.5cm rounds.
Peel the peppers, cut around the stalk into three pieces, remove the membrane, and chop into bite-size chunks.
Score a small cross on the base of each tomato and place in a heatproof bowl.
Pour boiling water over the tomatoes, leave for 20 seconds, then drain.
Cover the tomatoes with cold water, let cool, then peel away the skins.
Quarter the tomatoes, scrape out the seeds with a spoon, then roughly chop the flesh.
Heat a sauté pan over medium heat and add 2 tbsp olive oil.
Brown the aubergine pieces for 5 minutes on each side until soft, then set aside.
Fry the courgettes in another tbsp of oil for 5 minutes until golden on both sides, then set aside.
Repeat the frying process with the peppers, being careful not to overcook since they'll cook further later.
Tear the basil leaves and set aside.
Cook the onion in the same pan for 5 minutes.
Add the garlic and fry for another minute.
Stir in the vinegar and sugar, then add the chopped tomatoes and half the basil.
Return the browned vegetables to the pan, season with salt and pepper, and cook for 5 minutes.
Serve garnished with the remaining basil.

estimated 150g per courgette x4
estimated 150g per pepper x2
estimated 150g per large tomato x4
5 tbsp olive oil (~13.5g/tbsp); oil remains in the finished stew, not drained

title: Tarte Tatin
instructions: Roll the puff pastry into a 3mm-thick round and trim it to a 24cm circle using a plate as a guide.
Prick the pastry all over with a fork, wrap it in cling film on a baking sheet, and freeze while you prep the apples.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4.
Peel, quarter, and core the apples.
Put the sugar in a flameproof 20cm Tatin dish or ovenproof frying pan over medium-high heat and cook to a dark amber caramel, 5-7 minutes, until it just starts to smoke.
Turn off the heat and stir the diced chilled butter into the caramel.
Arrange the apple quarters tightly rounded-side down around the edge of the dish, then fill the centre the same way, pressing to close any gaps.
Brush the apples with melted butter.
Bake for 30 minutes.
Lay the frozen pastry disc on top of the apples — it will defrost quickly — and tuck the edges down inside the dish.
Prick a few holes in the pastry to let steam escape and bake for a further 40-45 minutes until golden and crisp.
Cool for 1 hour, then run a knife around the edge and invert onto a deep serving plate to catch the juices.
Serve with crème fraîche or vanilla ice cream.

ingredients.note: dusting amount not quantified in original; estimated small amount for rolling out pastry
6 apples, no weight given; estimated 150g per apple
combines 60g diced chilled butter stirred into caramel and remaining butter melted for brushing fruit; stays in the dish, not a frying medium

title: Chinon Apple Tarts
instructions: Combine red wine, jam sugar, star anise, clove, cinnamon stick, allspice, and the split vanilla pod with its seeds in a saucepan; heat gently until the sugar dissolves.
Turn up the heat and boil for 20 minutes until reduced to a syrup.
Strain into a sterilised jam jar and let cool completely; it keeps refrigerated for up to a month.
Rest the puff pastry at room temperature for 10 minutes, then unroll it.
Heat the grill to high and preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan / gas 4).
Cut two 13cm circles from the pastry and place on a non-stick baking sheet.
Sprinkle each circle with 1 tbsp sugar and grill for 5 minutes to caramelise, watching closely so it doesn't burn.
Remove from the grill; this stage can be done hours ahead and left covered at room temperature.
Peel, quarter and core the apples, slice them 2mm thin, and arrange on top of the pastry.
Sprinkle the remaining sugar over the apples and bake for 20-25 minutes until the pastry is cooked through and golden and the apples are soft.
Let the tarts cool slightly.
Warm 3 tbsp of the red wine jelly with 1 tsp water in a small pan over low heat until runny, then brush over the tarts.
Mix the crème fraîche with sifted icing sugar and cardamom in a bowl.
Lift the warm tarts onto plates and serve with the cardamom crème fraîche.

ingredients.note: converted from 4 tbsp (~12g each)
no weight given; estimated ~180g per apple x3
converted from 4 tbsp (~20g each); homemade jelly, only part of batch used
converted from 1 tbsp (~8g)
assumed 3 pods at ~0.6g each; exact weight uncertain

title: Summer Pistou
instructions: Warm the rapeseed oil in a large pan and fry the leeks and courgette for about 5 minutes until softened.
Pour in the vegetable stock, then add three-quarters of the cannellini beans along with the green beans and half the chopped tomatoes.
Simmer for 5-8 minutes until the vegetables are tender.
Meanwhile, blitz the remaining beans and tomatoes with the garlic and basil until smooth, then stir in the Parmesan to make the pistou.
Stir the pistou into the soup and cook for 1 minute more.
Ladle half into bowls to serve now, and chill the rest — it keeps for a couple of days.

ingredients.note: soup base — oil stays in the dish, not a frying medium
