'1 large courgette' — grams inferred from typical size
'3 chopped tomatoes' — grams inferred at ~50g each
'3 chopped garlic cloves' — grams inferred at ~3g each
'small pack basil' — grams inferred, typical small herb pack

title: Three-cheese souffles
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan/gas 6) and butter four small (about 200ml) ramekins.
Coat the inside of each ramekin with the Parmesan, turning until every surface is covered.
Warm the milk with the bay leaves in a large saucepan, bring just to the boil, then take off the heat and let it infuse for 15 minutes.
Discard the bay leaves, stir in the butter and flour, and return the pan to a low heat.
Whisk continuously for about 6 minutes until you have a smooth, thick white sauce, working right into the corners of the pan so it doesn't catch or go lumpy.
Tip the sauce into a large bowl and beat in the mustard powder, cayenne pepper, Gruyère and egg yolks until fully combined.
In a spotlessly clean bowl with a clean whisk, beat the egg whites until soft peaks just form.
Fold the egg whites into the cheese sauce in three additions, folding gently rather than stirring to keep the mixture light and airy.
Spoon the soufflé mixture into the prepared ramekins.
Top each with a slice of goat's cheese, set on a baking tray, and bake for 20-25 minutes until well risen, springy and cooked through.
Let the soufflés cool, then run a knife around the edge of each and turn them out.
If making ahead, sit the soufflés upside down on a tray, cover with cling film, and chill for a few days or freeze for up to a month.

To rebake, heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan/gas 6), place the upside-down soufflés in a shallow dish, top with the remaining goat's cheese, pour over the cream, and bake for 8-10 minutes until golden.
Serve immediately alongside a simply dressed salad.

ingredients.note: 300ml converted 1:1 to grams
2 leaves, weight inferred (~1g each); discarded after infusing the milk
5 tbsp converted at ~8g/tbsp
½ tsp converted at ~5g/tsp; instructions call it 'mustard powder' but ingredient line specifies English mustard
quantity given only as 'Pod of Cayenne Pepper' — inferred a pinch (~1g)
3 eggs estimated at 50g each; yolks and whites are separated per the method
8 slices estimated at 20g each; split between the first bake topping and the reserved slices used on rebaking
150ml converted 1:1 to grams
listed only as 'to serve' with no quantity; inferred a light side portion (~15g per serving)

title: Brie wrapped in prosciutto & brioche
instructions: Whisk the flour with a teaspoon of salt, caster sugar, yeast, milk and eggs in a stand mixer fitted with the dough hook, and beat for 5 minutes until the dough turns smooth.
Add the butter and keep mixing on medium speed for another 4 minutes.
Scrape down the bowl and give it one more minute in the mixer.
Tip the dough into a container, cover with cling film and chill for at least 6 hours before you shape it.
Wrap the whole Brie in the prosciutto slices and set it aside.
Turn the chilled dough onto a lightly floured surface and roll it into a 25cm circle.
Sit the wrapped Brie in the centre of the dough and fold the edges up and over to seal it neatly into a parcel.
Set the parcel on a parchment-lined baking tray and brush all over with beaten egg.
Chill for 30 minutes, brush again with egg, then chill for a further 30 minutes.
Let the parcel rise at room temperature for 1 hour.
Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6 and bake for 22 minutes.
Serve warm.

ingredients.note: original states '3 Large Eggs'; grams inferred at ~60g per large egg
original just says 'To Glaze Eggs' with no quantity; assumed one beaten egg's worth for brushing
original states '8 slices'; grams inferred at ~15g per slice

title: Boulangère Potatoes
instructions: Crank the oven to 200°C (180°C fan/gas 6).
Sizzle the finely chopped onions and thyme sprigs in the olive oil for about 5 minutes until softened and lightly coloured.
Spread a layer of sliced potatoes over the base of an oiled 1.5-litre gratin dish.
Scatter over a few of the softened onions.
Keep layering potatoes and onions, finishing with a top layer of potatoes.
Pour the vegetable stock over the layered potatoes.
Bake for 50-60 minutes until the potatoes are cooked through and the top is golden and crisp.

ingredients.note: '2 finely chopped Onions' — grams inferred from typical medium onion weight (~100g each).
'sprigs of fresh Thyme' — no quantity given; small amount inferred for aromatic use.
2 tbsp olive oil (~13.5g/tbsp); oil is fried with onions but stays in the dish as they're layered into the gratin, so not a frying medium.

title: French Omelette
instructions: Get every ingredient prepped and within reach before you start.
Warm a 20cm non-stick frying pan over medium heat.
Crack the eggs into a bowl and beat with a fork just enough to break them up and mix, not as smooth as scrambled egg.
Turn the heat to medium-hot and drop in one knob of butter, letting it bubble and sizzle without browning.
Season the eggs with the Parmesan, a little salt and pepper, then pour into the pan.
Let the eggs bubble for a couple of seconds, then use a wooden fork or spatula to gently draw the mixture in from the edges toward the centre a few times so it gathers in folds.
Pause briefly, stir again to combine the uncooked egg with the cooked, then pause once more and stir a touch faster, stopping while some egg is still barely cooked.
Keep the pan flat on the heat and shake it back and forth so the mixture settles, sliding easily and looking soft and moist on top.
Give it a quick burst of heat to brown the underside.
Grip the handle from underneath, tilt the pan away from you, and let the omelette slide to the far edge.
Fold the near side over by a third with your fork and keep rolling it so the omelette tips out onto a plate, or simply fold it in half.
For a neat finish, lay a piece of kitchen paper over the omelette and plump it gently with your fingers.
Rub the second knob of butter over the top to glaze, then serve immediately.

ingredients.note: 3 eggs, ~50g each assumed (standard egg weight); no explicit gram amount given
used to fry and then to glaze the finished omelette, so it stays in the served dish; ~15g per knob assumed
1 tsp grated Parmesan estimated at ~2g
3 chopped tarragon leaves, tiny amount estimated; not referenced in the rewritten method text, likely a fines herbes addition
1 tbsp chopped parsley estimated at ~4g; not referenced in the rewritten method text
1 tbsp chopped chives estimated at ~3g; not referenced in the rewritten method text
key recovered by deterministic re-match; alternative cheese to Parmesan; instructions only call for Parmesan, so Gruyère is treated as a choose-one alternative and not summed

title: Pear Tarte Tatin
instructions: Core, peel and halve the pears neatly; if you like, prep them up to a day ahead and let them dry out uncovered in the fridge.
Tip the sugar, butter, star anise, cardamom and cinnamon into a 20cm ovenproof frying pan and set over high heat until bubbling.
Shake and stir the buttery sauce until it splits and the sugar turns a deep toffee colour.
Lay the pears into the caramel and cook for 10-12 minutes, tossing now and then, until they're thoroughly caramelised - don't fret about burning, it won't happen.
Splash in the brandy, let it flambé, then pull the pears out and set aside.
Heat the oven to 200C/fan 180C/gas 6.
Roll the pastry out to the thickness of a £1 coin, cut a circle slightly larger than the pan using a plate as a guide, and thin the edges by pressing them out.
Once the pears have cooled a little, arrange them cut-side up in the pan in a floral pattern, edges pointing inward.
Rest the cinnamon stick in the centre and scatter the cardamom pods around it.
Drape the pastry over the pears, tucking the edges down the sides and under the fruit.
Pierce the pastry a few times and bake for 15 minutes.
If a lot of juice bubbles up the sides, pour it off now.
Drop the oven to 180C/fan 160C/gas 4 and bake for another 15 minutes until the pastry is golden.
Let the tart rest for 10 minutes, then invert it carefully onto a serving dish.

ingredients.note: 8 pears, weight per pear inferred at ~170g (no gram amount given)
caramel sauce remains as part of the served tart, not a frying medium
2 whole star anise, weight inferred at ~1g each
3 pods, weight inferred at ~0.5g each
1 large cinnamon stick, weight inferred
2 tbsp ≈ 30g; flambéed into the dish, not drained off

title: White chocolate creme brulee
instructions: Warm the double cream, white chocolate chips and vanilla pod together in a pan until the chocolate melts fully.
Take the pan off the heat and let it infuse for 10 minutes, then scrape the vanilla seeds into the cream (if using vanilla extract instead, stir it in right away).
Preheat the oven to 160C/fan 140C/gas 3.
Beat the egg yolks with the caster sugar until pale and thick.
Stir the infused chocolate cream into the egg mixture.
Strain the mixture into a jug, then pour it into ramekins.
Set the ramekins in a deep roasting tray and pour boiling water halfway up their sides.
Bake for 15-20 minutes until just set with a slight wobble in the centre.
Chill in the fridge for at least 4 hours.
To serve, sprinkle caster sugar over the top of each brulee and caramelise with a blowtorch or briefly under a hot grill.
Let the caramel harden before serving.

ingredients.note: 568ml converted to grams using ~1g/ml for cream
weight inferred for a single vanilla pod; recipe gives count only
6 yolks estimated at ~18g each
2 tbsp estimated at ~12.5g each
no quantity given for topping sugar; inferred amount to caramelise 6 ramekins

title: Fish Stew with Rouille
instructions: Twist the heads off the prawns, peel away the shells and legs but leave the tails on, then devein each prawn.
Fry the prawn shells in 1 tablespoon of the olive oil for about 5 minutes until dark pink and golden in patches.
Pour in the white wine, boil until reduced by two-thirds, then add the fish stock.
Strain the liquid into a jug and discard the shells.
Heat the remaining olive oil in a deep frying pan or casserole, add the fennel, onion and garlic, season, cover and cook gently for 10 minutes until softened.
Meanwhile peel the potato, cut into roughly 2cm chunks, boil in cold water for 5 minutes until almost tender, then drain.
Peel a strip of zest from the orange and add it to the pan with the star anise, bay leaf and half the harissa; fry uncovered for 5-10 minutes until the vegetables are soft, sweet and golden.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes, then add the chopped tomatoes and strained stock, and simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly; season to taste.
Scrub the mussels, pull away any beards, and discard any that don't close when tapped sharply.
Reheat the sauce if needed, then gently stir in the potato, fish chunks and prawns, bring back to the boil, cover and simmer for 3 minutes.
Scatter the mussels over the stew, cover and cook for 2 minutes more until the shells open wide, discarding any that stay shut.
Once the fish flakes easily and the prawns are pink through, scatter with the thyme leaves.
Stir the remaining harissa into the mayonnaise to make the quick rouille.
Serve the stew in bowls topped with spoonfuls of rouille and plenty of bread for mopping up the juices.

ingredients.note: weight estimated: 6 large prawns ≈150g
1 of 3 tbsp used to fry prawn shells (discarded after straining), remaining 2 tbsp cooks the vegetables and stays in the dish; classified as non-frying since majority remains in the sauce

title: Fennel Dauphinoise
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (160C fan/gas 4).
Put the potatoes, fennel and garlic into a medium non-stick pan.
Pour in the milk and double cream, season well, cover and simmer gently for about 10 minutes, stirring halfway through, until the potatoes are just tender.
Divide the mixture between two small (about 150ml) buttered ramekins.
Scatter Parmesan over the top.
Bake for 40 minutes until the potatoes are golden and tender when pierced with a knife.
Snip the reserved fennel fronds over the dish just before serving.

ingredients.note: Servings taken from recipe text: 'Divide the mixture between 2 small (about 150ml) ramekins' -> servings=2.

'to serve' - no amount given, estimated ~15g for scattering over two ramekins.

title: Chicken Marengo
instructions: Heat the oil in a large flameproof casserole and stir-fry the mushrooms until they start to soften.
Add the chicken legs and brown them briefly on each side just to colour.
Pour in the passata, crumble in the stock cube, and stir in the olives.
Season with black pepper only — no salt needed.
Cover and simmer for 40 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Sprinkle with parsley and serve with pasta, mash, or a salad and green veg.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp converted to grams; oil remains in the braise as part of the sauce, not a frying medium
4 chicken legs; weight inferred at ~350g each (bone-in) as no gram weight given
1 stock cube; standard cube weight assumed at ~10g
no quantity given for garnish parsley; inferred a small ~5g sprinkle

title: Provençal Omelette Cake
instructions: Crack the eggs into two bowls, five in each, then whisk lightly and season with salt and pepper.
Heat the oil in a pan and fry the courgettes and spring onions gently for about 10 minutes until softened, then cool and stir into one bowl of eggs with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Stir the roasted red peppers, garlic and chilli into the second bowl of eggs with salt and pepper.
Heat a little oil in a 20-23cm non-stick frying pan and pour in about one-third of the courgette egg mixture, swirling to coat the base.
Cook until the egg is set and lightly browned underneath, then flip onto a plate and slide it back into the pan to cook the other side.
Repeat twice more with the remaining courgette mixture, adding a touch of oil each time, to make three omelettes, then stack them on a plate.
Make three more omelettes the same way with the red pepper mixture and stack them separately.
Beat the cream cheese until soft, then beat in the milk until spreadable, and stir in the chives and basil with salt and pepper.
Line a deep 20-23cm round cake tin with cling film and place a red pepper omelette in the base, prettiest side down.
Spread a thin layer of the cheese filling over it, then cover with a courgette omelette, repeating and alternating layers until all the omelettes and filling are used, finishing with an omelette.
Fold the cling film over the top and chill for up to 24 hours.
To serve, invert the cake onto a plate, peel off the cling film, pile rocket on top, scatter over parmesan, drizzle with olive oil and add a little black pepper, then cut into wedges.

ingredients.note: 10 eggs estimated at 50g each = 500g.
1 tbsp used to sauté courgettes and cook the omelettes; oil is absorbed into the dish, not drained, so treated as staying in the food.
2 courgettes estimated at 200g each = 400g; fried and cooled before being stirred into the raw eggs.
3 spring onions estimated at 15g each = 45g; cooked with the courgettes before adding to eggs.
4 red peppers estimated at 120g each = 480g; instructions call for 'roasted peppers' though the roasting step isn't detailed in the method—assumed pre-roasted.
1 clove estimated at 5g; added raw to egg mixture before cooking.
1 chilli estimated at 15g.
6 tbsp estimated at 15g each = 90g.
4 tbsp chopped chives estimated at 3g each = 12g.
2 tbsp chopped basil estimated at 2g each = 4g.
no quantity given for garnish; estimated a small handful ~20g.

title: Prawn & Fennel Bisque
instructions: Peel the prawns and set the flesh aside, keeping the shells.
Fry the shells in the olive oil in a large pan for about 5 minutes until fragrant.
Add the onion, fennel and carrots and cook for 10 minutes until they start to soften.
Pour in the wine and brandy and let it bubble hard for a minute to burn off the alcohol.
Stir in the chopped tomatoes, fish stock and paprika, cover and simmer gently for 30 minutes.
Meanwhile, roughly chop the peeled prawns.
Blitz the soup as smooth as you can with a stick blender, then push it through a sieve into a bowl, working it well for a silky texture.
Pour the strained soup back into a clean pan, add the chopped prawns and cook for 10 minutes.
Blitz again until fully smooth.
Cool and chill for up to a day, or freeze for up to a month, thawing overnight in the fridge before use.
To serve, gently reheat the bisque with the cream stirred through.
If garnishing, fry the whole prawns briefly in a little butter until just cooked.
Ladle into small bowls and top with the garnish prawns and snipped fennel fronds.

ingredients.note: used to fry shells then remains in the strained soup base; stays in the served dish
weight for '1 large onion' inferred, not stated
weight for '1 large fennel' inferred, not stated
weight for '2 chopped carrots' inferred, not stated
1 tbs ≈ 15g, standard conversion
'2 pinches' estimated at ~0.5g each
garnish prawns cooked in butter per instructions ('cook the 8 prawns in a little butter'); weight estimated at ~15g each

title: Canadian Butter Tarts
instructions: Preheat your oven to fan 170C/conventional 190C/gas 5.
Roll the pastry out on a lightly floured surface until it's slightly thinner than it comes from the pack.
Cut out 18-20 rounds with a 7.5cm fluted cutter, re-rolling the trimmings as needed.
Line two deep 12-hole tart tins with the pastry rounds.
Beat the eggs in a large bowl.
Combine the beaten eggs with the sugar, raisins, vanilla extract, butter and cream, leaving out the walnuts.
Tip the mixture into a pan and stir continuously for 3-4 minutes until the butter melts and the mixture bubbles and starts to thicken, thick enough to coat the back of a wooden spoon.
Watch it closely and keep stirring so it doesn't burn or overcook.
Remove the pan from the heat and stir in the walnuts.
Spoon the filling into the unbaked tart shells so it's level with the pastry.
Bake for 15-18 minutes until set and pale golden.
Leave the tarts in the tin to cool for a few minutes before lifting them onto a wire rack.
Serve warm or cold.

ingredients.note: 2 large eggs estimated at ~50g each
1 tsp estimated at ~4g
melted into the filling and served in the tart, not a frying medium
4 tsp estimated at ~5g per tsp

title: Nanaimo Bars
instructions: Melt the butter with the sugar and cocoa powder in a bowl set over a pan of simmering water, stirring until smooth.
Whisk in the beaten egg and keep whisking over the heat for 2-3 minutes until the mixture thickens.
Take the bowl off the heat and stir in the biscuit crumbs, coconut and almonds.
Press the mixture firmly into the base of a lined 20cm square tin and chill for 10 minutes.
Whisk the butter, cream and custard powder together until light and fluffy, then gradually beat in the icing sugar.
Spread the custard icing over the chilled biscuit base and return to the fridge for at least 10 minutes until firm.
Melt the chocolate with the remaining butter, then spread evenly over the set custard layer.
Chill until the chocolate is fully set, about 2 hours, then lift out and slice into squares to serve.

ingredients.note: Listed as a separate ingredient but not referenced in the instructions apart from custard powder in the middle layer; kept as-is, possible redundancy.
Converted from '5 tbs' at ~5g per tbsp; inferred gram amount.
1 beaten egg assumed ~50g standard large egg weight; inferred.
Instructions say 'almonds if using' — optional ingredient, included as listed.
Converted from '4 tbs' at ~15g per tbsp; inferred gram amount.
Converted from '3 tbs' at ~8g per tbsp; inferred gram amount.

title: Split Pea Soup
instructions: Place the ham in a large pot with 2 litres of water and bring it to a boil.
Drain off the water to remove excess saltiness.
Cover the ham with another 2 litres of fresh cold water and bring back to a boil.
Add the onions, carrots, celery, bay leaves and soaked peas to the pot along with the ham.
Bring everything to a boil, then reduce to a simmer.
Simmer for 1½–2½ hours, topping up water as needed to keep the level steady.
Halve the ham once it softens so it stays fully submerged.
Cook until the ham is tender enough to shred.
Lift the ham out, discard the skin, and shred the meat while still hot.
Remove the bay leaves from the soup.
Stir in the frozen peas and simmer for 1 minute.
Blend the soup until smooth, adding a splash of water if it's too thick.
Return the soup to the pot to reheat if it has cooled or if making ahead.
Mix most of the shredded ham into the hot soup before serving.
Ladle into bowls, scatter the remaining ham on top, and serve with crusty bread and butter.

ingredients.note: gammon simmered raw in the soup until tender
dried split peas, soaked overnight; weight is dry pre-soak amount
inferred ~110g per onion, no weight given
inferred ~120g per carrot, no weight given
inferred nominal weight for 2 bay leaves
inferred weight for 1 stalk, no amount given
listed as 'to serve' with no quantity; estimated accompaniment weight, not part of the soup itself

title: Tourtiere
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan, gas 6).
Boil the potato until tender, then drain, mash, and set aside to cool.
Heat the oil in a non-stick pan and fry the minced pork with the onion until nicely browned.
Stir in the garlic, cinnamon, allspice, nutmeg, stock, plenty of pepper, and a little salt, mixing well.
Take off the heat, fold the meat mixture into the mashed potato, and let it cool completely.
Roll out half the pastry and line a 20-23cm pie plate or flan tin.
Spoon in the pork and potato filling, then brush the pastry edges with water.
Roll out the remaining pastry and lay it over the pie, pressing the edges to seal and trimming off any excess.
Prick the top to let steam escape, then brush with the beaten egg to glaze.
Bake for 30 minutes until the pastry is crisp and golden.
Cut into wedges and serve with a crisp green salad; leftovers are great cold the next day with pickles.

ingredients.note: '1 medium' potato estimated at ~170g
oil used to fry mince/onion which stays in the filling, not drained off

title: Montreal Smoked Meat
instructions: Mix the salt, black pepper, coriander, sugar, bay leaf, and cloves in a small bowl to make your cure.
Coat the brisket all over with the cure and seal it in an extra-large resealable bag.
Refrigerate in the coldest spot for 4 days, flipping the brisket twice a day.
Remove the brisket and rinse off as much cure as you can under cold running water.
Submerge it in a large container of water for 2 hours, changing the water every 30 minutes.
Take it out and pat dry thoroughly with paper towels.
Combine black pepper, coriander, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, dill, mustard, celery salt, and crushed red pepper flakes for the rub.
Coat the brisket generously with the rub.
Fire up your smoker or grill to 225°F, adding wood chunks once it's at temperature.
Place the brisket fat-side up and smoke until it reads 165°F internally, about 6 hours.
Transfer the brisket to a roasting pan fitted with a V-rack.
Pour about an inch of water into the pan, bring to a boil on the stovetop, then reduce heat to medium and cover with foil.
Steam the brisket until it reaches 180°F internally, 1 to 2 hours, adding more hot water as needed.
Let the brisket rest briefly on a cutting board.
Slice and serve, ideally piled on rye bread with mustard.

ingredients.note: No weight given for the brisket; inferred a standard whole brisket (~2.5 kg / 5-6 lb) typical for Montreal smoked meat, since no yield or weight was stated.
Listed simply as 'Coriander' but used as a ground spice in the cure; mapped to ground coriander.
Listed simply as 'Coriander' but used as a ground spice in the rub; mapped to ground coriander.

title: BeaverTails
instructions: Wake the yeast: stir it into warm water with a pinch of sugar, then let it sit until foamy.
In the same bowl, whisk in the rest of the sugar, warm milk, melted butter, eggs and salt until smooth.
Fit the dough hook and beat in the flour until you get a smooth, slightly sticky dough.
Cover the dough and let it proof for about 1½ hours until puffy.
Divide the dough into 12 pieces and stretch each into a long oval, about ¼ inch thick, shaped like a beaver's tail.
Heat oil in a deep pot to 350°F, then fry each piece for 30–45 seconds a side until golden.
Drain on paper towels and finish with your topping of choice — cinnamon sugar, sugar with a squeeze of lemon, or Nutella and toasted almonds.

title: Timbits
instructions: Sift the flour, baking powder and salt together in a bowl.
In a separate bowl, whisk the egg, milk and oil until combined, then fold into the dry ingredients and stir just until smooth.
Heat oil to 365°F (185°C) and drop small teaspoon-sized balls of the batter into the hot oil, turning occasionally until golden brown on all sides.
Lift the Timbits out and drain them on paper towels.
While still warm, roll the Timbits in cinnamon sugar (or dust with icing sugar) and serve.

ingredients.note: amount inferred; oil used for deep frying, most drained off and not part of served dish
amount inferred; used as garnish/dusting, quantity not specified in original

title: Pate Chinois
instructions: Boil the potatoes in salted water until fork-tender, then drain well.
Mash the potatoes with at least 2 tablespoons of the butter, then whip in the milk with an electric mixer until smooth; season with salt and pepper and set aside.
Preheat the oven to 190°C (375°F), rack in the middle.
In a large skillet, brown the chopped onion in the remaining butter, then add the minced beef and cook until golden; season with salt and pepper and remove from heat.
Press the cooked meat mixture into the bottom of a 20cm square baking dish.
Layer the creamed corn over the meat, then top with the mashed potatoes.
Sprinkle the top with paprika and parsley.
Bake for about 30 minutes, then finish under the broiler until golden.
Let cool for 10 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: 4 cups converted using ~150g/cup for chopped raw potato; inferred conversion

title: Sugar Pie
instructions: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C) and grease a 9-inch pie dish.
Beat the brown sugar and butter together until creamy, smooth, and lump-free.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, fully incorporating each before adding the next.
Mix in the vanilla extract and salt.
Beat in the flour a little at a time, then stir in the milk to form a creamy batter.
Pour the batter into the prepared pie dish.
Bake for 35 minutes, then cover the rim with foil to stop it burning.
Return to the oven and bake for about 15 more minutes, until the middle sets and the top forms a crusty layer.
Let the pie cool to room temperature, then chill it for at least 1 hour before serving.

title: Pouding chomeur
instructions: Cream the butter and sugar together with an electric mixer until light and fluffy.
Beat in the eggs and vanilla extract until fully combined.
In a separate bowl, whisk together the flour and baking powder.
Alternate adding the flour mixture and the milk into the butter mixture, mixing until smooth.
Pour the batter into a greased 13x9 inch pan.
For the maple sauce, bring the maple syrup, brown sugar, cream and butter to a boil in a large saucepan, stirring constantly.
Reduce the heat and simmer gently for about 2 minutes, until the sauce has reduced slightly.
Gently pour the maple sauce over the batter in the pan.
Bake at 325°F (160°C) for about 35 minutes, until the cake is light brown and a toothpick inserted comes out clean.

1 1/2 cup brown sugar ≈220 g/cup, standard conversion

title: Rappie Pie
instructions: Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C) and grease a 10x14x2-inch baking pan.
Melt the butter in a skillet over medium heat and stir in the chopped onions.
Cook until the onions soften and turn translucent, about 5 minutes, then lower the heat and keep cooking until they turn dark brown and very tender, about 40 minutes more.
Bring the chicken stock to a boil in a large pot, add the chicken breasts, reduce the heat and simmer until the chicken is cooked through, about 20 minutes.
Lift the chicken out with a slotted spoon and set aside, reserving the broth.
Run the potatoes through an electric juicer, keeping the dry potato flesh and discarding the juice.
Season the potato flesh with salt and pepper, then stir in enough of the reserved broth to bring it to an oatmeal-like consistency.
Spread half of the potato mixture evenly into the prepared pan.
Lay the cooked chicken evenly over the potatoes, then scatter the caramelized onions on top.
Cover with the remaining potato mixture.
Bake until golden brown, about 1 hour.
Reheat the remaining chicken broth and pour it over individual servings as desired.

ingredients.note: 2 tbsp butter used as sauté fat for onions; stays in the dish (caramelized onion layer), not a frying medium.
2 chopped onions, weight inferred at ~150g each (no weight given in original).
4 qt converted at ~946ml/qt (~1g/ml); only part is folded into the potato mixture, remainder reheated and served alongside as broth.
Raw potato weight before juicing; juice is discarded during processing so actual solid mass used is somewhat less than 4kg.

1 tbsp black pepper mapped to generic 'pepper' key (no exact 'black pepper' entry in allowed keys); weight estimated at ~6g.

title: Chicken Basquaise
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (Gas mark 4).
Get the chicken joints ready to cook.
Melt the butter with 3 tbsp of the olive oil in a flameproof casserole or large frying pan.
Brown the chicken pieces in batches on both sides, seasoning with salt and pepper as you go, and don't crowd the pan.
Lift the browned chicken out onto kitchen paper as each batch finishes.
Add a splash more olive oil to the casserole and fry the onions over medium heat for 10 minutes, stirring often, until softened but not coloured.
Pour in the rest of the oil, add the peppers, and cook for another 5 minutes.
Stir in the chorizo, sun-dried tomatoes and garlic, and cook for 2-3 minutes.
Tip in the rice and stir until every grain is coated in the oil.
Stir in the tomato puree, paprika, bay leaves and chopped thyme.
Pour in the stock and wine and bring to a bubble, then turn the heat down to a gentle simmer.
Press the rice under the liquid if it isn't already submerged, then sit the chicken on top.
Tuck the lemon wedges and olives in around the chicken.
Cover and cook in the oven for 50 minutes, until the rice is tender with a little bite and the chicken juices run clear when pierced in the thickest part.
If it needs it, give it another 5 minutes in the oven and check again.

ingredients.note: used to brown chicken but stays in the casserole as part of the braise, so counted as consumed
6 tbsp ≈ 84 g; oil remains in the finished dish so not treated as frying medium

title: Steak Diane
instructions: Heat the canola oil in a 12-inch skillet over medium-high heat.
Season the beef fillets with salt and pepper and sear them in the hot oil, turning once, until browned on both sides and cooked to your liking, about 4 to 5 minutes for medium-rare.
Transfer the steaks to a plate and set aside.
Return the skillet to high heat and pour in the beef stock; let it bubble down until reduced to about half a cup, roughly 10 minutes, then pour into a bowl and set aside.
Return the skillet to the heat and melt the butter, then add the garlic and shallots, stirring until softened, about 2 minutes.
Add the mushrooms and cook, stirring, until they release their liquid, it evaporates, and they start to brown, about 2 minutes.
Pour in the brandy and carefully flambé with a match, cooking until the flame dies down.
Stir in the reserved stock, cream, Dijon mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and Tabasco.
Return the steaks to the skillet, turning them in the sauce until warmed through and the sauce has thickened, about 4 minutes.
Transfer the steaks to serving plates, stir the parsley and chives into the sauce, and spoon the sauce over the steaks to serve.

title: Saltfish and Ackee
instructions: Soak the salt cod overnight, changing the water a couple of times.
Drain, cover with fresh water and bring to a boil, then drain again, add fresh water and boil once more.
Simmer the cod for about 5 minutes until cooked through, then drain and flake into large pieces, discarding any skin or bones.
For the dumplings, mix the flour and suet with a pinch of salt and 250ml water to form a dough.
Wrap the dough in clingfilm and rest it in the fridge.
Open the tin of ackee, drain and rinse, then set aside.
Heat a tablespoon of olive oil in a pan and fry the onion until softened but not browned.
Stir in the spices, seasoning, hot sauce and sliced peppers, and cook until the peppers are tender.
Add the chopped tomatoes, then the flaked salt cod, and mix together.
Gently fold in the ackee and let it simmer until ready to serve.
Just before serving, heat about 1cm of vegetable oil in a frying pan until almost smoking.
Shape the dumpling dough into plum-sized balls and shallow-fry until golden brown, keeping a close eye on the hot oil.
Drain the dumplings on kitchen paper and serve with the saltfish and ackee.

title: Jerk chicken with rice & peas
instructions: Blitz the ginger, garlic, onion, spring onions, chilli, thyme, lime juice, soy sauce, oil, brown sugar, allspice and a teaspoon of salt in a food processor until it forms a thick, smooth jerk paste — pause to scrape down the sides if it stalls, but don't add water.
Taste the paste and balance it: it should read pleasantly salty rather than sharp, so add more chilli for heat or a touch more brown sugar if it's too salty or sour.
Slash the chicken thighs a few times, then rub the jerk paste all over and into the cuts.
Cover and marinate the chicken in the fridge overnight.
For a barbecue: light the coals an hour ahead, add wood chips for smoke, and cook the chicken over slow, indirect heat for 30 minutes.
For the oven: heat to 180C/160C fan/gas 4, put the chicken in a roasting tin with the lime halves, and roast for 45 minutes until cooked through.
While the chicken cooks, rinse the rice thoroughly, then tip it into a saucepan with the coconut milk, spring onions, thyme and garlic, keeping the kidney beans aside for later.
Season with salt, add 300ml cold water, bring to the boil, then reduce to medium, cover and simmer for 10 minutes.
Stir in the kidney beans, cover and leave off the heat for 5 minutes until the liquid is absorbed.
Squeeze the roasted lime over the chicken and serve with the rice and peas, plus hot sauce on the side if you like it fiery.

title: Jamaican Beef Patties
instructions: Whisk the flour, salt, and curry powder together in a large bowl.
Rub the butter into the flour until every piece is coated and the mixture looks like coarse crumbs.
Pour in about half a cup of ice water and work it in with your hands, adding more ice water a tablespoon or two at a time until the dough just comes together.
Divide the dough into two large pieces or ten to twelve equal pieces, wrap tightly in plastic, and chill for 30 minutes while you make the filling.
Season the minced beef with allspice and black pepper and set aside.
Heat the vegetable oil in a skillet until shimmering.
Sauté the onions until translucent, then stir in the ground red pepper, garlic, and thyme and cook for another minute.
Season with a little salt, then add the beef, breaking up any clumps, and cook until no longer pink.
Stir in the ketchup and add more salt to taste.
Pour in the water, bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer until most of the liquid evaporates and the mixture thickens into a sauce.
Fold in the spring onions, then take off the heat and let the filling cool completely.
Beat the egg with a little water to make an egg wash and set it aside.
Roll the dough out on a floured surface and cut into circles about five inches across.
Spoon a generous three tablespoons of filling onto half of each circle, dampen the edges with water, fold over, and press to seal.
Crimp the sealed edge with a fork and trim away any ragged excess for a neat patty.
Set the patties on a parchment-lined baking sheet and chill briefly while the oven heats to 350°F.
Brush the chilled patties with egg wash just before they go into the oven.
Bake for about 30 minutes, until deep golden brown.
Cool the patties on a wire rack, then serve warm.

title: Callaloo Jamaican Style
instructions: Trim the kale leaves and soft stems from the branches, then soak them in a bowl of cold water for 5-10 minutes while you finish the rest of the prep.
Slice the onion, mince the garlic, and dice the tomato, then set them aside.
Drain the kale and cut it into chunks.
Fry the bacon in a saucepan until crispy, then add the onion, garlic, and thyme, stirring for a minute or so.
Add the tomato, scotch bonnet pepper, and smoked paprika, and sauté for 2-3 minutes more.
Stir in the kale and salt, cover, and steam for 6-8 minutes until the leaves are tender, adding a splash of water if it looks dry, then adjust the seasoning and turn off the heat.
Trim both ends off the plantains, slit the skin lengthwise, and peel it back.
Slice the plantains lengthwise into medium pieces and set aside.
Coat a large frying pan with cooking oil spray, then spray the plantain slices with oil and season with salt and pepper.
Fry the plantains over medium heat, shaking the pan every few minutes to redistribute them.
Keep adding a little more oil, salt, and pepper as needed until the plantains are browned and cooked through.
Serve the fried plantains alongside the kale.

ingredients.note: '1 bunch' inferred as ~200g; no gram amount given in original.
2 strips estimated at ~15g each.
3 cloves estimated at ~3g each.
'1 medium' onion estimated at ~110g.

title: Brown Stew Chicken
instructions: Squeeze the lime juice over the chicken and rub it in well, then drain off any excess juice.
Toss the chicken with the chopped tomato, onions, garlic, red pepper, thyme, allspice and soy sauce in a large bowl, cover and let it marinate for at least an hour.
Heat the vegetable oil in a dutch pot or large saucepan until very hot.
Shake the marinade off each piece of chicken as you lift it out, reserving the marinade for the sauce, and brown the chicken in batches in the hot oil, resting the browned pieces on a plate.
Drain off the excess oil, return the chicken to the pot, pour over the reserved marinade, add the carrots, and cook over medium heat for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Whisk the cornstarch into the coconut milk, stir it into the stew, then turn the heat down to low and simmer for another 20 minutes until the chicken is tender.

title: Red Peas Soup
instructions: Rinse the dried kidney beans well, then soak them in a big bowl of water — they'll triple in size, so use plenty — for at least 2 hours or overnight, then toss the soaking water.
Get the pigtail cut into 2-inch pieces, boil it in a pot of water for 20 minutes, then drain, rinse, and repeat the boil to tenderize it and pull out the cure salt.
Load the pot with the beef, soaked beans, carrot, spring onions, thyme, onion, black pepper, red pepper, garlic, and allspice, cover with water, and bring to a boil.
Skim off the scum that rises, then drop to a gentle boil and simmer for 1 hour 15 minutes until the beans are tender and starting to break down.
Stir in the potatoes and coconut milk and keep cooking for 15 minutes.
While that cooks, mix the flour with water (a pinch of salt if you like) into a smooth dough, rest it 5 minutes, then roll tablespoon pieces between your palms into thin cigarette-shaped spinners.
Drop the spinners into the pot, stir well, and cook another 15 minutes at a rolling boil.
Taste and adjust seasoning — the salted pigtail usually seasons the whole pot on its own.
Serve hot, keeping in mind the soup thickens quite a bit as it cools.

title: Roast fennel and aubergine paella
instructions: Toss the aubergine, fennel, pepper and courgette in a roasting tray with a good glug of olive oil, salt and pepper.
Roast for 20 minutes, turning the veg a couple of times, until softened and turning golden.
While the veg roasts, warm a paella pan or large frying pan over low-medium heat with a glug of olive oil.
Sauté the onion for 8-10 minutes until softened.
Turn the heat up to medium, stir in the rice, paprika and saffron, and toast for about a minute.
Pour in the white wine and let it reduce by about half.
Stir in two-thirds of the stock, bring to a simmer and cook uncovered for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the peas and season, then gently fold in the roasted vegetables.
Pour over the remaining stock, lay the lemon wedges on top and cover with a lid or foil.
Cook for a further 10 minutes.
Crank the heat to high until you hear a slight crackle to form the toasted rice crust at the bottom.
Take off the heat and let it rest for 5 minutes.
Scatter over the parsley and serve.

title: Oxtail with broad beans
instructions: Toss the oxtail with the chopped onion, spring onion, garlic, ginger, scotch bonnet, soy sauce, thyme, salt and pepper until well coated.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Brown the oxtail all over, about 10 minutes, then lift it into a pressure cooker.
Pour in the water, seal the cooker and cook at pressure for 25 minutes, then release the lid following your cooker's instructions.
Add the broad beans, bring to a simmer over medium-high heat.
Dissolve the cornflour in the remaining water and stir it into the simmering oxtail.
Cook, stirring, for a few minutes until the sauce thickens and the broad beans are tender.

title: Escovitch Fish
instructions: Rinse the red snapper fillets and pat them dry.
Rub the fish all over with lime juice, then season generously with salt, pepper, or your preferred seasoning blend (creole seasoning works well).
Set the seasoned fish aside, or keep it warm in a low oven while you prepare the sauce.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large skillet over medium heat until shimmering.
Fry the fish for about 5-7 minutes per side, until cooked through and crisp on both sides, then remove and set aside.
Drain most of the oil from the skillet, leaving behind about 2-3 tablespoons.
Add the bay leaf, crushed garlic, and ginger, and stir-fry for about a minute, watching closely so the garlic doesn't burn.
Add the sliced onion, chopped carrot, red and yellow peppers, thyme, scotch bonnet, sugar, and allspice, and continue stirring for 2-3 minutes.
Pour in the malt vinegar and worcestershire sauce, then adjust the salt and pepper to taste.
Let the sauce simmer gently for another 2 minutes.
Discard the bay leaf and thyme sprigs, then spoon the sauce over the fried fish.
Serve alongside bammy; the sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead.

title: Kung Pao Chicken
instructions: Whisk together the sake, soy sauce, sesame oil and cornflour dissolved in water.
Split this mixture in half.
Toss half of it with the chicken pieces in a dish, cover and marinate in the fridge for about 30 minutes.
In a frying pan, combine the remaining sake mixture with the chilli, vinegar and sugar.
Stir in the spring onions, garlic, water chestnuts and peanuts.
Warm the sauce gently over medium heat until it turns fragrant.
Meanwhile, lift the chicken from its marinade and sauté it in a separate pan until the juices run clear.
Add the sautéed chicken to the aromatic sauce and let everything simmer together until the sauce thickens.

title: Kung Po Prawns
instructions: Whisk the cornflour with 1 tablespoon of soy sauce, toss in the prawns, and let them marinate for 10 minutes.
In a separate bowl, mix the remaining soy sauce, tomato purée, sugar, and 2 tablespoons of water into a sauce.
Heat a large frying pan or wok until it's smoking hot, then add 1 tablespoon of oil.
Fry the prawns until golden in spots and curled open, then remove them from the pan.
Add the remaining oil to the pan, then stir-fry the peanuts, chillies, and water chestnuts for about 2 minutes until the peanuts start to colour.
Add the ginger and garlic and fry for another minute.
Return the prawns to the pan with the sauce and simmer for 2 minutes until slightly thickened.
Serve hot with rice.

title: Ma Po Tofu
instructions: Season the minced beef with a small pinch of salt and a dash of sesame oil, mix well and set aside to marinate.
Whisk the cornstarch into the water in a small bowl to make a smooth slurry.
Cut the tofu into roughly 2cm cubes.
Bring a large pot of water to a boil, add a pinch of salt, slide in the tofu and blanch for 1 minute, then drain.
Heat about 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok and fry the minced beef until crispy, then remove it and leave the oil in the wok.
Fry the doubanjiang over low heat for 1 minute, then add the garlic, scallion whites, ginger and fermented black beans, cooking for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Stir the chilli flakes into the aromatics.
Pour in the water and bring everything to a boil over high heat.
Gently slide in the tofu cubes, then add the soy sauce and the fried beef.
Lower the heat and simmer for 6-8 minutes, then stir in the chopped garlic greens.
Give the cornstarch slurry a stir, pour in half, wait about 30 seconds, then pour in the rest.
Taste and add a pinch of salt if needed, or a little sugar if it's too spicy, taking care as the broth is very hot.
Take the pot off the heat once the sauce clings to the tofu cubes.
Sprinkle with Sichuan peppercorn powder and extra chopped garlic greens if you like.
Serve immediately with steamed rice.

title: Wontons
instructions: Mix the pork with the garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and the finely chopped vegetables in a bowl.
Lay out the wonton skins and separate them.
Spoon a heaping teaspoon of filling into the center of each wrapper.
Brush water along two edges of the skin, about a quarter inch in from the border.
Fold the skin over into a triangle, pressing the edges to seal.
Pinch the two long outer points together to shape the wonton.
Heat the frying oil to 450°F and fry the wontons in batches of four or five until golden.
Drain well and serve with your favorite dipping sauce.

title: Sweet and Sour Pork
instructions: Crack the egg and separate the white from the yolk, saving the white for later.
Slice the pork tenderloin into strips.
Whisk the egg white with a pinch of salt, a teaspoon of starch, and two teaspoons of light soy sauce to make the marinade.
Toss the pork strips in the marinade and let them sit for about 20 minutes.
Mix the remaining starch with a splash of water and vinegar to form a starchy slurry.
Heat oil in a wok to 190°C (375°F), fry the marinated pork strips until browned, then lift them out onto a plate.
Leave a little oil in the wok, add the tomato sauce and sugar, and heat until they melt together.
Pour in some water and bring the sweet and sour sauce to a full simmer, then return the pork strips to the wok.
Stir in the starchy slurry and toss everything together until the pork is well coated and the sauce thickens.
Plate up and scatter over some coriander to finish.

title: Szechuan Beef
instructions: Combine the beef, salt, sesame oil, a pinch of pepper, egg white, cornstarch, and a tablespoon of oil in a bowl and let it marinate.
Sear the marinated beef in two tablespoons of oil until golden brown, then set it aside.
Heat the remaining tablespoon of oil in the wok and stir-fry the minced ginger and garlic for a few seconds.
Add the onion, carrots, green pepper, celery, and mushrooms and stir-fry together.
Pour in the cooking wine and the cup of water.
Stir in the oyster sauce, hot sauce, and sugar to build the sauce.
Return the seared beef to the wok along with the soy sauce.
Whisk the remaining cornstarch with a little water and stir it into the wok until the sauce thickens to your liking.

title: General Tsos Chicken
instructions: Whisk together water, soy sauce, white vinegar, sherry cooking wine, white pepper, minced ginger, minced garlic, hot pepper, ketchup, hoisin sauce, and sugar to make the sauce, then set it aside.
In a separate bowl, toss the chicken with salt, white pepper, egg whites, and cornstarch to marinate.
Heat oil to 350°F and deep-fry the chicken in batches for 3-4 minutes until golden brown, keeping the pieces loose so they don't stick together, then drain and set aside.
Pour the sauce into a hot wok, add the broccoli, and bring it to a boil.
Whisk the extra cornstarch with water and stir it into the simmering sauce until it thickens to the right consistency.
Return the fried chicken to the wok, stir-fry for a minute to coat well, and serve straight away.

ingredients.note: Weight not stated; assumed ~200g per raw breast (1.5 breasts). Servings inferred as 3 (no yield given in recipe), based on total dish mass ~1040g and a single main-dish serving of ~300-350g.

title: Beef Lo Mein
instructions: Marinate the beef with salt, a pinch of white pepper, sesame seed oil, half an egg, corn starch and a tablespoon of oil; mix well and let it sit.
Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil, drop in the noodles until submerged, boil for about 10 seconds, then drain and rinse under cold water to stop the cooking.
Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok over high heat and sear the marinated beef until it's about medium doneness, then lift it out and set aside.

In the same wok, heat another 2 tablespoons of oil and stir-fry the onions, minced garlic, minced ginger, bean sprouts, mushrooms and pea pods, pouring in about 1.5 cups of water until the vegetables are just submerged.
Add the drained noodles into the wok with the vegetables.
Stir together the oyster sauce, a pinch of white pepper, sesame seed oil, sugar and soy sauce to build the sauce, and pour it over the noodles.
Return the seared beef to the wok and stir-fry everything together until it's all combined and piping hot.

title: Shrimp Chow Fun
instructions: Soak the rice stick noodles overnight until they're fully softened.
Boil the softened noodles for 10-15 minutes, then rinse under cold water to halt the cooking and keep them from clumping.
In a bowl, combine the shrimp, egg, a pinch of white pepper, sesame seed oil, cornstarch and a tablespoon of oil; mix well to coat the shrimp.
Heat oil in a wok and stir-fry the marinated shrimp until golden brown, then remove and set aside.
Add a fresh tablespoon of oil to the wok, then stir-fry the minced garlic, ginger and all the vegetables together.
Toss in the boiled noodles and stir-fry to combine with the vegetables.
Pour in the cooking wine, oyster sauce, sugar, vinegar, sesame seed oil, a pinch of white pepper and the soy sauce, stirring everything through.
Return the cooked shrimp to the wok and toss to combine.
Whisk the remaining cornstarch with water and stir it into the wok gradually until the sauce reaches the right thickness.

ingredients.note: '1/2 bag' assumed standard 400g bag; grams inferred, not stated in text

title: Hot and Sour Soup
instructions: Bring the chicken stock to a boil in a wok.
Stir in salt, sugar, sesame oil, white pepper, hot sauce, vinegar and soy sauce; let it bubble for a few seconds.
Add the tofu, mushrooms and wood ear mushrooms to the wok.
Whisk the cornstarch with the water in a small bowl, then stir it slowly into the soup until it thickens to your liking.
Beat the egg lightly and drizzle it into the soup, stirring for about 8 seconds so it forms ribbons.
Ladle the soup into bowls and top with the barbecued pork and sliced spring onions.

title: Egg Drop Soup
instructions: Bring the chicken stock to a boil in a wok.
Stir in the salt, sugar, pepper and sesame seed oil.
Once the stock returns to a boil, add the peas and mushrooms.
Whisk the cornstarch into the water to make a slurry, then drizzle it into the simmering soup, stirring until the broth thickens to your liking.
Beat the egg lightly, then pour it slowly into the soup while stirring for about 8 seconds so it forms ribbons.
Ladle the soup into bowls and scatter the spring onions on top before serving.

title: Chicken Congee
instructions: Season the chicken with salt, white pepper, and ginger juice, then mix well and set aside to marinate.
Rinse the rice a couple of times in a pot until the water runs fairly clear, then drain.
Add 8 cups of water to the rinsed rice and bring to a boil over high heat.
Once boiling, reduce to low heat and let the congee simmer, stirring every 8-10 minutes, for about 20-25 minutes.
If you'd like a thinner porridge, stir in a little more water at this point.
Add the marinated chicken to the porridge and let it simmer on low heat for another 10 minutes.
Stir in the green onions, sliced ginger, a pinch of salt, and a pinch of white pepper, and cook for about 10 seconds more.
Ladle the congee into bowls to serve.
Top with fresh coriander if you like.

title: Fruit and Cream Cheese Breakfast Pastries
instructions: Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C) and line two baking sheets with parchment paper.
Beat the cream cheese, sugar, and vanilla extract together until smooth and fully combined.
Dust your work surface with flour, then roll out the puff pastry sheets to flatten them slightly.
Cut each pastry sheet into 9 equal squares.

On each square, cut an L-shaped slit about half an inch from two opposite corners (top-right and bottom-left), reaching across the square without the two L-cuts meeting - like a picture frame with two intact corners.
Fold the upper right corner down and across to the inner bottom corner, forming a diamond shape.
Spoon 1 to 2 teaspoons of the cream cheese filling into the center of each diamond, then top with a scattering of berries.
Repeat with the remaining squares, arranging them on the prepared baking sheets.
Bake for 15 to 20 minutes, until the pastries are golden brown and puffed.
Serve warm and enjoy.

title: Peanut Butter Cookies
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F).
In a large bowl, mix the peanut butter, sugar, and egg together until well combined.
Scoop out a spoonful of dough and roll it into a ball, then place the balls on a nonstick baking sheet.
Flatten each ball with a fork, pressing once and then again at a 90° angle to make a criss-cross pattern.
Bake for 8-10 minutes, until the bottoms turn golden brown.
Remove from the baking sheet and let cool before serving.

ingredients.note: 1 cup peanut butter converted to grams using standard density (~258 g/cup)
1/2 cup sugar converted to grams using standard density (~200 g/cup)
1 egg estimated at standard 50 g

title: Baked salmon with fennel & tomatoes
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (fan 160°C / gas 4).
Trim the fronds from the fennel bulbs and set them aside for later.
Halve the fennel bulbs, then cut each half into three wedges.
Boil the fennel wedges in salted water for 10 minutes, then drain well.
Roughly chop the reserved fennel fronds and mix them with the parsley and lemon zest.
Spread the drained fennel over a shallow ovenproof dish and scatter the cherry tomatoes on top.
Drizzle everything with olive oil and bake for 10 minutes.
Nestle the salmon among the vegetables, sprinkle with lemon juice, and bake for a further 15 minutes until the fish is just cooked through.
Scatter the parsley mixture over the top and serve, with black olives alongside if you like.

ingredients.note: '2 medium' fennel bulbs; weight inferred assuming ~300g per bulb, no gram amount given in original.
2 tbsp chopped parsley converted to grams (~4g/tbsp), inferred.
'Juice of 1 lemon' estimated at ~30g, inferred.
1 tbsp olive oil (~14g); oil stays in the baking dish and is served with the vegetables, so not treated as a frying medium.

title: Salmon Avocado Salad
instructions: Season the salmon fillets, then rub them with oil.
Whisk together the dressing ingredients.
Halve, stone, peel and slice the avocados.
Halve and quarter the cucumber lengthwise, then cut into slices.
Divide the spinach, avocado and cucumber across four plates, then drizzle with half the dressing.
Heat a non-stick pan and fry the salmon for 3-4 minutes on each side until crisp outside but still moist inside.
Top each salad with a salmon fillet, drizzle over the remaining dressing, and serve warm.

ingredients.note: no weight given for '3 Avocado'; inferred ~150g flesh per avocado

title: Budino Di Ricotta
instructions: Mash the ricotta until smooth, then beat it well with the egg yolks.
Stir in the flour, sugar, cinnamon, grated lemon zest and the rum, mixing everything together (a food processor works fine for this).
In a separate bowl, whisk the egg whites until stiff peaks form.
Fold the whipped egg whites gently into the ricotta mixture.
Pour the batter into a buttered and floured 25cm cake tin.
Bake at 180°C (160°C fan / gas 4) for about 40 minutes, until firm.
Serve the budino hot or cold, dusted with icing sugar.

ingredients.note: 4 large eggs, yolks and whites both used per instructions; estimated 50g each

title: Salmon Eggs Eggs Benedict
instructions: Whisk the lemon juice, vinegar and egg yolks together in a bowl until light and frothy.
Set the bowl over a pan of simmering water and keep whisking until the mixture thickens.
Gradually whisk in the butter, a little at a time, until the sauce is thick and glossy; if it looks like it's splitting, lift it off the heat and keep whisking.
Season the hollandaise and keep it warm.
Bring a large pan of water to the boil, add a splash of vinegar, then lower the heat to a gentle simmer.
Stir the water into a light whirlpool and slide the eggs in one at a time.
Poach each egg for about 4 minutes, then lift out with a slotted spoon.
Lightly toast and butter the muffins, then lay a couple of slices of smoked salmon on each half.
Top each half with a poached egg, spoon over the hollandaise, and finish with chopped chives.

title: Shakshuka
instructions: Warm the olive oil in a lidded frying pan over medium heat.
Soften the red onions, chilli, garlic and coriander stalks for about 5 minutes until tender.
Stir in the cherry tomatoes and caster sugar, then let the sauce bubble for 8-10 minutes until thickened.
Make 4 dips in the sauce with the back of a spoon and crack an egg into each one.
Cover the pan and cook over low heat for 6-8 minutes, until the eggs are done to your liking.
Scatter with the reserved coriander leaves and crumbled feta.
Serve straight from the pan with crusty bread.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp olive oil ≈ 14g; stays in the sauce, not a frying medium
2 medium red onions estimated at ~150g each

title: Smoked Haddock Kedgeree
instructions: Melt the butter in a large saucepan over medium heat, then add the chopped onion and cook gently for 5 minutes until softened but not browned.
Stir in the cardamom pods, turmeric, cinnamon stick and bay leaves, and cook for 1 minute to release the spices' aroma.
Tip in the basmati rice and stir until every grain is coated in the spiced butter.
Pour in the chicken stock, add the salt, and bring to the boil, stirring once to loosen any rice from the base of the pan.
Cover with a tight lid, reduce the heat to low, and let the rice cook gently for 12 minutes.
Meanwhile, bring a separate pan of water to the boil, add the smoked haddock and simmer for 4 minutes until just cooked, then lift onto a plate to cool.
Hard-boil the eggs for 8 minutes, then drain, cool, peel and chop them.
Flake the cooled fish, discarding skin and bones.
Uncover the rice, remove the bay leaves, cinnamon stick and cardamom pods if you like, then gently fork in the flaked fish and chopped eggs.
Cover again and return to low heat for 2-3 minutes until the fish is heated through.
Stir in most of the chopped parsley and season with a little salt and black pepper.
Serve scattered with the remaining parsley and garnished with lemon wedges.

title: Breakfast Potatoes
instructions: Freeze your bacon slices ahead of time — it makes chopping them up so much easier.
Wash the potatoes and cut them into medium dice; drop the pieces into a bowl of water to stop them browning while you work.
Heat 1-2 tablespoons of oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat, tilting the pan so it coats evenly.
Drain the potatoes and add them to the hot oil; season with salt, pepper, and Old Bay to taste.
Cook for about 10 minutes, stirring often, until the potatoes are browned — add another tablespoon of oil if needed.
Chop the bacon and stir it into the potatoes; let it render its fat for 5-6 minutes until crisp, tossing occasionally.
Reduce the heat to medium-low, stir in the minced garlic, season again, and add the parsley; adjust the heat as needed.
Cook until the garlic turns fragrant, about a minute.
Just before serving, drizzle over the maple syrup, toss to coat, and let it cook another minute for a caramelized finish.
Serve hot in a bowl topped with a sunny side up egg.

oil remains in the skillet and coats the finished potatoes, not drained off

title: Chocolate Caramel Crispy
instructions: Grease a 20 x 20 cm (8 x 8 inch) baking tin and line it with parchment paper.
Melt the chopped Mars bars and butter together in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, whisking until smooth.
Tip the rice krispies into a mixing bowl, pour the melted mixture over them, and stir until every krispie is coated.
Press the mixture firmly and evenly into the prepared tin, then set aside.
Melt the milk chocolate in the microwave or over a pan of barely simmering water.
Spread the melted chocolate over the set rice krispie base and leave in a cool place to firm up.
Once fully set, slice into squares and serve.

ingredients.note: Assumed 6 standard Mars bars at ~51g each (306g total); size not stated in recipe.

title: Home-made Mandazi
instructions: Mix the self-raising flour, cinnamon and sugar together in a bowl.
In a separate bowl, whisk the egg into the milk until combined.
Make a well in the centre of the flour mixture and pour in the milk and egg mixture, mixing slowly to form a dough.
Knead the dough for 3-4 minutes until it stops sticking to the bowl and the surface turns smooth.
Cover the dough with a damp cloth and let it rest for 15 minutes.
On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough out to about 1cm thick.
Cut the dough into your desired mandazi shapes with a sharp knife, setting them aside for frying.
Heat oil in a pot and gently lower the mandazi pieces in, frying until light brown on one side, then flipping to brown the other side.
Serve the mandazi warm or cold.

ingredients.note: 6 tablespoons converted to grams at ~12.5g per tablespoon; inferred
2 eggs converted at ~50g each; inferred
1 cup converted to grams at ~240g per cup; inferred

title: Mbuzi Choma (Roasted Goat)
instructions: Roast the goat meat over medium heat for about 50 minutes, salting it as you turn the pieces.
Bring the water and salt to a boil in a heavy-bottomed saucepan.
Slowly stir in the cornmeal, letting it sift through your fingers as it goes in.
Lower the heat to medium-low and keep stirring, breaking up any lumps with a spoon, until the mixture pulls away from the sides of the pot and turns thick, roughly 10 minutes.
Take the ugali off the heat and let it cool.
Wet your hands, shape the ugali into a ball, and place it in a serving bowl.
Mix the tomatoes, onions, chilli and coriander leaves together for the kachumbari.
Serve everything together and enjoy.

ingredients.note: used for ugali; treated as cornmeal-style corn flour per recipe text
grams inferred from '2 Tomatoes', assumed ~120g each
'Pinch Salt' - grams inferred as a small nominal amount
grams inferred from '1 Onion', assumed medium onion weight
grams inferred from '1 Green Chilli', assumed small chilli weight
grams inferred from '1 bunch Coriander Leaves', assumed typical bunch weight

title: Chakchouka
instructions: Heat the oil in a large cast iron skillet or sauté pan over medium-high heat.
Add the onion and sauté for 2-3 minutes until softened.
Stir in the peppers and garlic, and sauté for another 3-5 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, cumin, paprika, salt, and chili powder, then mix well and bring to a simmer.
Lower the heat to medium-low and simmer uncovered for 10-15 minutes, until the sauce has thickened to your liking.
Taste and adjust the salt and spice as needed.
Use the back of a spoon to make four craters in the sauce, each big enough to hold an egg.
Crack one egg into each crater.
Cover the skillet and simmer for 5-7 minutes, until the eggs have set.
Serve immediately with crusty bread or pita.

title: Tunisian Orange Cake
instructions: Preheat the oven to 190°C (Gas 5) and grease a 23cm round springform tin.
Trim the hard ends off the orange, slice it, and pick out all the seeds.
Puree the whole orange, peel and all, in a food processor.
Blend in a third of the sugar and the olive oil until smooth.
Sieve the flour together with the baking powder.
Beat the eggs with the remaining sugar using an electric hand mixer for at least five minutes, until very fluffy.
Fold in half the flour mixture, then the orange puree and vanilla extract, then fold in the rest of the flour, mixing just enough to combine.
Pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top.
Bake for 20 minutes at 190°C, then lower the oven to 160°C (Gas 2) and bake for a further 30 minutes, until golden brown and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool the cake on a wire rack before serving.

ingredients.note: no stated yield anywhere; inferred 8 servings from a 23cm springform cake and total batter mass (~1kg, ~134g/slice); '1 large orange' weight assumed ~200g.

title: Kafteji
instructions: Peel the potatoes and cut them into 5cm cubes.
Heat 1-2cm of olive oil in a large pan until very hot, then fry the potatoes for about 20 minutes, turning occasionally, until golden brown.
Drain the fried potatoes on kitchen paper.
Halve the green pepper, remove the seeds, rub with a little olive oil, and grill skin-side down until the skin blisters and blackens.
Seal the hot grilled pepper in a plastic bag for 15 minutes, then peel off the skin.
Heat more olive oil in another pan, peel and thinly slice the onions into rings, and fry for 15 minutes, turning often, until golden brown.
Stir the ras el hanout into the onions right at the end of frying.
Cube the pumpkin into 5cm pieces and fry in the same pan used for the potatoes for 10-15 minutes until soft and lightly browned, then drain on kitchen paper.
Pour off the excess olive oil from the pan and combine all the cooked vegetables together in it.
Whisk the eggs and pour them over the vegetables, then cover the pan so the eggs cook through.
Tip everything onto a large chopping board, season with salt and pepper, and chop and mix everything together with a big knife.

ingredients.note: No yield stated in recipe text; servings and weights inferred. Assumed 'large potato' ≈200g each, 5 potatoes ≈1000g.

title: Tunisian Lamb Soup
instructions: Brown the lamb mince in a hot casserole over high heat, then remove it and set aside.
Drain off all but a tablespoon of the fat left in the pot.
Lower the heat to medium and sauté the garlic, onion and spinach until the onion turns translucent and the spinach wilts, about 5 minutes.
Return the browned lamb to the casserole with the onion-spinach mixture.
Stir in the tomato puree, cumin, harissa, chicken stock, chickpeas, lemon juice, salt and pepper.
Simmer gently over low heat for about 20 minutes.
Add the macaroni and cook for a further 15 minutes, or until the pasta is tender.

ingredients.note: 2 cloves minced garlic estimated at ~3g each

title: Leblebi Soup
instructions: Warm the olive oil in a large pot.
Toss in the diced onion and cook until it turns translucent.
Drain your soaked chickpeas and tip them into the pot with the vegetable stock.
Bring everything to a boil, then lower the heat, cover, and simmer for 30 minutes.
Meanwhile, toast the cumin seeds in a dry frying pan until fragrant, then grind them to a powder in a mortar.
Add the garlic and salt to the mortar and pound into a smooth paste.
Stir the garlic-cumin paste and the harissa into the soup, then keep simmering until the chickpeas are tender, about another 30 minutes.
Season to taste with salt, pepper, and a squeeze of lime juice, then serve hot.

title: Keleya Zaara
instructions: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Add the lamb and brown on all sides, about 5 minutes.
Season with saffron, salt and pepper to taste.
Stir in all but 4 tablespoons of the chopped onion.
Pour in the water and bring to the boil.
Cover, reduce the heat to medium-low, and simmer until the lamb is tender, about 15 minutes.
Uncover the pan and stir in the butter, letting the sauce reduce for 5 to 10 minutes until it reaches the desired consistency.
Season again with salt and pepper to taste, then transfer to a serving dish.
Sprinkle with the reserved chopped onion and parsley.
Garnish with lemon wedges and serve.

ingredients.note: 4 tbsp olive oil converted at ~13.5g/tbsp; oil is used to brown the lamb but stays in the braise and reduces into the served sauce, so not treated as a frying medium.

title: Tuna and Egg Briks
instructions: Warm the olive oil in a large saucepan and let the spring onions soften over low heat for about 3 minutes.
Tip in the spinach, cover tightly and steam for 2-3 minutes until wilted, stirring once or twice.
Drain the spinach mixture in a sieve and leave to cool completely.
Cut 24 rounds, about 12.5cm across, from the filo sheets - six per sheet - and stack them under cling film so they don't dry out.
Once the spinach is cool, squeeze out every bit of excess liquid, then tip into a bowl.
Stir in the tuna, eggs, a dash of hot sauce, salt and pepper until well combined.
Heat the oven to 200°C (400°F, gas 6).
Brush a filo round lightly with oil, stack a second on top and brush again, then a third round brushed with oil.
Spoon a heaped tablespoon of filling into the centre, fold into a half-moon, and twist the edges to seal.
Set the brik on a non-stick baking sheet and repeat with the rest of the pastry and filling to make 8 briks.
Brush the briks with the last of the oil and bake for 12-15 minutes until golden and crisp.
While they bake, toss the tomatoes and cucumber with lemon juice, salt and pepper for a quick side salad.
Serve the hot briks with the tomato-cucumber salad and the apricot jam on the side.

title: Cashew Ghoriba Biscuits
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (Gas 4) and line a baking tray with greaseproof paper.
In a bowl, combine the cashews and icing sugar.
Add the egg yolks and orange blossom water, then mix until you have a smooth, homogeneous paste.
Pinch off small lumps of the cashew paste and roll them into balls.
Roll each ball in icing sugar to coat, then place on the prepared baking tray.
Press an almond into the centre of each ghribia.
Bake for about 20 minutes until lightly golden, watching closely as they burn quickly.

title: Corba
instructions: Pick over the lentils for any grit, rinse two or three times, and drain well.
Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium-high heat, add the onion and a pinch of salt, and sauté about 3 minutes.
Stir in the carrots and cook another 3 minutes.
Add the tomato puree and stir for about a minute to cook it out.
Quickly stir in the cumin, paprika, mint, thyme, black pepper, and red pepper flakes, and toast for about 10 seconds until fragrant.
Add the lentils, vegetable stock, water, and salt, then bring to a gentle boil.
Reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the pot halfway, and simmer 15-20 minutes until the lentils fall apart and the carrots are fully tender.
Blend the soup with a hand blender or in a regular blender to your preferred consistency, then taste and adjust the salt.
Serve hot, topped with crushed crackers or torn bread for texture if you like.

1 large onion, weight inferred ≈150g

title: Kumpir
instructions: Bake or boil the potatoes until they're tender enough to mash.
Grate about 150g of cheese roughly, using as much as you like.
Finely chop the onion and the sweet red pepper.
Mash the butter into the hot potato flesh until well combined.
Fold in the grated cheese.
Stir in the chopped onion and pepper along with a good sprinkling of salt, pepper, and chilli flakes if using.
Pile everything back into the potato skins or a bowl and serve straight away, adding any extra toppings you fancy.

ingredients.note: '2 large Potatoes' — assumed ~300g each for a large potato, no weight given
'2 tbs Butter' — assumed ~14g per tablespoon; mashed into the potato and eaten, not a frying medium
'1 large Onion' — assumed ~150g for a large onion, no weight given
'1 large Red Pepper' — assumed ~120g for a large pepper, no weight given
'Pinch' interpreted as ~0.5g; optional per instructions

title: Bitterballen (Dutch meatballs)
instructions: Melt the butter in a skillet over medium heat.
Whisk in the flour a little at a time until it forms a thick paste.
Slowly pour in the beef stock, stirring so the roux soaks it all up.
Simmer gently for a couple of minutes, stirring in the onion, parsley, and shredded beef.
Keep stirring until the mixture thickens into a heavy, thick ragout.
Spread the ragout into a shallow dish, cover, and chill for several hours until solid.
Scoop a heaping tablespoon of the cold ragout and quickly roll it into a small ball.
Roll each ball through the flour, then the beaten egg, then the breadcrumbs, making sure the egg fully coats the surface.
Chill the coated bitterballen while you heat the frying oil to 190°C (375°F).
Fry four bitterballen at a time until golden brown.
Serve hot with a grainy or spicy mustard.

ingredients.note: assumed 1ml≈1g for the 700ml stock
1 tbsp chopped parsley inferred as ~4g

title: Stamppot
instructions: Scrub and peel the potatoes, then cut them into evenly sized chunks so they cook uniformly.
Boil the potato chunks with the bay leaves in salted water for about 20 minutes, then discard the bay leaves.
If your kale isn't pre-washed and cut, rinse it thoroughly to remove grit, trim the coarse stems, discard any brown leaves, and slice it into thin strips.
Peel and finely chop the shallots.
Melt a tablespoon of butter in a frying pan and sauté the shallots for a few minutes, then add the kale and a splash of water, season, and cook for about 10 minutes until tender.
Warm the milk on the stove or in the microwave.
Drain the potatoes well, pat them dry, then mash them with a masher or ricer, working in the warm milk and the remaining butter while hot.
Season the mash to taste with grated nutmeg, salt, and pepper.
Fold the cooked kale through the mashed potato.
Top with sliced smoked sausage and serve hot with mustard or gravy on the side.

ingredients.note: 2 bay leaves; weight inferred as negligible (~0.5g each) since discarded before serving
2 shallots; weight inferred at ~25g each, typical shallot size

pinch, weight inferred at ~1g

title: Snert (Dutch Split Pea Soup)
instructions: Gather all your ingredients before you start.
Bring the water, split peas, pork, and stock cube to a boil in a large soup pot.
Reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook for 45 minutes, stirring now and then and skimming off any foam.
Lift out any bone-in pork, debone it, slice the meat thinly, and set it aside.
Stir in the celery, carrots, potato, onion, leek, and celeriac.
Bring back to a boil, then simmer uncovered for 30 minutes, topping up with a little water if it starts sticking.
Add the sausage for the final 15 minutes of cooking.
Once the vegetables are tender, lift out the sausage, slice it thinly, and set aside.
If you like a smooth soup, blitz it with a stick blender.
Season with salt and pepper to taste, then stir the sliced meat back into the pot, reserving a few sausage slices for garnish.
Ladle into heated bowls, garnish with the reserved sausage slices and chopped celery leaf, and serve.

title: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
instructions: Put a large pot of water on to boil for the spaghetti.
Finely chop the bacon after trimming off any rind, and finely grate the pecorino cheese.
Beat the egg yolks in a bowl, season with a good crack of black pepper, and set aside.
Salt the boiling water, drop in the spaghetti, and simmer covered for about 10 minutes until al dente.
Bruise two peeled garlic cloves with the flat of a knife blade.
Melt butter in a large frying pan or wok, then add the bacon and garlic together.
Cook on medium heat for about 5 minutes, stirring often, until the bacon turns golden and crisp, then fish out and discard the garlic.
Lower the heat under the bacon, lift the cooked spaghetti straight from its water into the pan, letting a little pasta water drip in too.
Stir most of the grated pecorino into the egg yolks, keeping a small handful back for serving.
Take the pan off the heat, pour in the egg and cheese mixture, and toss the spaghetti quickly so the eggs thicken into a glossy sauce without scrambling.
Splash in a little more pasta water if needed to keep the sauce moist and saucy, and season with salt to taste.
Twist the pasta onto plates, finish with the reserved pecorino and a final grating of black pepper, and serve at once, loosening with more hot pasta water if it dries out.

ingredients.note: 6 egg yolks with no stated weight; estimated at ~18g per yolk
listed as 'as required' with no amount; inferred a typical seasoning quantity for pasta water and sauce
fried in butter but the fat and crisped bacon remain in the pan and become part of the served sauce, so counted as eaten fat, not a frying medium
listed as 'as required' with no amount; inferred a small seasoning quantity

title: Lasagna Sandwiches
instructions: Stir together the sour cream, chopped onion, dried oregano and salt in a small bowl until well blended.
Spread this mixture over four slices of bread.
Layer bacon, tomato and mozzarella on top of the spread bread.
Cap each stack with the remaining slice of bread to form four sandwiches.
Melt 2 tablespoons of butter in a large skillet or on a griddle over medium heat.
Toast the sandwiches until golden brown on both sides and the cheese has melted, adding more butter to the pan as needed.

2 tbsp chopped onion ≈ 20 g
1/2 tbsp dried oregano ≈ 1.5 g
1/4 tsp salt ≈ 1.5 g

4 slices mozzarella, ~28 g each ≈ 112 g
