title: Traditional Croatian Goulash
instructions: Trim any veins from the beef and cut it into 3cm cubes.
Toss the beef with the mustard and spices, then let it marinate in the fridge for an hour.
Heat a tablespoon of oil in a pot and sear the beef in batches until browned on all sides, then set it aside on a plate.
Add another tablespoon of oil to the pot, finely chop the onions and grate the carrots, then cook them over low heat for 15 minutes, salting lightly to help them soften.
Once the vegetables are browned and slightly mushy, return the beef to the pot along with the bay leaves and garlic.
Pour in the wine and simmer for 10-15 minutes to cook off the alcohol, then add about two-thirds of the water.
Cover and cook over low heat for an hour, stirring occasionally.
Pour in the remaining water and cook for a further 30-45 minutes.
Let the stew cool slightly, then serve scattered with chopped parsley and a few slices of fresh hot pepper if you like it spicy.
Slice some fresh bread, dress a salad, and enjoy.

title: Croatian Bean Stew
instructions: Warm the oil in a pan and sauté the chopped vegetables until they turn tender.
Tip the beans and the sautéed vegetables into a pot, add the sausages, and let everything simmer together on low heat for a further 20 minutes.
Alternatively, transfer the mixture to an oven dish and bake at 180°C/350°F for 30 minutes.
Serve now, or better yet, reheat the next day for even deeper flavour.

title: Mushroom soup with buckwheat
instructions: Finely chop the onion and garlic, slice the mushrooms, and rinse the buckwheat.
Warm the oil in a pot and gently sauté the onion until soft.
Stir in the mushrooms and garlic, and keep sautéing until fragrant.
Add the salt, vegetable stock, buckwheat and bay leaf, then cover everything with water.
Let it simmer gently until the buckwheat is nearly cooked.
Just before it's done, stir in the pepper, the sour cream mixed with flour, the chopped parsley, and a splash of vinegar to taste.

title: Burek
instructions: Fry the finely chopped onions with the minced beef in oil until the meat is cooked through.
Season the mixture with salt and pepper.
Grease a round baking tray and lay down a sheet of filo pastry.
Spread a thin layer of the meat and onion filling over the pastry.
Add another sheet of filo pastry, brushing it well with oil.
Repeat the layering of filling and oiled filo until you have five or six layers.
Top with a final sheet of filo pastry.
Bake at 200°C (392°F) for 30 minutes.
Cut into quarters and serve.

ingredients.note: Packet weight not specified; assumed a standard 250g filo pastry packet. Servings inferred as 4 from 'cut in quarters and serve' since no explicit yield was stated.

title: Fresh sardines
instructions: Rinse the sardines well under cold running water.
Roll each sardine in flour to coat.
Deep fry in hot oil until golden and crispy.
Lift onto kitchen paper to drain off the excess oil.
Serve hot or cold with a wedge of lemon.

title: Walnut Roll Gužvara
instructions: Combine all the dough ingredients and knead well until smooth and elastic.
Cover the dough and let it rise until doubled in size, about 2 hours.
Knock back the dough and knead lightly.
Divide the dough into two equal pieces and roll each into a rectangle about 12 by 8 inches.
Mix the filling ingredients together and spread evenly over each piece of dough.
Roll each piece up tightly to form a 12-inch log.
Place the two logs side by side, touching, on a greased baking sheet.
Cover and let rise for about 40 minutes.
Preheat the oven to 200°C (425°F).
Bake for 30-35 minutes until well risen and golden brown, and the loaf sounds hollow when the base is tapped.
Remove from the oven and brush the hot bread top with milk.
Sift a generous covering of icing sugar over the top before serving.

title: Chivito uruguayo
instructions: Pound the beef brisket thin so it cooks evenly.
Sear the pounded brisket on a hot griddle until browned.
Fry the eggs, bacon, and ham in a separate pan until cooked through.
Slice the bread rolls in half.
Layer the seared brisket, fried egg, bacon, ham, mozzarella, tomato, and lettuce onto the bottom half of each roll.
Cap with the top half of the bread and serve right away.

title: Fettuccine Alfredo
instructions: Boil a big pot of salted water and cook the fettuccine according to the package until al dente.
Meanwhile, warm the heavy cream and butter together in a large skillet over medium heat until the butter melts and the cream just starts to bubble.
Whisk in the parmesan, season with salt and black pepper, and let the sauce thicken slightly.
Drain the pasta and toss it straight into the sauce until every strand is coated.
Scatter over the parsley and serve.

title: Sushi
instructions: Lay a nori sheet shiny-side down on your bamboo mat.
Dip your hands in vinegared water and press a 1cm layer of sushi rice over the nori, leaving the far edge clear.
Spread a thin line of Japanese mayonnaise down the middle of the rice.
Top the mayonnaise with your chosen fillings, such as tuna and cucumber.
Lift the near edge of the mat and roll it over the filling, pressing firmly to keep the roll tight.
Continue rolling to the clear edge, brushing it with a little water to seal like a stamp.
Remove the mat, wrap the roll tightly in cling film, then have a grown-up slice it into thick pieces before unwrapping.
For pressed sushi, line a loaf tin with cling film and lay a thin layer of smoked salmon inside.
Press about 3cm of rice over the salmon, fold the cling film over, and press down firmly, using a second tin if you have one for extra weight.
Turn the pressed block out onto a chopping board, have a grown-up cut it into fingers, then peel away the cling film.
For sushi balls, place a topping like half a prawn or a piece of smoked salmon onto a small square of cling film.
With damp hands, roll a walnut-sized ball of rice, set it on the topping, then gather the cling film corners and twist to shape a tight ball before unwrapping to serve.

title: Stuffed Bell Peppers with Quinoa and Black Beans
instructions: Preheat the oven to 190°C (375°F) and lightly grease a 9x13-inch baking dish.
Sit the pepper halves cut-side up in the dish and bake for 15-20 minutes until they soften slightly.
Meanwhile, heat the olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat and cook the onion for 3-4 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Stir in the cooked quinoa, black beans, corn, diced tomatoes, cumin, chili powder, smoked paprika, salt, and pepper, and cook for 5-7 minutes until heated through.
Take the skillet off the heat and stir in 1 cup of the shredded cheese, if using.
Pull the peppers from the oven and pack each half with the quinoa and black bean filling.
Scatter the remaining 1/2 cup of cheese over the tops, if using.
Return the stuffed peppers to the oven and bake for another 15-20 minutes until the cheese melts and the peppers are tender.
Let the peppers rest for 5 minutes, then garnish with chopped cilantro before serving.

title: Beef Mechado
instructions: Sauté the garlic and onion in olive oil until soft and fragrant.
Add the beef cubes and brown on all sides.
Stir in the tomato puree, soy sauce, water, bay leaves and black pepper, then bring to a simmer.
Cover and let the beef braise until tender, about an hour.
Add the potatoes and continue cooking until they're fork-tender and the sauce has thickened.
Season with salt to taste and finish with a squeeze of the lemon slice.
Serve hot alongside steamed rice.

ingredients.note: Original instructions text described an unrelated beef-kebab marinade recipe, not this Mechado; rewrote steps to match the actual ingredient list. No yield stated in text; servings=4 inferred from ~1.8kg total food mass (~450g/serving), sanity-checked against typical stew portions.
Assumed standard 8oz can weight (227g).
Assumed weight of a single lemon slice (~8g).

title: Bistek
instructions: Marinate the beef in soy sauce, lemon juice, and ground black pepper for at least an hour — overnight gives the best flavor.
Heat the oil in a pan and fry half the onions until soft, then set them aside.
Drain the beef, reserving the marinade, and sear the beef in the same pan for about a minute per side, then remove and set aside.
Add a little more oil if the pan looks dry, then sauté the garlic and the remaining raw onions until softened.
Pour in the reserved marinade and the water, and bring everything to a boil.
Return the beef to the pan, cover, and simmer until the meat turns tender, topping up with water if it runs low.
Season with black pepper and salt to taste, then scatter the fried onions on top.
Plate it up hot and serve.

ingredients.note: 1 lb converted to grams
5 tbsp converted to grams using ~1.1 g/ml density
recipe says '1 Lemon (or calamansi)' used for juice in marinade; grams inferred from typical lemon juice yield

'1 pinch' estimated at ~0.5g

title: Beef Caldereta
instructions: Heat oil in a pot and sauté the onion and garlic until the onion softens.
Add the beef and sauté until it browns lightly on the outside.
Pour in the soy sauce, tomato puree and water, and bring to a boil.
Drop in the beef stock cube, cover the pot (or pressure cooker) and cook for 30 minutes.
In a separate pan, fry the carrot and potato until browned, then set aside.
Stir the chilli powder, liver spread and peanut butter into the pot.
Add the bell peppers along with the fried potato and carrot, cover, and continue cooking for 5 to 7 minutes.
Season with salt and ground black pepper, then serve.

ingredients.note: quantity given as '1' with no unit; assumed one standard bouillon cube (~10g)
converted from '2 cups' (~470 ml) to grams

title: Beef Asado
instructions: Combine the beef with crushed peppercorns, soy sauce, vinegar, bay leaves, lemon, and tomato sauce; mix well and marinate for at least 30 minutes.
Transfer the marinated beef and its marinade to a pot, add the water, and bring to a boil.
Stir in the beef stock concentrate, cover, and simmer on low heat for 40 minutes.
Turn the beef over, add the tomato puree, and keep cooking until the beef is tender; set aside.
Heat oil in a pan and fry the potato slices until browned on both sides, then remove to a plate; repeat with the carrots.
Reserve 3 tablespoons of the frying oil in the pan and sauté the onion and garlic until the onion softens.
Pour in the sauce from the beef stew, bring to a boil, add the beef, and cook for 2 minutes.
Add the butter, let it melt, and continue cooking until the sauce reduces by half.

ingredients.note: No yield stated in the recipe; servings inferred at 6 from total food mass (~2.8kg including water) divided by ~470g per serving for a family-style stew.

Assumed an average onion weighs ~110g; size not specified.

title: Crispy Eggplant
instructions: Slice the eggplant into 1 cm (0.4-inch) rounds.
Lay the slices in a bowl, sprinkle generously with salt, and let them sit for 30 minutes to draw out bitterness and moisture.
Rinse the salt off the eggplant slices and pat them thoroughly dry with a kitchen towel.
Spread the breadcrumbs and sesame seeds on a large plate or bowl.
In a separate bowl, beat the eggs with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Heat the oil in a large skillet over high heat.
Dip each eggplant slice into the egg, then coat in the breadcrumb-sesame mixture, and lay it into the hot oil.
Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side, until golden brown, then drain on paper towels.

ingredients.note: '1 large' eggplant estimated at 400g
1 cup breadcrumbs estimated at ~108g
2 eggs estimated at 50g each
'to taste' estimated at a pinch, 3g
'to taste' estimated at 1g
shallow-fried in a skillet and drained on paper towel; oil acts as frying medium, not served, amount estimated

title: Eggplant Adobo
instructions: Slice the eggplant into quarters lengthwise, then crosswise into 2-inch pieces.
Toss the eggplant with half the sugar, half the salt, and pepper; let it sit at room temperature 20 minutes to 2 hours to draw out moisture.
Peel and thinly slice the garlic.
Heat the oil in a heavy pot over medium-high, fry half the garlic until light golden and crisp, then lift out onto a plate and season with a pinch of salt.
Add the ground pork to the same pot, break it up, season with a little salt, and let it brown deeply on one side undisturbed, about 5 minutes.
Lift the pork out with a slotted spoon, leaving the fat behind in the pot.
Blot the salted eggplant dry on a clean towel.
Working in batches, brown the eggplant in the same pot, adding more oil if needed, about 3 minutes per side, then set aside with the pork.
Pour water into the pot and scrape up the browned bits.
Stir in the remaining garlic, vinegar, soy sauce, bay leaves, remaining pepper, and remaining sugar; bring to a simmer.
Return the pork and eggplant to the pot, partially cover, and simmer over medium-low until the eggplant is silky and the sauce has reduced by half, 20-25 minutes.
Taste and adjust with more salt, pepper, or sugar as needed.
Top with the crisp garlic chips and serve over white rice.

ingredients.note: tsp of kosher salt approximated to 5g; original specifies Diamond Crystal or Morton varieties with differing density
'1 whole Garlic' interpreted as one bulb (~8 cloves per instructions), estimated at 45g
oil sizzles garlic/pork/eggplant in the same pot that becomes the braising sauce, so it stays in the served dish; original recipe calls for vegetable oil but ingredient list specifies olive oil
ground pork used, closest available key is generic pork; added raw and cooked in the dish

title: Grilled eggplant with coconut milk
instructions: Prick the eggplants all over with a fork so they don't burst on the grill as steam builds inside.
Grill the eggplants, turning frequently, until the skins are charred dark brown to black and the flesh feels soft.
Soak the grilled eggplants in a bowl of water to cool, then peel off the skins.
Lay the whole eggplants in a shallow dish, or cut into bite-sized pieces if you prefer.
In a small bowl, mix the coconut milk with lemon juice, salt and red pepper flakes until the salt dissolves, tasting and adjusting the seasoning to your liking.
Pour the coconut milk mixture over the eggplant.
Scatter the sliced spring onions over the top and stir gently to combine.

ingredients.note: No yield stated; grams estimated assuming ~200g per eggplant for 6 eggplants.
Assumed a standard 400ml/g can of coconut milk.
1 tbsp converted to grams; recipe calls it 'lemon powder' in the method but the ingredient list specifies lemon juice.
'To taste' with no quantity given; small amount inferred.
'4 sticks Onions' interpreted as spring onion stalks (matches 'green onions' in the method); grams estimated at ~10g per stalk.

title: Tortang Talong
instructions: Grill the eggplants over the flame until their skins turn almost black and blistered.
Let the eggplants cool, then peel away the charred skin and set the flesh aside.
Crack the eggs into a bowl.
Add the salt and beat the eggs well.
Lay each eggplant on a flat surface and flatten it gently with a fork.
Dip each flattened eggplant into the beaten egg mixture, coating it well.
Heat a pan and pour in the oil.
Fry the egg-dipped eggplants, turning once, until both sides are golden and cooked through, about 3 to 4 minutes per side over medium heat.

ingredients.note: assumed ~250g per eggplant for 4 eggplants; no weight given in original
assumed ~50g per egg for 2 eggs

title: Bread omelette
instructions: Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt until well combined.
Toast or lightly fry the bread slices.
Pour the beaten egg over the bread in a hot pan and cook until set, then flip to cook both sides.
Serve hot.

ingredients.note: assumed 2 slices at ~30 g each; no gram amount given in source
assumed 2 eggs at ~50 g each
amount '0.5' unit unspecified (assumed tsp); inferred gram weight

title: Cabbage Soup (Shchi)
instructions: Melt the butter in a large Dutch oven over medium heat.
Add the onion and cook until translucent.
Stir in the cabbage, carrot, and celery, and sauté for about 3 minutes until they start to soften.
Add the bay leaf and vegetable stock, bring to a boil, then cover and simmer on low for about 15 minutes until the vegetables are crisp-tender.
Add the potatoes, bring back to a boil, then cover and simmer on low for about 10 minutes until the potatoes are tender.
Add the tomatoes, bring back to a boil, then simmer uncovered for 5 minutes.
Season with salt and pepper to taste.
Remove and discard the bay leaf.
Serve hot, topped with sour cream and fresh dill.

ingredients.note: 3 tbs converted to grams (~14g/tbsp); stays in the soup as sautéed base, not a frying medium

title: Beetroot Soup (Borscht)
instructions: Chop the beetroot into chunks and drop it into the pot.
Pour in the water, crumble in the chicken stock cube, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add the olive oil and potatoes, then keep boiling everything together until the vegetables are soft.
Stir in the cannellini beans and let the soup cook for a final 5 minutes.
Ladle into bowls straight from the pot and finish with a scatter of fresh dill.

ingredients.note: Assumed ~150 g per beetroot for 3 beetroot; no weight given in original.

title: Fish Soup (Ukha)
instructions: Warm the olive oil in a medium pot over medium-high heat.
Add the sliced onion and cook, stirring now and then, until it starts to caramelize.
Stir in the carrots and cook about 4 minutes more, until they begin to soften.
Pour in the fish stock and water, then add the potatoes, bay leaves and black peppercorns.
Season with salt, bring to a boil, then reduce the heat, cover and simmer for about 25 minutes until the potatoes are tender.
Gently lower the fish cubes into the pot and bring back to a simmer.
Cook the fish briefly, just until the flesh turns opaque and flakes easily — don't overcook it.
Finish with a scatter of fresh dill or parsley before serving.

ingredients.note: Aromatics sizzled in the same pot that becomes the soup base, so the oil stays in the dish; not treated as a frying medium.

title: Blini Pancakes
instructions: Whisk the buckwheat flour, all-purpose flour, salt, and yeast together in a large bowl.
Make a well in the center, pour in the warm milk, and whisk until the batter is smooth.
Cover the bowl and let the batter rise until doubled, about 1 hour.
Stir the melted butter and egg yolk into the risen batter.
In a separate bowl, whisk the egg white until stiff but not dry.
Fold the whisked egg white gently into the batter.
Cover the bowl again and let the batter rest for 20 minutes.
Heat a little butter in a large nonstick skillet over medium heat.
Drop quarter-sized dollops of batter into the pan, leaving space between them, and cook about 1 minute until bubbles form on top.
Flip each blini and cook for another 30 seconds or so.
Transfer the finished blini to a plate, cover with a clean kitchen towel to keep warm, then add more butter to the pan and repeat with the remaining batter.

ingredients.note: 1/2 cup converted at ~120 g/cup for buckwheat flour
2/3 cup converted at ~125 g/cup for all-purpose flour
1/2 tsp converted at ~6 g/tsp
1 tsp converted at ~4 g/tsp
1 cup converted at ~240 g/cup

title: Potato Salad (Olivier Salad)
instructions: Dice the potatoes and carrots into small, even cubes.
Tip them into a large pot and cover with water.
Stir in the salt and vinegar, then bring to a boil over medium-high heat.
Reduce the heat to medium and simmer until the potatoes are cooked through, about 15 minutes.
Drain and let the potatoes and carrots cool to room temperature.
Meanwhile, cube the sausage and pickles, and finely chop the green onions.
Cube the hard-boiled eggs.
If using fresh dill, chop it as well.
In a large bowl, combine the potatoes, carrots, sausage, pickles, peas and green onions.
Fold in the mayonnaise and dill until everything is well coated.
Season with salt and pepper to taste.
Cover with plastic wrap and refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving.

assumed 3 medium carrots ≈60g each; no explicit weight given

title: Strawberries Romanoff
instructions: Hull and quarter the strawberries, then toss them in a bowl with the sugar and Grand Marnier.
Cover and stash in the fridge for at least 1 hour, up to 2 hours, giving it a stir once or twice.
Right before serving, whip the cold heavy cream with the powdered sugar in a large bowl until stiff peaks form.
Gently fold in the sour cream with a spatula until just combined.
Stir the macerated strawberries, then divide them among 6 serving glasses or bowls.
Spoon over a little of the syrup if you like, or save it to soak a cake.
Top each glass with a generous scoop of the cream mixture, dividing it evenly.
Serve immediately, or chill and enjoy within 2 hours of assembling.

ingredients.note: 2 US pints of whole strawberries approximated at ~340g/pint
4 tbsp granulated sugar approximated at 12.5g/tbsp
4 tbsp liqueur approximated at ~15g/tbsp
1 US cup heavy cream ~240g
1/4 US cup sour cream ~60g

title: Lamb Pilaf (Plov)
instructions: Soak the raisins and prunes in a small bowl of water with a splash of lemon juice for at least an hour, then drain and roughly chop the prunes.
Melt the butter in a large pan, add the chopped onion, and cook for 5 minutes until softened.
Stir in the cubed lamb, ground lamb, and crushed garlic, frying for 5 minutes until browned all over.
Pour in a third of the stock, bring to a boil, then reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 1 hour until the lamb is tender.
Add the remaining stock, bring back to a boil, then stir in the rinsed rice and a pinch of saffron, cover, and simmer for 15 minutes until the rice is tender.
Fold in the drained raisins and chopped prunes, season with salt and pepper, and heat through for a few minutes.
Turn out onto a warmed serving dish and garnish with sprigs of flat-leaf parsley.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp converted to ~15 g; standard conversion assumed
2 tbsp converted to ~28 g; stays in the braise so counted as eaten, not a frying medium

title: 15-minute chicken & halloumi burgers
instructions: Lay the chicken breasts between two sheets of baking parchment and bash them with a rolling pin until roughly 1cm thick, then slice each breast into two equal pieces.
If using a frying pan, heat two pans over medium-high heat, adding oil to one; fry the chicken in the oiled pan for 3-4 minutes per side until cooked through.
Season the chicken, lower the heat, add the hot sauce and half the lemon juice, and simmer 1-2 minutes until the sauce reduces, then take off the heat.

If using an air-fryer, preheat to 180C for 4 minutes, cook the chicken for 12 minutes, then drizzle over the hot sauce and remaining lemon juice and cook 1-2 minutes more until cooked through and glazed; keep warm.
While the chicken cooks, toast the buns in a dry pan for 30 seconds or warm them in the air-fryer for 1-2 minutes.
Raise the air-fryer to 200C, add the halloumi and cook 10 minutes, turning halfway, until golden.
Toss the cabbage with the mayonnaise and the rest of the lemon juice.
Spread hummus or your chosen dip on the toasted buns, then layer rocket, the chilli chicken, halloumi, and peppers on top.
Drizzle with extra hot sauce, spoon over the dressed cabbage, season with black pepper, and close with the bun lids.
Serve with any leftover cabbage or a green salad on the side.

used to fry chicken in a separate oiled pan; medium not served

title: Migas
instructions: Crumble the bread into small pieces, sprinkle with cold water, cover with a damp cloth, and leave for 30 minutes to soften.
Heat the olive oil in a deep pan.
Add the garlic cloves, skins on but slit with a knife, and fry for about 5 minutes until fragrant, then set them aside.
In the same oil, add the softened bread and simmer, stirring constantly, for 15 minutes; season with a good grinding of black pepper.
Return the garlic to the pan and keep stirring for a further 20 minutes, until the bread turns soft and golden.

ingredients.note: '1 large' loaf; no gram weight given, estimated 400 g for a large loaf as basis for servings
ingredient list says '1 1/2 L' but instructions specify 'Heat 2 tsp of olive oil'; used the instructed 2 tsp (~10 g) as the actual amount, oil remains in the dish so not treated as frying medium

title: Syrian Bread
instructions: Add water, oil, sugar, salt, flour, and yeast to your bread machine pan in the order your manufacturer recommends.
Run the Dough cycle.
Near the end of the cycle, heat your oven to 475°F (245°C).
Turn the dough onto a floured surface and divide it into eight equal pieces, shaping each into a round.
Cover the rounds with a damp cloth and let them rest.
Roll each round into a thin 8-inch circle.
Bake two at a time on a hot baking sheet or stone until puffed and golden, about 5 minutes, then repeat with the rest.

title: Falafel Pita Sandwich with Tahini Sauce
instructions: Fire up the oven to 450°F (230°C) and lay the falafel out on a baking sheet.
Slide them in and bake until heated through, about 8 to 10 minutes.
While that's happening, whisk together the tahini, water, lemon juice, garlic, and paprika in a bowl until smooth.
Snip about an inch off the top of each pita to open up a pocket.
Tuck 2 falafel into each pita along with even portions of lettuce, tomato, cucumber, pickle, and red onion.
Finish each sandwich with a drizzle of about a tablespoon of the tahini sauce and a dab of harissa.

title: Fasoliyyeh Bi Z-Zayt (Syrian Green Beans with Olive Oil)
instructions: Tip the green beans into a large pot and drizzle over the olive oil.
Season with salt to taste, then clamp on the lid.

Cook over medium-high heat, stirring now and then, letting the beans steam in their own released moisture rather than sauté, until they reach the doneness you like — Syrians push it until the beans turn faintly brown.
Stir in the cilantro and garlic and cook just until the cilantro starts to wilt.
Serve scooped up with warm pita as a main, or alongside other dishes as a side.

ingredients.note: oil is cooked with and served in the dish, not drained off
"Dash" salt inferred as ~1g, no precise amount given
1/4 cup loosely packed cilantro leaves estimated at ~4g

title: Syrian Spaghetti
instructions: Preheat your oven to 175°C and grease a 9x13 inch baking dish.
Bring a large pot of lightly salted water to a boil and cook the spaghetti for 8 to 10 minutes until al dente, then drain.
Stir the drained spaghetti with the tomato sauce, tomato puree, cinnamon, oil, salt and pepper until well combined.
Transfer the mixture to the prepared baking dish.
Bake for 1 hour, or until the top turns crunchy.

oil is mixed into and baked with the dish, remains in the served food

title: Baba Ghanoush
instructions: Fire up an outdoor grill to medium-high and lightly oil the grate.
Prick each eggplant's skin all over with the tip of a knife.
Grill the eggplants directly on the grate, turning often with tongs as the skin chars, until they collapse and turn very soft, about 25 to 30 minutes.
Transfer the eggplants to a bowl, cover tightly with foil, and let them cool for about 15 minutes.
Once cool enough to handle, split the eggplants and scrape the flesh into a colander set over a bowl; let it drain for 5 to 10 minutes.
Move the drained eggplant flesh to a mixing bowl.
Add the crushed garlic and salt, then mash together until creamy but still a little textured, about 5 minutes.
Whisk in the lemon juice, tahini, olive oil, and cayenne pepper.
Stir in the yogurt.
Cover and refrigerate until completely chilled.
Stir in the mint and parsley, taste, and adjust the seasoning before serving.

title: Syrian Rice with Meat
instructions: Melt a quarter cup of butter in a large saucepan over medium-high heat.
Add the ground beef, season with salt, allspice, cinnamon, and black pepper, and cook, stirring, until browned and crumbly, about 7 to 10 minutes.
Pour in the chicken stock and rice, bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer until the liquid is fully absorbed, about 20 minutes.
Meanwhile, melt the remaining two tablespoons of butter in a small skillet over medium heat and toast the pine nuts until lightly golden, 3 to 5 minutes.
Fold the toasted pine nuts into the beef and rice mixture just before serving.

title: Corned Beef Hash
instructions: Melt the butter in a large skillet, preferably cast iron, over medium heat.
Add the chopped onion and cook for a few minutes until translucent.
Stir in the chopped corned beef and potatoes, spreading the mixture evenly across the pan.
Raise the heat to high or medium-high and press the mixture down firmly with a metal spatula.
Let the mixture cook undisturbed until it sizzles and browns on the bottom - resist the urge to stir.
Peek underneath with the spatula to check browning, then flip sections over to brown the other side, pressing down again.
If it starts sticking, add a touch more butter to the pan.
Keep cooking and flipping until the potatoes and corned beef are nicely browned all over.
Remove from the heat, stir in the chopped parsley, and season generously with black pepper and salt to taste.

ingredients.note: 3 tablespoons converted at ~14g/tbsp; butter remains in the finished hash, not a frying medium
1 medium onion estimated at ~110g
3 cups chopped corned beef estimated at ~150g/cup (~450g); canned corned beef is pre-cooked

title: Yorkshire Puddings
instructions: Heat your oven to 230C/fan 210C/gas 8.
Drizzle a little sunflower oil evenly into two 4-hole Yorkshire pudding tins (or two 12-hole muffin tins) and slide them into the oven to get properly hot.
Tip the flour into a bowl and beat in the eggs until you have a smooth paste.
Gradually whisk in the milk until the batter is completely lump-free, then season with salt and pepper.
Pour the batter into a jug, pull the scorching-hot tins from the oven, and carefully pour the batter evenly into the holes.
Return the tins to the oven and leave undisturbed for 20-25 minutes until the puddings have puffed up and browned.
Serve immediately, or cool and freeze for up to a month.

ingredients.note: 4 eggs estimated at 50g each
200ml converted to grams using milk density ~1.03g/ml
amount given only as 'drizzle'; estimated ~2 tbsp total for two tins; oil is absorbed into the batter as it rises so counted as eaten, not a frying medium

title: Cumberland Pie
instructions: Heat the oven to 160C fan 140C gas 3.
Soften the celery, onion, carrots, bay leaves and one thyme sprig in a casserole with 1 tablespoon of oil and the butter for about 10 minutes.
Stir in the flour, then the tomato purée, Worcestershire sauce and beef stock cubes.
Gradually stir in 600ml of hot water, then add the beef and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and cook in the oven for 2 hours 30 minutes, then uncover and cook a further 30 minutes to 1 hour until the meat is tender and the sauce has thickened.
Meanwhile, boil the potatoes until they are about three-quarters cooked.
Transfer the meat and sauce to a baking dish.
Slice the potatoes into 1cm rounds and toss gently with seasoning, the remaining oil and the thyme leaves.
Layer the potato slices over the beef, scattering the cheddar and parmesan between the layers.
The pie can be covered and chilled for up to a day, or frozen for up to 3 months at this point.
Increase the oven to 200C fan 180C gas 6 and bake for 30-40 minutes until golden and bubbling.
Serve with peas.

title: Aussie Burgers
instructions: Season the minced steak with 1 tsp salt and a good grind of black pepper, working it in with wet hands.
Divide the meat into four and shape into burgers (freeze at this stage if you like).
Slice the beetroot and split the naan breads in half.
Heat a griddle pan or barbecue; toast the naans on both sides until lightly charred, then set aside.
Grill or barbecue the burgers for 2-3 minutes a side until cooked through.
Lay half a toasted naan on each plate, pile on rocket, top with a burger, a few slices of beetroot and a dollop of soured cream and chive dip.
Season with salt and black pepper and serve at once with a green salad, chips, and ideally a glass of red wine.

title: Blueberry & lemon friands
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional, gas 6).
Butter six friand or muffin tins generously.
Melt the butter and let it cool.
Sift the icing sugar and flour into a large bowl.
Stir in the almonds, rubbing everything together with your fingers to combine.
In a separate bowl, whisk the egg whites until they form a light, floppy foam.
Make a well in the dry ingredients, tip in the egg whites and lemon zest, then fold in the cooled butter to form a soft batter.
Divide the batter evenly among the tins using a large serving spoon.
Scatter a handful of blueberries over each cake.
Bake for 15-20 minutes until golden brown and just firm to the touch.
Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
Dust lightly with icing sugar before serving.

ingredients.note: Grams inferred: '3 Egg White' converted at ~33g per large egg white; original gave count not weight.

title: Chocolate Coconut Squares
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (fan 160C/gas 4), then butter a 20cm square tin and line the base with paper.
Beat the butter and caster sugar together until pale and creamy, then beat in the eggs, adding a spoonful of the flour if the mixture looks like it's curdling.
Sieve in the remaining self-raising flour, baking powder and cocoa powder, folding them in gently with a metal spoon.
Stir in the milk, then scrape the batter into the tin and level the top.
Bake for 18-20 minutes, until the sponge springs back when pressed, then let it cool completely in the tin.
For the icing, melt the plain chocolate, butter and 4 tablespoons of water together gently in a pan.
Let the icing cool slightly, then beat in the icing sugar until smooth.
Turn the cooled cake out of the tin, peel off the lining paper, and cut into 16 squares.
Dip each square into the icing, then roll it in the desiccated coconut, and leave to set on a cooling rack.

title: Squid, chickpea & chorizo salad
instructions: Char the peppers whole under a grill, on a barbecue, or on a griddle until blackened all over.
Pop the charred peppers into a bowl, cover with a plate, and let them steam until cool enough to handle.
Peel, deseed and finely slice the peppers.
Tip the peppers and any juices into a large bowl with the chickpeas, parsley, chilli and garlic; set aside.
Get a large frying pan smoking hot.
Add a splash of oil, then the squid, and stir-fry hard for about 30 seconds.
Scatter the chorizo over the squid and cook for another 30 seconds.
Tip everything from the pan into the bowl with the peppers.
Season with salt and pepper, then dress with the remaining oil, lemon juice and lemon zest.
Mix well, pile onto a platter, and let everyone help themselves.

title: Barramundi with Moroccan spices
instructions: Blitz the cumin, coriander, paprika, chilli powder, garlic, lemon juice and olive oil in a food processor with a pinch of salt until you've got a smooth dressing.
Slash the barramundi three times on each side, then rub half the dressing all over the fish and let it marinate for about 30 minutes.
Heat your oven to 220C/fan 200C/gas 7.
Lay the fish on a roasting tray and roast for 20 minutes, until the flesh is firm and the eyes have turned white.
Serve the fish with the reserved dressing spooned over, alongside steamed couscous or rice.

title: Lamingtons
instructions: Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6. Butter and line a 20 x 30cm rectangular tin.

Beat the salted butter and golden caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Beat in the flour, milk and salt until combined, then spoon into the tin. Bake for 25 minutes until golden and firm. Cool completely.

Slice the sponge horizontally into two halves and trim the edges square. Cut into 18 squares. Whip the cream with the icing sugar to soft peaks. Spread jam then cream on half the squares, sandwich with the remaining squares, and chill in the fridge.

For the icing, whisk the melted unsalted butter with the milk. Sieve the cocoa powder and icing sugar together, then gradually whisk into the butter mixture until smooth. Blend with a hand blender if it turns lumpy.
Divide the desiccated coconut between three shallow bowls.
Dip each lamington in the icing until fully coated, roll in the coconut and set on a wire rack. Chill at least 1 hour.

title: Spiced smoky barbecued chicken
instructions: Crush the garlic with a pinch of salt using a pestle and mortar until it turns to paste.
Stir in the lemon zest and juice, the curry powder, chilli flakes, olive oil, and a good grind of black pepper — mix until it forms a smooth marinade (a mini food processor works too).
Toss the chicken thighs through the marinade so they're well coated, then set aside while you get the barbecue going.
Once the barbecue is hot, scatter the rosemary and thyme bunches over the grill grid, then lay the chicken skin-side up on top of the herbs.
Close the lid if you have one and let the chicken smoulder over the herbs for about 10 minutes, until it just starts to colour.
Turn the chicken and keep cooking for another 20-30 minutes, turning as needed, until it's slightly charred from the burnt herbs and cooked through to the bone.

title: Warm roast asparagus salad
instructions: Fire up the oven to 200C (Gas 6, fan 180C).
Snap off the woody ends of the asparagus and toss them.
Lay the asparagus in a single layer in a rimmed baking tray.
Halve the tomatoes widthways and tuck them in alongside the asparagus.
Season everything with sea salt and pepper, then drizzle with olive oil.
Roll each bacon rasher into a tight roll, halving first if they're large, and nestle them into the tray.
Drizzle the bacon rolls with honey and roast for 20 minutes, until the tomatoes are soft and the bacon crisp.
Meanwhile, boil the potatoes until tender.
Whisk the vinegar, olive oil, mustard, and a good pinch of sea salt and pepper together in a large bowl until blended.
Toss the rocket or spinach in half the dressing and spread it over a large serving platter.
Drain the potatoes, halve them, then gently toss in the remaining dressing.
Arrange the potatoes on the platter alongside the roasted asparagus, tucking in the tomatoes and bacon rolls to serve.

title: Avocado dip with new potatoes
instructions: Blitz half the avocado flesh with the yoghurt, lime juice, lemon juice and seasoning until smooth.
Dice the remaining avocado and fold it into the whizzed mixture along with most of the lime zest.
Cover the dip and chill it until you're ready to serve.
Boil the potatoes for 6 minutes, then drain well.
Toss the drained potatoes with olive oil, chilli powder and cumin seeds.
Set the potatoes aside until about half an hour before your guests arrive.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan/Gas 6).
Roast the potatoes for about 30 minutes, shaking the tray halfway, until golden and tender.
Spoon the dip into one or two bowls and scatter with the remaining lime zest.
Serve the dip alongside the hot potatoes and tortilla chips for dipping.

title: Quick salt & pepper squid
instructions: Get the fishmonger to clean the squid for you, or use ready-cleaned little ones.
Snip the body open with kitchen scissors and lay it flat, then rinse well and pat dry.
If working with a large squid, cut the body into four roughly square portions; leave small squid opened up whole.
Score the inside of each piece in a neat criss-cross pattern using the tip of a very sharp knife.
Brush the squid all over with oil and set aside while you get the barbecue or griddle screaming hot.
Stir together the sea salt, Chinese five-spice and black pepper.
Just before cooking, sprinkle the spice mix over both sides of the squid to taste — you may not need it all.
Griddle the squid for about a minute per side, until it curls up.
Lift onto a serving plate with tongs and drizzle with a little sesame oil.
Scatter over coriander leaves and serve with small bowls of sweet chilli sauce for dipping.

title: Mini chilli beef pies
instructions: Heat the oil in a pan and fry the onion for 5 minutes until soft, then stir in the chilli powder and cumin and fry for a further minute.
Add the minced beef and cook for a few minutes until browned.
Stir in the tomato purée, beef stock and cinnamon, bring to the boil, then simmer for 15-20 minutes until the liquid has mostly reduced.
Add the kidney beans 5 minutes before the end of cooking, check the seasoning, then let the chilli cool.
Heat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C / gas 6).
Cut 12 circles from the pastry using a 7cm cutter and use them to line a 12-hole mini muffin tray.
Prick the base of each pastry case with a fork and bake for 10 minutes, then cool on a wire rack.
Repeat with the remaining pastry to make more cases.
Meanwhile, cook the potato in boiling water until tender, then drain and mash with the sour cream and seasoning, stirring through the chopped chives.
Spoon 1-2 teaspoons of the chilli mix into each pastry case and top with a teaspoon of the mash.
Ruffle the mash with a fork and return the pies to the oven for 15 minutes or until golden.

title: Sticky Chicken
instructions: Slash each drumstick three times to let the marinade in.
Whisk together the soy sauce, honey, olive oil, tomato purée and mustard.
Pour the marinade over the chicken and turn to coat every piece thoroughly.
Let it marinate at room temperature for 30 minutes, or overnight in the fridge for deeper flavour.
Heat your oven to 200C/fan 180C/gas 6.
Tip the chicken into a shallow roasting tray and roast for 35 minutes, turning occasionally, until tender and glistening with the sticky marinade.

title: Anzac biscuits
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180C (fan 160C / gas 4).
Tip the oats, coconut, flour and sugar into a bowl and stir to combine.
Melt the butter in a small pan, then stir in the golden syrup.
Dissolve the bicarbonate of soda in 2 tbsp boiling water and stir it into the butter and syrup mixture.
Make a well in the dry ingredients and pour in the butter mixture, stirring gently until just combined.
Drop dessertspoonfuls of the mixture onto buttered baking sheets, spaced about 2.5cm apart.
Bake in batches for 8-10 minutes until golden.
Cool on a wire rack before serving.

title: Kenyan Beef Curry
instructions: Bring the water to a boil in a Dutch oven, then add the beef, garlic, and ginger and stir well.
Simmer gently, covered, for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally and skimming or stirring in any foam that rises.
Take the pot off the heat and drain the beef, keeping the cooking water aside for later.
Return the pot to medium heat with the oil and, once shimmering, cook the onions until softened, 5 to 7 minutes.
Add the tomatoes and keep cooking until they break down, 3 to 5 minutes.
Stir the drained beef back into the tomato and onion mixture and cook for a further 5 minutes.
Stir in the paprika, pepper, curry powder, tomato paste, and salt to taste.

Pour back the reserved cooking water plus enough extra to cover, bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer uncovered for about an hour until the meat is tender and the sauce has thickened, adding more water if it dries out.
Taste and adjust the seasoning, then garnish with fresh chillies and cilantro before serving.

title: Sukuma Wiki
instructions: Warm the oil in a large skillet or pot over medium heat until it shimmers.
Tip in the onions and cook, stirring now and then, until they soften, about 5 to 7 minutes.
Pile in the shredded kale, mix it through the onions, then turn the heat down to medium-low, cover, and let it steam for 5 minutes.

Lift the lid, season with salt to taste, and stir in the cream. Keep cooking uncovered for another 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the cream thickens slightly into a rich creamed kale and onions.
Serve alongside your favorite Kenyan stew and a side of ugali.

title: Ugali – Kenyan cornmeal
instructions: Get the water boiling in a medium saucepan.
Drop the heat right down, and while whisking non-stop, pour the cornmeal in a steady stream into the boiling water — it thickens fast.
Keep it on low heat, stirring hard with a sturdy wooden spoon, until the ugali pulls away from the sides of the pan, holds together, and smells like roasted corn.
Turn it out onto a plate right away, and if you like, shape it quickly into a thick round with a spoon or spatula.
Let it sit — it firms up as it cools, ending up firm enough to slice like polenta.

title: Red onion pickle
instructions: Peel the red onions, halve them top to bottom, and slice thinly into half-moons.
Tip the onion into a colander set over a bowl, sprinkle with the sea salt, and toss with your hands to coat every piece.
Leave the onions to brine for about an hour.
Meanwhile, combine the cider vinegar, 50ml water, and granulated sugar in a saucepan.
Bring to a simmer, stirring to dissolve the sugar, and cook for a couple of minutes, then set aside.
Pack the brined onions into sterilised jars, scattering in the black pepper as you go.
Pour the warm vinegar mixture over the onions to cover, then tuck the bay leaves down the sides of the jars.
Seal the jars and store in the fridge, using the pickle within 4 weeks.

ingredients.note: 3 large red onions; no weight given, estimated at ~150g each.

title: Cinnamon buns
instructions: Melt the butter with the milk and salt in a small saucepan, then let the mixture cool until lukewarm.
Stir together both flours, the yeast, cardamom and sugar in a large bowl.
Make a well in the centre, crack in the eggs, pour in the lukewarm milk mixture, and stir into a sticky dough.
Oil your work surface, turn the dough out onto it, and knead vigorously for 5-8 minutes until smooth and elastic, resisting the urge to add more flour.
Shape the dough into a ball, place it in a greased bowl, cover with a tea towel, and let it prove in a warm spot for about an hour until doubled in size.
Meanwhile, beat the softened butter with half the sugar and a tablespoon of cinnamon to make the filling.
Mix the remaining sugar and cinnamon together in a separate bowl and set aside for the topping.
Once risen, turn the dough out onto a floured surface and roll into a 36x24cm rectangle.
Spread the cinnamon-butter filling evenly over the dough.
Roll the dough up tightly from the long edge into a cylinder, then cut into 12 even slices.
Place each slice into a flattened paper cupcake case on a tray, or into a greased muffin tin.
Cover and let the buns prove again for 30-45 minutes until risen.
Preheat the oven to 200C/180C Fan/Gas 6.
Brush the tops of the buns with beaten egg and dust generously with the reserved cinnamon sugar.
Bake for 12 minutes until dark golden-brown, then serve warm with coffee.

title: Nordic smørrebrød with asparagus and horseradish cream
instructions: Stir the crème fraîche, grated horseradish, lemon juice, salt and white pepper together in a bowl.
Chill the horseradish cream until you're ready to plate.
Warm a splash of vegetable oil in a frying pan over medium heat.
Fry the sliced shallots until golden and crisp, then lift out with a slotted spoon onto paper towel to drain.
Season the crispy shallots with a pinch of salt.
Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil and blanch half the asparagus (four white, four green) for 2-3 minutes until tender-crisp.
Plunge the blanched asparagus straight into a bowl of iced water to stop it cooking.
Thinly slice the remaining raw asparagus lengthwise.
Heat the grill, spread the rye bread slices with butter, and grill until golden and crisp.
Lay the grilled rye bread on serving plates and top with both the blanched and raw asparagus.
Drizzle over the horseradish cream and scatter with grated västerbottensost cheese.
Finish with the crispy shallots and a scattering of fresh dill.

title: Rømmegrøt – Norwegian Sour Cream Porridge
instructions: Cook the sour cream in a covered saucepan over medium heat for about 5 minutes.
Lower the heat and whisk in half the flour until fully incorporated, then keep cooking, stirring occasionally, until fat starts to separate out.
Spoon off as much of the released fat as you can into a small bowl and set aside for serving (don't worry if little comes out — butter can stand in later).
Whisk in the remaining flour, then slowly pour in the milk, whisking constantly to keep the porridge smooth and lump-free.
Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes, then stir in the salt.
Serve the porridge topped with sugar, cinnamon, and the reserved fat — or a knob of butter if your sour cream was lower-fat.

title: Tall Skoleboller
instructions: Warm the milk until it's just lukewarm, then stir in the yeast and let it dissolve.
Stir in the sugar, cardamom, vanilla, and about two-thirds of the flour.
Knead the dough by hand or with a mixer's dough hook, working in the rest of the flour a little at a time until it forms a smooth, not-too-sticky ball.
Once the dough comes together, add the softened butter and keep kneading for about ten minutes.
Roll the dough into a cylinder, cut it into six equal pieces, shape each into a round bun, and set them in a greased muffin or cake tin.
Cover the buns and let them rise until doubled in size, about an hour.

title: Æbleskiver
instructions: Whisk the flour, salt, sugar, baking powder, and cardamom together in a large bowl.
Separate the eggs, add the yolks to the dry mix, pour in the buttermilk, and whisk until smooth; cover and chill for about 30 minutes.
Beat the egg whites to stiff peaks and gently fold them into the chilled batter.
Heat an æbleskiver pan over medium heat, brush each hole with butter, and fill almost to the top with batter.
Once the edges firm up, use a skewer to rotate each ball a quarter turn, repeating until they form golden-brown spheres.
Lift the æbleskiver from the pan and serve dusted with powdered sugar and a spoonful of raspberry jam.

title: Norwegian Potato Lefse
instructions: Boil the potatoes until tender, then peel them while still warm and pass them through a potato ricer twice.
Let the riced potatoes cool, uncovered, in the fridge.
Stir the salt, sugar, melted butter, and cream into the cooled potatoes.
Slowly work in the flour, kneading by hand just until the dough comes together — don't add more flour than you need.
Roll the dough into a long log and divide it into about 7 or 8 pieces for an 18-inch griddle, or 10-12 pieces for a smaller griddle or frying pan.
Shape each piece into a ball, then press it into a flat, crack-free circle.
Heat your griddle to medium-high.

Flour your work surface and roll each piece into a large, thin circle slightly smaller than the griddle, starting with a smooth rolling pin and switching to a corrugated one as it thins out — go easy on the flour so the edges stay tender.
Roll the lefse onto a lefse stick, unroll it onto the hot griddle, and cook until brown spots appear underneath, then flip with the stick and cook the other side.
Lift the cooked lefse off the griddle with the stick and stack it in a folded damp cloth to keep it soft.

title: Fiskesuppe (Creamy Norwegian Fish Soup)
instructions: Cut the fish fillets into cubes or strips, ready for later.
Crush or finely chop the garlic clove.
Rinse the potatoes, carrots and leek, then cut into thin strips.
Melt the butter in a pot and add the garlic, cooking until it just turns golden.
Whisk in the flour to form a smooth roux.
Pour in the fish stock, whisking constantly until no lumps remain.
Add the potatoes, carrots and leek along with the milk, bring to a boil, then simmer for about 10 minutes.
Stir in the crème fraîche and bring the soup back to a gentle simmer until it just starts to bubble, then turn off the heat.
Keep the pot on the warm burner, add the fish, and let it poach in the hot soup for 5 minutes.
If using shrimp, stir them in right before serving.
Sprinkle with chives before serving.

title: Fårikål (Norwegian National Dish)
instructions: Cut the lamb into large chunks.
Slice the cabbage into large wedges, keeping the core attached so they hold together.
Layer the lamb and cabbage in a big pot: lamb pieces fatty-side down, sprinkled with peppercorns and salt, then a layer of cabbage, repeating and finishing with cabbage on top.
If you want a thicker stew, dust a little flour over the lamb before adding the water.
Pour in the water, bring to a boil, then cover, lower the heat, and simmer gently for 2-3 hours until the lamb falls easily off the bone.
Serve with boiled potatoes and fresh parsley, ladling plenty of the fårikål broth over each bowl.

ingredients.note: 3 lbs converted to grams; assumed bone-in shoulder/breast cut typical for fårikål, no cut specified in original
'1 head White Cabbage' - weight inferred as average head ~1000g, not stated in original

3 tsp converted using ~6g/tsp; approximate

title: Raspeballer (Norwegian Potato Dumplings)
instructions: If you're making pork knuckle, simmer it in water for about 3 hours until the meat falls off the bone, then pull the meat and save the broth for cooking the dumplings.
Boil the potatoes meant for mashing until tender, then peel them once they've cooled.
Peel the remaining raw potatoes and grate them (or pulse through a food processor), then press out excess moisture with a paper towel.
Rice or mash the boiled potatoes until completely smooth with no lumps.
Stir the grated raw potatoes into the mashed potatoes in a large mixing bowl.
Work in the flour, all-purpose flour, and salt by hand until the dough is fully blended.
Bring your broth of choice — vegetable, beef, or the reserved pork knuckle broth — to a gentle simmer, never a full boil, or the dumplings will fall apart.
Dip a tablespoon in cold water and shape each dumpling smoothly in your palm, then gently lower it into the simmering broth, redipping the spoon between each one.
Let the dumplings simmer for about 30 minutes; if using smoked sausage, warm it in the same pot alongside them.
Scatter fresh chopped parsley over the top before serving.
Peel and dice the rutabaga and carrots, then boil in water for about 30 minutes until tender.
Drain, then mash with cream (or milk), butter, and nutmeg until smooth.
Serve the mashed rutabaga alongside the dumplings and meat.

title: Karbonader (Lean Beef Patties) with Caramelized Onions
instructions: Grate half an onion and set it aside, then slice the rest and fry gently in butter until soft and caramelized.
In a bowl, mix the ground beef with the grated onion, salt, pepper, nutmeg, cornstarch, and water until well combined.
Shape the mixture into a log and cut into six even patties, then score a light grid pattern into each with a knife.
Sear both sides of the patties in butter over high heat, then reduce to low heat and cook for another 2-3 minutes until done.
Serve the karbonader and caramelized onions with potatoes, stewed peas, and brown sauce for dinner, or on a slice of bread for lunch or a snack.

title: Brun Lapskaus (Norwegian Beef Vegetable Stew)
instructions: Heat the olive oil in a large pot, then brown the beef cubes (cut into 1-inch pieces) in batches until well seared.
Dice the onion, add it to the pot with the browning beef, pour in the beef stock, bring to a boil, then lower the heat, cover, and simmer for about 30 minutes.
Peel the swede and celeriac and cut into 1-inch cubes, add to the pot, cover, and continue simmering for another 30 minutes.
Peel and chop the carrots and potatoes into 1-inch cubes, slice the leek into rings, add everything to the pot, cover, and simmer for about 20 minutes, stirring as little as possible.
Top with fresh parsley before serving.

title: Authentic Norwegian Kransekake
instructions: Grind the almonds fine in a food processor or almond grinder.
In a large bowl, mix the ground almonds with the first batch of powdered sugar, then work in three of the egg whites, kneading by hand until the dough comes together into a ball.
Wrap the dough in cling film and chill for at least an hour, ideally overnight.
Grease your kransekake ring forms well and dust them with semolina, flour, or bread crumbs.
Preheat the oven to 210°C (410°F) with top and bottom heat, and divide the chilled dough into six portions.
Work the remaining egg white into the dough a little at a time until you can roll it into finger-thick ropes.
Press the dough ropes into the greased forms, pinching the ends together to form rings.
Set the forms on a baking sheet and bake in the middle of the oven for 10-12 minutes, until the tops turn golden brown.
Once cooled, whisk the remaining egg white with the second batch of powdered sugar and the lemon juice to make a stiff icing, then pipe it over the baked rings before stacking them.

ingredients.note: 3 cups whole almonds converted at ~143g/cup; approximate.
4 egg whites converted at ~30g each; matches 3+1 used per instructions.
4 cups powdered sugar for the dough, converted at ~120g/cup.
2 cups powdered sugar for the icing (not mentioned in given instructions but standard kransekake glaze), converted at ~120g/cup.
1 additional egg white for the icing, converted at ~30g; not covered by the given instructions.
1/2 teaspoon converted at ~5g/tsp for the icing.

title: Kvæfjord Cake “Verdens Beste” (World’s Best Cake)
instructions: Separate the egg yolks from the whites, keeping the whites aside for the meringue.
Whisk the egg yolks with the cornstarch and sugar in a bowl.
Heat the cream and milk in a saucepan with the split vanilla pod's seeds until it just begins to bubble.
Pour half the hot milk into the egg mixture while whisking, then return everything to the saucepan and cook, whisking, until thickened; keep whisking for another 30 seconds.
Transfer the custard to a clean bowl, press plastic wrap onto its surface, and chill.
Cream the butter and sugar until light and fluffy.
Beat in the egg yolks one at a time, saving the whites for the meringue.
Fold in the flour mixed with baking powder, then stir in the milk until smooth.
Preheat the oven to 160°C (320°F) and spread the batter evenly onto a paper-lined baking sheet.
Whisk the egg whites with sugar until glossy and holding stiff peaks.
Spread the meringue over the cake batter, swirl with a spatula, and scatter almonds on top.
Bake for about 30 minutes until the meringue and almonds turn golden.
Whip the cream to stiff peaks and fold in the chilled vanilla custard.
Once the cake has cooled, cut it in half using scissors or a knife, slicing through the baking paper.
Place one half meringue-side down on a platter and peel off the paper.
Spread the cream filling over it, then top with the other half, meringue-side up, after peeling off its paper.
Serve with fresh berries.

ingredients.note: 2 large yolks ≈18g each
estimated weight of one whole pod
5 large yolks ≈18g each
amount not specified in original ('Sliced Almonds'); estimated typical topping quantity

title: Norwegian Krumkake
instructions: Whisk the egg and sugar together until the mixture turns pale and thickens.
Melt the butter, let it cool slightly, then stir it into the egg mixture.
Mix in the cardamom, nutmeg and salt.
Slowly add the flour while stirring, working out any lumps.
Let the batter rest for at least 30 minutes to improve the texture.
Spoon about a heaping tablespoon of batter onto the hot krumkake iron and bake; thin the batter with a splash of water if it's too thick.
While still hot, roll each krumkake around a wooden roller or shape it over a cup, working quickly before it hardens.
Once fully cooled, store the krumkaker in a metal or glass tin lined with paper towels, or freeze them for later.

ingredients.note: 3 large eggs approximated at 50g each
3/4 cup sugar approximated at 200g/cup
2/3 cup butter approximated at 227g/cup
1 teaspoon approximated
a pinch approximated

title: Suksessterte (Norwegian almond “success cake”)
instructions: Preheat the oven to 175°C (345°F) and grease a 27 cm springform pan, lining the base with baking paper if you like.
Grind the almonds coarsely in a food processor or almond grinder.
Gently whisk the eggs and sugar together until just combined, then stir in the ground almonds, flour and baking powder.
Pour the batter into the prepared tin and bake for 45–50 minutes, watching the top so it doesn't burn.

For the egg cream, combine the egg yolks, heavy cream, sugar and vanilla in a saucepan and cook over low-medium heat, stirring constantly, until thickened (about 15 minutes) — increase heat slightly if needed but never let it boil.
Cool the egg cream to room temperature, then beat in the butter until fluffy.
Let the almond cake cool completely before removing from the tin and frosting with the yellow egg cream.

title: Fyrstekake – Norwegian Prince Cake
instructions: Cream together the sugar, vanilla and butter until light and fluffy.
Beat in the egg yolks.
Work in the flour and baking powder, then knead by hand until the dough turns smooth.
Wrap the dough in plastic and chill in the fridge for at least 30 minutes.
Pulse the almonds and sugar together in a food processor until finely ground, to your preferred coarseness.
Stir the butter, egg, and cognac and/or almond extract into the ground almonds to make the filling.
Line the base of your cake tin with baking paper and grease the sides.
Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F).
Slice off two-thirds of the chilled dough and press it into the tin, covering the base and building the sides up 2-3 cm.
Spread the almond filling evenly over the crust.
Roll out the remaining dough, cut it into long strips, and lay them across the top in a lattice, pressing any breaks back together.
Brush the lattice gently with egg wash.
Bake for about 45 minutes, until the top is golden brown while the filling inside stays soft.

title: Mazariner – Scandinavian Almond Tartlets
instructions: Pulse the flour and powdered sugar together in a food processor, then add the butter and pulse until it disappears into fine crumbs.
Add the egg and pulse just until the dough comes together.
Roll the dough out on a floured surface and cut circles with a large mug.
Press the circles into a greased muffin pan and chill while you make the almond filling.
Preheat the oven to 180°C (355°F).
Whisk the eggs and powdered sugar together until thick and pale.
Melt the butter and stir it into the egg mixture.
Stir in the vanilla, flour, and almond extract, then fold in the almond flour.
Pipe or spoon the almond filling into the shells, filling each about three-quarters full.
Bake for 20–25 minutes until light golden brown, then cool on a wire rack.
Whisk together the powdered sugar and heavy cream until you have a spreadable glaze.
Spread the glaze over the cooled mazariner and decorate as you like.

title: Asado
instructions: Get a proper wood fire going in the grill and let it burn right down to glowing coals.
Salt the beef cuts generously.
Lay the beef on the grill, thickest pieces farthest from the coals, and let it start cooking.
Add the chorizo and morcilla to the grill once the beef has been going for a while.
Keep turning the meat until it hits your desired doneness — ribs can take up to 2 hours, thinner cuts go quicker.
Rest the meat for about 10 minutes before slicing.
Serve with chimichurri sauce and grilled vegetables.

ingredients.note: amount inferred: recipe gives '4 Chorizo' with no weight, assumed ~80g per link
amount inferred: recipe gives '2 Morcilla' with no weight, assumed ~150g per piece

title: Empanadas
instructions: Mix the flour and salt together in a large bowl.
Rub in the butter with your fingers until the mixture turns crumbly.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a dough comes together.
Wrap the dough and chill it for 30 minutes.
Cook the onions in a pan until they turn translucent.
Add the ground beef and cook until browned.
Stir in the cumin and paprika, then take the pan off the heat.
Let the filling cool, then mix in the chopped eggs and olives.
Roll out the chilled dough and cut it into circles.
Spoon filling onto each circle, fold over, and seal the edges with a fork.
Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 20-25 minutes, until golden.
For a flakier crust, work a tablespoon of vinegar into the dough to limit gluten formation.

title: Milanesa
instructions: Season the beef cutlets generously with salt and pepper on both sides.
Dip each cutlet in the beaten egg, then coat thoroughly in the breadcrumb mixture combined with chopped parsley and minced garlic.
Heat oil in a large pan over medium heat.
Fry the cutlets until golden and cooked through, about 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve hot with lemon wedges or a side salad.
For extra crunch, swap in panko breadcrumbs mixed with finely grated Parmesan.
Pound the cutlets to an even thickness before breading for uniform cooking.

title: Choripán
instructions: Fire up the grill or a hot pan and cook the chorizos slowly over medium heat for 10-15 minutes, turning until fully cooked through and the skins stay intact.
Slice the crusty rolls open and toast them lightly on the grill or in a pan, letting them soak up some of the chorizo's flavor.
Split each chorizo lengthwise and tuck it into a roll.
Spoon a generous amount of chimichurri sauce over the top.
Serve immediately while everything's still hot.

title: Dulce de Leche
instructions: Pour the milk into a large, heavy-bottomed pot, stir in the sugar and baking soda, and set it over low heat.
Keep stirring constantly so the mixture doesn't scorch or stick to the bottom.
Let it simmer gently, stirring often, until it turns thick and caramel-colored - this takes about 1-2 hours.
Once thickened, stir in the vanilla extract for extra flavor.
Take the pot off the heat and let the dulce de leche cool before transferring it to a jar; it will thicken further as it cools.

ingredients.note: Ingredient list says 'Baking Powder' but instructions mention 'baking soda' - traditional dulce de leche uses bicarbonate of soda; kept literal list ingredient, flagging discrepancy.

title: Alfajores
instructions: Cream the butter and sugar together until light and fluffy.
Beat in the egg yolks and lemon zest.
Gradually work in the flour and cornstarch until a smooth dough forms.
Chill the dough for at least 1 hour to firm it up.
Roll out the chilled dough and cut into circles.
Bake the circles at 180°C (350°F) for 12-15 minutes, then let them cool completely.
Spread dulce de leche on the flat side of one cookie and sandwich with another.
Roll the sandwiched edges in desiccated coconut to coat.
For an extra treat, dip the finished alfajores in melted chocolate and set on a wire rack to firm up.

ingredients.note: 2 egg yolks; weight inferred at ~18g each
1 teaspoon zest; weight inferred (~2g)

title: Fainá
instructions: Whisk the chickpea flour with water, salt, and pepper until smooth.
Let the batter rest for at least 4 hours (or overnight in the fridge) so the flour fully hydrates.
Preheat the oven to 220°C (430°F).
Pour the olive oil into a round baking dish and place it in the oven to heat through.
Pour the rested batter into the hot, oiled dish.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until golden and set.
Slice and serve hot, with a little extra black pepper on top if you like.

title: Matambre a la Pizza
instructions: Season the flank steak generously with salt and pepper.
Score the steak lightly on both sides to tenderize it and help it cook evenly.
Grill one side until the steak is about half-cooked through.
Flip and spread tomato sauce over the cooked side, then scatter mozzarella, oregano, and green olives on top.
Keep grilling until the cheese melts and turns bubbly and golden, making sure the steak is fully cooked without scorching the cheese.
Slice the matambre and serve it hot straight off the grill.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp dried oregano estimated at ~2g

title: Carbonada Criolla
instructions: Sear the beef cubes in a large pot until nicely browned, working in batches if needed for a good crust, then set the meat aside.
In the same pot, soften the chopped onion until translucent.
Toss in the carrots, potatoes, and pumpkin, and let them cook for a few minutes to pick up flavor.
Return the beef to the pot, pour in the stock, and stir in the dried apricots.
Season with salt and pepper, then simmer gently for 1-2 hours until the beef turns tender.
Ladle into bowls and serve hot with a crusty piece of bread on the side.

title: Spiced tortilla
instructions: Warm the sunflower oil in a large frying pan over medium heat.
Add the onion and half the chilli, and fry for about 5 minutes until softened.
Stir in the curry powder and cook for a further minute to release the spice aromas.
Add the cherry tomatoes, potatoes and coriander stalks to the pan.
Season the eggs generously, then pour them over the vegetables.
Let the tortilla cook gently for 8-10 minutes until almost set.
Heat the grill and flash the tortilla underneath for 1-2 minutes until the top is just set.
Scatter over the coriander leaves and the remaining chilli, slice into wedges, and serve with a green salad.

title: Easy Spanish chicken
instructions: Preheat your oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6.

Warm the olive oil in a large ovenproof frying pan over medium heat, then fry the onion, chorizo and peppers with a pinch of salt and pepper for about 15 minutes until softened and the chorizo has released its oils.
Stir in the paprika and garlic and cook for a few more minutes until fragrant.
Tip in the chopped tomatoes, olives and butter beans, stir well and season.
Nestle the chicken thighs into the pan and season them generously.
Transfer the pan to the oven and bake for 40 minutes until the chicken skin is crisp and the meat is cooked through and tender.
Scatter over the parsley and serve.

title: Gambas al ajillo
instructions: Peel the prawns, leaving the tails on, and use a cocktail stick to pull out the dark vein running along the back.
If you're cooking in a regular frying pan instead of a terracotta dish, you can skip peeling and leave the shells on.
Season the prawns lightly with sea salt.
Set a flameproof terracotta pot or frying pan over high heat and add the garlic, olive oil and chillies.
Once the garlic turns golden, tip in the prawns and cook for 1-2 minutes per side until just turned pink.
Scatter over the chopped parsley and a generous crack of black pepper.
Serve immediately, straight from the pot if using terracotta — but warn everyone the oil and dish stay scorching hot for several minutes.

title: Jamon & wild garlic croquetas
instructions: Wash the wild garlic leaves in a colander, then pour over boiling water until just wilted.
Rinse the wilted leaves under cold water, squeeze out the excess moisture, and chop finely.
Warm the milk in a pan over low heat until steaming, but don't let it boil.
In a separate pan, heat the oil, then stir in the flour and cook for a couple of minutes until it turns a light golden colour.
Gradually whisk in the warm milk a little at a time until you have a thick, silky sauce.
Let the sauce bubble gently for a minute or two, stirring, so the flour cooks out fully, then season.
Beat the manchego, jamón and chopped wild garlic into the sauce until combined.
Spread the mixture onto a lightly oiled tray, cover and chill for at least 1 hour (or up to 24 hours).
Oil your hands and roll the chilled mixture into 18-20 even balls.
Arrange the balls on a tray and freeze for 30 minutes to firm up.
Beat the egg with a little seasoning in one shallow dish and tip the breadcrumbs into another.
Dip each croqueta in egg, then roll in the breadcrumbs to coat.
Heat the vegetable oil in a deep pan, filled no more than a third full, to 170C.
Fry the croquetas in batches for 2-3 minutes until deeply golden.
If frying from frozen, fry at 160C for a few extra minutes until piping hot inside.
Lift out with a slotted spoon onto kitchen paper to drain.
Serve warm, ideally with a glass of sherry.

ingredients.note: wilted/blanched with boiling water before combining into the roux; treated as a pre-cooked addition
used to cook the roux, which becomes the sauce and stays in the dish

title: Locro
instructions: Soak the dried corn and beans in water overnight.
Brown the beef and pork in a large pot.
Add the chopped onion and spices, and cook until the onion turns translucent.
Tip in the soaked corn and beans along with the pumpkin and potatoes.
Pour in enough water to cover everything.
Simmer for 2-3 hours until the stew thickens.
Serve hot with bread on the side.
For extra flavor, lightly toast the corn before adding it to the pot.
Stir in a little extra paprika or a dash of cumin for added warmth.

ingredients.note: Browned in the same pot that becomes the stew; fat stays in the dish, so not treated as a frying medium.

title: Arroz con gambas y calamar
instructions: Peel and devein most of the king prawns, saving a few whole ones for a nice presentation if you fancy it.
Warm the olive oil in a large frying pan or shallow flameproof casserole over medium-low heat, then soften the onion for about 5 minutes.
Stir in the bay leaf, saffron, rice and tomato purée, and let everything toast together for 1-2 minutes.
Pour in the white wine and let it bubble away for 1-2 minutes.
Add the seafood stock and 150ml water, then cook for 5 minutes.
Stir in the squid, season well, bring to the boil, then cover and drop to a gentle simmer for 12 minutes, topping up with a splash more water if it looks dry.
Uncover, stir the peeled prawns through the rice, and arrange any whole prawns on top.
Cover again and simmer for a further 5-6 minutes until the prawns turn pink and are cooked through.
Rest for a couple of minutes off the heat before serving straight from the pan.

ingredients.note: 24 prawns; assumed ~20g each as no weight given
oil sizzles onion then stays in the rice as it's served from the same pan

title: Crema Catalana
instructions: Warm the milk, cream, cinnamon stick and citrus zests in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring often, until steaming but not boiling, about 3-5 minutes.
Take off the heat, cover with a plate and let the mixture infuse for at least 30 minutes.
Whisk the egg yolks, sugar and cornflour together for 3-5 minutes until light and pale.
Strain the infused milk through a sieve into the egg mixture, whisking constantly, then return everything to the saucepan.
Whisk over medium-high heat for 10-12 minutes until the mixture thickens to a custard consistency, testing by running a finger through the coating on the back of a spoon.
Sieve the custard into a jug to remove any froth.
Divide the custard between six 150ml ramekins and let cool at room temperature for 1 hour until set with a slight wobble.
Chill the ramekins overnight.
Just before serving, sprinkle 1 tablespoon of caster sugar over each ramekin and caramelise with a kitchen blowtorch, or under a hot grill until golden and bubbling.
Serve immediately.

ingredients.note: weight of one cinnamon stick inferred (~3 g)
weight of zest from one orange inferred (~6 g)
weight of zest from one lemon inferred (~6 g)
weight inferred: 7 yolks x ~18 g average
converted from 6 tbsp at ~12.5 g per tbsp; used for caramelised topping, remains on the served dish

title: Ensaimada
instructions: Stir the yeast into 230ml lukewarm water and let it sit for 3 minutes.
Add the caster sugar, eggs, flour and a teaspoon of sea salt flakes, then mix into a rough dough.
Knead the dough for 10 minutes in a stand mixer with a dough hook (or 15 minutes by hand) until it's elastic enough to stretch almost see-through.
Cover the dough and let it rest for 30 minutes, then divide it into four equal pieces.
Place the pieces on a parchment-lined tray and let them rest for another 30 minutes.
Oil your work surface and rolling pin with vegetable oil.
Flatten one portion of dough with your palm, then roll it into a thin rectangle about 30 x 50cm.
Let it rest for 2 minutes, then spread a quarter of the lard over the top (or mix 50g lard with sobrasada for a filled version and spread that instead).
Stretch the corners of the dough outward every 10cm or so until the rectangle reaches about 50 x 70cm.
Cut a strip from each short side and lay them along one long side to form the 'heart' of the ensaimada.
Roll the dough up into a long snake shape, then repeat with the remaining portions.
Stretch the first roll of dough until it's over a metre long.
Coil it into a spiral, leaving 1cm gaps between turns so it can expand, then flatten slightly and transfer to a parchment-lined baking sheet.
Repeat with the remaining dough and leave everything to rise in a warm place for at least 12 hours, ideally 24.
Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6, put the ensaimadas in the top third, and immediately drop the temperature to 180C/160C fan/gas 4.
Bake for 18 minutes until dark golden, then cool on a wire rack.
If filling with whipped cream, slice each ensaimada open, spread the cream inside and close it up.
Dust generously with icing sugar if you like, then cut into pieces to serve.

ingredients.note: 2 medium eggs inferred at ~50g each
'Drizzle' amount inferred as ~5g; used only to oil work surface and rolling pin, not a frying medium and not eaten
Spread as filling inside the dough and baked in, so counted as eaten, not a frying medium

title: Padron peppers
instructions: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over high heat (or preheat an air-fryer to 205°C for 3 minutes).
Fry the padron peppers, stirring frequently, for about 5 minutes until blistered, wilted and slightly charred.
Transfer the peppers to a serving plate and season with sea salt.
Serve with dips or as part of a tapas spread.

ingredients.note: 1 tablespoon ≈ 14g; oil is fried into and coats the peppers, remains in the served dish

title: Paella
instructions: Warm a tablespoon of the oil in a wide, shallow pan and sizzle the prawn heads with the parsley stalks until the heads turn pink, then mash them well with a potato masher.
Pour in the sherry and 300ml water, season with salt, and let it simmer for 10 minutes, mashing the heads as it reduces to build a stock.
Scatter the mussels into the pan, cover loosely, and cook over high heat for 3-4 minutes until they open, stirring to release their juices.
Strain the pan contents through a colander into a bowl holding the saffron, letting it steep in the hot stock, topping up with water to reach about 700ml total.
Pick the mussels from the colander and set them aside.
Wipe out the pan, add the remaining olive oil, and sizzle the chorizo until it releases its oil.
Add the onion and garlic and cook until softened, then stir in the squid until it turns white.
Add the tomatoes and cook down briefly, then pour in most of the stock, stir, and bring to a boil.
Scatter the rice over the stock, stir once, and boil vigorously for 5 minutes.
Lower the heat to a gentle simmer for 10 minutes without stirring, letting the rice absorb most of the liquid.
Tuck the prawn tails into the rice and simmer for 5 minutes, turning until cooked through.
Stir in the mussels and broad beans (or peas), adjusting with a splash of stock if too dry or extra heat if too soupy.
Once the rice is just cooked, turn off the heat and cover with a tea towel for a few minutes.
Scatter over parsley leaves and lemon zest, season with smoked salt if desired, stir once, and serve straight from the pan with lemon wedges.

ingredients.note: 3 tbsp estimated at ~15g each; oil remains in the sofrito/rice, not drained, so not a frying medium
10 prawns estimated at ~20g each (tails used whole, heads used for stock); weight inferred
'Bunch' inferred as ~30g typical bunch, split between stalks (stock) and leaves (garnish)
'Pinch' estimated at ~0.1g
1 medium onion estimated at ~150g
3 cloves estimated at ~3g each
1 medium squid estimated at ~200g
2 tomatoes estimated at ~100g each
listed as alternative to peas in instructions ('broad beans or peas'); broad beans chosen as the canonical variant, peas not summed
