title: Smoked aubergine purée
instructions: Fire up the grill until it's very hot.
Slice the aubergines in half lengthways and grill for about 25 minutes, turning occasionally, until the flesh has softened but the skin stays firm.
Let the aubergines cool until you can handle them.
Score the grilled flesh with a sharp knife and scoop it out with a spoon into a bowl.
Mash the flesh with a fork until you get a thick pulp.
Beat in the lemon juice and crushed garlic.
Stir in the yogurt and dill, season to taste, and serve while still warm.

title: Slow-roast lamb with cinnamon, fennel & citrus
instructions: Place the lamb leg in a large food bag with all the marinade juice and let it marinate overnight in the fridge.
The next day, pull the lamb out of the fridge an hour before cooking to let it come to room temperature.
Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan/gas 7).
Lift the lamb from the marinade, reserving the leftover liquid, and pat it dry.
Rub the lamb with half the olive oil and roast for 15-20 minutes until nicely browned, then remove and drop the oven to 160°C (140°C fan/gas 3).
Mix the lemon zest, remaining olive oil, honey, cinnamon, fennel seeds, cumin and crushed garlic with a good pinch of seasoning to make a paste.
Lay a large sheet of parchment over a large sheet of foil, sit the lamb on top and rub it all over with the spice paste.
Pull up the foil sides, pour the reserved marinade into the base, then scrunch the foil to seal it into a parcel.
Roast for 4 hours until the lamb is very tender.
Rest the lamb, still wrapped, for 30 minutes before unwrapping and serving with the juices.

ingredients.note: no yield stated; whole lamb leg weight inferred at ~2kg to size a 6-serving roast
zest and juice of 1 lemon approximated as one whole lemon's worth
4 tbsp olive oil, ~13.5g per tbsp
2 tbsp honey, ~21g per tbsp
1 tbsp ground cinnamon
1 tbsp fennel seeds
1 tbsp ground cumin
3 cloves crushed garlic, ~3g each

title: Chicken wings with cumin, lemon & garlic
instructions: Snip each chicken wing at the knuckle into two pieces with kitchen scissors.
Whisk together the minced garlic, lemon zest, lemon juice, cumin seeds and olive oil, seasoning well.
Toss the wing pieces in this marinade until fully coated, then cover and chill for at least 1 hour, or overnight if you have time.
Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6 (or fire up an outdoor barbecue).

Spread the wings on an oven tray and bake for 45-50 minutes until crisp, drizzling over the honey for the final 10 minutes; if barbecuing, cook for about 20 minutes total, adding the honey drizzle in the last 10 minutes.
Pile the wings onto a platter with plenty of napkins and serve alongside bowls of olives, pistachios or almonds, dates, pickled chillies and flatbreads.

ingredients.note: No yield stated; estimated 90g per wing x12 wings, then oven-baked as directed.

title: Adana kebab
instructions: Pulse the romano peppers in a food processor until finely chopped, then tip into a sieve and press hard to squeeze out all their juice.
Tip the drained peppers into a bowl with the lamb mince, red pepper paste, pul biber, 1½ tsp flaky sea salt and 2 tbsp of the sunflower oil.
Knead the mixture by hand for 2-3 minutes until sticky, wetting your hands with cold water if it starts sticking.
Cover the bowl and chill the mixture for at least 2 hours, up to 12 hours.
Heat the grill to high or the oven to 220C/200C fan/gas 6.
Divide the mixture into 8 equal portions if skewering, or 12 portions of about 85g each if not.
Wet your hands and mould the portions onto skewers, pressing and massaging the mixture down the length of each skewer so the meat is fully and evenly wrapped with no metal showing.
If not using skewers, shape the portions into 20cm köfte and lay them on a parchment-lined tray for the oven or a foil-lined tray for the grill.
Wet your fingers and press indents along each köfte for the traditional ridged shape.
Brush each köfte with the remaining 1 tbsp sunflower oil.
Grill on the top shelf for 10-12 minutes, turning regularly, or bake in the oven for 16-18 minutes, until crispy outside and juicy in the middle.

ingredients.note: servings inferred: no stated yield, only piece counts (8 skewers or 12 köfte); assumed 2 skewers per serving = 4 servings. Pepper weight estimated at ~150g each for '2 large romano pepper'.
3 tbsp estimated at 15g/tbsp

mixed into and brushed onto the meat, remains in the finished dish so not a frying medium; 3 tbsp estimated at 15g/tbsp

title: Turkish lamb pilau
instructions: Dry-fry the pine nuts (or almonds) in a large pan until lightly toasted, then tip them onto a plate.
Add the olive oil to the pan and fry the onion with the cinnamon until it starts to turn golden.
Turn up the heat, stir in the lamb, and fry until the meat changes colour.
Tip in the rice and cook for 1 minute, stirring constantly.
Pour in 500ml boiling water, crumble in the stock cube, add the apricots, and season to taste.
Reduce the heat, cover, and simmer for 12 minutes until the rice is tender and the stock has been absorbed.
Toss in the toasted pine nuts and mint, then serve.

title: Smoky chicken skewers
instructions: Soak 15 wooden skewers in water for 10 minutes if using.
Cut the chicken thighs into 3cm pieces and place in a bowl.
Add 1 tablespoon of the olive oil, the fennel seeds, cumin, smoked paprika, garlic and red wine vinegar; toss well and season.
Marinate for up to a day in the fridge if you have time.
Thread 2-3 pieces of chicken onto each skewer.
Pour the remaining oil into a frying pan or rub it over a griddle pan and get it hot.
Sear the skewers for 3-4 minutes on each side, working in batches if needed and keeping cooked skewers warm in a low oven.
Serve with smoky aïoli if you like.

ingredients.note: 6 thighs estimated at ~120 g each; no stated weight.
2 tbsp total (~14 g each); half used in marinade (eaten), half used to grease the pan for searing — treated as eaten since marinade portion coats the meat.
Optional serving condiment ('if you like'); quantity not specified, estimated a small dollop per serving.

title: Chilli ginger lamb chops
instructions: Blitz the crushed garlic, ginger, lemon juice, olive oil, chilli powder, cumin and seasoning together with a hand blender until smooth.
Rub the paste all over the lamb chops on both sides.
Cover and marinate in the fridge for a couple of hours, or overnight for best flavour.
Fire up the barbecue until hot.
Grill the chops over the coals for 3 minutes on each side, until cooked but still pink and juicy in the middle.

ingredients.note: 4 cloves crushed garlic, estimated ~3g per clove

title: Falafel
instructions: Warm a tablespoon of the oil in a large pan and sizzle the onion and garlic over low heat for about 5 minutes until soft.
Tip the softened onion and garlic into a mixing bowl with the chickpeas and spices, then mash everything together with a fork or potato masher until the chickpeas are fully broken down.
Stir in the parsley and season to taste, then work in the beaten egg, squeezing the mixture together with your hands until it holds.
Shape the mixture into 6 balls and flatten each into a patty.
Heat the remaining oil in the pan and fry the falafels over medium heat for about 3 minutes per side until golden brown and firm.
Serve hot or cold with couscous, pitta bread or salad.

ingredients.note: 2 tbsp converted to grams; used to fry onion/garlic then the patties, oil stays in pan/drains off
grams inferred for '1 chopped onion' (medium ~110g); softened in pan before mixing, so counted cooked

title: Aubergine couscous salad
instructions: Fire up the grill to high heat.
Lay the sliced aubergine on a baking sheet, brush with oil, season well, and grill for about 15 minutes, turning and brushing with more oil halfway through, until browned and soft.
While the aubergine grills, tip the couscous into a large bowl, pour over the hot stock, cover, and let it sit for 10 minutes to soak up the liquid.
Mix the cherry tomatoes, mint, goat's cheese, and the remaining oil together in a separate bowl.
Fluff the soaked couscous with a fork, then stir through the grilled aubergine, the tomato mixture, and the lemon juice.

ingredients.note: weight inferred for '1 sliced aubergine' (~300g medium aubergine)
brushed onto aubergine and mixed into salad; remains in the dish, not a frying medium

title: Roasted chicken with creamy walnut sauce
instructions: Heat your oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6.
Toss the chicken thighs with cumin, paprika, 1 tablespoon of olive oil and seasoning in a roasting tin.
Roast for about 40 minutes until the chicken is crisp and cooked through.
Meanwhile, tear up one pitta bread and place it in a small bowl, then pour over a couple of tablespoons of the chicken stock and let it soak.
Dry-fry the walnuts in a frying pan for about 3 minutes until golden and toasted, then set them aside.
Heat the remaining olive oil in the same pan and cook the onion and garlic until softened.
Blitz the soaked pitta, the onion mixture and most of the walnuts in a blender with the rest of the chicken stock until you get a rough paste.
Return the paste to the pan, stir in the cream and lemon juice, season, and keep warm.
When the chicken is cooked, arrange it on a platter.
Stir the coriander through the sauce and spoon it into a bowl.
Roughly chop the remaining walnuts and scatter them over the chicken.
Toast the remaining pittas, cut into wedges, and serve alongside.

ingredients.note: weight per thigh inferred at ~120g since none given
oil sears chicken and later stays in the sauce pan, so counted as eaten not frying medium
6 pittas counted; one is soaked into the sauce, the rest toasted and served alongside

title: Hummus
instructions: Drain the chickpeas well in a sieve set over a bowl, keeping the liquid for later.
Tip the chickpeas, tahini, garlic and yogurt into a food processor or blender and blitz until smooth.
Add the reserved chickpea liquid a tablespoon at a time, blending between additions, until the hummus reaches a nice, creamy consistency.
Scrape into a bowl, stir through a squeeze of lemon juice, and season to taste.

ingredients.note: 1 tbsp estimated as 15 g
1 large clove estimated as 5 g
3 tbsp estimated as 15 g each
juice of half a lemon estimated as 15 g

title: Turkish-style lamb
instructions: Fire up the grill to high heat.
Season the lamb leg steaks well, then grill for 2 minutes a side until nicely browned but still very rare inside.
While the lamb grills, mix the seasoning, oregano and half the mint into one tub of yogurt.
Smother the yogurt mixture over the lamb and return it to the grill for another 2-3 minutes, until the yogurt blisters and the meat is cooked to your liking.
Rest the meat on a board for a few minutes.
Toast the pitta breads while the meat rests.
Shred the lettuce and thinly slice the red onion.
Stir the remaining mint into the second tub of yogurt.
Thickly slice the rested lamb.
Stuff the lamb into the pitta breads with the shredded lettuce, sliced red onion and minted yogurt.
Squeeze fresh lemon juice over the top before serving, if you like.

ingredients.note: 4 lamb leg steaks; weight inferred at ~150 g each, no explicit weight given
generic 'seasoning' has no matching USDA key; missing ingredient, weight estimated for 2 tsp
1 tsp dried oregano, weight estimated

half a head of iceberg lettuce; weight estimated

1 whole lemon, weight estimated for juice yield

title: Walnut, date & honey cake
instructions: Preheat the oven to 160C (fan 140C, Gas 3).
Line the base and long sides of a 900g loaf tin with buttered greaseproof paper.
Combine the flour, cinnamon, butter, sugar, 2 tablespoons of honey and the eggs in a large mixing bowl.
Mash the bananas and chop the dates, then add them to the bowl.
Beat everything together for 2-3 minutes until well blended.
Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and level the top.
Scatter the walnut pieces over the top.
Bake for 1 hour, then press the top gently to check it's firm; if not, bake for a further 10 minutes.
Cool the cake in the tin for 15 minutes.
Lift the cake out using the paper lining.
Once cold, drizzle the remaining honey over the top.
Cut into thick slices to serve.

ingredients.note: 1/2 teaspoon converted to grams (~1.2g)
3 tablespoons total (2 in batter, 1 for drizzle), estimated at ~21g per tbsp
2 medium eggs estimated at ~50g each
2 medium bananas estimated at ~120g each

title: Griddled flatbreads
instructions: Tip both flours into a food processor with the yeast, sugar and a teaspoon of salt, then blitz to combine.
Pour in 350ml warm water and the olive oil, then process everything into a soft, smooth dough.
Run the machine for another minute, then let the dough rest somewhere warm until it's doubled in size, roughly an hour.
Give the dough a couple of quick pulses to knock the air out, then turn it onto a floured surface.
Cut the dough in half, roll one half into a rectangle about 20 x 40cm, and trim the edges neatly.
Cut that rectangle into eight 10cm squares and lay them out on a paper-lined tray in a single layer.
Repeat the rolling and cutting with the other half of the dough.
Leave the squares somewhere warm for about 30 minutes until they just start to puff.
Lay the breads straight onto the hot BBQ racks and grill for a couple of minutes until they puff up.
Flip and cook the other side, then pile them into a basket and serve with your dips.

ingredients.note: 1 tsp estimated as ~4g
2 tbsp estimated as ~28g; mixed into dough, not a frying medium since baked/grilled and eaten

title: Roast aubergine with goat's cheese & toasted flatbread
instructions: Heat your oven to 200°C (180°C fan / gas 6).
Brush the aubergine slices with 1 tablespoon of the olive oil and season with salt and pepper.
Arrange the aubergine on a baking tray and roast for 20 minutes until browned, adding the cherry tomatoes to the tray for the final 5 minutes.
Tear the pita bread into pieces, spread on a separate tray and bake for 8 minutes until crisp, then remove.
Whisk together the balsamic vinegar, mint, chopped shallot, chilli, the remaining olive oil, salt and pepper to make the dressing.
Scatter the roasted aubergine, tomatoes, sliced shallot and crisp pita into a serving bowl.
Pour the dressing over the top, crumble over the goat's cheese and finish with a scatter of rocket.

title: Griddled aubergines with sesame dressing
instructions: Brush the aubergine slices generously with olive oil and season with salt and pepper.
Heat a griddle pan or barbecue until hot, then griddle the aubergine slices for 2-3 minutes on each side until golden brown and tender.
Whisk the yogurt with the tahini, crushed garlic, lemon juice and chopped herbs, then season to taste.
Spoon the dressing over the griddled aubergines and scatter with the remaining herb leaves to serve.

title: Sweet potato salad
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan/gas 6).
Toss the sweet potato chunks with the olive oil and a pinch of seasoning, then spread them out on a parchment-lined baking sheet.
Roast for 30-35 minutes until tender and golden, then let them cool to room temperature.
While the potatoes are still slightly warm, whisk together all the dressing ingredients with a little extra seasoning.
Gently fold the dressing through the potato chunks by hand, taking care not to break them up.

title: Apricot & Turkish delight mess
instructions: Whisk the mascarpone, Greek yogurt, icing sugar, and orange blossom water together in a large bowl until thickened.
Fold in the meringue, apricot, Turkish delight, and almonds.
Divide the mixture between two dessert glasses or bowls and top with extra mint if you like.

title: Lamb & apricot meatballs
instructions: Warm most of the olive oil in a pan and let the red onions soften for about 5 minutes.
Stir in the crushed garlic, cumin and coriander, and cook for a couple more minutes.
Scoop half the onion mixture into a bowl and leave it to cool.
Tip the tinned tomatoes, sugar and seasoning into the onions left in the pan, then simmer for around 10 minutes until it reduces to a sauce.
While the sauce simmers, mix the mint, lamb mince, dried apricots and breadcrumbs into the cooled onions, season, and work it together with your hands.
Shape the mixture into small meatballs.
Heat the remaining oil in a non-stick pan and fry the meatballs until golden, working in batches if needed.
Stir the meatballs into the sauce with a splash of water and cook gently for a few minutes until cooked through.
Serve with pitta bread and a salad.

title: Aubergine & hummus grills
instructions: Arrange the aubergine slices in a single layer on a large baking sheet.
Brush them sparingly with vegetable oil and season generously.
Grill for 15 minutes, turning twice and brushing with more oil, until the slices are softened and fully cooked.
While the aubergine grills, whizz the bread into crumbs in a food processor.
Add 2 tsp of the oil to the crumbs and pulse briefly to coat.
Spread a couple of teaspoons of hummus over the top of each aubergine slice.
Tip the breadcrumbs onto a large plate and press the hummus side of each aubergine slice into the crumbs to coat.
Grill again, crumb-side up, for about 3 minutes until golden.
Toss the walnuts, parsley and cherry tomatoes together in a bowl and season.
Add the lemon juice and olive oil, then toss again to dress.
Serve the aubergine grills with the walnut salad, an extra dollop of hummus and some pitta bread.

title: Baklava with spiced nuts, ricotta & chocolate
instructions: Make the syrup: combine the sugar with 650ml water in a large saucepan. Stir over low heat until dissolved, then boil. Reduce to a simmer for 15 mins, add a few drops of lemon juice and simmer 5 mins more. Cool.
Melt the butter gently in a small pan for 5 mins, skimming off any froth. Set aside for assembly.
For the filling, crush all the nuts to a mostly-fine mix with a few chunky pieces. Stir in the cinnamon and cardamom and set aside.
In another bowl, mix the ricotta with the lemon and orange zests and the vanilla.
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Brush a large baking tray (about 35 x 47cm) with melted butter.
Working one filo sheet at a time (keep the rest under a damp tea towel), lay a sheet with a short end facing you. Scatter 30g of the nut mix over it, then spoon 1 tbsp ricotta along the near end.

Fold the near end over the filling, set a thin skewer alongside and roll into a long, chipolata-thick roll. Scrunch it accordion-style, slide it off the skewer onto the tray, and repeat for about 12 rolls.
Cut each roll into four for 48 large baklava (or eight for 96 mini). Brush with the remaining butter and bake 20-25 mins until golden, turning the tray halfway.
While the baklava is still hot, ladle over the syrup until it sizzles. Leave to cool and absorb.
Melt the dark chocolate over simmering water or in short microwave bursts, drizzle over the cooled baklava and finish with the ground pistachios.

ingredients.note: Recipe says 'a few drops' with no amount; inferred ~3g.
Inferred from 2 tbsp ground cinnamon at ~7g/tbsp.
Inferred from 1 tbsp ground cardamom at ~6g/tbsp.
Inferred zest yield of 1 lemon ~6g.
Inferred zest yield of 1 orange ~9g.
Inferred from 1 tbsp at ~15g/tbsp.

title: Poulet Roti a l'Algerienne (Algerian Roast Chicken)
instructions: Preheat your oven to 175°C (350°F).
In a roasting dish, whisk together the water, chopped onion, olive oil, balsamic vinegar, Dijon mustard, crushed garlic, black pepper, cayenne pepper, and salt.
Add the chicken to the dish and turn it to coat thoroughly in the marinade.
Roast until an instant-read thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the thigh, near the bone, reads 74°C (165°F), about 1 hour 30 minutes.

ingredients.note: 1 chopped onion, estimated medium size ~110g
marinade oil stays in the roasting dish and coats the served chicken, not a frying medium

title: Algerian Kefta (Meatballs)
instructions: Combine the ground beef with half the crushed garlic and a tablespoon of the chopped onion in a large bowl, then work it together with your hands until fully mixed.
Shape the mixture into oblong patties about 1 1/2 inches long — you'll get 12 to 14 meatballs.
Heat a large skillet over medium-high heat and brown the patties in batches until crispy on both sides and no longer pink in the middle, roughly 10 minutes total.
Set the browned meatballs aside in a rimmed serving dish.
Drop the heat to medium, stir the remaining chopped onion into the drippings left in the skillet, and season with salt and pepper.
Cook, stirring constantly, until the onion softens and turns translucent, about 5 minutes.
Stir in the remaining garlic and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the plum tomatoes, parsley, and ras el hanout, then pour in the water.
Simmer until the tomatoes soften, about 5 minutes.
Pour the tomato sauce over the meatballs and serve.

ingredients.note: 4 crushed garlic cloves estimated at 5g each; used split between meat mix and sauce per instructions
1/2 cup chopped onion estimated at ~80g; split between meat mix and sauce
'to taste' in original — no fixed amount given, estimated a small pinch
'to taste' in original — no fixed amount given, estimated a small pinch

title: Algerian Carrots
instructions: Set a steamer insert into a saucepan, fill with about 1 1/2 cups of water just below the steamer bottom, cover, and bring to a boil over high heat.
Add the sliced carrots, lower the heat to medium, cover again, and steam 4-6 minutes until tender but still firm.
Reserve 1/2 cup of the steaming liquid and set the carrots aside.
Warm the olive oil in a skillet over medium heat, then lower the heat and stir in the salt, pepper, cinnamon, cumin, garlic, and thyme.
Cook the spices and garlic gently, stirring often, until fragrant, about 10 minutes.
Pour in the reserved cooking liquid, add the bay leaf, cover, and simmer for 20 minutes.
Tip in the carrots, toss well to coat in the spiced oil, and cook 2-3 minutes until heated through.
Finish with a splash of lemon juice and pull out the bay leaf before serving.

oil is simmered into the sauce and coats the carrots served, not drained

title: Chtitha Batata (Algerian Potato Stew)
instructions: Pound the garlic, chile pepper, cumin, paprika, black pepper, cayenne, and salt in a mortar until it forms a smooth paste.
Work in the olive oil to loosen the paste into a dersa.
Heat a large saucepan over medium heat and stir-fry the dersa until fragrant, 2 to 4 minutes.
Add the potato halves and toss them through the dersa to coat.
Stir in the tomato puree.
Pour in just enough water to cover the potatoes and bring to a boil.
Lower the heat and simmer until the potatoes are tender, about 40 minutes.
Ladle the potatoes into a serving bowl and spoon over any remaining sauce.

ingredients.note: 4 cloves estimated at 3 g each
1 small chilli estimated
oil is worked into the dersa paste and remains in the finished stew, not a frying medium

title: Algerian Bouzgene Berber Bread with Roasted Pepper Sauce
instructions: Fire up your broiler and lay the red peppers and tomatoes on a baking sheet.
Roast them under the broiler for about 8 minutes, turning now and then, until the skins blister and blacken.
Let them cool, then peel off the charred skins and drop the flesh into a large bowl, discarding the pepper cores and seeds.
Warm a tablespoon of olive oil in a skillet over medium heat and cook the garlic and jalapeno until tender, stirring often.
Tip the garlic and jalapeno into the bowl with the tomatoes and peppers.
Take two sharp knives, one in each hand, and chop the mixture down to a coarse, soupy sauce.
Stir well and set the sauce aside.
In a large bowl, mix the semolina with the salt and 4 tablespoons of olive oil.
Gradually work in water with your hand, squeezing and kneading, until the dough comes together smooth and pliable, not sticky or dry.
Divide the dough into 6 pieces and roll each into a ball.
For each round, heat a tablespoon of olive oil in a heavy skillet over medium heat.
Roll the dough out thin, no more than a quarter inch thick, and fry until dark spots appear and the bread turns crispy.
Lift each flatbread out and wrap in a clean towel while you fry the rest.
Serve by tearing off pieces of bread and scooping up the roasted pepper sauce.

title: Dziriat (Algerian Almond Tarts)
instructions: The day before, boil about 6 cups of water, then take it off the heat and drop in the almonds; let them sit for 5 minutes, drain, and slip off their skins.
Spread the peeled almonds on baking sheets and dry them out in a low oven (200°F/95°C) for several hours until completely dry and lightly toasted — watch closely so they don't scorch and turn bitter.
For the syrup, bring 1 cup sugar and 1 cup water to a boil in a saucepan, stir in the lemon juice, then lower the heat and simmer gently for 30-40 minutes until syrupy.
Stir in the orange blossom water, remove from the heat, and set the syrup aside.
For the dough, mix the flour and salt in a large bowl, make a well, and add the oil, egg, lemon juice, and orange blossom water.
Work everything together with your fingers until it looks like coarse crumbs, then sprinkle in warm water gradually, mixing until you have a soft, pliable dough.
Divide the dough into 4 equal balls, cover with a damp cloth, and set aside to rest.
Finely grind the toasted almonds in a food processor.
Measure out 3 cups of the ground almonds and mix with sugar, baking powder, vanilla, lemon zest, and orange blossom water.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, mixing until you get a sticky, paste-like almond filling.
Dust your work surface with cornstarch to stop sticking, then roll each dough portion out very thinly, about 1-2 mm thick.
Cut the dough into 10 cm circles, dust each lightly with cornstarch, and press cornstarch-side down into tart molds, trimming the excess from the rim.
Fill each mold three-quarters full with the almond filling.
Bake on the top shelf at 350°F (175°C) for 20-25 minutes, until golden and firm.
As soon as the tarts come out of the oven, unmold them and dip each one, still hot, into the sugar syrup.
Press a pine nut into the center of each tart for decoration and set them on a wire rack to drain and cool.

ingredients.note: 2 US cups flour estimated at 120g/cup
pinch estimated as ~1g
4 US cups whole almonds estimated at ~143g/cup
zest of 1 lemon estimated at 6g
dusting amount inferred, not measured in original
garnish amount inferred; no quantity given in original

title: Khobz el Dar (Algerian Semolina Bread)
instructions: Combine the semolina, sesame seeds, sugar, yeast, and salt in a large bowl.
Whisk in the oil, whole egg, and egg white until fully blended.
Slowly stir in the warm milk to form a loose, liquid dough.
Cover the bowl and let the dough sit at room temperature until frothy, about 1 hour.
Stir in the flour with a wooden spoon until a sticky dough comes together.
Cover again and let the dough rest for 30 minutes.
Line a baking sheet with parchment paper or a baking mat.
Dust the dough and your hands with flour, kneading in more a tablespoon at a time until the dough pulls away from the bowl.
Shape the dough into a round loaf and place it on the prepared sheet.
Cover loosely with a towel and let rise in a warm spot for about 1 hour, until doubled in size.
Preheat the oven to 400°F (200°C).
Beat the egg yolk with the water and brush it over the entire loaf.
Sprinkle the remaining sesame seeds over the top.
Bake until golden brown, about 20 to 25 minutes.

ingredients.note: Instructions use this whole egg plus the white of the next egg in the dough; treated as one whole egg here.

title: Tajine de Poulet aux Carottes et Patates Douces (Chicken and Sweet Potato Tagine)
instructions: In a large pot, combine the sliced onions, celery, cilantro and parsley.
Stir in the turmeric, cumin, paprika, ginger, cayenne, salt and pepper.
Lay the chicken thighs on top of the vegetable mixture and drizzle with olive oil.
Cover and simmer gently over low heat until the chicken is tender, about 40 minutes.
Meanwhile, soak the raisins in a small bowl of hot water for 10 minutes, then drain.
Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F).
Transfer the chicken mixture to an oven-safe tagine or casserole dish and add the sweet potato and carrots.
Cover with a lid or foil and bake until the sweet potato is soft, about 40 minutes.
Stir in the drained raisins and prunes, then continue baking covered until heated through, about 10 minutes.

title: Algerian Flafla (Bell Pepper Salad)
instructions: Preheat your oven to 450°F (230°C) and lay the whole peppers on a foil-lined tray.
Roast the peppers for 30 to 45 minutes, turning once, until the skins are blistered black and the flesh is soft.
Pull the peppers out and let them cool for about 10 minutes, then peel off the skins and remove the stems and seeds.
Chop the roasted peppers into half-inch pieces.
Warm the olive oil in a skillet over medium heat and cook the onion, stirring often, until soft and translucent, about 5 minutes.
Stir in the garlic, salt, and pepper, then add the chopped roasted peppers and diced tomato.
Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the tomato softens and everything melds together, about 5 minutes.

title: Chorba Hamra bel Frik (Algerian Lamb, Tomato, and Freekeh Soup)
instructions: Soak the freekeh in cold water and set it aside.
Combine the lamb, onion, black pepper, paprika, cinnamon, and salt in a pot.
Stir in the oil, half the cilantro, half the mint, and the celery stalk.
Simmer gently over low heat for 15 minutes.
Stir in the chickpeas, pour in just enough water to cover, and bring back to a simmer.
Stir in the zucchini, carrot, and tomato paste.
Set a steamer over the pot, add the tomatoes, cover, and steam until soft, about 5 minutes.
Crush the softened tomatoes with a wooden spoon so the pulp drips into the soup, then discard the leftover skins.
Add the potato along with enough water to cover, and simmer until the potato is soft, about 10 minutes.
Drain the freekeh and stir it into the soup, simmering until tender, about 15 minutes.
Remove and discard the celery stalk.
Sprinkle the soup with the remaining cilantro and mint before serving.

title: Cheese Borek
instructions: Whisk the egg together with the parsley, garlic and crushed red pepper in a bowl.
Stir the gouda and emmentaler into the egg mixture until well combined.
Lay one phyllo sheet on a flat surface, brush it with melted butter, then cut it lengthwise into four strips.
Spoon a rounded teaspoon of the cheese filling onto the end of each strip and fold it corner over corner into a triangle, continuing the fold along the whole strip.
Repeat with the remaining phyllo sheets and filling.
Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F) and lightly butter a large baking sheet.
Arrange the stuffed triangles in a single layer on the prepared sheet.
Bake for about 30 minutes until lightly browned, then serve warm.

title: Cornes de Gazelle (Gazelle Horns)
instructions: Preheat your oven to 150°C (300°F) and line a baking sheet with parchment.
Spread the almonds over the sheet and roast for 20-25 minutes until fragrant.
While the almonds toast, make the pastry: rub the flour, butter and salt together in a bowl until it looks like breadcrumbs.
Add the beaten egg, vegetable oil and 1½ tablespoons orange blossom water, then knead into a smooth dough, adding a little water if it's too dry.
Shape the dough into a ball, wrap in plastic, and rest for 30 minutes.
Let the roasted almonds cool for about 5 minutes, then grind them finely in a food processor.
Add the sugar, cinnamon, egg and 1 tablespoon orange blossom water to the processor in that order, pulsing after each addition until you have a smooth paste.
With greased hands, pinch off walnut-sized pieces of the almond paste and roll into small logs with tapered ends.
Preheat the oven to 165°C (325°F) and line a fresh baking sheet with parchment.
Roll out half the pastry dough very thin on a floured surface.
Lay a filling log at the edge of the pastry, fold the dough over to enclose it, and seal the edges.
Shape into a crescent with your fingers, then trim around it with a pastry cutter and transfer to the baking sheet.
Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Bake for about 20 minutes until lightly golden but not too dark, then let cool for a few minutes.
Warm the honey gently in a small saucepan, remove from heat, and stir in the last tablespoon of orange blossom water.
Dip each gazelle horn into the warm honey, arrange on a serving plate, and scatter with crushed pistachios.

title: Piernik (Polish gingerbread)
instructions: Heat the oven to 160C (140C fan / gas 3) and grease and line a deep 20cm cake tin.
Melt the honey, sugar and butter together in a saucepan over medium heat, then remove from the heat and let cool.
Mix the flour and spices together in a large bowl.
Whisk the eggs into the cooled honey mixture.
Pour the wet mixture over the dry ingredients and whisk gently into a smooth batter, taking care not to overmix.
Pour the batter into the prepared tin and bake for 50-55 minutes, until risen and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool the cake in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
Slice the cooled cake into 3 layers with a serrated knife.
Spread jam over the two bottom layers and reassemble the cake.
Melt the cream, chocolate and honey together in a pan over low-medium heat until smooth and pourable.
Set the cake on a wire rack over a lined tray and pour the glaze over to coat completely.
Transfer the glazed cake to a serving plate and let the glaze set.
Dust with edible gold powder to decorate, if you like.

title: Pistachio cake
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (160C fan/gas 4) and butter and line a 23cm springform tin.
Blitz 150g of the pistachios in a food processor until finely ground, then tip into a bowl with the flour, baking powder and a pinch of salt.
In a separate bowl, beat the butter and sugar together until pale and fluffy, about 5 minutes.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, giving each a full minute of beating before adding the next.
Beat in the vanilla, then gently fold in the flour mixture.
Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and bake for 35-40 minutes, until a skewer comes out clean.
Cool the cake completely on a wire rack while still in the tin.
Meanwhile, sift the icing sugar into a bowl, then add the mascarpone, double cream and pistachio paste and beat until smooth.
Chill the cream mixture for 20-30 minutes until it thickens to a spoonable consistency.
Slice the cooled cake in half horizontally with a serrated knife to make two layers.
Place the bottom layer on a stand and spread over a generous spoonful of the pistachio cream, then scatter over a good handful of raspberries.
Top with the second sponge layer, then cover the top and sides with the remaining cream.
Finish with the last of the raspberries, the remaining pistachios and the lime zest.
Keep chilled in an airtight container for up to two days.

title: Zapiekanki
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan) / gas 6 and bake the baguettes on a tray for 8-10 minutes until lightly toasted, then let them cool.
Warm 2 tsp of the butter with 1 tsp of the oil in a pan over low heat and cook the chopped onion for about 5 minutes until soft.
Add the mushrooms, raise the heat to medium, and cook for 5-10 minutes more until softened and the liquid has evaporated.
Stir in the garlic and cook for 1-2 minutes until fragrant, then season and mix in the mayonnaise; take off the heat.
Halve the baguettes lengthways and lay them cut-side up on the tray, then spread over the mushroom mixture.
Scatter over the cheddar, ham, kabanos and mozzarella, then bake for 8-10 minutes until the cheese is melted and golden.
Meanwhile, heat the remaining butter and oil in a pan over medium heat and fry the sliced onions for about 5 minutes until soft and golden, then set aside.
Once the baguette pizzas are baked, top with the caramelised onions, dill pickles if using, the chives, and a zig-zag drizzle of ketchup.

title: Polish chocolate & walnut cake
instructions: Heat the oven to 170C/150C fan/gas 3½ and line a 30 x 22cm shallow baking tin with baking parchment.
Sift the flour and stir in the cocoa, then set the mixture aside.
Beat the egg whites until stiff, then whisk in the sugar in small batches.
Whisk in the egg yolks one at a time.
Fold in the flour-cocoa mixture gently with a spatula.
Pour the batter into the lined tin and bake for 35 minutes.
Cool the sponge for 5 minutes, turn it out of the tin, then re-line the tin with fresh parchment.
For the meringue, whisk the egg whites until stiff, then gradually whisk in the sugar a tablespoon at a time.
Once the meringue is stiff and glossy, fold in the finely chopped walnuts, flour and potato starch.
Spread the nut meringue into the re-lined tin and bake for 40 minutes at the same temperature, then let it cool.
For the glaze, warm the cream over low heat, remove from the heat, then stir in the chopped chocolate until melted and glossy.
Slice the cooled sponge and the meringue layer each into two horizontal layers.
Set the nut meringue on the bottom sponge layer and scatter over some reserved walnuts.
Add another meringue layer, top with the second sponge layer, then cover the top with the chocolate glaze.
Decorate with walnuts and chill the finished cake for 1-2 hours before serving.

title: Sauerkraut pierogi
instructions: Heat oil to 180°C and toss the shallots in a little flour, then deep-fry for about a minute until light golden and crispy; drain on paper (can be made up to two days ahead and stored airtight).
Heat oil in a frying pan and gently fry the shallots for 10 minutes until starting to turn golden.
Add the sauerkraut and cabbage, cook 5-10 minutes until the cabbage softens, season with a little salt or a pinch of sugar if needed, then scrape into a bowl and let cool completely.
Mix the eggs and oil with 125ml water, then gradually work in the flour by hand until you have a firm, elastic dough; wrap in cling film and rest in the fridge at least 30 minutes, or overnight.
Roll the dough out on a well-floured surface into a 40cm circle, about as thick as a £1 coin.
Cut discs with a 9cm cutter to get about 25 discs, keeping the off-cuts to boil along with the pierogi.
Spoon 1 tsp of filling into the centre of each disc, fold in half to seal into half-moon shapes, and lay on a floured tray without letting them touch.
Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil and lower in the pierogi, boiling for about 2 minutes until they float.
Drain and serve with a knob of butter, a spoonful of soured cream, and a scatter of the crispy shallots on top.

title: Pomegranate salad
instructions: Whisk together the olive oil, lemon juice, honey and thyme leaves in a large bowl, then season well.
Toss in the lettuce, cherry tomatoes, spring onions and chilli until fully coated in the dressing.
Scoop the pomegranate seeds into a bowl, removing any white membrane.
Arrange the dressed salad on a platter, then scatter over the pomegranate seeds and feta.

title: Polish doughnuts (Pączki)
instructions: Stir the yeast, sugar and flour together, mix in the warm milk, and let it sit in a warm spot for 30 minutes to form the leaven.
Whisk the egg yolks with the sugar and vanilla sugar over a pan of simmering water until the mixture turns smooth and thick.

Sift the flour into a stand mixer, add the warm yolk mixture and warm cream, mix thoroughly, then work in a pinch of salt and the leaven, kneading for 10 minutes on the machine or 15-20 minutes by hand.
Once the dough no longer clings to your hands, pour in the cooled melted butter and knead a few minutes more, then cover and let it rise somewhere warm for 1 to 1½ hours.
Stir the jam with the ground almonds and load it into a piping bag, then divide the dough into 16 pieces, shape each into a doughnut, cover and let them rise another 30 minutes.
Heat oil or melted lard in a deep pan to 180°C, fry the doughnuts in batches for 1-2 minutes per side until a pale ring forms around the middle, then lift out and drain on kitchen paper.

title: Polish patties (kotlety)
instructions: Soak the bread roll in the milk until fully softened.
Fry the chopped onion in a little of the oil for about 8 minutes, until soft and golden.
In a bowl, combine the beef mince, fried onion, egg, minced garlic, marjoram and a good pinch of seasoning.
Squeeze the excess milk from the softened bread roll and mix it into the meat mixture; combine everything thoroughly and season well.
Divide the mixture into 8 equal patties.
Tip the breadcrumbs into a shallow bowl and coat each patty all over.
Heat oil in a deep frying pan and fry the patties in batches, turning once, until browned and cooked through, about 6-7 minutes per side.

title: Bigos (Polish hunter's stew)
instructions: Place the sliced white cabbage in a heavy casserole with the beef stock and simmer gently for about 50 minutes until tender.
Slice the soaked mushrooms into strips, keeping their soaking liquid.
Melt the lard in a pan and fry the sausages and bacon until browned, then remove them, leaving the fat behind.
Fry the chopped onions in the same fat for 5-8 minutes until lightly golden.
Stir the mushrooms and their soaking liquid into the cabbage pot along with the cooked sausages, bacon, beef, onions and prunes; cover and cook for 20 minutes.
Add the bay leaf, cloves, peppercorns, juniper berries, allspice berries, red wine and tomato purée, bring to a simmer, then cover and cook for a further hour.
Season well and let the bigos cool; it keeps covered and chilled for up to two days and tastes better after a day or two.
Reheat until piping hot before serving.

title: Pork & sauerkraut goulash
instructions: Melt the lard in a saucepan over medium heat and fry the finely chopped onion until golden, about 5-8 minutes.
Add the cumin seeds and pork, frying for 10 minutes until browned all over.
Stir in the garlic, season well, and dust with the flour, cooking for a minute.
Sprinkle in the paprika and cook for another minute.
Pour in half the beef stock, add the bay leaves, cover and simmer gently for 30 minutes.
Add the sauerkraut, the rest of the stock, and a splash of the sauerkraut pickling juice.
Cover and simmer for 30 minutes more, stirring occasionally, until the pork is tender.
Stir in the whipping cream and simmer for 5 minutes to bring the flavours together.
Season to taste and serve topped with a spoonful of sour cream.

title: Slow-roasted ham with lemon, garlic & sage
instructions: Heat your oven to 200°C (180°C fan/gas 6).
Pound the garlic, sage, lemon zest, salt and pepper in a mortar and pestle until it forms a paste.
Stir the oil into the paste, then rub it all over the pork shoulder, keeping it off the skin.
Score the skin with a sharp knife and rub a good pinch of salt into the cuts.
Tie the pork with kitchen string to hold its shape.
Line a large baking tray with a double layer of foil and set the pork on top, skin-side up.
Bring the foil sides up around the pork like a parcel and pour the wine into the tray around the meat.
Put it in the oven and drop the temperature to 140°C (120°C fan/gas 1).
Roast for 4-5 hours, until a meat thermometer in the thickest part reads 70°C.
Turn the oven up to 240°C (220°C fan/gas 9).
Carefully spoon the roasting juices into a saucepan and simmer over medium heat for 10-15 minutes until reduced by a third, then season to taste.
Fold the foil back so only the skin is exposed and return the pork to the oven for 10-15 minutes until the skin is puffed and browned.
Rest the pork for 20 minutes before slicing and serve with the reduced sauce drizzled over.

title: Raspberry mousse
instructions: Soak the gelatine leaves in a bowl of warm water for about 5 minutes, then drain and squeeze out the excess water.
Put the raspberries, sugar and lemon juice in a pan over medium-low heat and cook for 5-6 minutes until the berries break down completely.
Push the raspberry mixture through a sieve into a bowl, discarding the seeds.
Stir the softened gelatine into the warm raspberry puree until fully dissolved, reheating gently in a clean pan if needed.
Set the mixture aside to cool for about 15 minutes.
Whip the cream to soft peaks with an electric whisk, then gently fold it into the cooled raspberry mixture.
Spoon the mousse into ramekins or moulds and chill overnight, or for at least 6 hours.
Serve topped with a scattering of fresh raspberries.

title: Mini bundt cakes
instructions: Heat your oven to 180°C (160°C fan / gas 4).
Melt a little of the butter and brush it generously into the holes of a mini bundt tin.
Dust the greased holes with a little flour, tapping out any excess.
Beat the remaining butter, caster sugar and a pinch of salt together with an electric whisk until pale and fluffy.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, mixing well after each addition.
Sift the flour and baking powder together, then fold into the wet mixture until smooth.
In a separate small bowl, stir the cocoa powder into the milk until smooth.
Spoon a tablespoon of the plain batter into each bundt hole.
Fold the cocoa mixture into the remaining plain batter, then divide it evenly among the holes on top of the plain layer.
Bake for 15-18 minutes, checking with a skewer after 15 minutes and giving it a few more minutes if it doesn't come out clean.
Leave the cakes to cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
Dust with icing sugar before serving.

title: Braised stuffed cabbage
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (fan 160C, gas 4).
Cut the tough central stalk out of the cabbage leaves.
Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil and blanch the leaves for 1-2 minutes until just starting to wilt.
Drain, refresh under cold water, then pat the leaves dry with a tea towel.
Heat the oil in a pan and fry the onion for 5 minutes until slightly browned.
Add the rosemary and celery and cook for a further 8 minutes.
Stir in the rice and cook for a minute until the grains glisten, then take off the heat.
Stir the cooked chestnuts and cranberries into the rice mixture and season well.
Spoon a little stuffing onto each cabbage leaf, roll up and tuck in the sides to enclose the filling.
Arrange the rolls join-side down in a single layer in a large, oiled, shallow ovenproof dish.
Mix the stock, vinegar and honey together and pour over the cabbage rolls.
Cover the dish tightly with foil and bake for 1 hour.
Uncover and cook for a further 15 minutes.

title: Pork rib bortsch
instructions: Cut the pork ribs into large chunks and put them in your biggest pot, covering with about 5 litres of water.
Bring to a boil over high heat, skimming off any foam that rises.
Add the bay leaves and season with salt.
Lower the heat to a gentle simmer and cook for about an hour, until the meat is falling off the bone.
If you're using dried beans, stir them in now.
Turn the heat back up to bring it to a boil, then reduce to a simmer for 20 minutes so the beans stay slightly firm.
Stir in the carrots, onion, garlic and red pepper.
Add the chillies if you want some heat, then keep cooking for 15 minutes.
Add the beetroot and simmer for 10 minutes.
Add the potatoes and cook for a further 15 minutes.
Stir in the tomato purée to taste, plus the canned beans if that's what you used, and bring back to a boil.
Cook for 5 minutes, then add the cabbage and cook for another 5 minutes.
Season to taste, scatter over the parsley and dill, turn off the heat and let it stand for 5 minutes.
Serve with a spoonful of sour cream on the side.

title: Beetroot & red cabbage sauerkraut
instructions: Tip the beetroot, red cabbage and onion into a large bowl.
Add 1-1½ tsp freshly ground black pepper.
Scrunch everything together with your hands for about 5 minutes until the juices start to release (gloves help avoid beetroot stains).
Press the vegetables down firmly in the bowl.
Cover the surface and sides with a sheet of compostable cling film or a reusable wrap.
Set another bowl of similar size on top and weigh it down with something heavy, like tins or bags of rice, so the juices rise above the vegetables.
Cover the whole setup again and leave to ferment at room temperature for at least five days, or up to five weeks for deeper flavour, until the bubbling settles.
Check the sauerkraut every few days, give it a stir when you see bubbles, then re-cover and re-weight it.
Taste occasionally as it sours, and once it's to your liking, transfer to sterilised jars and refrigerate.
Store chilled for up to six months.

title: Rye bread
instructions: Whisk the rye flour, bread flour, yeast and salt together in a large bowl.
Stir the honey into 250ml warm water, then pour it into the dry ingredients and mix into a dough, adding a splash more warm water if the rye makes it too dry.
Turn the dough out and knead for about 10 minutes until smooth — it won't feel as springy as a white dough since rye has less gluten.
Place the dough in an oiled bowl, cover with cling film and let it rise somewhere warm for 1-2 hours until roughly doubled.
Dust a 2lb/900g loaf tin with flour.
Knock back the dough on your work surface, kneading in the caraway seeds if using.
Shape into a smooth oval, place in the tin, cover with oiled cling film and let rise again for 1-1.5 hours until doubled.
Heat the oven to 220C/200C fan/gas 7.
Uncover the loaf, dust the top with rye flour and slash a few angled cuts across the surface.
Bake for 30 minutes until dark brown and it sounds hollow when tapped underneath.
Cool on a wire rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing and serving.

title: Cucumber & fennel salad
instructions: Place the cucumber in a sieve, sprinkle with salt and sugar, and let it drain for 10 minutes.
Toss the drained cucumber with the sliced fennel.
Whisk together the sour cream, lemon juice, vinegar and dill, then season with black pepper.
Pour the dressing over the cucumber and fennel and mix well before serving.

title: Challah
instructions: Whisk the yeast with a pinch of sugar and a couple tablespoons of lukewarm water; let it sit until foamy, about 10 minutes.
In a big bowl, combine the flour, remaining sugar and 2 tsp salt, then well the centre and add half the beaten egg, the yeast mixture and the oil.
Pour in 200ml lukewarm water and bring together first with a spoon then by hand, adding a splash more water if it's too dry, until you have a moist but not sticky dough.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and springy, dusting with a little flour only if needed.
Shape into a ball, oil a bowl, roll the dough in it to coat, cover with a tea towel and let it rise in a warm spot for about 1 hour until doubled.
Divide the risen dough into three equal pieces and roll each into a 25cm rope, tapering the ends.
Lay the ropes side by side, join at the top and plait them together, tucking the ends underneath.
Transfer the plaited loaf to a lined baking sheet, cover loosely and let it prove again for about 40 minutes until puffy.
Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6, brush the loaf all over with the remaining beaten egg and scatter over the poppy or sesame seeds if using.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until deep golden and hollow-sounding when tapped, tenting with foil if it browns too fast after 15 minutes.
Cool the loaf on a wire rack before slicing and serving.

title: Borsch
instructions: Simmer the beef shin with the whole onion, bay leaf and 2 litres of lightly salted cold water for 1½–3 hours, until the meat is fork-tender, skimming off any scum as it rises.
Break the beef into smaller pieces in the broth and discard the spent onion.
Add the potatoes to the pot, season well with salt and pepper, and simmer for 10-15 minutes until tender.
Meanwhile, heat the sunflower oil in a deep frying pan and sauté the diced onion and carrot until the carrot softens and just starts to caramelise.
Stir in the beetroot and cook for about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add the red pepper, if using, and cook for 2 minutes more.
Add the tinned tomatoes and prunes, stir, then boil hard to reduce slightly before tipping the whole mixture into the borsch.
Add the shredded cabbage and kidney beans, and simmer for 7-10 minutes until the cabbage is tender.
Serve the borsch with a dollop of crème fraîche, a scatter of chopped dill, and crusty bread on the side.

title: Tangy cabbage slaw
instructions: Whisk the mayonnaise together with the lemon zest and juice, cider vinegar, wholegrain mustard and celery salt in a small bowl, then season generously to taste.
Toss in the cabbage, carrots, onion and celery, mixing well until evenly coated, then refrigerate — this can be made up to a day ahead.

title: Beetroot latkes
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan / gas 4).
Mix all the latke ingredients together in a bowl until well combined.
Heat the rapeseed oil in a large non-stick pan.
Spoon the mixture into the pan to form six round latkes.
Fry the latkes for 4-5 minutes on each side until golden.
Transfer the fried latkes to a baking sheet and bake for 10 minutes.
Stir the yogurt and mint together in a small bowl to make the dressing.
Toss the salad leaves and cherry tomatoes together.
Serve the latkes topped with the mint yogurt alongside the salad.

title: Sauerkraut pierogii
instructions: Heat oil to 180°C, toss the shallots in a little flour, then deep-fry for about a minute until golden and crisp; drain on kitchen paper (these can be made up to two days ahead and stored airtight).
Gently fry the chopped shallots in oil for about 10 minutes until starting to turn golden.
Add the sauerkraut and cabbage, cook 5-10 minutes until softened, season with salt or a touch of sugar if needed, then scrape into a bowl and cool completely.
Mix the beaten egg and oil with water, then gradually work in the flour by hand and knead on a floured surface until you have a firm, elastic dough; wrap and rest in the fridge at least 30 minutes.
Roll the dough out on a well-floured surface into a large circle about the thickness of a coin.
Cut discs from the dough with a cutter, keeping the off-cuts to boil alongside the pierogi.
Spoon a teaspoon of the cooled filling into the centre of each disc, fold in half and seal into half-moon shapes, laying them on a floured tray without touching.
Lower the pierogi into a large pan of boiling salted water and cook for about 2 minutes until they float.
Drain and serve topped with a knob of butter, a spoon of soured cream, and the crispy shallots.

title: Rosemary braised red cabbage with kabanos
instructions: Halve the red cabbage, cut out the tough stem, and thinly slice it.
Tip the cabbage into a large pan with the olive oil, butter, red onion, vinegar, sugar, chilli, rosemary and apple, then stir in 300ml water and season with salt and pepper.
Bring to a simmer, then lower the heat, cover with a tight-fitting lid and cook gently for 1½ hours, stirring often and adding a splash more water if it looks dry.
Add the kabanos, cover again and simmer gently for 20 minutes.
Uncover and cook for a further 10 minutes.
Serve alongside simple mash or boiled potatoes.

title: Beetroot pancakes
instructions: Blitz the beetroot with the milk using a stick blender until smooth and vibrant.
Tip the beetroot mixture into a bowl with the flour, baking powder, maple syrup, vanilla extract and eggs, then whisk until you have a smooth, vividly purple batter.
Melt a small knob of butter in a large non-stick frying pan over medium-low heat until foamy.
Spoon in 2 tbsp portions of batter to make 3-4 pancakes at a time, cook for 2-3 minutes, flip and cook a further minute until set through.
Keep the cooked pancakes warm in a low oven while you repeat with the rest of the batter, adding more butter to the pan as needed.
For a quick compote, simmer the frozen berries with 1 tbsp of the blackcurrant jam for 5-10 minutes until bubbling and syrupy.
Stir the remaining jam through the Greek yogurt in a small bowl.
Stack the warm pancakes with the jammy yogurt and spoon the hot berry compote over the top to serve.

title: Beef Empanadas
instructions: Stir the lard, warm water, and salt together in a large bowl to start the dough.
Add the flour and oregano, then knead by hand or with a mixer for about five minutes.
Cover the dough and let it rest for at least half an hour, or overnight in the fridge.
Blanch the tomatoes for 30 seconds in boiling water, plunge into cold water, peel, and dice.
Press the garlic and dice the onions.
Sauté the garlic and onions in a little olive oil until soft, then remove from the pan.
Brown the sirloin steak in the same pan over high heat on all sides.
Season the meat with the herbs and spices, then return the onions, garlic, and tomatoes to the pan.
Simmer everything together for a few minutes, adjusting salt, pepper, and spices to taste.
If prepping ahead, cool and chill the filling overnight.
Boil the eggs, chop into cubes, and fold them through the meat filling.
Divide the dough in half and roll one half out thinly on a floured surface.
Cut circles about 12-15 cm in diameter from the dough.
Spoon 2-4 teaspoons of filling onto each circle, dampen the edges with water, fold into half-moons, and crimp the edges with a fork.
Repeat rolling and filling until the dough and filling are used up.
Preheat the oven to 200°C.
Brush the empanadas with egg wash and bake in batches of about eight on a parchment-lined sheet for 25 minutes, until golden.
Serve warm with chimichurri sauce.

title: Torta de fiambre
instructions: Line the bottom of a 20cm (8-9 inch) springform pan with parchment paper, letting some overhang since the batter is quite liquid and may spill.
Preheat the oven to 220°C (425°F).
Grate the cheddar cheese and take the ham out of its packaging.
In a large bowl, mix all the remaining ingredients together for one to two minutes until combined.
Pour half the batter into the prepared pan.
Layer the ham and grated cheese on top.
Pour the remaining batter over the ham and cheese.
Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the top turns golden.
Let the torta rest for a few minutes before releasing it from the pan.
Serve hot or cold, garnished with fresh herbs if you like.

ingredients.note: 3 eggs converted at ~50g each

title: Chivito sandwich
instructions: Warm the milk with the butter and molasses until the butter melts, then stir in the cold water and egg.
In a large bowl mix the flour, salt and yeast, then stir in the milk mixture with a wooden spoon until just combined — no kneading needed.
Cover the bowl with a damp towel and refrigerate the dough overnight.
The next morning, knead briefly on a floured surface and cut into eight equal pieces, shaping each into a ball.
Place the balls on a parchment-lined baking sheet, cover with a damp towel and let rise about an hour, until doubled in size.
Heat the oven to 220°C, brush the buns with milk, cream or egg wash, sprinkle with sesame seeds if you like, and bake 15-20 minutes until golden; let cool.
Chop the onions and sauté in a little butter until caramelized, about 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Rub the steaks with garlic, then grill or fry the steaks, bacon and eggs, seasoning with salt and pepper; toast the halved buns.
Spread mustard on one bun half and layer with steak, a slice of ham, a slice of cheese, a strip of bacon, a tomato slice and some lettuce.
Top with the fried egg, sprinkle generously with chimichurri sauce and onions, and serve.

title: Chocolate alfajores
instructions: Cream the butter with the sugar until light and fluffy, a few minutes with a mixer.
Beat in the egg and honey until fully combined.
Sift in the flour, cornstarch, cocoa, baking soda and baking powder, then mix until a soft dough forms.
Wrap the dough in plastic and chill for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for best results.
Preheat the oven to 200°C and line two baking sheets with parchment paper.
Divide the chilled dough in half, keeping one portion refrigerated while you work.
Roll the other half out to about 2mm thickness and cut into 5-6cm rounds using a cutter or glass.
Bake one sheet at a time for 8-10 minutes until just set, then let the cookies cool completely.
Match the cookies into pairs of equal size, spreading about a teaspoon of dulce de leche on one half before sandwiching with the other.
Melt the chopped milk chocolate with the butter and orange zest over low heat, stirring until smooth.
Dunk each sandwiched cookie into the melted chocolate, then set on parchment paper to firm up before serving.

ingredients.note: inferred weight for '1 Egg' ~50g
inferred weight for '1 tsp' baking powder ~4g

title: Vanilla alfajores
instructions: Cream the softened butter for about a minute until light.
Beat in the sugar and keep creaming for another two minutes.
Mix in the whole egg until fully incorporated, then beat in the egg yolk.
Stir in the remaining dry ingredients until a smooth dough comes together.
Shape the dough into a ball, wrap in plastic, and chill for at least 30 minutes or overnight.
Preheat the oven to 180°C and line two baking sheets with parchment paper.
Divide the chilled dough in half, keeping one portion in the fridge while you work.
Roll out the other half thickly on a lightly floured surface.
Cut rounds about 6cm across using a cookie cutter or the rim of a glass, repeating with all the dough.
Bake for 8-10 minutes, until the cookies are set but still pale.
Let the cookies cool completely before filling.
Spread a teaspoon of dulce de leche onto half of the cookies.
Top with the remaining cookies, pressing gently so the filling reaches the edges.
Roll the filled edges in coconut flakes to finish.

ingredients.note: 1 egg converted to grams using standard ~50g large egg estimate

title: Flan
instructions: Scatter the sugar in a dry frying pan and melt it over medium heat, swirling the pan instead of stirring, until it turns a deep amber and fully liquefies.
Quickly pour the hot caramel into the bottom of four flan containers before it hardens — mine hold about 120ml each.
Preheat the oven to 150°C and find a casserole or dish large enough to hold all four containers.
Heat the milk with the extra sugar and the scraped vanilla pod until warm but not boiling, then remove the pod after a few minutes.
Gently whisk the egg yolks and whole eggs together, taking care not to whip in any air.
Pour the warm milk into the egg mixture, stirring gently to combine.
Divide the custard among the caramel-lined containers and set them into the casserole.
Boil water and carefully pour it into the casserole around the containers, keeping water out of the custard.
Bake on the middle rack for 35-40 minutes, until a toothpick inserted comes out clean.
Let the flans cool in their containers for an hour, then chill overnight in the fridge.
The next day, run a knife around the rim, invert onto a plate, and serve with dulce de leche on the side.

title: Postre Chajá
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C and grease and line one or two 20cm cake pans.
Separate the eggs into whites and yolks, making sure the egg-white bowl is completely clean and fat-free.
Beat the egg whites with a pinch of salt to soft peaks, then set aside.
Without washing the beaters, beat the egg yolks with the sugar until pale and creamy.
Fold one-third of the beaten whites into the yolk mixture, then sift the flour and cornstarch over the top.
Gently fold in the remaining egg whites, keeping as much volume as you can — don't overmix.
Pour the batter into the prepared pan(s) right away and bake: 35 minutes for one pan or 25 minutes for two.
Cool briefly before turning out; if making ahead, wrap and chill overnight so it slices more easily.
For the meringue, beat the egg white to soft peaks, add a pinch of salt, then beat in the sugar on high until glossy, about 3 minutes more.
Pipe small meringue shapes onto a lined tray and dry in the oven at 100°C for about 45 minutes, then store airtight.
Whip the heavy cream with the vanilla extract and sugar to stiff peaks for the filling.
Slice the peaches thinly and cut the cooled sponge into four layers.
Brush each layer with reserved peach juice, then stack with whipped cream, peach slices, and dulce de leche between layers.
Spread a thin layer of cream on the sides and pipe rosettes around the edge if you like.
Heat the remaining peach juice, stir in the cornstarch slurry until it thickens into a glaze, and pour it over the top peach layer.
Crush the dried meringues and press them around the sides of the cake, then serve immediately.

title: Chocolate empanadas
instructions: Place the unopened can of condensed milk in a large pot, cover completely with water, and boil for two and a half hours, topping up the water as needed.
Let the can cool completely before opening — inside you'll have soft dulce de leche.
In a large bowl, combine the flour, cocoa powder, and salt.
Rub in the cold butter with your fingers until the mixture looks like crumbs.
Work in the egg to form a dough, adding milk a tablespoon at a time if it's too dry.
Wrap the dough and chill for at least half an hour, or overnight.
Line a baking sheet with parchment paper and preheat the oven to 180°C.
Roll the chilled dough out thin on a floured surface and cut into 12cm circles.
Spoon two to three teaspoons of dulce de leche into the centre of each circle.
Brush the edges of each circle with milk.
Fold each circle in half into a half-moon and crimp the edges with a fork, or fold them inward to make a rope pattern.
Arrange the empanadas on the baking sheet and brush the tops with egg wash.
Bake for about 20-25 minutes until golden.
While they bake, melt the remaining butter and stir together the sugar and cinnamon in a small bowl.
As soon as the empanadas come out of the oven, brush them with the melted butter and sprinkle with the cinnamon sugar.
Serve warm or cold, taking care of the hot filling if eating them warm.

1 egg estimated at standard 50g
3 tablespoons estimated at 15g each

title: Venezuelan Arepas
instructions: Warm your oven to 410°F.
Pour room-temperature water into a large bowl.
Whisk in the salt until fully dissolved.
Sprinkle in the cornmeal a little at a time while beating, until the masa is smooth, firm and malleable.
Let the masa rest for 5 minutes to hydrate fully; no kneading needed since this corn flour has no gluten.
Meanwhile heat a budare, comal, griddle or non-stick pan over medium heat and coat lightly with the oil.
Keep a bowl of water nearby to wet your hands for shaping.
Scoop about 2 tablespoons of masa into damp hands and roll into a small ball.
Cup the ball between your palms and rotate one hand to flatten it into a round disc while keeping the edges even.
Pass the disc between your hands to smooth it into a round about ¾ inch thick and 4 inches wide, wetting your fingertips to smooth any cracks at the edges.
Cook the arepas in batches on the hot griddle, turning once, until each side is golden, about 4-5 minutes per side, watching so they don't scorch.
Transfer the browned arepas to a baking sheet and finish in the oven for 10 minutes until they sound hollow when tapped and look slightly puffed.
Serve the arepas hot, split and stuffed with your favorite filling or alongside a Venezuelan guiso or stew.

ingredients.note: 2 cups precooked corn flour; converted at ~120g/cup, a standard estimate for masarepa
2 1/2 cups water; converted at 236g/cup
used only to coat the griddle for searing the arepas, not incorporated into the dough itself

title: Venezuelan Sancocho
instructions: Toss the hind shank into a big stockpot with a halved small onion, the halved bell pepper, four whole garlic cloves, the dark green leek leaves, and the beef stock.
Simmer for 45-60 minutes (longer if needed) until the meat is fork-tender.
Fish out and discard the big vegetable pieces and bones.
Stir in the diced onion, two minced garlic cloves, the sweet red peppers, sliced light-green leek, scallions, cassava (yucca) and corn.
Cover and simmer over medium heat for 5-8 minutes until the yucca starts to soften, giving it extra time if it needs it.
Add the yautia, white yam and butternut squash, stir to combine, and simmer covered for 5-6 minutes until the root vegetables are just tender - don't overcook or they'll fall apart.
Season with salt to taste, stir in the cilantro and the remaining minced garlic, and let it simmer for 2 more minutes.
Ladle into big bowls, dividing the meat and vegetables evenly, and finish with a squeeze of lime and/or hot sauce if you like.
Serve alongside arepas and/or casabe.

title: Venezuelan Coconut Chicken
instructions: Cut the chicken breasts into bite-size pieces, peel and chop the onions, and mince the garlic.
Gather the rest of the ingredients before you start cooking.
Heat a frying pan over medium heat, add the oil, and sauté the onion until translucent.
Transfer the softened onion to a blender or food processor.
Add the tomatoes, garlic, turmeric, cumin, sugar, ginger paste, coconut milk and water to the blender and blitz until smooth.
In the same pan you used for the onion, add the chicken and brown on all sides, about 3 minutes.
Pour in the blended sauce, add the bay leaves, bring to a simmer, and cook for 15 to 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce turns creamy.
Remove the bay leaves and serve with white rice.

title: Venezuelan Shredded Beef
instructions: Season the beef with salt, pepper, and minced garlic.
Cut one onion into four big wedges.
Place the beef in the pressure cooker with the onion wedges and beef stock.
Pressure cook for 20 minutes, then release the pressure and open the lid.
Remove the beef and set aside 2 cups of the cooking stock in a separate container.
Shred the beef with two forks until tender; if it resists, cook it for another 10 minutes.
Switch the Instant Pot to sauté and heat the olive oil.
Once it beeps, add the sliced onion, red pepper, and tomatoes, and cook until tender.
Return the shredded beef to the pot and mix well.
Pour in one and a half cups of the reserved stock along with the Worcestershire sauce, tomato paste, ketchup, and cumin, then mix.
If the sauce looks too dry, add a splash more of the reserved stock.
Season with salt and pepper to taste, then serve.

title: Arepa pelua
instructions: Simmer the flank steak in beef stock (or water) with a pinch of salt over low heat for about 2 hours, until fork-tender and easy to shred.
Drain the beef and shred it with two forks.
Sauté the onion, red pepper, and garlic in a little olive oil until softened.
Stir in the cumin, oregano, paprika, and a pinch of salt, then fold in the shredded beef and cook a few minutes until the flavors meld.
Mix the cornmeal with warm water and a pinch of salt into a soft dough, then let it rest for 5 minutes.
Divide the dough into 6 portions, roll into balls, and flatten into thick discs.
Cook the arepas on a hot griddle or skillet for 2-3 minutes per side until golden, then bake briefly if you want them crispier.
Split each arepa open, stuff with the hot shredded beef, and top with grated cheese.

title: Cassava pizza
instructions: Warm the oven to 200°C.
Slice the chorizo into medium chunks and cut the pepper into strips.
Lay out the casabe rounds and spread each with a little tomato sauce and mozzarella.
Scatter over the chorizo, sweetcorn, turkey ham, olives and pepper strips.
Slide into the oven and bake for 7 to 10 minutes until bubbling.
Pull out and serve straight away.

title: Oatmeal pancakes
instructions: Crack the egg into a blender and add the milk, sugar, baking powder, salt, vanilla extract, and ground oats.
Blitz everything together until you have a smooth batter, then let it rest for 10 minutes so the oats soften.
Heat a frying pan and grease it lightly with a little butter.
Pour a small ladle of batter into the pan.
Cook over medium-low heat until bubbles appear on the surface, then flip with a spatula and cook the other side, watching so it doesn't burn.
Repeat with the remaining batter, greasing the pan lightly between pancakes.
Serve topped with the sugar syrup (or caramel) and sliced strawberries.

title: Venezuelan turnovers
instructions: Season the ground beef with the pepper and cumin.
Heat the vegetable oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and brown the beef until no pink remains.
Stir in the onion, green pepper, red pepper, garlic powder, capers, tomato sauce and Sazón.
Cook, stirring occasionally, until most of the liquid has evaporated, about 20 minutes, then let the filling cool.
Check that the masarepa dough is moist enough to hold together without sticking to your fingers.
Take about ½ cup of dough and roll it into a ball between your palms.
On a sheet of non-stick parchment, flatten the ball into a 5-inch circle about ⅛ inch thick.
Spoon a generous tablespoon of filling onto one half of the circle.
Use the parchment to fold the dough over into a semi-circle, enclosing the filling.
Press the rim of an inverted bowl over the sealed edge to trim each empanada neatly.
Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Heat about ½ inch of vegetable oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat until hot but not smoking.
Fry the empanadas in batches, turning once or twice, until lightly browned, then drain on paper towels.
Avoid overcrowding the skillet or letting the oil get too hot.

title: Passion fruit mousse
instructions: Sprinkle the gelatin over a quarter cup of room-temperature water in a small bowl and let it sit for about 5 minutes until it softens.
In a medium saucepan over medium-high heat, stir together the passion fruit pulp, sugar and the softened gelatin mixture.
Cook, stirring occasionally, until the mixture is smooth and fully combined, keeping it well below a boil.
Remove from the heat and let it cool completely.
Stir the condensed milk into the cooled passion fruit mixture.
In a clean bowl, beat the egg whites with an electric mixer until stiff peaks form that hold their shape.
Fold a third of the egg whites into the cooled passion fruit mixture until combined.
Gently fold in the remaining egg whites with a spatula until just combined.
Divide the mousse evenly among clear glass serving cups and cover them.
Refrigerate the mousse for at least 2 hours until well chilled.
Serve chilled, garnished with mint if you like.

quantity given only as '1'; inferred ~1 cup (250g) of pulp

title: Arepa Pabellón
instructions: Season the meat with salt and pepper, then cook it through in a skillet, breaking it into shreds.
Warm the black beans over medium heat until heated through.
Fry or grill the ripe plantain following its packet instructions until golden, then set aside.
Dice the tomato into small cubes and set aside with the other fillings.
Grill or pan-cook the arepas, turning once, until both sides are golden brown.
Slice each arepa open along the edge with a knife to create a pocket.
Stuff the arepas with the plantain, beans, shredded meat, and diced tomato.
Serve alongside a spoonful of pico de gallo or guacamole for dipping.

ingredients.original: 2 Corn Arepa Filled With Mozarella Cheese

title: Jiggs Dinner
instructions: The night before, break the salt beef into big chunks and soak it in water for at least 8-10 hours.
Also soak the split peas in a bowl of water overnight.
Drain the beef and set it in a large stockpot, then cover with 6-7 litres of fresh water.

Tip the soaked peas into a pease pudding bag or a triple layer of cheesecloth, tie it loosely to leave room for expansion, and lower it into the pot, tying the strings to the handle so it doesn't touch the bottom.
Bring everything to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer for 2 hours.
Lift out the peas bag, empty the peas into a bowl, and mash them with butter and pepper to taste; set aside.
Add the cabbage to the pot and boil for 20 minutes.
Add the turnip, carrots, and potatoes, and boil for a further 20 minutes or until the vegetables are tender.
Lift the beef and vegetables onto a platter.
Serve the cooking liquid as a pot liquor to drink, or reduce it down into a jus or gravy to pour over the meal.

title: Molasses Baked Beans
instructions: Soak the dried navy beans overnight in a Dutch oven with 1.5 L of water.
Drain the beans, return them to the pot, and cover with fresh water by a couple of inches.
Simmer the beans for about 30 minutes until tender but still firm, then drain, reserving the cooking liquid.
Preheat the oven to 160°C (325°F).
While the oven heats, cook the bacon in the Dutch oven over medium-high heat until the fat renders and it starts to brown.
Add the onion to the pot and cook until tender.
Return the beans to the pot along with the molasses, brown sugar, cider vinegar, ground mustard, salt and pepper.
Pour in 750 mL of the reserved bean cooking liquid and stir to combine.
Bake uncovered for 3 to 5 hours, until the beans are tender, the sauce has thickened, and the edges turn sticky.
Check occasionally and stir in an extra cup of water if the beans look dry.

ingredients.note: 2 cups dried beans estimated at ~200 g/cup

title: Saskatoon Pie
instructions: Mix the flour and salt together in a large bowl.
Cut in the cubed butter and shortening, working it in by hand or with a pastry blender until the mixture looks like coarse oatmeal with pea-sized bits remaining.
Beat the egg with a fork in a 2-cup measure, then beat in the vinegar and enough ice-cold water to reach the 1-cup mark.
Stir the liquid into the flour mixture just until the dough clings together, taking care not to overmix.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface, gather into a ball, and divide into 5 even portions, shaping each into a disc.
Wrap the discs tightly in plastic; chill 2 of them for 1 hour for this pie and freeze the remaining 3 for future use.
Preheat the oven to 350°F with a rack in the lower third, and line a baking sheet with foil.
Roll one chilled disc into a 12-inch circle and fit it into a 9-inch pie plate, letting the pastry overhang the edges.
Stir the saskatoon berries with the 1/2 cup sugar and cornstarch until well combined, then pour into the pie shell.
Dot the filling with the butter.
Roll the second disc into a 12-inch circle and cut it into 6 wide strips with a ruler, saving the end pieces for patching.
Weave the strips over and under into a lattice, connecting them to the edges of the bottom crust.
Fold the bottom crust edges over the lattice ends to seal in the juices and give a rustic finish, or trim and crimp with a fork for a neater look.
Brush the top with egg wash and sprinkle with the remaining tablespoon of sugar.
Set the pie on the prepared baking sheet and bake for 90–100 minutes until golden brown and bubbling.
Cool the pie on a wire rack before serving warm or at room temperature with whipped cream or ice cream.

ingredients.note: Recipe makes 5 pastry discs; only 2 are used for this pie (3 frozen for future use), so scaled to 2/5 of the stated 6 cups (~720g).
