title: Date squares
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F) and grease an 8-inch square baking pan.
Simmer the dates with the water over high heat until boiling, then reduce to low and cook gently, stirring now and then, until thickened, about 6 to 8 minutes.
Take off the heat, stir in the vanilla, and let the date filling cool while you make the crust.
Pulse the oats, flour, sugar, baking soda and salt in a food processor just a few times until barely combined, not powdery.
Add the butter and pulse again until the mixture forms pea-sized crumbly clumps, then tip into a bowl and mix through by hand so the butter is evenly worked in.
Press half the oat mixture firmly into the bottom of the prepared pan to form an even base.
Spread the cooled date filling evenly over the base.
Scatter and press the remaining oat mixture over the top to flatten into an even layer.
Bake in the centre of the oven until golden on top, 30 to 35 minutes.
Cool completely in the pan before cutting into squares and serving.
Store covered in the fridge for up to a week, or freeze for 2 to 3 months.

ingredients.note: original states '1 1/2 cup Vanilla', which is implausible (a cup of straight vanilla); corrected to a more plausible 1 1/2 tsp vanilla extract as a filling flavoring — flagged doubt
ingredient list says 'Baking Powder' but the method text says 'baking soda' — kept as listed ingredient, flagging the discrepancy
butter is mixed into the crust and baked in, not used as a frying/searing medium, so counted whole

title: Jam jam cookies
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F) and line a large baking sheet with parchment paper.
Cream the butter, sugar and egg together in a stand mixer with the paddle attachment until smooth.
Beat in the molasses and vanilla until fully combined.
Stir in the baking soda dissolved in water and mix for another minute.
In a separate bowl, whisk together the baking powder, cinnamon, flour and salt.
Add the dry mixture to the wet ingredients a little at a time, mixing on low and scraping down the bowl, until a soft, easily shaped dough forms.
Roll the dough into small balls, set them on the lined sheet, then flatten each into a disc with a floured glass or a moistened palm.
Bake for 10-12 minutes, then let the cookies cool on the sheet for at least 10 minutes.
Once cooled, sandwich pairs of cookies together with a generous spoonful of your favourite jam and enjoy with a cup of tea.

ingredients.note: Instructions describe this as part of a 'baking soda water mixture' (paired with the water line), but the ingredient list names it 'Baking Powder' — likely a source error for baking soda; kept as listed, flagging the discrepancy.
Instructions call for only ~2 tsp jam per sandwich; 2 cups is far more than would be used across the batch, but kept as the listed ingredient amount.

title: Hodge Podge
instructions: Bring a big pot of salted water to a boil.
Par-cook the potatoes and carrots for about five minutes, until fork tender.
Toss the peas and beans into the same pot and blanch for about a minute.
Drain everything and set the vegetables aside.
In a heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, melt the butter over medium heat.
Add the cooked vegetables and warm them through in the butter.
Pour in the milk and cream, bring to a boil, then drop the heat to low.
Season with salt and pepper.
Stir in the dill and chives, let simmer for at least 10 minutes, and serve warm.

title: Flapper Pie
instructions: Warm your oven to 350°F.
Stir together the graham cracker crumbs, melted butter, sugar and cinnamon in a bowl, saving about 2 tablespoons of the mix for topping.
Press the rest into a 9-inch deep-dish pie plate, up the sides too, and bake for 10 minutes until just lightly browned.
Whisk the milk, sugar, cornstarch, egg yolks, vanilla and salt together in a saucepan and cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until it bubbles and thickens like pudding.
Set the custard aside to cool while you whip the meringue.
Beat the egg whites, sugar and cream of tartar together until you get stiff, glossy peaks.
Pour the cooled custard into the baked crust and pile the meringue on top, swirling it into peaks that'll catch some color.
Scatter the reserved crumb over the meringue and slide the pie back into the oven.
Bake for about 10 minutes, watching closely, until the meringue peaks are browned.
Chill the pie and serve within a few hours — the meringue softens and weeps if it sits too long.

title: Figgy Duff
instructions: Whisk the flour, brown sugar, baking powder, ginger, allspice, and cinnamon together in a bowl.
Stir in the raisins, coating them well in the flour mixture.
Mix in the melted butter, molasses, and water with a wooden spoon until a dough forms.
Shape the dough into a ball and pack it into a cotton pudding bag, tying it loosely to leave about an inch of room for expansion.
Boil the pudding for 1.5 hours until firm.
Lift the duff from the water and let it cool slightly before unwrapping it from the bag.
Slice the pudding like a cake and serve warm with rum butter sauce, warmed molasses, or a Molasses Coady sauce.

title: Classic Tourtière
instructions: Heat the oil in a large skillet over medium-high and saute the onion and garlic until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add the ground beef and pork with the summer savoury, clove and nutmeg. Cook, breaking up the meat, until browned.
Pour in the beef stock and bring to a simmer, then lower to medium-low. Stir in the grated potato and cook until the liquid is almost fully absorbed, about 15 minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, season with salt and pepper, then take off the heat and let the filling cool completely — it bakes best chilled.
Heat the oven to 375F (190C). Roll one pastry disc just under 1/4 inch thick and line a 9-inch pie plate. Fill with cooled tourtiere mixture and spread even.
Roll a second disc to the same thickness, cut a steam hole in the centre, and lay it over the filling. Trim to 1/2 inch beyond the rim and pinch the edges to seal. Brush with beaten egg.
Bake about 45 minutes, until the pastry is deep golden. Let rest 15 minutes before slicing. Repeat with the remaining pastry for the second pie.

title: Jamaican Sweet Potato Pudding
instructions: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C) and grease and line a 9-inch round or loaf pan with parchment paper.
Peel and cut the sweet potatoes, then blitz them in a food processor until finely grated.
Toss the raisins in 2 teaspoons of flour taken from the measured ½ cup and set them aside.
In a large bowl, combine the grated sweet potato with the remaining flour, brown sugar, vanilla extract, allspice, cinnamon, nutmeg, ginger and salt.
Stir in 1½ cups of coconut milk until a smooth batter forms.
Fold the floured raisins through the batter.
Pour the batter into the prepared pan and smooth the top.
Bake uncovered for 1 hour 30 minutes, until mostly set but still a little soft in the centre.

title: Jamaican Curry Chicken Recipe
instructions: Toss the chicken thighs in a big bowl with the curry powder, all-purpose seasoning, chopped onion, spring onions, green pepper, garlic, scotch bonnet (if you're using it) and thyme, making sure every piece gets coated.
Cover and let it marinate in the fridge for at least an hour, or overnight if you can wait.
Once marinated, pull the onions, peppers and garlic out of the chicken and set them aside for later.
Warm the olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat, stir in the remaining curry powder and let it toast for 20-30 seconds.

Tip in the reserved onions, green pepper, garlic and thyme along with the scotch bonnet, and cook, stirring, until the onions soften, about 3-5 minutes - splash in a tablespoon of water if things look dry.
Add the marinated chicken and a quarter cup of water to the pot, cover, drop the heat to medium-low and cook, stirring occasionally, for 30-35 minutes.
With about 15 minutes left, stir in the potato and keep cooking covered.
Once the chicken is cooked through and the potato is tender, take it off the heat and season with salt and pepper to taste.

title: Grape Nut Ice Cream
instructions: Whip the cold heavy cream in a large bowl with an electric mixer until stiff peaks form.
Gently fold in the sweetened condensed milk and vanilla extract until fully combined.
Fold in the grape nut cereal, stirring until it's evenly distributed through the cream mixture.
Pour everything into a freezer-safe container, press plastic wrap directly onto the surface to guard against freezer burn, cover, and freeze for at least 6 hours or until solid.
Let the ice cream sit at room temperature for a few minutes before scooping and serving.

ingredients.note: Original text states '1 1/4 oz Condensed Milk' — unusually small for this dish (typical recipes use a 14oz can); converted literally but flagged as a likely unit/quantity error.

title: No-Churn Rum Raisin Ice Cream
instructions: Warm the rum in a small saucepan over medium heat until it's hot but not boiling, then take it off the heat.
Stir the raisins and vanilla extract into the warm rum and let everything sit for at least 30 minutes to soak.
Drain the raisins, keeping back just a quarter cup of the soaking rum for the base and setting the raisins aside.
Whisk the sweetened condensed milk, dark brown sugar, cinnamon, allspice, salt, and reserved rum together in a large bowl until the sugar dissolves completely.
In a separate bowl, whip the cold heavy cream with an electric mixer until it holds stiff peaks.
Gently fold the whipped cream into the condensed milk mixture, keeping the fold light so you don't knock the air out.
Fold in the rum-soaked raisins until they're evenly spread through the mixture.
Pour everything into a loaf pan or freezer-safe container, smooth the top, and cover tightly.
Freeze for at least 6 hours, or until firm.
Let the ice cream sit at room temperature for 5-10 minutes before scooping.

ingredients.note: Recipe uses 1/2 cup rum to soak raisins but only reserves 1/4 cup for the actual ice cream base; the rest is drained off and discarded, so grams reflect only the retained 1/4 cup (~56 g).
14 oz jar converted at 28.35 g/oz.

title: Jamaican Spice Bun Recipe
instructions: Soak the cranberries, raisins, and cherries in the cup of beer for 30 minutes and set aside.
Preheat the oven to 325°F (163°C) and grease an 8x4 loaf pan.
Whisk the flour, baking powder, cinnamon, and salt together in a large bowl.
In a separate bowl, beat the brown sugar, egg, milk, honey, melted butter, molasses, caramelized sugar sauce, and vanilla extract with the soaking beer (leave the fruit behind for now) until smooth.
Toss the soaked fruit in the 2 tablespoons of flour set aside from the dry mix.
Make a well in the dry ingredients, pour in the wet mixture, and stir until just combined.
Fold the floured fruit through the batter.
Pour the batter into the prepared loaf pan and bake for 50-60 minutes, until a toothpick comes out clean.
Cool the bun in the pan for 5 minutes, then turn it out onto a rack to cool completely.
Serve sliced, with cheddar cheese on the side.

title: Jamaican Pepper Shrimp
instructions: Toss the shrimp with minced Scotch Bonnet, all-purpose seasoning, ground annatto and ground allspice; let it marinate for 10 minutes.
Warm a large pan over medium heat, pour in the shrimp stock and bring to a gentle simmer.
Stir in the diced onion and garlic, cooking until softened and fragrant, about 2 minutes.
Add the seasoned shrimp and thyme sprigs, spread them in the pan, cover and cook 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the shrimp turn bright orange and are cooked through.
Stir in the white vinegar and cook one more minute.
Season with salt to taste.
Lift the shrimp out with a slotted spoon onto a plate and serve hot, drizzled with some of the cooking liquid.

title: Corned Beef and Cabbage – Jamaican Style
instructions: Warm the olive oil in a skillet or Dutch pot over medium-high heat.
Toss in the onion and green pepper, and sauté for 3-5 minutes until softened.
Stir in the garlic and cook for about a minute until fragrant.
Add the shredded cabbage and cook, stirring often, for 3-5 minutes until it wilts down.
Mix in the corned beef, chopped plum tomatoes, and thyme.
Stir in the tomato ketchup and a dash of scotch bonnet pepper sauce, then season with salt and pepper.
Reduce the heat and let everything simmer gently for 3-5 minutes until the corned beef is heated through.
Take off the heat and serve with white rice, bread, or on its own.

title: Jamaican Banana Fritters
instructions: Mash the bananas with a fork in a bowl until mostly smooth.
Stir in the brown sugar, vanilla extract, cinnamon, nutmeg, and a pinch of salt.
Sift the flour into the bowl and stir with a spoon until fully combined into a batter.
Heat oil in a 10-inch skillet over medium-high heat.
Once the oil is hot, drop spoonfuls of the banana batter into the oil.
Fry until the underside is golden and bubbles appear on top, then flip and cook the other side until browned.
Remove the fritters and drain them on paper towels before serving.

title: Jamaican Instant Pot Rice and Beans
instructions: Set your Instant Pot to Sauté and let it get hot.
Pour in the olive oil, then add the chopped onion and cook, stirring, until softened, about 3 minutes.
Stir in the garlic and spring onions and cook for another 30 seconds.
Cancel the Sauté function.
Add the rice, coconut milk, water, salt, allspice and black pepper, and stir everything together.
Pour the kidney beans (with their liquid) on top of the rice mixture without stirring.
Lay the thyme sprigs over the top.
Seal the lid with the valve set to Sealing.
Cook on Manual/Pressure Cook at High pressure for 6 minutes.
Let the pressure release naturally for 10 minutes, then quick-release any remaining pressure.
Open the lid, discard the thyme sprigs, fluff the rice with a fork and serve.

title: Jamaican Boiled Dumplings
instructions: Bring a large pot of water and salt to a rolling boil — this will be your cooking liquid for the dumplings.
In a mixing bowl, whisk the flour and salt together so the salt is evenly distributed.
Gradually work in the water with your hands until a smooth, firm dough comes together.
Divide the dough into equal pieces, roll each into a ball, then flatten slightly into a thick round disk.
Drop the dumplings into the boiling water one at a time, stirring gently with a wooden spoon so they don't stick together.
Boil for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the dumplings are cooked through and float to the surface.
Lift the dumplings out with a slotted spoon, letting the excess water drain off.
Serve alongside your favorite dishes.

title: Jamaican Curry Goat
instructions: Rinse the goat meat well with vinegar and water.
Season the goat with Jamaican curry powder, all-purpose seasoning, ground ginger, allspice, chopped onion, sliced garlic, and thyme; marinate for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Pull the onion and garlic off the meat and set them aside for later.
Heat oil in a pressure cooker on high sauté and brown the goat meat in batches, about 2-3 minutes per side, then remove it.
Add a splash more oil with the remaining curry powder to the pot and sauté briefly, about 10 seconds.
Tip in the reserved onion and garlic and sauté until softened, about 4 minutes, adding a little water if things look dry.
Return the goat meat to the pot, add water, seal the pressure cooker, and cook on high pressure for 40 minutes.
Let the pressure release naturally for 10 minutes, then release the rest manually.
Switch back to sauté, add the potato and a whole scotch bonnet pepper, and cook until the potato is tender.
Fish out the scotch bonnet before serving, then season with salt and pepper to taste.

1/2 tsp estimated at ~3g/tsp

title: Jamaican Festival (Sweet Dumpling)
instructions: Pour oil into a heavy-bottomed pot so it's at least 3 inches deep, or fire up your deep fryer, and bring it to 350°F over medium heat.
In a large bowl, stir together the flour, cornmeal, sugar, baking powder, and salt.
Add the vanilla extract and milk, stirring until the dough comes together, then use your hands to gently work it into a ball.
Pinch off pieces of dough and roll each into a long oval shape — you should get about 12 dumplings.
Once the oil hits 350°F, fry the dumplings on all sides until golden brown, about 4-6 minutes.
Lift the dumplings out, drain off the excess oil, and serve.

title: Jamaican Fried Dumplings
instructions: Whisk the flour, baking powder, and salt together in a large bowl.
Pour in the milk and stir until the dough comes together.
Turn the dough out and knead lightly until smooth.
Break off about 10 pieces and roll each into a ball.
Heat oil in a 10-inch skillet over medium heat until it reaches about 350°F.
Fry the dumplings, flipping once, until both sides are browned, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Lift the dumplings out and drain on paper towels or a rack before serving.

ingredients.note: 2 tsp converted to grams using standard density
1 tsp converted to grams using standard density

title: Jamaican Rice and Peas
instructions: Combine the rice, coconut milk, kidney beans, water, kosher salt, allspice, and black pepper in a large pot, stirring until well mixed.
Lay the spring onions, thyme, and whole Scotch bonnet pepper on top of the mixture.
Bring everything to a boil, then reduce the heat to low and cover with a lid.
Let it cook covered over low heat for 18 minutes, then take it off the heat and let it sit, still covered, for another 5 minutes.
Uncover and discard the spring onions, Scotch bonnet, and thyme sprigs.
Fluff the rice and peas with a fork before serving.

ingredients.note: 2 cups rice converted at ~185 g/cup

title: Chicken Mandi
instructions: Clean the chicken and cut it into pieces, then rub with salt, turmeric and a little oil to marinate briefly.
Rinse the basmati rice and let it soak for 20–30 minutes.
Heat ghee or oil in a large pot and fry the chopped onion until golden.
Stir in the minced garlic and green chillies and fry for 1–2 minutes.
Add the whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaves) along with the ground coriander and cumin, stirring until fragrant.
Add the chicken pieces, brown lightly, then pour in enough water or stock to cover and simmer until the chicken is nearly cooked.
Lift out the chicken, measure the remaining liquid, and stir in the soaked rice; bring to a boil, then reduce the heat, cover and cook until the rice is almost done.
Set the chicken back on top of the rice, cover tightly and steam on low for 10–15 minutes to let the flavors meld.

If you want a smoky finish, place a red-hot piece of charcoal in a small foil cup in the centre of the pot, add a teaspoon of butter or oil on it, cover immediately and let the smoke infuse for 5–10 minutes, then remove the coal.
Garnish with fried onions and chopped coriander, and serve with chutney or raita.

4 cups converted at ~236 g/cup

title: Beef Mandi
instructions: Wash the beef and cut it into large chunks, then rub with salt and turmeric.
Heat ghee or oil in a large pot and sauté the sliced onions until light golden.
Stir in the garlic, green chillies and tomato, cooking until they soften.
Add the mandi spice mix — coriander, cumin, black pepper, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves and bay leaves — and toast briefly.
Add the beef pieces and stir over medium heat until well coated in the spice mixture.
Pour in the water or beef stock, cover, and simmer until the beef is tender, about 1.5–2 hours depending on the cut.
Lift out the beef carefully, then strain and measure the broth.
Tip the washed, soaked basmati rice into the broth (roughly 1 cup rice to 1.5–2 cups liquid), adjust the seasoning and bring to a boil.
Lower the heat, cover, and cook the rice until fluffy.
Lay the beef pieces over the rice and steam on low heat for 10 minutes so the flavours meld.
For a smoky finish, set a small hot coal on foil in the pot, add a teaspoon of butter or oil, cover immediately for 5 minutes, then remove the coal before serving.
Fluff the rice and serve the beef mandi with salad or chutney.

title: Caribbean Tamarind balls
instructions: Mash the tamarind pulp together with 1 cup of the granulated sugar in a bowl until well combined.
Pinch off small amounts of the mixture and roll between your palms into marble-sized balls.
Scatter the remaining sugar over a plate or sheet pan and roll each ball through it until fully coated.
Serve right away, or let the balls set for a few hours or overnight for a firmer bite and a more crystallized coating.

ingredients.note: 1 1/2 cups granulated sugar converted at ~200g/cup, split between the mix-in and the coating step per instructions

title: Callaloo and SaltFish
instructions: Soak the salt cod in water overnight to draw out the salt.
Bring the soaked cod to a boil in fresh water on the stove, watching for a foam to rise to the top.
Drain the fish, let it cool, then shred it into flakes.
Fry the bacon in a skillet over medium heat until crispy.
Drain off most of the bacon fat, leaving about a tablespoon in the pan.
Toss in the yellow onion, spring onion, scotch bonnet, and garlic, stirring until the onion softens, about 2 minutes.
Stir in the shredded salt cod and cook for another minute.
Add the callaloo, plum tomatoes, thyme, and black pepper, stirring to combine.
Cook everything together until heated through, about 2 minutes, then serve.

ingredients.note: 1/2 lb converted to grams
4 slices estimated at ~28g each; grease is mostly drained but residue stays in dish, so not counted as frying medium

title: Jamaican Curry Shrimp Recipe
instructions: Toss the shrimp with a tablespoon of curry powder and the all-purpose seasoning, then let it sit for about 10 minutes while you get everything else chopped.

Warm the olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat, then tip in the sliced onion, red pepper, green pepper, scotch bonnet if you're using it, and the chopped garlic, stirring for about 5 minutes until the peppers soften slightly.
Stir in the remaining curry powder and cook it for another minute to wake up the spices.
Pour in the coconut milk, add the seasoned shrimp, ketchup, and thyme, and make sure the shrimp is fully coated in the sauce.

Bring everything to a gentle simmer and cook over medium heat, stirring now and then and flipping the shrimp halfway through, until it's cooked through on both sides — about 5 to 6 minutes. Finish with salt and pepper to taste.

title: Jamaican Cornmeal Porridge
instructions: Pour the water and coconut milk into a heavy-bottomed pot and bring it to a boil over medium-high heat.
Meanwhile, whisk the cornmeal into the remaining measured water in a separate cup until completely smooth.
Once the pot reaches a boil, whisk in the cornmeal slurry and keep whisking for about a minute so no lumps survive.
Drop the heat to low, cover with a tight-fitting lid, and let it cook 15-20 minutes, giving it an occasional stir.
With about five minutes left on the clock, stir in the vanilla extract, cinnamon, nutmeg, and allspice.
Pull the pot off the heat and stir in the condensed milk to sweeten.
Ladle into bowls and serve warm.

title: Jamaican Steamed Cabbage
instructions: Tip the cabbage, carrots, tomato, onion, garlic, spring onions, thyme, scotch bonnet, water, olive oil, and butter into a large pan, cover with a lid, and cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, for 10 minutes.
Turn the heat down low and let everything simmer for another 5 minutes, until the cabbage is soft and tender.
Take the pan off the heat, pull out the thyme stalks, and serve.

ingredients.note: '1/2 cabbage' - no weight given, assumed a whole cabbage ~900g so half is ~450g
'2 sliced carrots' - no weight given, assumed ~60g per carrot

title: Chinese Orange Chicken
instructions: Whisk together the orange sauce ingredients in a bowl and set aside.
Cut the chicken into roughly 1-inch cubes.
Whisk the eggs with the salt and black pepper, then stir in the chicken to coat.
In a separate bowl, whisk together the flour and cornstarch.
Lift the chicken from the egg mixture, letting the excess drip off, then dredge it in the flour-cornstarch mix.
Heat the oil in a large skillet to about 350°F.
Fry the chicken in two batches, about 3-4 minutes per side, until cooked through (6-8 minutes total).
Drain the fried chicken on paper towels.
Pour out the frying oil and wipe the pan clean.
Add a fresh tablespoon of oil with the garlic and shallot, and cook for a minute.
Pour in the sauce and simmer until it starts to thicken.
Add the fried chicken back in and toss to coat, letting the sauce cling and thicken further.
Serve the orange chicken over cooked white rice, garnished with sesame seeds and scallions.

title: Beef and Broccoli Stir-Fry
instructions: Whisk together 1 tsp soy sauce, 1 tsp dry sherry, 1/2 tsp cornstarch and the black pepper in a bowl.
Toss the sliced sirloin in this marinade and let it sit for 10 minutes.
In a separate bowl, mix the oyster sauce, 1 tsp dry sherry, 1 tbsp soy sauce and the chicken stock; set this sauce aside.
Bring a pot of water to a boil, drop in the broccoli and cook for about 2 minutes until crisp-tender, then drain well.
Heat a wok or large frying pan over high heat until a drop of water flicked in sizzles and vanishes instantly.
Swirl in the high heat cooking oil to coat the pan.
Lay the marinated beef in a single layer across the pan and let it sear undisturbed for 1 minute.
Flip the beef, scatter in the minced garlic, and fry for another 30 seconds to a minute until the beef is no longer pink.
Stir the cornstarch into the tablespoon of water to make a slurry, then pour this and the reserved sauce into the pan.
Stir until the sauce comes to a boil and thickens, about 30 seconds.
Fold the drained broccoli back in to coat it in the sauce.
Serve right away, over steamed rice or on its own.

ingredients.note: Chinese rice wine approximated as dry sherry
Chinese rice wine approximated as dry sherry
Oil is used to sear beef then the sauce is finished in the same pan and served over it, so it remains in the dish

title: Chicken Fried Rice
instructions: Use rice that's at least a day old, or spread freshly cooked rice on a tray and let it dry for 1–2 hours, so it won't turn gummy in the wok.
Cut the chicken into small ½-inch cubes, toss with half the salt, and let it rest about 10 minutes while you prep the vegetables.
Heat a swirl of oil in a wok over medium-high heat, pour in the whisked eggs, and scramble quickly, breaking the curds small; transfer to a plate.
Add another swirl of oil, cook the chicken for 4–5 minutes until done, then move it to a plate and scrape any stuck bits from the wok.
Add more oil, stir-fry the onions for a minute until they soften, then add the garlic and ginger and cook until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
Toss in the carrots and cook for 2 minutes, then add the remaining salt and the peas, stirring to combine.
Add the rice on top of the vegetables, breaking up any clumps with your spatula, then stir in the white and light-green scallion pieces and the five-spice powder.
Drizzle over the soy sauce and sesame oil, stir well, then fold in the cooked chicken, scrambled eggs, and the reserved dark green scallion tops.
Cook everything together for another 1–2 minutes, taste, add extra soy sauce if you like, and serve immediately.

ingredients.note: No stated yield; servings=4 inferred from ~1.8kg total food mass (~450g/serving).
Oil is stir-fried into the dish and stays on the plate, not drained off, so counted as eaten.
Cup-to-gram estimate for 2/3 cup chopped onion (~150g/cup).
Estimated 5g per clove.

title: Singapore Noodles with Shrimp
instructions: Whisk together the sesame oil, soy sauce, and rice vinegar to make the sauce; set aside.
Bring a large pot of water to a boil and cook the rice noodles for about 2 minutes until tender but still with a bite.
Drain the noodles, rinse under cold water, and snip them a few times with scissors to shorten them.
Whisk the eggs in a small bowl.
Heat a large skillet or Dutch oven over medium heat, add a third of the oil, pour in the eggs, and scramble for about 2 minutes into soft curds; transfer to a plate.
Add another third of the oil to the hot pan, then stir in the ginger, garlic, carrots, jalapeño, onion, and salt; cook for 2 minutes until the vegetables start to soften.
Drizzle the remaining oil over the vegetables, then add the ham, cabbage, scallions, red pepper, and curry powder; cook, stirring, for 1 minute.
Add the shrimp and cook for about 3 minutes, stirring, until bright pink and cooked through.
Return the scrambled eggs to the pan along with the sauce and half the noodles; toss for 1 minute.
Add the rest of the noodles and toss for another minute until everything is combined and heated through.
Taste and adjust with more salt or soy sauce if needed, then scatter over the cilantro leaves and serve.

title: Silken Tofu with Sesame Soy Sauce
instructions: Drain the silken tofu and gently ease it out of its packaging onto a large plate.
Slice the tofu into 1/2-inch slabs widthwise, then use the palm of your hand to nudge the slices sideways so they fan out across the plate.
Drizzle the soy sauce and sesame sauce over the top.
Scatter with sliced scallions and sesame seeds, then serve.
Cover any leftovers tightly and refrigerate for up to 2 days, enjoying cold from the fridge or reheating in 30-second bursts in the microwave until warmed through.

title: Egg Foo Young
instructions: Whisk the beef stock, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, oyster sauce, and white pepper together in a small saucepan over medium heat and bring to a simmer.
Dissolve one tablespoon of cornstarch in one tablespoon of water, then whisk it into the simmering broth until the gravy thickens and coats the back of a spoon, about 2 to 3 minutes.
Cover the gravy and keep it warm over the lowest possible heat.
In a separate bowl, whisk the second measure of cornstarch and water together until fully dissolved.
Whisk in the eggs, salt, and sugar until smooth with no egg white clumps left.
Fold in the spring onions, bean sprouts, and shrimp until everything is evenly coated in the batter.
Heat about two inches of vegetable oil in a large wok over medium-high heat to 350°F, or until bubbles form vigorously around an inserted wooden chopstick.
Ladle a quarter of the batter into the hot oil and let it puff up immediately.
Fry the omelet until golden and crispy, about 2 minutes per side, basting any pale patches with hot oil using a ladle.
Lift the omelet out, drain it on a paper towel-lined tray, and let it rest 5 minutes.
Repeat with the remaining batter to make 4 omelets in total, topping up the oil between batches if it runs low.
Plate each omelet over a bed of rice and spoon the warm gravy over the top to serve.

ingredients.note: assumed density ~20g per tablespoon for thick sauce
original calls for white pepper powder; mapped to generic pepper key
assumed 50g per large egg
assumed medium shrimp ~15g each for 20 shrimp

title: Sichuan Style Stir-Fried Chinese Long Beans
instructions: Grab your long beans — green or purple both work fine here, they taste the same.
Heat peanut oil in a wok or a sturdy large sauté pan with a good two-inch lip.
Toss in the dried chillies and Sichuan pepper, let them sizzle and perfume the oil.
Add the long beans and stir-fry over high heat until blistered and just tender.
Season with salt and sugar, toss well.
Finish with a drizzle of sesame seed oil and a splash of soy sauce, toss once more and serve.

ingredients.note: stir-fry oil remains in the dish, not a frying medium
weight inferred from 6 whole dried chillies at ~2g each

title: Chinese Tomato Egg Stir Fry
instructions: Slice the tomatoes in half, trim off the tough stem, then cut each half into thirds.
Beat the eggs in a bowl and set aside.
Pour vegetable oil into a cold nonstick skillet, add the beaten eggs, and set the heat to medium.
Once a thin layer of egg starts to set, push it gently to one side to form soft layers, stirring gently until mostly set but still glossy, about 2 to 5 minutes.
Transfer the eggs to a plate and wipe out the pan if needed.
Add the sesame oil to the pan, then the tomatoes, and stir-fry over medium heat until softened but not mushy, about 3 minutes.
Sprinkle in the chicken bouillon powder, sugar, and pepper, then toss until dissolved, about 1 minute.
Return the eggs to the pan with the tomatoes and stir-fry for about 2 minutes to heat through and combine.
Taste and add salt only if needed.
Scatter over the spring onions and serve with steamed jasmine rice.

ingredients.note: oil is scrambled with eggs and remains in the dish, not drained off
raw rice measure, cooked separately and served alongside

title: Air Fryer Egg Rolls
instructions: Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat and add the ground pork, breaking it apart with a spoon as it cooks until fully done, about 6 to 8 minutes.
Stir in the garlic, ginger, carrot, scallions, and cabbage, and cook until the cabbage wilts and softens, roughly 3 to 4 minutes more.
Season the filling with soy sauce and rice vinegar, then remove from heat and let it cool (this can be made ahead).
Lay an egg roll wrapper on a dry surface like a diamond and spoon about 1/4 cup of filling into the center.
Wet the edges with a little water, fold the sides over the filling, and roll tightly away from you into a cylinder; repeat with the remaining wrappers and filling.
Arrange the rolls in the air fryer basket without touching, brushing lightly with oil.
Air fry at 350°F for 6 to 7 minutes, flip and brush the other side with oil, then cook another 4 to 5 minutes until golden and crispy.
Alternatively, pan fry in about 1/2 inch of oil heated to 350°F, frying 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden, then drain on paper towels.
Serve immediately with duck sauce, plum sauce, and extra soy sauce for dipping.

title: Sichuan Eggplant
instructions: Quarter the eggplant lengthwise and chop into large batons; set aside.
In a small bowl, stir together the chicken stock, sugar, and soy sauce; set aside.
In a second bowl, mix the chilli bean paste, garlic, ginger, and sichuan peppercorns; set aside.
In a third bowl, whisk the cornstarch with a tablespoon of water; set aside.
In a fourth bowl, combine the scallions and vinegar; set aside.
Heat the oil in a wok or large sauté pan over medium-high heat until almost smoking.
Add the eggplant and sauté, letting it sit a few seconds between stirs so it browns and blisters, adding a splash more oil if it soaks it all up.
Add the chili bean paste, garlic, ginger, and sichuan peppercorns and sauté until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
Pour in the chicken stock mixture, lower the heat to medium-low, and simmer for 90 seconds.
Stir in the cornstarch slurry and cook until the sauce thickens slightly.
Add the scallions and vinegar and cook 15 seconds to mellow their sharpness.
Garnish with cilantro and serve.

ingredients.note: oil remains in the stir-fry sauce and is served with the dish, not drained off

title: Shrimp With Snow Peas
instructions: Whisk the cornstarch, dry sherry and kosher salt together in a large bowl, then toss in the shrimp to coat.
Let the shrimp marinate for 15 to 20 minutes while you prep the snow peas, ginger and garlic.
Heat a wok or large sauté pan over high heat for a minute, then add the peanut oil and let it get hot for about 30 seconds.
Toss in the ginger and garlic and stir-fry for about 30 seconds.
Scrape in the shrimp along with all the marinade, then add the snow peas, soy sauce and chicken stock.
Stir-fry until the shrimp turns pink, about 2 minutes.
Add the scallions and stir-fry for one more minute.
Turn off the heat, stir in the sesame oil, toss once more and serve with steamed rice.

ingredients.note: cornstarch same as corn flour in table
stir-fry oil coats and remains in the dish, not drained off
estimated ~15g per scallion

title: Sweet and Sour Chicken
instructions: Toss the chicken thighs with the egg white, half the kosher salt, and the cornstarch until evenly coated; let it rest 15 minutes at room temperature or overnight in the fridge.
Whisk together the pineapple juice, vinegar, ketchup, the remaining kosher salt, and brown sugar to make the sweet and sour sauce.

Get a wok or large pan screaming hot, add a little oil, then sear the chicken in a single layer for about a minute per side until browned but still slightly pink in the middle; lift it out onto a plate, leaving the oil behind in the pan.
Drop the heat to medium, add the rest of the oil, then stir-fry the bell peppers and ginger for about a minute.
Tip in the pineapple chunks and the sauce, crank the heat back to high, and once it's bubbling return the chicken to the pan.
Simmer for 1-2 minutes until the chicken is cooked through, checking a piece for any pink and giving it another minute if needed.
Taste the sauce and adjust with a touch more brown sugar or vinegar if you like.
Serve hot over steamed white or brown rice.

ingredients.note: inferred weight for '1 large egg white'
1 tsp kosher salt ≈ 6g, split between coating and sauce per instructions
2 tsp cornstarch ≈ 16g, inferred conversion
10 oz converted to grams
1/4 cup ≈ 60g, inferred conversion
1/4 cup ≈ 60g, inferred conversion
1/4 cup ≈ 60g, inferred conversion

title: Napa Cabbage with Dried Shrimp
instructions: Soak the dried shrimp in a bowl and pour boiling water over it, then cover and let it plump for 30 minutes.
Halve the napa cabbage lengthwise, rinse it, shake off the water, then cut out and discard the core.
Chop the remaining cabbage into 1-inch pieces, keeping the thick white stem pieces separate from the thinner leafy green pieces.
Drain the rehydrated shrimp and discard the soaking liquid.
Heat the peanut oil in a wok or large skillet over high heat until it shimmers.
Add the scallions, ginger, and drained shrimp, tossing carefully since the moisture can make the oil spatter, until fragrant and the scallions turn bright green, about 1 minute.
Add the thick white cabbage pieces, lower the heat to medium-high, and cook, stirring, for 3 to 4 minutes until the edges turn translucent but the centers stay opaque.
Drop the heat to medium-low, add the leafy green cabbage, and cook until wilted, about 2 minutes.
Whisk together the soy sauce, cold water, and cornstarch into a slurry, then pour it over the cabbage and stir until the sauce thickens to the consistency of whole milk.
Season with salt and pepper, tasting and adjusting before serving.

ingredients.note: 2 tbsp dried shrimp estimated at ~7g/tbsp
boiling water used only to rehydrate shrimp then drained and discarded; not part of final dish
inferred weight for '1 head' napa cabbage, no yield stated; used medium-head estimate
oil remains in the stir-fry sauce, not drained off
4 scallions estimated at ~15g each
cornstarch mapped to corn flour key; 2 tsp estimated at 4g/tsp

title: Sesame Cucumber Salad
instructions: Peel the cucumbers, then cut them into quarters lengthwise.
If the seeds taste bitter, scrape them out and discard.
Slice the cucumber spears crosswise into half-inch pieces.
Pile the cucumbers into a serving bowl and sprinkle with salt.
Toss with the sesame oil, seasoned rice vinegar, basil (if using), and chili flakes.
Finish with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds if using, and serve.

ingredients.note: 'cucumber' not in allowed key list; 2 medium cucumbers estimated at 300g each
oil is tossed into and served with the salad, not a frying medium
optional ingredient ('if using'); estimated weight for 6 leaves
optional garnish ('if using')

title: Dutch poffertjes (mini pancakes)
instructions: Stir the dry yeast into a little of the lukewarm milk and let it dissolve.
Tip the flour and buckwheat flour into a bowl and make a well in the centre.
Pour the yeast mixture into the well, then add the rest of the milk and stir until you get a smooth batter.
Beat in the eggs, salt and vanilla sugar until fully combined.
Cover and let the batter stand and rise for about 45 minutes.
Heat the poffertjespan and drop a small knob of butter into each hole.
Fill each hole halfway with batter and cook the first side until the top looks dry.
Flip each poffertje with a small fork and cook the other side until golden brown and cooked through.
Serve the poffertjes warm with butter and a dusting of icing sugar.

ingredients.note: assumed ~50g per egg for 2 eggs
knob of butter melted in each pan hole to fry the pancakes; acts as frying medium, estimated 10g
1 tsp estimated at ~4g
serving portion, quantity not specified, estimated 20g for finishing/serving
serving portion, quantity not specified, estimated 15g for dusting

title: Apple cake
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F) and grease a cake pan, lining it with baking paper.
Crack the eggs into a large bowl with the sugar, then beat until tripled in volume and fluffy.
Sift the self-raising flour into the egg mixture and fold gently to keep the air in.
Pour in the melted, slightly cooled butter and mix until just combined.
Stir in the cinnamon, a pinch of salt and the vanilla extract.
Fold the diced apple into the batter gently so the pieces spread evenly; toss them in a little extra cinnamon first if you like.
Pour the batter into the prepared pan and smooth the top with a spatula.
Arrange the apple slices on top, pressing lightly, and scatter over almond shavings if using.
Bake for about 45-50 minutes, until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean.
Let the cake cool in the pan for a few minutes, then turn it out onto a wire rack to cool completely.
Dust the cooled cake with powdered sugar before serving.

ingredients.note: 4 eggs converted at ~50g each
1 tsp inferred as ~4g
1 tsp inferred as ~3g
3 apples converted at ~182g each
pinch inferred as ~0.3g
sprinkling for dusting, inferred as ~10g; recipe text calls this 'powdered sugar'

title: Gevulde speculaas
instructions: Combine the self-raising flour, brown sugar, speculaas spice mix and salt in a bowl.
Work in the butter and milk, starting with about 6 tablespoons of milk and adding more only if needed, until you have a firm ball of dough.
Wrap the dough in plastic wrap and chill it overnight for the best flavor, though you can bake it straight away if you must.
Mix the almond paste with half of the beaten egg and knead until smooth and easy to work with.
Roll out half of the chilled dough into a 20 x 20 cm square and lay it in a parchment-lined baking pan.
Spread the almond paste mixture evenly over the dough.
Roll out the remaining dough and lay it on top of the filling.
Brush the top with the rest of the beaten egg, scatter over the almonds, then brush with another layer of egg wash.
Bake at 180°C (350°F) for about 40 minutes, or until done.

ingredients.note: pinch inferred as ~1g
10 tbsp converted to grams (1 tbsp≈15g); recipe text says start with 6 tbsp and add more as needed
amount not specified for brushing; assumed 1 tbsp (~15g)
garnish amount not specified; assumed 15g

title: Dutch stroopwafel
instructions: Combine the milk and yeast in a bowl and let it stand until the yeast dissolves.
In a separate bowl, mix the flour, butter, sugar, egg, and salt.
Pour in the yeast mixture and knead until the dough is smooth.
Cover the dough and let it rise for one hour.
While the dough rises, make the stroop filling: combine the stroop, brown sugar, butter, and cinnamon in a saucepan and stir until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves.
Let the filling simmer briefly - it will keep thickening as it cools.
Shape the dough into 14 balls of about 35 g each.
Heat your stroopwafel iron to its highest setting.
Place a dough ball in the iron, close it, and bake for 1-2 minutes until golden brown, without pressing it too flat.
As soon as the waffle is done, cut out a neat circle about 8-9 cm across while it's still hot.
Slice the hot waffle horizontally with a sharp knife, holding it steady with an oven mitt.
Spread the hot stroop filling over one half, top with the other half, and press gently to seal.
Cool the assembled stroopwafel on a wire rack, then repeat with the remaining balls of dough.

ingredients.note: 15 ml converted 1:1 to grams; unusually small amount for this dough, possibly a typo for 150 ml in the original.
inferred: 1 egg ≈ 50 g.
inferred: a 'pinch' ≈ 1 g.
inferred: 1 tsp cinnamon ≈ 2.6 g.

title: Ramen Noodles with Boiled Egg
instructions: Bring a pot of water to a boil and gently lower in the egg.
Cook the egg for exactly 6½ to 7 minutes.
Immediately transfer the egg to a bowl of ice water and let it sit for 3–5 minutes to stop the cooking.
Peel the egg and, if you have time, marinate it in a mix of soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and water for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Cook the ramen noodles in the water according to the packet, then season with soy sauce, chilli sauce, salt, and pepper.
Stir in the chopped spring onions.
Slice the marinated egg in half and lay it over the noodles so the yolk runs into the broth.

ingredients.note: 1 packet ramen noodles; inferred typical dry-packet weight of 85g
2 cups converted to grams

title: Boterkoek (Dutch Butter Cake)
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F).
Cream the softened butter and sugar together until light and fluffy.
Add the flour and salt, then knead by hand into a smooth dough; split the dough into two equal portions.
Line a square or round cake tin (not springform) with baking parchment and press the first half of the dough evenly into the base, flattening with fingers and a spoon.
Dot the base with spoonfuls of dulce de leche, then scatter the chopped dark chocolate over the top.
Cover with the second half of the dough, patching any gaps by hand - it doesn't need to be perfect, it will bake together fine.
Brush the top of the dough with the beaten egg.
Bake for about 30 minutes until golden brown; the cake will still look wobbly - that's normal, it firms up as it cools.
Let the cake cool completely before cutting into small pieces to serve.

ingredients.note: quantity given as '1' with no unit; inferred a pinch (~3g) based on typical boterkoek proportions
assumed standard large egg weight (~50g); used beaten as a glaze, not baked into the dough itself

title: Dutch Apple Pie
instructions: Pulse the flour, 150g of the sugar, a pinch of salt and 1 egg yolk in a food processor.
Cut the butter into small cubes, add to the processor and pulse just until a firm dough forms - don't overwork it or let it get warm.
Turn the dough onto plastic wrap, shape into a ball, wrap and chill in the fridge for 30 minutes.
Line a 22cm round baking tin with baking paper and brush the sides with butter.
Preheat the oven to 170°C (340°F).
Cut the apples into cubes and toss with the raisins, the remaining sugar and the cinnamon.
On a floured surface, roll out three-quarters of the chilled dough and press it into the tin, covering the base and sides; set aside a quarter of the dough for the top strips.
Crumble the rusks (or breadcrumbs) evenly over the base of the tin.
Spread the apple mixture evenly over the crumbed base.
Roll out the reserved dough, cut into strips, and lay them over the top in a diamond pattern.
Brush the strips and sides with the second egg yolk.
Bake for about 1 hour, until golden and cooked through.
Let the pie cool completely in the tin, and loosen the sides before removing it.

ingredients.note: 2 egg yolks estimated at 17g each
pinch estimated as 1g
quantity given only as '1' with no unit; assumed 1 teaspoon (~2.6g)
6 apples estimated at 180g each; no weight given in original
1 tablespoon ≈15g; instructions don't explicitly mention adding water to dough, inferred as binding liquid

title: Dutch Spiced Breakfast Cake (Ontbijtkoek)
instructions: Preheat your oven to 160°C (320°F) and line a cake pan with parchment paper.
Whisk all the dry ingredients together in a large bowl.
Heat the milk in a small saucepan until it's almost boiling.
Slowly whisk the hot milk into the dry ingredients until you get a smooth batter — don't overmix.
Pour the batter into the prepared cake pan and sprinkle the pearl sugar over the top.
Bake for about 1 hour, checking the cake's progress after 50 minutes.
Test with a skewer; if it comes out clean the cake is done, otherwise give it a little more time in the oven.

ingredients.note: Original line reads '4 Sugar' with no unit — likely the pearl sugar sprinkled on top per instructions; grams inferred (~4 tbsp) and flagged as uncertain.
1 tablespoon ground ginger estimated at ~6g.
1/2 tsp ground cardamom estimated at ~1g.
1 tsp baking powder estimated at ~4g.
'Pinch' salt estimated at ~0.3g.

title: Runner Bean Mash (Snijbonen Stamppot)
instructions: Wash and chop the runner beans, then get them boiling in a pot of salted water.
Simmer the beans about 10 minutes, until just al dente, then drain and set them aside.
While that's going, boil the potatoes in a separate pot until they're fork-tender.
In a small skillet, melt the butter and soften the onion until translucent, then toss in the garlic for a minute.
Tip the cooked beans into the skillet and stir-fry everything together for a few minutes.
Mash the potatoes with a masher, working in a knob of butter and a splash of milk.
Fold the bean mixture and grated cheese into the mash, then season with salt, pepper and mustard.
Fry the bacon separately and serve it on top of the bean mash.

ingredients.note: 1 clove estimated at 5g
1 medium chopped onion estimated at 110g
butter used both to saute onion (remains in dish) and stirred into mash; not a discarded frying medium

'dash' estimated at 15g
fried in its own fat and served atop the mash, not used as a discarded frying medium
'pinch' estimated at 1g

title: Apple Potato Mash (Hete bliksem)
instructions: Peel the potatoes and cut them into even chunks, then boil in salted water for about 10 minutes.
Add the chopped apples and the cinnamon stick to the boiling water and simmer another 10 minutes, until the apples and potatoes are as soft as you like them.
Meanwhile, fry the bacon in a dry pan until crispy, then drain on paper towels.
In a separate pan, melt a little butter and sauté a few apple slices with a splash of maple syrup and a pinch of cinnamon until soft.
Beat the egg onto one plate and spread the sesame seeds on another; cut the cheese into small, easy-to-handle pieces.
Dip each piece of cheese first in the egg, then coat thoroughly in sesame seeds.
Heat a non-stick pan until very hot, then fry the cheese quickly on each side until the sesame coating browns - work fast so it doesn't melt away.
Drain the boiled apples and potatoes, remove the cinnamon stick, and mash together.
Season with salt and a touch more cinnamon to taste, then serve topped with the crispy bacon, sautéed apple slices, and sesame-crusted cheese.

ingredients.note: No yield stated in recipe text; servings inferred from total dish mass (~880g) and side-dish portioning, giving 2 servings of ~440g each.
Weight of '1 cinnamon stick' inferred at ~2g (removed before mashing, minimal residual mass).

1 egg estimated at ~50g (standard medium egg).

title: Traditional Dutch rice tart (rijstevlaai)
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C.
For the dough, stir the flour and sugar together in a bowl.
Warm the 100 g of vegetable milk to lukewarm, dissolve the yeast in it, and let it sit until it starts to bubble.
Add the yeast mixture and the coconut oil to the flour and knead into a smooth dough.
Cover and let the dough rise for 30 minutes in a warm spot.
Meanwhile, make the rice pudding: put the dessert rice, sugar, the 700 g of vegetable milk, and the vanilla extract into a saucepan with the cardamom, star anise, and cinnamon stick.
Bring to a boil and cook, stirring constantly, until thickened, then remove the whole spices.
In a small bowl, dissolve the baking powder in the lemon juice, stirring until fully combined, then stir this into the rice pudding.
On a floured surface, roll out the dough (it will be sticky, but that's fine) and line the base and sides of a greased tart mould with it.
Spread the rice pudding evenly over the dough base.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until set and lightly golden.

ingredients.note: cooked into pudding within this recipe, not pre-cooked
1 tsp converted to ~4 g by volume-to-weight estimate
2 pods estimated at ~1 g each
2 whole pods estimated at ~1 g each
1 stick estimated at ~3 g
1/2 tsp converted to ~2 g by volume-to-weight estimate; instructions call this 'baking soda' but ingredient list states baking powder
1/2 tsp converted to ~2 g by volume-to-weight estimate

title: Arnhemse meisjes
instructions: Pull the puff pastry sheets out of the freezer and let them defrost fully.
Dust the counter with flour, then stack all five sheets together and roll them out as one thin, unified sheet.
Cut cookies from the sheet with a round or oval cutter, working economically to get as many as possible.
In a small bowl, mix the vanilla sugar with the cinnamon, cardamom, and lime zest.
Wipe the flour off the counter and dust it lightly with the sugar mix instead.
Brush each pastry round with egg wash, then dip and roll it in the sugar mix, pressing gently so the sugar sticks.
Arrange the sugared pastry rounds on a greased or lined baking sheet and let them rest for 20 to 25 minutes so they crisp up better.
Preheat the oven to 180°C while the pastry rests, and slide the tray in once hot.
Bake for about 25 minutes until golden brown and the sugar has caramelized nicely.
Pull them out and enjoy your Arnhemse Meisjes with an Almere twist.

ingredients.note: no sheet weight given; assumed ~45g per puff pastry sheet (5 sheets stacked and rolled thin)
converted from '1/2 tsp' using ~2.6g per tsp ground cinnamon
original lists '1/2' with no unit; assumed 1/2 tsp ground cardamom (~1g)
lime zest has no matching table key (only lemon/orange zest exist); flagged as missing ingredient, grams estimated as zest of one average lime

title: Dutch doughnuts
instructions: Tip the flour into a large bowl and stir in the yeast and sugar.
Warm the milk to lukewarm if it's cold, then whisk it into the flour a little at a time until you get a smooth batter.
Mix in the salt with a wooden spoon until the batter turns smooth and pourable.
Cover the bowl with a clean tea towel and let it rise in a warm spot for about an hour.
Heat oil in a deep fryer or large pot to 180°C (350°F).
Give the risen batter a good stir, then scoop it with two spoons or an ice cream scoop, dipping the scoop in the hot oil first so the batter slides off easily.
Drop the batter into the oil carefully, working in batches so the fryer isn't overcrowded and the oil stays hot.
Let each doughnut turn itself once the underside is golden; nudge it over with two forks if it doesn't flip on its own.
Once golden all over, lift the doughnuts out and drain them on kitchen paper.

title: Zeeuwse bolussen
instructions: Whisk the dried yeast into the milk and sugar in your mixer bowl, then let it stand a few minutes until foamy.
Add the flour, egg, butter, salt, 1 tsp of the cinnamon and the cardamom, then process into a smooth dough.
Shape the dough into a ball, cover, and let it rise for 1 hour or until doubled in size.
Mix the caster sugar with the remaining tablespoon of cinnamon, tip it over the dough and roll the dough through it.
Divide the dough into 10 equal balls (about 75g each) and let them rest a few minutes so the sugar softens slightly.
Roll each ball into a strand about 35-40cm long, rolling it through the powdered sugar as you go.
Coil the strand around itself, tuck the tail underneath, and pin the end in place to secure it.
Arrange the buns on a lined baking sheet, cover with cling film, and let rise for 30-60 minutes.
Preheat the oven to 250°C (480°F) with top and bottom heat.
Bake for about 8 minutes, watching closely — overbaking dries them out and they should stay sticky.
Cool for 1 minute out of the oven, then flip and let cool fully, on a wire rack if you have one.

ingredients.note: 200 ml converted to grams at roughly 1 g/ml.
‘1 medium egg’ estimated at 50 g; no exact weight given.
1 tsp salt estimated at 6 g.
1 tablespoon ground cinnamon estimated at 7 g; instructions also mention a separate 1 tsp cinnamon added to the dough, not itemised separately.
1 tablespoon ground cardamom estimated at 6 g; instructions also mention cardamom added with the dough spices, not itemised separately.
Listed as 'Brown Sugar' but instructions call for 'caster sugar' mixed with cinnamon to coat the dough before forming balls; closest available key used, quantity as given.
Listed as 'Brown Sugar' but instructions call for 'powdered sugar' to roll the strands through; closest available key used, quantity as given, sugar type uncertain.

title: Slagroomtaart
instructions: Beat the eggs, sugar, and vanilla sugar together until light and fluffy — about 10 minutes on medium-high speed if using a stand mixer.
Sift the flour and cornstarch together, then fold gently into the egg mixture, keeping as much air in as you can.
Pour the batter into a greased baking pan and bake at 180°C (350°F) in a conventional oven for 30 minutes, until the top springs back when pressed lightly.
Let the cake cool in the pan for a few minutes, then turn it out onto a rack to cool completely.
Meanwhile, prepare the nougatine and jam if you haven't already.
Once the cake is fully cooled, slice it into three layers (if using a 20 cm/8 inch springform pan).

ingredients.note: amount given as '6 tablespoons'; converted using ~20g per tbsp of jam
amount given as '6 tablespoons'; converted using ~12.5g per tbsp of sugar
quantity given only as '5' (pieces, no unit); estimated ~50g per piece for topping

title: Baghlaw-e-Khanagi
instructions: Sift the flour into a bowl and make a well in the centre.
Whisk the eggs and pour them into the well.
Stir in the milk until fully combined.
Mix in the ground walnuts, baking powder and cardamom, folding until the dough turns smooth and elastic.
Roll the dough out into a thin rectangle on a clean surface.
Fold the dough into 3cm-wide strips from the longest side, then cut across into 3cm pieces.
Heat the vegetable oil in a deep pan until it reaches frying temperature.
Meanwhile bring a litre of water to the boil in another pan, stir in the sugar and simmer gently until it becomes a thick syrup.
Fry the dough pieces in the hot oil until golden brown.
Lift the fried pieces straight into the warm syrup to coat them.
Remove from the syrup while still warm and sprinkle with more ground walnuts so they stick.
Leave to cool before serving to friends and family.

ingredients.note: assumed ~50g per egg for 3 eggs
converted from 75ml oil (~0.92g/ml); used as deep-frying medium, mostly drained off, not part of served dish
1 tsp assumed ~4g
boiled with sugar to make syrup; some water evaporates as it thickens

title: Haft Mewa Recipe
instructions: Rinse the dried apricots and both types of raisins, then tip them into a bowl and cover with cold water so it sits about 5cm above the fruit.
In a separate bowl or pan, cover the walnuts, pistachios and almonds with boiling water and let them soak until the skins soften.
Peel the skins off each nut once softened - it's fiddly and takes a while, but worth it - then discard that soaking water.
Combine the soaked fruits (with their soaking liquid), the peeled nuts and the cherries in one bowl.
Stir in the rose water, cover, and refrigerate for two days so the juices turn sweet and syrupy.
Spoon the fruit, nuts and syrupy juice into individual bowls or cups to serve.

ingredients.note: No allowed key for 'rose water' - flagged as missing ingredient. Original amount was 'a splash', so 5g is an inferred estimate.

title: Kadu Borani
instructions: Peel the squash and cut it into 1cm pieces, then deep fry in hot oil until golden; set aside.
Using the same oil, deep fry the onions until golden, then grind them with the garlic (if using garlic paste, stir it into the ground onions).
Heat a little oil in a pan over medium heat and add the turmeric.
Stir the salt into a cup of water and pour it into the turmeric.
Layer the fried squash with the onion-garlic mixture, repeating until everything is used up.
Add the sugar and another cup of water, lower the heat, and simmer until the water has reduced.
Spread a couple of tablespoons of melted yogurt on a serving plate, top with the squash, then add another layer of melted yogurt.
Scatter over the green chilli and serve.

ingredients.note: '1 medium squash' – no weight given, estimated at 800 g for a medium pumpkin/squash used in this dish.
'5 chopped onion' – estimated ~100 g per onion, no weight stated.
'To taste' – no quantity given, estimated a small garnish amount.

'Splash oil' – amount not specified; oil is reused across deep-frying and for cooking the turmeric/onion layer that stays in the dish, so treated as non-frying-medium (in-dish) per the single-line ambiguity; likely undercounts actual deep-frying oil used.
'1 bulb garlic' estimated at 40 g, standard bulb weight.
'7 tablespoons sugar' estimated at 12.5 g/tbsp.
'1/2 tsp salt' estimated at ~3 g.
'2 cups water' estimated at ~235 g/cup.
'4 tablespoons melted curd' estimated at ~15 g/tbsp; 'curd' treated as yogurt.

title: Firni
instructions: Whisk 1 cup of the milk with the cornstarch in a small bowl until completely smooth.
Pour the remaining milk into a non-stick saucepan and set it over medium heat with the sugar and cardamom (add rosewater here too if you like).
Just before it boils, slowly whisk in the cornstarch-milk mixture, stirring constantly to keep lumps away.
Keep stirring until the mixture thickens nicely.
Take the pan off the heat and pour the pudding into a large shallow serving bowl.
Let it cool at room temperature, then chill in the refrigerator for 1-2 hours.
Scatter sliced almonds and pistachios over the top before serving.

ingredients.note: 6 cups converted at ~240 g/cup
5 tbsp converted at ~8 g/tbsp
1 1/2 cups converted at ~200 g/cup
1/2 tsp converted at ~2 g/tsp
rosewater/rose water has no matching key in the allowed list; flagged as missing ingredient, estimated 1 tsp ~5 g
"handful" is not a precise measure; estimated 30 g
"handful" is not a precise measure; estimated 30 g

title: Mastawa Lamb and Rice
instructions: Put the lamb pieces and onions into a large pan, add the turmeric and enough water to cover the meat.
Bring to the boil, then simmer gently until the lamb is very tender and falls off the bone.
While the meat cooks, peel the orange into thin matchstick strips, trimming away as much pith as possible, and soak the strips in a bowl of water.
Once the lamb is tender, lift it out, let it cool slightly, then pull the meat off the bones and shred it into strips with your hands.
Return the shredded lamb to the pan.
Rinse the rice and add it to the pan with salt to taste, making sure the liquid covers the rice by about 2cm, topping up with water if needed.
Bring back to the boil, then lower to a medium heat and cook uncovered, stirring occasionally, until the rice is al dente and the liquid has been absorbed.
Stir in the natural yoghurt gently but thoroughly.
Fry the crushed garlic cloves in the oil, then add them to the pan along with the dill, black pepper, chickpeas and extra salt to taste.
Mix everything together well, then cover the pan with a clean thick cloth followed by the lid.
Leave on a low heat for about half an hour to let the flavours meld.
Serve warm with naan bread for extra comfort.

ingredients.note: 3 medium onions estimated at ~150g each
1 tsp estimated at ~2g
only the peel/zest is used, pith discarded; weight estimated for the strips, not the whole orange
4 cloves estimated at ~5g each; fried then stirred into the dish so oil and garlic remain in the food
used to fry garlic which is then added to the dish, so oil stays in the served food
1 tbsp estimated at ~2g
1 tbsp estimated at ~6g
1 standard can estimated at ~400g
amount not specified, added to taste

title: Bolani with Potato Filling
instructions: Whisk the flour with a teaspoon of the salt in a mixing bowl, then work in just enough water to bring it into a stiff dough.
Turn the dough out and knead for 5-10 minutes until smooth, elastic and shiny; shape into a ball, cover with a damp cloth and rest for at least 30 minutes.
Peel and boil the potatoes in salted water until soft, then drain and mash thoroughly.
Stir the spring onions, remaining salt and black pepper into the mashed potato.
Divide the rested dough into 3-4 balls and roll each out on a floured surface to no more than 1.5mm thick.
Cut rounds about 13-15cm across using a cutter or pan lid.
Spoon 1-2 tablespoons of the potato filling onto each round, moisten the edges, fold over and press to seal.
Lay the filled bolani on a floured surface until ready to cook.
Shallow fry one or two at a time in hot vegetable oil, browning both sides.
Keep the fried bolani warm while you finish the rest, then serve immediately.

ingredients.note: 3 tsp converted to grams (~6g per tsp)
boiled and mashed before assembly
1 tsp converted to grams (~2g per tsp)
quantity not specified ('for frying'); estimated typical shallow-fry amount, oil acts as frying medium and is not eaten with the dish

title: Mantu Afghan Dumpling
instructions: Sift the flour with a teaspoon of salt into a mixing bowl, then gradually work in the water until a stiff dough forms.
Knead the dough on a clean surface for 5-10 minutes until elastic and shiny, then cover with a damp cloth and rest for about an hour.
Mix the chopped lamb, onions, chilli, a teaspoon of salt, ground pepper and spices together thoroughly in a bowl.
Divide the rested dough into 4 balls and roll each out to about 1.5mm thick, then cut into 10cm squares.
Spoon a heaped teaspoon of the lamb mixture into the centre of each square.
Bring two opposite corners together and pinch firmly to seal, then repeat with the other two corners, leaving a small gap so steam can reach the filling.
Grease the steamer shelves with a little fat, arrange the mantu with space between them, and steam over medium heat for 30-45 minutes.
Meanwhile, heat the oil in a pan, stir in the tomato puree, bring to the boil, then simmer gently until the mantu are done.
Carefully lift the mantu from the steamer onto a warm serving dish.
Spoon the tomato sauce over the mantu, top with the strained yoghurt, and finish with a scatter of chopped coriander.

ingredients.note: 225ml converted at 1g/ml
estimated weight for 1 whole chilli
2 tbsp ≈14g; unusually large quantity for black pepper, possibly a typo in original
1 tbsp ≈14g; oil remains in the sauce served over the dumplings, so counted as eaten

title: Muraba-E-Kadu (Pumpkin Jam)
instructions: Peel the pumpkin and scoop out the seeds.
Cut the pumpkin flesh into 2cm cubes.
Zest the oranges thinly, avoiding the bitter pith, and slice the zest into matchstick strips.
Juice the oranges and set the juice aside.
Split the cardamom pods and remove the seeds, discarding the pods.
Combine the pumpkin cubes, orange juice, orange zest strips, sugar and cardamom seeds in a large pan, stir well, and let it marinate for 10 hours or overnight, stirring occasionally.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer until the liquid thickens into a syrup, about an hour or a bit more.
Let the jam cool, then spoon it into clean, dry jars.

ingredients.note: whole oranges used for zest and juice; weight as given

title: Tavë Kosi Baked Lamb and Yogurt
instructions: Melt the butter and olive oil together in a large lidded pan, then brown the diced lamb over medium heat for about 5 minutes.
Stir in the garlic and oregano, add 300ml of water, season with salt and pepper, cover and simmer for 50 minutes, stirring occasionally so the lamb doesn't catch.
Add another 150ml of water along with the rice, mix well, bring to the boil, cover and cook for about 10 minutes until the liquid reduces to a moist, oily coating over the lamb and rice.
Transfer the lamb and rice into a casserole dish (or split between four ceramic bowls), leaving room at the top for the yogurt topping.
Melt the butter for the roux in a small saucepan, whisk in the flour and cook for two minutes until it thickens slightly and darkens a touch, then set aside.
Whisk the yogurt and eggs together with salt and pepper, then beat in the roux quickly until fully combined.
Pour the yogurt mixture over the lamb and rice, then sprinkle with a little extra oregano.
Bake on the bottom shelf of the oven at 180°C for 35 minutes, then move to the middle shelf for a further 10 minutes until the topping puffs up and turns golden.
Let the Tavë Kosi rest for 5–10 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: 2 tbsp ≈27g olive oil (assumed 15ml/tbsp, 0.91g/ml)
2 tsp dried oregano ≈2g assumed
2 cloves ≈10g assumed (5g/clove)
pinch inferred as ~0.5g
pinch inferred as ~0.5g

6 eggs ≈300g assumed (50g/egg)
pinch inferred as ~0.5g
pinch inferred as ~0.5g

title: Tavë me Presh ska Mish Meatless Leek Bake
instructions: Warm a small dash of olive oil in a pan and fry the leeks for a few minutes until they start to brown.
Stir in the roasted red peppers, garlic, vegetable stock, oregano, parsley, basil, salt, pepper and paprika, then mix in the rice.
Bring the mixture to the boil and let it simmer for 2 minutes.
Pour everything into a baking tray at least 2 inches deep, mixing thoroughly and levelling it out.
Bake on the bottom shelf of a 180°C preheated oven for 1 hour.
Turn off the oven and, leaving the door open, let the leek bake cool for 10 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: 3 diced peppers, weight estimated at ~120g each; instructions call them 'roasted red peppers'

title: Arra të Mbushura me Fik Walnut Stuffed Figs (with Fig Syrup)
instructions: Trim the hard stalk ends off the figs, then slice each fig in half lengthways.
Lay the figs cut-side up in a small frying pan and pour the water around them.
Cook over medium heat until the figs soften and are cooked through, about 10-15 minutes, then lift them out onto a serving dish.
Leave the reddish fig juice in the pan, add the sugar, and keep it on medium heat, stirring constantly for a couple of minutes until it thickens into a syrup.
Take the syrup off the heat.
Press a walnut half into each fig half, then drizzle the syrup over the top.
Serve the figs warm or cooled.

ingredients.note: estimated 50g per fig; no weight given
estimated weight for 4 whole walnuts, halved to match the 8 fig halves
remains in pan and becomes the served syrup, not a frying medium

title: Lakror me Kungull Summer Squash Pie
instructions: Whisk together the grated squash, sugar, eggs and baking soda in a mixing bowl.
Fold in the flour a bit at a time until you have a thick, sticky batter — add more flour if it still feels thin or runny.
Rub olive oil over the base and edges of a large round baking tray, about 2 inches deep.
Scoop the mixture into the tray and spread it out evenly.
Rub a little olive oil over your palm and smooth it gently over the top of the mixture in circles to flatten it and give it a glossy finish.
Bake at 180°C for 40 minutes, until the top turns golden brown.
Let it cool completely before slicing into squares to serve.

ingredients.note: '2 Squash' — no weight given, estimated 200g each for summer squash
'2 Eggs' — standard 50g per egg assumed
ingredient list says Baking Powder but instructions say baking soda; used the listed ingredient, 1/4 tsp ≈1.2g
'Splash Olive Oil' — no quantity given, estimated 10g; oil is rubbed into and stays in the baked pie, not a frying medium

title: Kulaç Soda Bread
instructions: Mix the flour, baking powder and salt together on a large tray or clean surface.
Make a well in the centre and add the yogurt, then use your hands to slowly bring the mixture into a dough.
Knead the dough on a floured surface, adding a little extra flour if it feels too sticky, until it reaches a pizza-dough texture that's still very slightly tacky.
Rub a little oil on a baking tray, place the dough on top, and shape it into a flattened circle about 5cm high.
Coat the top of the dough fully with a little olive oil.
Optionally score the dough with the back of a fork to create line patterns or spike it all over, which helps it cook evenly.
Place the loaf on the bottom shelf of a preheated oven and bake for 40 minutes, until golden and firm to the touch.
Once out of the oven, stand the loaf upright or set it on a cooling rack to cool.
Slice the Kulaç into 8 wedges like a cake, or snap and pull it apart by hand if serving warm.

ingredients.note: 1/2 tsp salt converted to grams (~6g/tsp basis)
1 tsp baking powder converted to grams (~4g/tsp basis); instructions call it 'baking soda' but ingredient list specifies baking powder
amount inferred; original just says 'splash' with no quantity given; oil is brushed onto and baked into the dough so it remains in the served bread, not a frying medium

title: Shendetlie Honey and Nut Cake
instructions: Cream together the eggs, sugar, and cooled melted butter, then stir in the honey and baking powder.
Mix in half the flour along with the walnuts (whole or ground, your choice).
Add the remaining flour and knead by hand until a firm dough forms.
Oil a large baking tray (a 30cm round tin, 2-3 inches deep is traditional), press the dough in to fill it evenly and flat.
Set the tray on the bottom shelf of a cold oven, turn the oven to 180°C and bake for 35 minutes until the top is dark brown and firm to the touch.
Remove from the oven and let the cake cool while you make the syrup.
Bring the sugar and water to the boil in a saucepan, boil for 10 minutes stirring occasionally, then remove from the heat.
Cut the cooled cake into squares or diamonds, pour over the hot syrup until it covers the cake with about 1cm pooling on top, then leave to soak and cool for at least an hour before serving.

converted from 4 tablespoons using ~21g/tbsp

title: Presh me Oriz Leek and Rice Bake
instructions: Fry the leeks in a splash of olive oil for a few minutes until they start to brown.
Add the diced onions and lamb mince, frying until everything is nicely browned, stirring often so the leeks don't catch or burn.
Stir in the rice for a few minutes until it's well coated in the pan juices.
Pour in the lamb stock, season with salt and pepper, bring to the boil and let it bubble for 2 minutes.
Tip the mixture into a large baking tray and bake on the bottom shelf of a 180°C oven for up to 1 hour, until all the liquid has evaporated and the rice is soft.
Turn off the oven, leave the door open and let the bake cool for 10 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: '1 Diced Onion' has no stated weight; inferred ~150g for a medium onion.
'Pinch Salt' has no stated weight; inferred ~2g.
'Pinch Pepper' has no stated weight; inferred ~1g.
'Splash Olive Oil' has no stated weight; inferred ~15g. Oil is used to fry leeks in the same pot that becomes the bake, so it remains in the dish (frying_medium=false).

title: Dolma Japrak Stuffed Vine Leaves
instructions: Sauté the chopped onions in olive oil for about 5 minutes until they start to brown, then take the pan off the heat.
Stir in the lamb mince, rice, pepper, paprika, vegetable stock, parsley and basil until fully combined.
Carefully unwrap the vine leaves and overlap two leaves point-to-base to create a sturdy wrapping surface.
Spoon a portion of the meat and rice mixture into the centre of the leaves, fold the bottom over, tuck in the sides, then roll upward into a neat parcel.
Line the bottom of a deep saucepan with a layer of vine leaves, then arrange the parcels seam-side down on top.
Continue rolling and packing parcels in tight layers until all the filling is used.

Pour in enough boiling water to just cover the parcels, bring to the boil, then reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook for 1.5 hours, topping up with a little more boiling water if it dries out before then.
Take the pot off the heat and let the dolma rest for 10 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: 5 onions, weight inferred at ~150g each (average medium onion)
1 tsp converted to grams (~2g)

title: Lakra me Mish Cabbage and Meat Stew
instructions: Warm a splash of olive oil in a lidded sauté pan or saucepan.
Add the diced onion, diced beef and diced red peppers, and fry for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the meat is fully browned.
Stir in the paprika, tomato purée and chilli flakes, season with salt and pepper, and fry for a further 2 minutes.
Add the diced cabbage and stir well, frying for 3-5 minutes until most of the oil has cooked away.
Pour in the boiling water so it covers the cabbage by about an inch, cover with the lid and bring to the boil.
Reduce to a simmer and cook for 2.5 hours, until the cabbage is soft and the meat is tender.
Ladle into wide bowls and leave to cool for 5 minutes before serving.

ingredients.note: No weight given for '1 diced cabbage'; inferred using an average whole cabbage weight of ~900g.

title: Flija Layered Pancake / Crepe
instructions: Whisk the flour, water, and salt together until every lump dissolves; the batter should be thick but still pourable — add a splash more water if it's too stiff.
Set the flour batter aside.
Whisk the melted butter, oil, and your chosen yogurt or cream together until smooth; let it rest 10 minutes, then whisk again until it's the consistency of custard.
Set the filling aside.
Preheat the grill to a medium setting.
Rub a little olive oil over the base of a large, deep baking tray or pie pan.
Pipe or ladle the flour batter in thin lines across the pan in a star pattern, leaving triangular gaps between the lines — the batter is too thick to pour.
Grill this first layer until golden brown, 3–5 minutes.
Brush the whole surface, both battered and bare patches, with a layer of the filling mixture.
Pipe another star-patterned layer of flour batter, threading it through the gaps left by the previous layer and again leaving triangular spaces.
Grill until golden, another 3–5 minutes.
Repeat the filling-then-batter layering, topping up any thin or bare patches every couple of rounds, until all the batter and filling are used up — this takes roughly 2 hours total.
Serve the finished flija straight away with honey or yogurt.

title: Kurabie Butter Cookies
instructions: Combine all the dough ingredients in a large mixing bowl and mix until a soft, sticky dough forms; work in a little extra flour if it's too sticky to handle.
Rub melted butter over your hands, then divide the dough into 20 equal pieces and roll each into a ball, re-buttering your hands between each ball.
Arrange the balls 5cm apart on a greased or lined baking tray, baking in two batches.
Brush each ball with beaten egg, then bake at 180°C for 20 minutes until lightly golden and still slightly soft in the middle.
Transfer the cookies to a cooling rack and let them cool completely.

ingredients.note: 275ml treated as ~275g using 1:1 density
