title: Torrijas
instructions: Grate the zest of the orange and lemon into a saucepan.
Add the whole milk, granulated sugar, and cinnamon stick, then bring to a boil over medium heat.
Remove from the heat and stir in the dry sherry, then let the mixture cool until safe to handle.
Meanwhile, cut the day-old baguette crosswise into 1-inch-thick slices and arrange them in a single layer on a baking sheet.
Pour the warm milk mixture over the bread and let it soak for 5 minutes.
Flip each slice and let it sit for another 5 minutes until it has absorbed most of the liquid without falling apart.
Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat until shimmering.
Whisk the eggs in a bowl while the oil heats.
Dip each soaked bread slice into the egg to coat, letting the excess drip off, then fry in batches until golden-brown on the bottom, about 4 minutes.
Flip and fry the second side until golden-brown, 3 to 4 minutes more.
Transfer the fried torrijas to a paper towel-lined plate to drain.
Repeat dipping and frying with the remaining bread slices.
Drizzle with honey before serving.

title: Trinxat (Potato, Cabbage and Bacon Hash)
instructions: Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil.
Add the cabbage and potatoes and cook until tender, about 30-40 minutes.
Drain the vegetables very well.
Return them to the pot, set the burner to low, and let them steam off excess moisture.
Meanwhile, cook the bacon, reserving the rendered fat for frying the hash.
Chop the cooked bacon into small pieces.
Mash the potatoes and cabbage together with a potato masher, then stir in the minced garlic.
Season with salt and pepper to taste.
Press the mixture into individual forms, top with the bacon pieces, and fry in the reserved bacon fat until golden brown on both sides, flipping once.
Remove the forms and garnish with chopped parsley.
For one large hash instead, press everything into a skillet with the bacon on top, fry until golden, flip once, then cut into servings.
Garnish with chopped parsley before serving.

ingredients.note: '1/2 Cabbage' - no yield stated; inferred a half of a ~1kg cabbage.
'2 medium Russet Potato' - inferred ~150g per medium potato.

title: Pa Amb Tomaquet (Bread with Tomato)
instructions: Toast the bread slices until crispy and golden, either in a toaster or in a 400°F oven — timing depends on your bread's type and thickness.
Rub a garlic clove, cut-side down, all over the top of each toasted bread slice.
Rub a tomato half, cut-side down, all over the top of the bread.
Drizzle the bread evenly with a little olive oil and sprinkle with a pinch of salt.
Repeat with the remaining bread slices and serve immediately while warm.

ingredients.note: '2 sliced Bread' — estimated 35g per slice; servings inferred as 2 from the small ingredient count, no yield stated in text.

'3 chopped Tomato' — estimated 120g per tomato.

title: Cambodian Stir-fried Morning Glory with Pork, Fermented Soybeans, and Garlic
instructions: Heat a tablespoon of vegetable oil in a wok or large pan over medium heat.
Once the oil shimmers, add the chopped garlic and stir-fry for about 30 seconds until fragrant, being careful not to let it burn.
Add the minced pork, breaking it up with a spatula, and stir-fry until fully cooked and browned, roughly 2-3 minutes.
Stir in the fermented soya beans and cook for another minute, mixing thoroughly with the pork.
Toss in the morning glory and stir-fry for 2-3 minutes, just enough to keep its bright green colour and crisp bite.
Taste and adjust the seasoning with fish sauce or sugar as needed, then sprinkle over chilli.
Transfer to a serving dish and serve immediately with steamed rice.

2 tablespoons fermented soy beans — inferred ~10g per tbsp

title: Crispy fried fish with ginger and fermented soybeans (trey chien chuon)
instructions: Fry the ginger in plenty of oil until golden and slightly crispy, then set it aside.
Cut the fish into strips and dredge in a thin layer of tempura flour.
Fry the fish in the same oil until golden brown and crispy.
Crush the garlic and chop it finely; chop the spring onions.
Drain the fermented soybeans in a colander, rinsing first if using salted beans.
Heat a tablespoon of oil in a deep pan or large pot and sauté the garlic until slightly golden.
Add the soybeans and stir-fry for a few minutes, adding a little sugar if the beans are very salty.
Pour in the water and fry for another minute.
Just before serving, stir in the fried ginger, spring onion, and crispy fish, then serve with steamed rice.

title: Cambodian Waffles (Num Poum)
instructions: Whisk the rice flour, wheat flour, coconut milk, palm sugar, eggs and salt together in a bowl until smooth.
Brush a hot waffle iron with oil.
Pour in the batter.
Cook over an open fire until the waffle turns light brown and crispy.

title: BEEF LOK LAK (Lok Lak Sach Ko)
instructions: Fry the eggs sunny-side up and set them aside.
Finely chop the garlic, wash and drain the lettuce leaves, and thinly slice the onion and tomatoes.
Cut the beef tenderloin into 2–3 cm cubes and marinate it with sea salt, palm sugar, Chinese cooking wine, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sesame oil, mixing well.
Heat a frying pan on high with the oil, add the chopped garlic, and stir until lightly browned.
Add the marinated beef along with all its marinade and cook for 5–7 minutes depending on how rare you like it.
Dry roast the Kampot peppercorns in a frying pan over medium-low heat for 2–3 minutes until fragrant.
Grind the roasted pepper, place it in a bowl, and mix with sea salt and lime juice to make the dipping sauce.
On a serving plate, layer the lettuce, then tomatoes, then onions.
Spoon the cooked beef over the vegetables and top with the fried eggs.
Serve with the Kampot pepper sauce and fragrant steamed rice.
Sear hot and fast — Lok Lak is best when the beef stays tender and juicy, paired with a bold, bright dipping sauce to cut the richness.

title: Num E (Sticky Rice Balls in Sugar Syrup and Coconut Cream)
instructions: Simmer the coconut cream with torn pandan leaves, brown sugar, salt and ginger over low heat, bring to a boil, then cook 2 more minutes.
Turn off the heat, discard the pandan leaves, and let the syrup cool.
Blend the remaining pandan leaves with a little water until deep green, then strain well.
Knead the pandan liquid into 200g sticky rice flour until smooth.
Peel and blend the beetroot with a little water until deep red, then strain well.
Knead the beetroot liquid into another 200g sticky rice flour until smooth.
Bring 3 litres of water to a boil in a large pot.
Roll each dough into small balls, about a third the size of a ping pong ball.
Drop the balls into the boiling water and cook until they float, then transfer to iced water to keep them separate.
Drain the balls well, then stir them into the cooled coconut syrup.
Serve in small bowls, topped with longan and shredded coconut as desired.
Add the coloring liquids gradually since each flour absorbs differently, aiming for a smooth, pliable, non-sticky dough.

title: Meang Nem
instructions: Rinse the rice and let it soak in water for about 10 minutes, then drain thoroughly.
Heat a wok or large skillet over medium heat and add the drained rice, stirring constantly until it turns evenly dark golden brown and smells toasty, about 5 minutes.
Tip the toasted rice into a bowl to cool, then grind it finely with a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Sift the ground rice through a fine mesh strainer to remove any coarse bits and set the roasted rice powder aside.
Thinly slice the shallots lengthwise.
Finely chop the galangal, pickled scallion heads, chillies, and bitter orange zest.
Coarsely grind the roasted peanuts and set aside.
Heat a frying pan over low heat, add the shredded coconut, and toast, stirring, for 2–3 minutes, then set aside.
In a large bowl, combine the lime juice, bitter orange juice, palm sugar, pickled scallion brine, and fish sauce, stirring until the sugar fully dissolves.
In a mixing bowl, combine the dried shrimp, toasted coconut, chopped aromatics, and the dressing, mixing well.
Stir in the roasted rice powder, then top with the coarsely ground roasted peanuts.
Arrange the salad on a large plate for guests to spoon onto small plates, wrap in leafy greens with vegetables, and eat in bite-size portions.
For a vegetarian version, omit the dried shrimp and fish sauce, seasoning to taste with soy sauce and a pinch of sea salt to keep the sweet-sour balance.

title: Laos Plantain Coconut Bake (Flourless Muffin)
instructions: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C).
Mash the plantains until they form a smooth paste.
Combine the coconut cream and granulated sugar with the mashed plantain, mixing well until fully incorporated.
Line a muffin pan with paper liners.
Spoon the mixture evenly into the muffin cups.
Bake for 45 minutes, until cooked all the way through.

estimated 200g per large plantain x4; no explicit weight given

title: Authentic Laos Tapioca Pudding with Bananas
instructions: Soak the tapioca pearls in a bowl of warm water for 20 minutes.
Peel the bananas and slice them into half-inch rounds, then set aside.
Bring 4 cups of water to a boil in a pot on the stove.
Add the banana slices to the boiling water.
After the tapioca has soaked for 20 minutes, drain it and rinse under cold water so the pearls separate.
Tip the drained tapioca pearls into the pot with the bananas and simmer for 3 minutes, until the pearls turn clear.
Stir in the sugar and pour in the coconut cream, mixing gently for 3 minutes to blend the flavors.
Season with salt to taste before serving.

title: Lao Som Pak Pickled Cabbage
instructions: Cut the cabbage into 1-inch pieces.
Rinse the cabbage in cold water, washing and draining it three times to clean it thoroughly.
Pour the bottle of water into a large clean bowl.
Add the cabbage and chopped spring onions to the water.
Stir in the salt and mix well before letting the cabbage pickle.

title: Lao Naem Khao
instructions: Thinly slice the onion and shallots, then let them soak in an ice-water bath while you prep everything else.
Grate the coconut into a large bowl.
Add the cooked rice to the coconut and mix everything together with your hands.
Roll the mixture into 2-inch rice ball croquettes.
Heat the oil in a pot or pan for deep-frying.
Beat the eggs in a large bowl, dip each rice ball in the egg wash, then drop it into the hot oil.
Fry until the balls turn dark amber brown and crispy, then lift out onto paper towel to drain.
Once cooled, break the rice balls apart into a larger bowl.
Drain the soaked onion and shallots and add them in.
Tear the sour pork sausage into small pieces (or knife-chop if you prefer) and add to the bowl.
Mix in the pickled garlic, lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, cilantro, hot mint, and regular mint.
Top with roasted peanuts and serve alongside lettuce, dried chilli, and fresh vegetables.
Store leftovers chilled for up to 4 days; do not freeze.

ingredients.note: Assumed pre-cooked leftover jasmine rice per traditional naem khao prep; 3 cups ≈ 555 g, cooked state inferred not stated explicitly.
Whole coconut yields ~400 g grated flesh; weight inferred.
Amount for deep-frying not specified; assumed ~500 g as frying medium, most not retained in food.
2 cups estimated ~300 g; fermented sausage not cooked in this recipe.
2 cloves diced, estimated ~20 g.
Juice of 1 lime estimated at 30 g.

1 cup loosely packed leaves estimated at 16 g.
1/2 cup leaves estimated at 20 g.

title: Camaro Grelhado Com Molho Cru (Grilled Prawns with Green Onion Sauce)
instructions: Blend the garlic, spring onions, cumin, salt, vinegar, and water together into a rough paste for the sauce.
Thread the prawns onto skewers and brush generously with the sauce.
Grill the prawns for 3-4 minutes per side, until they turn opaque and lose their translucent color.
Serve hot with the remaining sauce on the side for dipping.

ingredients.note: 1/2 cup estimated as 50g

title: Paracuca (Roasted Peanuts)
instructions: Tip the peanuts into a large heavy saucepan with the sugar and water.
Bring to a boil and keep cooking, stirring now and then, until the syrup has evaporated, about 10 minutes.
Spread the sugared peanuts in a single layer on a greased 15x10x1-inch baking pan and sprinkle with the salt.
Bake at 300°F for 30-35 minutes, until dry and lightly browned.
Let cool completely, then store in an airtight container.

ingredients.note: 5 cups unsalted peanuts, converted at ~146g/cup
1 cup sugar, converted at ~200g/cup
1 cup water, converted at ~236g/cup
1/4 tsp salt, converted at ~6g/tsp

title: Antiguan Breakfast (Chop Up and ‘Saltfish’)
instructions: Peel the eggplant and pumpkin, then chop both into medium-sized pieces.
Melt the butter in a medium pot over medium-high heat, then add the onion and garlic and sauté for about 2 minutes until softened but not browned.
Add the pumpkin and eggplant and sauté for another 2 to 3 minutes.
Pour in just enough water to cover the vegetables, bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook until the vegetables are soft.
Stir in the chopped callaloo (spinach) and cook for a further 3 minutes.
Drain the vegetable mixture in a colander and let it sit to release excess liquid.
Return the drained vegetables to the pot and mash vigorously with a potato masher or thick whisk until well combined.
Season with salt and pepper, tasting and adjusting as needed.

ingredients.note: 4 medium eggplants; weight estimated at ~300g each, not stated in original
3 cloves estimated at ~3g each
1 diced onion; weight estimated at ~150g, not stated in original
butter remains in the pot as part of the mashed dish, not a frying/discard medium

title: Corn on the cob with lime, green chilli and coconut butter
instructions: Bring the vegetable stock to a rolling boil in a big, deep pan, then slip in the corn cobs and let them cook for about five minutes.
Turn off the heat and leave the cobs to sit in the hot stock as it cools, then drain them well.
Get a heavy dry frying pan blazing hot and char the drained cobs for a couple of minutes on each side, working in batches of two or three so the pan stays hot.
Warm the coconut milk, lime juice and green chilli together in a small saucepan, season lightly, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Stir the butter into the coconut milk mixture until melted, then take it off the heat and set aside.
For the topping, toast the desiccated coconut in a hot dry frying pan, tossing constantly until golden and burnished, watching closely since it burns fast.
Tip the toasted coconut into a bowl, let it cool, then mix in the shallots, coriander and parsley, and season to taste.
To serve, spoon the coconut milk sauce over each charred cob, scatter over the toasted coconut topping and lime zest, and tuck in.

ingredients.note: No stated yield; servings inferred as 6 (one cob per person) based on '6 Corn On The Cob'. Weight per cob estimated at ~350g.
Line covers zest and juice of 2 limes; weight estimated at ~66g per lime, combined under one 'lime' key since separate zest/juice keys not used together here.
Weight of '1 Green Chilli' inferred at ~8g, typical small chilli size.
'Pinch' inferred as ~1g.
'Pinch' inferred as ~0.5g; no exact 'black pepper' key exists, used generic 'pepper'.
Butter is stirred into the coconut milk sauce and served with the dish, so it remains in the food.
Toasted in a dry pan before serving as topping, counted as cooked.
Recipe references 'crispy shallots' as a pre-made component with no cooking method described here, so left uncooked/as-is.

title: Slow-cooked, Wadadli-spiced Cubano pork belly
instructions: Blitz all the marinade ingredients together until smooth.
Rub the marinade all over the pork belly, working it into every crevice.
Cover the dish and leave the meat to marinate overnight in the fridge.
The next day, heat the oven to 140°C (120°C fan).
Lift the pork out of the marinade, leaving most of it behind in the dish, and set the meat in a large roasting tin.
Cover tightly with foil and roast for four and a half hours, lifting the foil every hour or so to baste the meat with the pan juices, then re-covering.
Remove the foil, raise the oven to 180°C (160°C fan) and roast for a further 15-20 minutes until the top is richly coloured and the meat is soft enough to cut with a spoon.
For the crackling, heat a second batch of the oven to 200°C (180°C fan) and line a tray with baking paper.
Score the pork skin, rub with a few pinches of salt and, if you like, a scattering of caraway, cumin or fennel seeds, then cut the skin into long strips.
Lay the strips fat-side down on the tray, cover with another sheet of baking paper and a second tray of the same size, and roast for 45 minutes to an hour until crisp.
Cut or pull the pork apart, scatter over the chopped coriander and parsley, top with the crackling shards if made, and serve at once.

ingredients.note: 175ml converted at ~0.91g/ml (olive oil density); no pork weight given in ingredient list, so servings (8) inferred from marinade quantity for a whole belly joint - no yield stated in the recipe text
assumed fresh coriander leaves, ~3g/tbsp

assumed fresh oregano leaves, ~3g/tbsp

title: Mango chow
instructions: Toss the diced mango, spring onions, scotch bonnet and coriander together in a bowl.
Add the lime zest and juice, then season with salt and pepper to taste.
Serve alongside just about anything.

ingredients.note: '1/2 Mango' assumed ~300g whole mango, half used
'1 chopped Spring Onions' estimated at 15g for one stalk
'1 Scotch Bonnet' estimated at 5g for a small whole pepper
'2 tablespoons Coriander' estimated at ~3g per tablespoon of chopped fresh coriander

'To taste Pepper' estimated at a pinch, ~1g

title: Koulenje
instructions: Sift together the flour, baking powder, salt and sugar into a large bowl.
In a separate bowl, beat six eggs, then whisk in the melted butter and milk until blended.
Scatter a tablespoon of black seeds (sev dzandig) and all the ground mahleb over the dry ingredients.
Make a well in the center of the dry ingredients and pour in the wet mixture all at once, stirring with just a few strokes until it comes together.
Turn the dough onto a floured board, adding a little more flour if it feels sticky.
Knead gently a few times, then roll out to about half an inch thick.
Cut the dough into diamonds, circles, or long strips, and braid the strips into loaves if desired.
Prick each piece several times with a fork.
Beat the remaining egg with a little extra black seed and brush it over each piece as a glaze.
Arrange the pieces on a parchment-lined baking sheet.
Bake at 400°F for 12-15 minutes, until just golden.
Cool on a rack and serve warm, or reheat later wrapped in foil.

ingredients.note: 6 cups flour estimated at 120 g/cup
6 large eggs estimated at 50 g each
2 1/2 Tbs estimated at ~14 g/Tbs
1 cup melted butter, baked into dough, not a frying medium
1 Tbs salt estimated at ~18 g
1 cup milk estimated at 240 g
1 cup sugar estimated at 200 g

title: Stuffed Grape Leaves
instructions: Rinse the rice in cold water until the water runs clear.
Sauté the chopped onion in olive oil until soft and translucent.
Stir in the ground allspice and cinnamon, cooking until fragrant.
Add the rice, chicken stock, tomato sauce, raisins, dill, pine nuts, lemon juice, salt and pepper.
Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat and simmer for about 30 minutes, until the stock is fully absorbed and the rice is tender.
Serve warm as a side dish, or use as a filling for stuffed grape leaves (dolmas).

ingredients.note: 1 cup uncooked rice ≈185g, standard conversion
2 cups ≈480g (240g/cup)
1 cup ≈245g

title: Jajek
instructions: Toss the yogurt, cold water, crushed garlic and salt into the food processor and pulse for a few seconds until smooth.
Pour into a bowl and stir in the dried mint and cucumber.
Chill for a few hours before serving for the best flavor.
Drop in a few ice cubes just before serving for extra refreshment.

ingredients.note: 2 cups yogurt converted at ~245g/cup
1 cup water converted at ~240g/cup

title: Leg of Lamb Armenian-Style
instructions: Combine the lamb leg with the garlic, parsley, onion, black pepper, balsamic vinegar, olive oil, mint, and salt in a roasting pan.
Let everything marinate together for at least two days, stirring occasionally so the marinade coats the meat evenly.
When ready, bake at 350°F for 1 to 1½ hours, until the lamb is no longer pink when sliced.
Alternatively, skewer the marinated lamb with vegetables and grill instead of baking.
The dish freezes well if you want to make it ahead.

ingredients.note: no weight given for '1 Lamb Leg'; estimated typical whole leg of lamb ~2kg

title: Bolita di Keshi
instructions: Grate the cheddar and feta finely and toss them together in a bowl.
Stir in the beaten eggs and cornstarch until the mixture holds together.
Shape the mixture into small balls; these can be frozen for later use.
Drop the balls into deep, hot oil and fry until golden brown.
Serve piping hot on toothpicks.

ingredients.note: 1 lb converted to grams

title: Arepa
instructions: Tip the corn flour into a bowl.
Season with the salt, then work in the warm water a little at a time until you've got a smooth, workable dough.
Let the dough rest for five minutes so it firms up.
Divide and shape into rounds about 3 inches across and 1/2 inch thick.
Sear the arepas in a lightly greased skillet over low heat until a golden crust forms on both sides.
Transfer to a casserole dish and bake at 350°F (175°C) for about 20 minutes, until they sound hollow when tapped.
For extra flavour, swap the water for chicken or beef broth.

ingredients.note: 1 tsp salt converted to grams using standard density (~6g/tsp); inferred conversion.
1 cup corn flour converted to grams (~155g/cup); inferred conversion, arepas traditionally use masarepa but recipe specifies plain corn flour.

title: Balchi di Pisca
instructions: Soak the salted cod in water for twenty-four hours, then discard the soaking water.
Place the cod in a saucepan, cover with fresh water, and simmer gently until it flakes easily when tested with a fork.
Strain the cod, reserving a little of the cooking broth, then debone it and set aside.
Boil the diced potatoes in water until tender, then drain well.
Mash the drained potatoes together with the flaked cod until thoroughly combined.
In a blender, combine the chopped tomato, green pepper, onion, garlic, tabasco sauce, nutmeg, salt and pepper.
Blend briefly into a sauce and pour it over the mashed fish mixture.
Mix everything together well, then stir in the beaten egg.
If the mixture is too dry to hold together, work in a little of the reserved fish broth, a tablespoon at a time.
Shape the mixture into balls about one and a half inches in diameter.
Deep-fry the balls in hot fat until golden brown.

ingredients.note: Weight of dried salt cod not given; estimated ~450g (typical 1 lb piece). No stated yield for the recipe; servings estimated at 6 based on total mixture mass (~1.25kg) shaped into ~30g balls, several per serving.
3 medium potatoes estimated at ~150g each; exact weight not given.

title: Banana den Forno
instructions: Peel the bananas and simmer them in a pot with the cinnamon, vanilla extract, sugar and water for about 20 minutes, until softened.
Transfer the bananas to an oven dish and bake at 275°F (about 135°C) for 10 minutes.
Serve warm with your favorite ice cream.

title: Cheese Sticks
instructions: Slice the mozzarella into long strips and chill them in the fridge so they stay firm.
Mix the flour, butter and egg in a bowl, then work in the sugar, salt and olive oil to taste.
Gradually add the milk, kneading until you have a smooth, workable dough.
Roll the dough out and cut it into strips about 2-3 cm wide.
Wrap each dough strip around a cheese strip, rolling into a spiral so the cheese is fully enclosed.
Dust the wrapped sticks lightly with flour to stop them sticking together.
Heat oil in a frying pan and fry the cheese sticks until golden all over.

title: Satee
instructions: Blend the chopped onion, garlic, lemon juice, sugar, soy sauce, salt, and ground coriander (swap in ground ginger if you're using pork) until smooth.
With the blender still running, add the first cup of oil to finish the marinade.
Pour the marinade over the cubed beef or pork tenderloin and turn to coat well.
Marinate for only a few hours in the fridge — do not leave it overnight.
Thread the marinated meat onto 36 skewers.
Whisk together the second cup of oil and the remaining soy sauce in a shallow dish to make a seasoned basting oil.
Roll each loaded skewer through the seasoned oil just before cooking to keep the meat from drying out.
Fry the skewers, or better yet grill them over a barbecue, until cooked through.

ingredients.note: Recipe states a weight range '4-5 lbs. of beef or pork tenderloin'; used low end (4 lb ≈ 1814 g) for calorie-safe estimate. Text also states '36 skewers' as total yield; assumed ~4 skewers per serving to infer 9 servings (not explicitly stated).
Second oil batch is a seasoned coating brushed onto skewers before frying/grilling; assumed it stays on the meat and is eaten rather than being a discarded frying medium.

title: Tirolean Dumplings
instructions: Finely chop the shallots and cut the smoked ham into small cubes.
Melt the butter in a pan and sweat the shallots until translucent.
Add the ham and parsley and fry briefly, then remove from the heat.
Place the bread in a bowl.
Beat the milk with the eggs, season with salt, pepper and nutmeg, and pour over the bread.
Stir in the ham and shallot mixture, then mix in the flour to form a firm dough, adding more bread or flour if needed.
Smooth the dough's surface and let it rest for about 30 minutes.
Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil.
Shape the dough into small or medium dumplings, wetting your hands with cold water so they stay smooth.
Drop the dumplings into the boiling water, bring back to the boil, then reduce the heat and simmer gently for 8-20 minutes depending on size.
Remove the dumplings from the water, dry them well, and serve garnished with chopped parsley and nut-brown melted butter.

ingredients.note: 200ml converted at ~1g/ml
3 eggs estimated at 50g each
2 shallots estimated at 30g each
"knob" of butter estimated as 15g; remains in mixture, not a frying medium
2 tbsp chopped parsley estimated at ~4g/tbsp
2 tbsp flour estimated at ~8g/tbsp
"to taste" quantity inferred as 2g
"to taste" quantity inferred as 1g
"pinch" quantity inferred as 0.5g
"garnish" quantity of melted nut-brown butter inferred as 20g; remains on the served dish, not a frying medium
"garnish" quantity of chopped parsley inferred as 5g

title: Kaspressknödel - Cheese Dumplings
instructions: Cube the white bread, mix it with the milk, season with a pinch of salt, and let the mixture rest so the bread soaks up the milk.
Stir in the shredded potatoes, grated cheese, eggs, and chopped parsley.
Melt the butter in a pan and fry the diced onion until golden brown, then fold it into the bread mixture.
Add the flour, knead gently into a firm dough, shape into round dumplings, press them flat, and pan-fry in butter on both sides until golden.
Serve the dumplings hot in a bowl of clear broth.

ingredients.note: 2 eggs, inferred at ~50g each
'pinch' inferred as ~1g
'pinch' inferred as ~1g
3 medium potatoes inferred at ~150g each
'1 small onion' inferred at ~70g
'for frying' amount not given, inferred 30g; butter stays on the dumplings and served with the dish, so not treated as a discarded frying medium

title: Clear Soup with Semolina Dumplings
instructions: Let the butter soften to room temperature, then beat it in a bowl until creamy.
Season the butter with a pinch of salt and a delicate pinch of nutmeg.
Beat in the egg until well combined.
Sprinkle in the semolina and mix until the mixture is smooth.
Cover the bowl with cling film and let the dough rest for 15–20 minutes.
Using two spoons dipped in hot water, shape the dough into small dumplings.
Lay the dumplings on a lightly oiled board or plate and let them rest briefly so they puff up a little more.
Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil.
Add the dumplings, return to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer gently for 10–15 minutes without letting it boil hard, turning the dumplings occasionally.
Lift the cooked dumplings out carefully and place them into the hot beef stock.
Serve immediately, garnished with a sprinkle of chopped parsley or chives.

Note: the dumplings can also be simmered directly in the beef stock, though this makes the broth look slightly cloudy; don't let cooked dumplings sit too long in the soup or they'll soak it up and turn soft.

ingredients.note: assumed standard egg weight ~50g; not specified in text
'pinch' inferred as ~1g
'pinch' inferred as ~0.3g
'splash' used only to lightly oil the shaping board, not eaten; grams inferred
garnish amount inferred, not specified in text

title: Tafelspitz
instructions: Halve the unpeeled onion widthways and dry-fry the cut sides in a hot pan with no fat until they're well browned.
Pour about 3 litres of water into a large saucepan, then add the carrots, celeriac, parsnips, leek, browned onion halves, bay leaves and peppercorns and bring to a boil.
Add the washed beef brisket and any bones to the pot and simmer gently for about 2.5 to 3 hours until the meat is tender, topping up with water as needed and skimming off any foam that rises.
Wait until the broth has simmered for a good 2 hours before seasoning well with salt.
Once the meat is tender, lift it out and keep it warm in a little of the broth, then taste the remaining soup and season again with salt, straining it if you like.

Slice the boiled beef on the bias and arrange it on warmed plates, or serve it straight in the hot broth from a tureen, alongside semolina dumplings or sliced pancake strips and freshly chopped chives as a starter.

Serve the beef with roast potatoes, a bread-and-horseradish mix, green beans in dill sauce, or creamed spinach with chive sauce, cooking a portion of the root vegetables separately if you want them served al dente.

ingredients.note: amount inferred: '1 bunch' has no stated weight, estimated typical bunch mass
amount inferred: '1 Celeriac' has no stated weight, estimated typical whole bulb mass
amount inferred: '2 Parsnips' has no stated weight, estimated ~100g each
amount inferred: 'Sprigs of fresh Parsley' has no stated weight, estimated small garnish quantity
amount inferred: '1/2 Leek' estimated as half of a typical ~200g leek
amount inferred: '1 large Onion' has no stated weight, estimated typical large onion mass
amount inferred: '2 Bay Leaf' negligible mass estimated
amount inferred: '5 Peppercorns' negligible mass estimated
amount inferred: 'Pinch Salt' negligible mass estimated

title: Tendir Choreyi (Tandoori Bread)
instructions: Note: this bread is designed for a clay tandoor oven, but here we bake it in a regular oven for a home-friendly version.
If using a bread machine, add the yeast to the yeast dispenser, then add the warm water, flour and salt to the bucket, and run the dough cycle.
If doing it by hand, mix the yeast with the warm water and let stand a few minutes until foamy.
Sift the flour with the salt into a large bowl.
Gradually add the yeast and water mixture, mixing by hand until the dough comes together into a ball.
Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 8 to 10 minutes, until smooth.
Place the dough in a large bowl, cover, and let rise in a warm spot until doubled, about 1 to 1 1/2 hours.
Punch down the dough and turn it out onto a lightly floured surface.
Stretch the dough into an oblong shape, then roll with a rolling pin until about 1/2 inch thick.
Transfer to a greased or non-stick baking sheet and let stand for 15 minutes.
Cut crosshatches into the top of the dough, about four in each direction.
Brush the top with beaten egg yolk (thinned with a little water if needed) and sprinkle with poppy seeds.
Bake in a preheated oven at about 400°F for 20 to 25 minutes, until golden on top.

ingredients.note: 2 1/2 tsp yeast converted at ~3.1 g/tsp
1 1/2 cups converted at ~237 g/cup
1 tsp salt ≈ 6 g
3 cups converted at ~120 g/cup
amount for brushing not specified; estimated as one egg yolk (~18 g)
1 tsp poppy seeds ≈ 3 g

title: Kelem dolmasi
instructions: Boil the rice for eight minutes, then drain and set aside.
Peel and finely chop the onion, then chop the cilantro and dill.
Roughly chop or mash the canned chickpeas so they blend into the filling.
Mince the lamb and the lamb fat together.
In a bowl, combine the minced lamb, lamb fat, chopped onion, turmeric, cinnamon, chickpeas, boiled rice and chopped herbs; season with salt and pepper and mix thoroughly by hand.
Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil, pull off the outer cabbage leaves and reserve them, then boil the whole cabbage head for 3-4 minutes to loosen the leaves.
Lift the cabbage out, let it cool briefly, then peel off the leaves, returning the head to the boiling water as needed until all usable leaves are freed.
Trim the tough stalks from the leaves, reserving the trimmings, and cut the largest leaves in half.
Spoon a heaping tablespoon of filling onto each leaf, fold the ends over, and roll tightly into a small cylinder.
Line the base of a stockpot with the reserved cabbage trimmings and stalks, then arrange the rolled dolma on top.
Add water up to the top of the cabbage bed without covering the dolma, then weight the rolls down with a heatproof plate or pan.
Bring the water to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook for 25 minutes.
Meanwhile, make a simple sauce by heating the tomato puree with water for a few minutes.
Pour the tomato sauce over the dolma and continue cooking for a further 5 minutes.

ingredients.note: No weight given for '1 large cabbage'; inferred ~1000g typical large head. No stated yield for the recipe; servings inferred at 4 based on total filling mass (~950g) and typical dolma roll size (~40g each), giving ~20-24 rolls at 5-6 per serving.

title: Grits
instructions: Bring the water to a boil with the salt stirred in.
Add the grits, then reduce the heat and cover the pot.
Simmer for about 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the grits reach your desired consistency.
Let cool slightly, then serve.

ingredients.note: 1/4 cup grits estimated at 40g
1 tsp salt estimated at 6g
1 cup water estimated at 240g

title: Bangladeshi fish curry with daikon
instructions: If using rohu fish, soak it in cold water for 30-45 minutes to defrost, then descale, cut each steak into quarters, and rinse in cold saltwater a few times before draining; if using an alternative fish like cod, cut into 2½‑inch chunks, rinse and drain.
Top and tail the daikon, peel it, halve it widthways, then quarter it lengthways and slice into 2cm‑thick half‑moons; rinse and set aside.
Heat the oil in a large shallow saucepan over medium heat, add the crushed garlic and stir for a minute until pale golden.
Add the onion paste and salt, cooking for 3‑5 minutes until the onion softens and turns translucent, stirring occasionally.
Pour in 200ml water, cover and cook for 10 minutes, stirring once or twice.
Stir in the ground spices and cook for a couple of minutes to toast them, then cover and cook for 5 minutes on low heat before adding the daikon.
After a minute, pour in another 200ml water, cover and cook for 10 minutes on medium heat.
Add the fish pieces, gently mixing so they're coated in the spice mix, then shake the pan, cover and cook for 5 minutes on medium heat without stirring.
Boil some water, then after the 5 minutes carefully pour 500ml hot water down the sides of the pan to avoid pouring directly onto the fish.
Give the pan a gentle shake, cover and simmer on high heat for 15 minutes.
Stir in the chopped coriander and chillies, cook for another 5 minutes, then turn off the heat.
Serve immediately with rice and lime wedges.

ingredients.note: 4 tbsp converted to grams (~55g); oil is sizzled with aromatics in the same pot that becomes the curry sauce, so it stays in the served dish.

title: Handesh – Bangladeshi rice flour and date molasses cakes
instructions: Whisk the date molasses together with the water in a large bowl until fully combined.
Add the rice flour, flour, cinnamon and cardamom, then whisk for 3-5 minutes until you get a smooth, lump-free batter - lumps stop the cakes rising and make them split.
Cover the batter and let it rest for 2 hours.
After resting, whisk the batter thoroughly again since the rice flour will have settled to the bottom; it should be thick but pourable, forming ribbons off the whisk.
Heat plenty of oil in a cast-iron kadai or wok until hot, then drop in a teaspoon of batter - if it rises slowly, the oil is ready; turn heat to low-medium.
Pour a steady stream of batter (about 45ml) into the centre of the oil to form one cake at a time.
Fry each cake about 45 seconds until golden underneath, flip and fry a further 30-45 seconds until golden on the other side, frying only one at a time.
If a cake splits in the oil, whisk 2 tablespoons of extra flour into the batter and try again - aim for cakes about the size of a digestive biscuit.
Lift the cakes out with a slotted spoon and rest them on kitchen paper, frilled edge down, to hold their shape while you fry the rest.
Serve hot and fresh, or cool and store airtight in the fridge for up to a week, reheating gently in the oven or on a dry pan before serving.

ingredients.note: Ingredient list states 400ml water but the method text suggests only 300-325ml may be needed depending on molasses consistency; used the stated ingredient amount.
1/4 tsp converted to grams using standard spice density (~1.2g/tsp).
1/4 tsp converted to grams using standard spice density (~1.2g/tsp).

title: Bengali Chicken Curry with Potatoes
instructions: Heat the olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat.
Cook the onions in the hot oil, stirring, until translucent, about 5 minutes.
Stir in the ginger-garlic paste and cook for another 5 minutes.
Reduce the heat to medium and stir in the tomatoes; cook until they turn pulpy, 5 to 10 minutes.
Season with the cayenne pepper, curry powder, garam masala, turmeric, and cumin; cook and stir for another 5 minutes.
Add the chicken and potatoes to the skillet and simmer, stirring occasionally, until the potatoes are tender and the chicken is no longer pink in the center, about 20 minutes.
Sprinkle the cilantro over the curry and simmer for a further 10 minutes.
Serve hot.

ingredients.note: 2 tablespoons olive oil ≈27g; stays in the sauce, not a frying medium
2 diced onions estimated at ~110g each
1 tablespoon ≈15g
2 diced tomatoes estimated at ~123g each
1 tsp ≈2g

title: Breadfruit in Butter Sauce Recipe
instructions: Peel the breadfruit, halve it, and scoop out the seeds from the core.
Boil the breadfruit in salted water with a little onion and olive oil over high heat until tender, then drain and let it cool.
Meanwhile, melt the butter in a frying pan and lightly brown the onion rings.
Stir in the garlic, tomatoes, parsley, lime juice, salt, and pepper, and sauté for a couple of minutes.
Pour in the water, bring to a boil, and simmer for 5–10 minutes to build the butter sauce.
Slice the cooled breadfruit and arrange it in a serving dish.
Pour the butter sauce over the sliced breadfruit.
Garnish with chopped fresh parsley and serve.

ingredients.note: Half of a 'medium breadfruit' with no stated weight; inferred ~500g assuming a ~1kg whole fruit.
Half of a 'small onion' with no stated weight; inferred ~35g.

title: Chicken Liver Pate Recipe
instructions: Trim the chicken livers, removing any sinews and gall.
Roughly chop the bacon, garlic, and onion, then place them in a fireproof casserole with the livers.
Season with salt and pepper, then pour in enough milk to just cover everything.
Cover the casserole and cook in a slow oven at 300°F for 2 hours.
Blitz the cooked mixture in a blender until smooth.
Taste and adjust the seasoning as needed.
Stir in the sherry (or brandy).
Spoon the pâté into a dish and seal the top with melted butter.

ingredients.note: assumed ~5g for 1 clove; weight not stated

title: Bajan Salt Bread Recipe
instructions: Sift half the flour into a large bowl, stir in the salt and yeast, then slowly work in warm water until a stiff dough forms.
Sift in the rest of the flour a bit at a time until the dough is firm enough to knead.
Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Place the dough in a greased bowl and set it somewhere warm for 40-50 minutes until doubled — poke a finger in, and if the dent stays, it's ready.
Preheat the oven to 220°C (425°F).
Divide the dough into 12 equal portions, roll each into a fist-sized ball, and place on a greased baking sheet.
Leave the shaped rolls in a warm spot for another 40 minutes, or until doubled again.
Bake in the centre of the oven for 25-30 minutes, until golden brown and the rolls sound hollow when tapped.

ingredients.note: '2 tubs' is an unclear unit for yeast; treated as roughly 2 tablespoons active dry yeast (~9g each)
mixed into the dough and baked, so it stays in the food; not a frying medium

title: Barbados Pepperpot
instructions: Rinse the stewing steak and let it sit in lime or lemon juice while you prep everything else.
Thinly slice the garlic and the onion.
Chop the basil and thyme.
Carefully slice the scotch bonnet peppers into fine pieces, using a knife without touching the flesh or wearing gloves, then wash your hands well and avoid your eyes.
Rinse the lime or lemon juice off the steak and cut the meat into cubes.
Chop the pig's trotters and the oxtail into cubes.
Bring a large pot of water to a boil.
Add the pig's trotters and boil.
Drain the excess water, then add the steak and cover with fresh hot water.
Stir in the chopped garlic, onion, and scotch bonnet.
Add the cinnamon stick, cloves, basil, thyme, a pinch of salt, and a pinch of sugar to taste.
Simmer until the meat is tender, reheating the whole pot to a full boil each time you serve it over the following days for best flavor.

title: Breadfruit in Butter Sauce
instructions: Cut the breadfruit, scoop out the seeds from the core, and peel it.
Boil the breadfruit in salted water with a little onion and a splash of olive oil over high heat until tender, then drain and let it cool.
Meanwhile, melt the butter in a frying pan and lightly brown the sliced onions.
Stir in the garlic, tinned tomatoes, parsley, lime juice, salt, and pepper, and sauté for a couple of minutes.
Pour in the water, bring to a boil, then simmer for 5-10 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly.
Slice the cooled breadfruit and arrange it in a serving dish.
Pour the hot butter sauce over the breadfruit.
Garnish with chopped fresh parsley before serving.

ingredients.note: whole breadfruit ~1kg; half inferred as ~500g

title: Chicken and Potato Roti
instructions: Bring a pint of chicken stock to a simmer, then melt three tablespoons of sugar in a large saucepan over high heat until it caramelizes.
Add the cubed chicken breasts and stir until browned all over.
Stir in the chopped onions, garlic, parsley, thyme, jerk seasoning, and salt, then drop in the whole scotch bonnet pepper without cutting it.
Lower the heat and let everything simmer gently for 25 minutes.
Sift the flour into a large bowl and add a pinch of salt.
Rub in the shortening a tablespoon at a time, working it into the flour with your fingers until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs.
Gradually add small amounts of water, mixing with a butter knife, until a soft dough forms.
Dust the surface with flour and roll the dough into wafer-thin rounds, enough for at least 12 roti skins.
Cover the rolled skins with a tea towel while you return to the pot.
Peel and cube the potatoes into small bite-size pieces.
Once the stew has simmered for 25 minutes, stir in half a pint of water along with the potatoes.
Cover and cook on low heat for a further 30 minutes.
Heat an upturned flat baking tray lightly covered in foil on the stovetop and cook each roti skin for a minute on each side until browned but still soft.
Stack the cooked roti skins, then remove the pot from the heat and discard the whole scotch bonnet.
Bring the pot to the table with a stack of plates, letting everyone lay a roti skin flat, spoon filling into the centre, fold it up, and enjoy.

title: Bajan Sweet Bread
instructions: Combine the flour, baking powder, and salt in a large mixing bowl.
Melt the butter in a saucepan.

In a separate bowl, mix the coconut with most of the sugar, reserving a little for the topping, then stir in the raisins, egg, milk, almond essence, vanilla essence, and melted butter until you have a smooth, creamy paste.
Fold this mixture into the flour bowl and mix until a stiff dough forms.
Divide the dough between the baking tins.
Boil the water.
Stir two tablespoons of sugar into some hot water and brush the top of each loaf.
Bake in the center of the oven for 1 hour.
Leave the loaves to cool in their tins before slicing and serving with a fresh pot of tea.

title: Macaroni Pie
instructions: Bring a pot of salted water to a boil and add the broken macaroni; cook uncovered until just tender, about 8 minutes.
Preheat your oven to 180C (350F, Gas Mark 4).
Drain the macaroni well, return it to the hot pot, and stir in the butter.
Grate the cheese and mix it into the warm macaroni until it melts through.
Whisk the egg until fluffy, then whisk in the milk, ground onion, pepper, salt, pepper sauce, and mustard.
Pour the egg mixture over the macaroni and stir to combine.
Transfer everything to a greased ovenproof casserole dish.
Top with the remaining butter, a scattering of grated cheese, and the breadcrumbs.
Bake in the center of the oven for 30-45 minutes, depending on the depth of your dish, until set and golden.

title: Cinnamon Roll Cookies
instructions: Beat the butter and caster sugar together until creamy, then work in the egg and a pinch of salt.
Stir in the sifted flour until you get a smooth, even dough.
Wrap the dough in plastic and chill it in the fridge for 15 minutes to firm up.
While it chills, mix the brown sugar with the ground cinnamon.
Turn the dough onto a floured surface, knead briefly, then roll it into a rectangle about 5mm thick, adding extra flour if it sticks.
Sprinkle the cinnamon sugar evenly over the dough.
Roll the dough up tightly into a log, chilling briefly again if it gets too soft, then wrap it in plastic.
Chill the log in the fridge for 30 minutes until firm, then unwrap and slice it into even rounds.
Arrange the slices on a lined baking tray and bake at 180°C (356°F) for 14 to 16 minutes.
Let the cookies cool completely before eating or storing them.

ingredients.note: '1 medium egg' converted to grams using standard medium-egg weight (~50g).
'Pinch' estimated as ~1g.
'2 tsp' converted to grams using standard spoon density (~2.5g/tsp).

title: Belgian Meatballs in Liège Syrup Sauce
instructions: Combine the minced beef and pork with the chopped onion, garlic, egg, breadcrumbs, 1 tablespoon of mustard, parsley, salt, and pepper.
Shape the mixture into meatballs, sized to your preference — traditional Liège style makes one or two large boulets, but smaller ones brown faster for a weeknight meal.
Heat the oil (or butter) in a large pan and brown the meatballs on all sides until well crusted.
Remove the meatballs from the pan and set them aside.
In the same pan, melt the butter and add the sliced onions, cooking until soft and lightly caramelised.
Sprinkle in the brown sugar and let it melt into the onions.
Pour in the white wine vinegar, scraping up the browned bits from the bottom of the pan.
Stir in the remaining mustard and the sirop de Liège.
Pour in the beef stock, plus the beer if using.
Add the bay leaf and stir until the syrup dissolves and the sauce turns glossy.
Return the meatballs to the pan, cover, and simmer gently for 25–30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens naturally.
Taste and adjust — more syrup for sweetness, more vinegar for tang, more mustard for depth — until the sauce is sweet-sour, rich, and velvety.
Serve hot with fries or mashed potatoes.

1 clove, estimated ~5g
1 egg, estimated ~50g
4 tbsp, estimated ~10g per tbsp

title: Flemish Hutsepot
instructions: Peel and finely chop the onion and garlic.
Melt the butter in a large pan over medium heat and add the onion and garlic.
Sweat the onion and garlic for about 5 minutes until soft.
Peel and chop the potatoes, then stir them into the pan with the bay leaves and thyme.
Cook for a further 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Peel and slice the carrots.
Peel and dice the turnip into bite-size pieces.
Slice the leek in half lengthwise and rinse thoroughly to remove any sand, then chop.
Add the carrots, turnip and leek to the pan.
Stir in the mustard and chicken stock, then season with salt and pepper.
Cover the pan and simmer for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring regularly, until the vegetables and potatoes are tender, adding a splash of water if it looks dry.
Meanwhile, pan-fry the sausages in a little oil or butter until golden brown and cooked through, then keep them warm.
Once the potatoes begin to fall apart, stir well to break everything into a mushy, chunky stew.
Taste and adjust the seasoning with extra pepper, salt or mustard as needed.
Spoon the hutsepot into deep plates, top with the sausages, and serve hot with an extra dollop of mustard if you like.

ingredients.note: 4 sausages, weight inferred at ~75g each (typical pork sausage) since no gram amount given; servings also inferred as 4 based on one sausage per plate.

title: Frog Legs Recipe in Garlic Butter
instructions: If you like, soak the frog legs in milk in the fridge for about an hour to mellow their flavour.
Rinse the legs well and pat them completely dry with kitchen paper.
Warm the olive oil and half the garlic butter in a large wok or frying pan over high heat until the butter foams — watch it closely so it doesn't burn.
Lay the frog legs in the hot pan and fry for about 10 minutes, turning regularly, until golden brown on both sides.
Stir in the remaining garlic butter and the chopped parsley right at the end, then season with a pinch of salt and pepper to taste.
Divide the frog legs between plates and serve with grilled bread and a green salad.

ingredients.note: used only as an optional soaking liquid, rinsed off and discarded before cooking; not part of the served dish
garlic butter and oil are folded back in and served with the legs, so counted as eaten, not a frying medium

title: Belgian Waterzooi Chicken
instructions: Place the chicken legs, chicken breast, water and one garlic clove in a large saucepan.
Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, then reduce to low, cover and simmer for 10 minutes.
Remove the chicken from the pan and set aside, keeping the poaching stock for later.
Let the chicken cool, then dice the breast and pick the meat off the leg, discarding bones and skin.
Finely slice the leek, celery and carrot into julienne strips.
Melt the butter with the rosemary and bay leaves in a large pan over medium heat.
Add the sliced vegetables and season with salt and pepper.
Stir and cook the vegetables for 3 minutes.
Add the shredded and diced chicken to the pan.
Cover and cook for 5 minutes.
Stir in the cream and 120ml of the reserved chicken stock.
Cover and simmer for 20 minutes, until the potatoes are tender.
Whisk the egg yolk with 2 tablespoons of the warm cream from the pan in a cup.
Stir this mixture back into the pan to thicken the sauce.
Turn the heat to very low and check the seasoning, adding more salt or pepper if needed.
Remove the rosemary sprig and bay leaves.
Divide the stew among deep plates and drizzle with the remaining cream sauce.
Serve hot.

title: Liegeoise Salad
instructions: Scrub the potatoes without peeling them, then cut into bite-size pieces.
Boil the potatoes in salted water until tender, then lift out with a slotted spoon and let them cool to room temperature.
Trim and clean the green beans, then boil them in the same water until just tender but still crunchy.
Drain the beans and plunge them into cold water to stop the cooking.
Fry the bacon in a large non-stick pan over medium heat until cooked through, then remove from the heat and let it cool slightly.
Whisk together the olive oil, vinegar and mustard to make a vinaigrette.
Toss the potatoes, green beans, bacon, chopped onion, parsley and vinaigrette together, then season with salt and pepper.
Stir well, taste, and adjust with extra vinegar, salt or pepper as needed.
Transfer to a serving bowl or two deep plates, garnish with the boiled eggs, and serve lukewarm.

ingredients.note: added as boiled eggs per instructions; weight inferred at ~50g per egg
2 tbsp chopped onion inferred at ~10g/tbsp
3 tbsp inferred at 15g/tbsp; oil stays in the dressing, not a frying medium

title: Belgian Stoemp
instructions: Boil the chopped potatoes and garlic in salted water until tender.
Meanwhile, rinse, drain, and finely slice the savoy cabbage.
Melt a tablespoon of butter in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat and add the cabbage, seasoning with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Stir-fry the cabbage for 15-20 minutes until soft and tender, then remove from the heat.
In another non-stick pan, fry the sausages in a tablespoon of butter or oil until golden and cooked through, then set aside covered to keep warm.
In the same pan, add the diced onion and a little more butter, and cook gently over medium heat for 10 minutes until softened.
Pour in the beer, turn up the heat, and let it reduce into a beer-onion sauce, thickening with a touch of flour if you like.
Return the sausages to the pan and cover to keep warm.
Drain the cooked potatoes and garlic, then return them to the pot with the fried cabbage and chopped parsley.
Season with salt, pepper, and nutmeg, then add the milk and remaining butter.
Mash everything together, adjusting with more butter or milk and seasoning to taste.
Serve the cabbage mash topped with the sausages and drizzled with the beer-onion sauce.

title: Beef pumpkin Stew
instructions: Cut the beef into 1-inch chunks and dredge it in the flour until fully coated.
Melt the butter in a large soup pot over medium-high heat.
Brown the beef in small batches on all sides, removing each batch to a bowl as it browns.
Add the onion and garlic to the pot and sauté for a minute, scraping up the browned bits from the bottom.
Return the browned beef to the pot along with the beef stock, pumpkin puree, Worcestershire sauce, salt and pepper; stir well and bring to a boil.
Reduce the heat to medium-low and simmer for 3 hours, stirring occasionally.
Add the potatoes, carrots and thyme sprigs, raise the heat to medium and cook for a further 30 minutes.
Season with salt to taste and serve.

title: Seswaa
instructions: Heat the oven to 160°C.
Cut the beef into large chunks and brown it in a cast-iron or other oven-safe pot on the stove.
Add the whole peeled onion, salt, cracked black pepper, water and bay leaves.
Bring everything to the boil, then cover and transfer to the oven for 4 hours.
After 4 hours, pull the pot out and set it back on the stove to boil off most of the remaining liquid.
Pound and mash the meat with a wooden spoon until it falls apart into shreds, browning it a little more if you like.
Check the seasoning and serve with polenta, pap or sadza, alongside some green vegetables.

ingredients.note: 1 whole onion, weight estimated
3 bay leaves, weight estimated (negligible mass)

'sprinkling' - no amount given, estimated a small dash

title: Phaphatha Flatbreads
instructions: Whisk together the flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar in a stand mixer bowl.
With the dough hook running on low, drizzle in the milk.
Keep kneading until the dough comes together into a smooth ball.
Turn the dough out, lightly oil the bowl, return the dough, cover it, and let it rest for 15-30 minutes.
Divide the rested dough into 12 roughly even portions.
Heat a little oil in a large flat skillet, flatten each portion into a thick round about 10cm across with your palm, and lay them in the hot skillet, fitting as many as will fit.
Cook for 3-4 minutes until the underside is browned in spots, then flip and cook 1-2 minutes more on the second side.

ingredients.note: 1 tablespoon converted to grams (~14g) using standard baking-powder density
1 1/2 tsp converted to grams (~9g) using standard salt density
2 tsp converted to grams (~8g) using standard sugar density
1 cup converted to ~240g assuming standard US cup volume

title: Madombi: Dumplings from Botswana
instructions: Get a stew going in a lidded, oven-proof pot — you'll steam the dumplings on top of it during its last 30 minutes of cooking, at a gentle 180°C/350°F, give or take.
In a measuring cup, stir together the warm water, sugar, and yeast; let it sit until foamy, about 5 minutes.
In a stand mixer bowl (or a large bowl if kneading by hand), combine the flour and salt.
Stream in the yeast mixture while kneading.
Keep kneading for several minutes until the dough comes together into a smooth ball.
Place the dough in an oiled bowl, cover, and let it rise until doubled, about 1 hour.
Divide the risen dough into 10 equal portions for large dumplings, or 12-14 for smaller ones.
Shape each portion into a smooth, round ball.
In the last 30 minutes of the stew's cooking time, set the shaped dumplings directly on top of the stew and press them down gently.
Cover tightly and return to the oven for 25-30 minutes, until the dumplings are risen and light golden.

ingredients.note: 1 1/4 cup warm water converted using ~237 g/cup
2 tsp converted using ~4 g/tsp
2 1/2 tsp converted using ~3 g/tsp for dry yeast
3 cups converted using ~125 g/cup
1 1/2 tsp converted using ~6 g/tsp

title: Curried Spinach from Botswana
instructions: Warm the oil in a skillet over medium heat.
Toss in the diced onion and cook until lightly browned.
Stir in the curry powder and a pinch of salt, toasting for a few seconds until fragrant.
Add the chopped tomatoes and water, stir well, and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes until the tomatoes break down.
Fold in the chopped spinach and cook for a further 7-10 minutes, until soft and wilted.

ingredients.note: 1 bunch spinach estimated at 300g; no weight given
2 medium tomatoes estimated at ~125g each
1 small onion estimated at 70g

title: Magwinya: Doughnut Bites from Botswana
instructions: Combine the warm water, sugar, and yeast in a stand mixer bowl and let it sit until foamy, about 5 minutes.
Add the flour, salt, and vegetable oil to the yeast mixture.
Knead with the dough hook until a smooth dough forms, roughly 5 minutes.
Shape the dough into a ball, lightly oil the bowl, return the dough, cover, and let it rise in a warm spot for 1 to 1½ hours until doubled.
Heat about 5cm of vegetable oil in a pot to 180°C / 350°F.
Scoop portions of dough into rough balls and drop them into the hot oil, working in batches without overcrowding.
Fry each doughnut about a minute per side, turning halfway, until golden.
Drain the doughnuts on a rack or paper towels.
Once slightly cooled, dust the doughnuts with powdered sugar.

title: Brazilian chocolate truffles - brigadeiro
instructions: Melt the butter with the condensed milk, a pinch of salt, and the cocoa powder in a medium heavy-based saucepan over heat, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon until it comes to a boil.
Turn the heat down to medium-low and keep stirring for 10-15 minutes, until the mixture thickens, turns glossy, and starts pulling away from the bottom of the pan.
Stir in the vanilla extract and mix vigorously to combine.
Pour the mixture onto a buttered plate and chill for at least 2 hours.
Butter your hands and pinch off pieces of the chilled dough, rolling them into 2.5cm balls.
Roll each ball in your chosen topping, then set them in mini paper cases or on a tray lined with non-stick baking paper.

title: Brazilian cheese bread (pão de queijo)
instructions: Heat the oven to 220C/200C fan/gas 7 and grease a baking sheet with oil or butter.
Bring the milk, butter and salt to a boil in a medium saucepan.
Take the pan off the heat, stir in the tapioca flour vigorously, then let the dough cool a little.
Transfer the dough to a stand mixer (or beat by hand with a wooden spoon) until it cools.
Beat in the eggs one at a time, waiting for each to be fully incorporated before adding the next.
Once the dough is glossy, mix in the parmesan and beat until combined.
Spoon the dough onto the baking sheet in 1 tbsp balls, spacing them 5cm apart.
Bake for 30 minutes until crisp and golden, then serve while still warm.

title: Coconut quindim
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4.
Rub four 175ml ramekins or dariole moulds generously with soft butter, then dust with caster sugar to coat.
In a medium bowl, press the egg yolks through a wire sieve, then whisk in the coconut milk, sugar, desiccated coconut and vanilla extract until smooth.
Pour the mixture into the prepared ramekins and set them in a large roasting tin inside the oven.
Pour hot water into the tin until it comes three-quarters up the sides of the ramekins, creating a bain marie.
Bake for 30-35 minutes until the tops turn golden.
Lift the ramekins out of the water bath and let them cool for 5 minutes.
Chill for 4 hours, then run a knife around the edge of each ramekin.
Place a plate on top of each ramekin and turn out the quindims to serve.

Just before barbecuing, lift the cobs from the water, pat them dry, and rub the garlic mayo all over each one.
Season the cobs and grill until char marks appear, about 5 minutes.
Scatter over the grated Parmesan and coriander, then squeeze lime juice over the top to serve.

title: Bahia-style Moqueca prawn stew
instructions: Toss the prawns with 2 tablespoons of lime juice, a teaspoon of salt and a tablespoon of the chopped garlic, then chill for an hour.
Warm the coconut oil in a large saucepan and fry the spring onions and onion for five minutes.
Stir in the red pepper, chilli flakes, paprika and the remaining garlic.
Pour in the tomatoes and coconut milk with a little salt, bring to a simmer and let it reduce for ten minutes.
Add the prawns with all their marinade and the remaining lime juice, then simmer gently until the prawns turn white, about three minutes.
Serve scattered with fresh coriander alongside rice.

title: Black bean & meat stew - feijoada
instructions: Heat a large heavy-based saucepan with a lid, add the bacon, and fry until crisp.
Remove the bacon and keep the rendered fat in the pan.
Sear the ribs, sausages, and pork shoulder in batches in that fat, seasoning each batch with salt and pepper.
Remove the meat and set it aside.
Add the onion, garlic, and chilli to the pan, with a splash of olive oil if needed, and season with salt and pepper.
Fry for 8 minutes until soft.
Return the meat, bacon, bay leaves, white wine vinegar, and drained black beans to the pan.
Pour in about 650ml water, just enough to cover everything.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover, and cook for 2 hours until the beans are soft and the meat is tender.
If the stew looks too wet, take the lid off for the last hour to reduce it.
Alternatively, cook in a slow cooker for 4 hours, in a pressure cooker in batches of 30 minutes, or in the oven at 160°C/140°C fan/gas 3 for 3-4 hours.
Serve with rice, a sprinkle of parsley or coriander, hot pepper sauce, and orange slices.

title: Acaraje black-eyed pea fritters with shrimp filling
instructions: Blitz the onion, ginger, garlic, chilli and a pinch of salt in a food processor until smooth.
Heat oil in a frying pan and fry the purée for 5 minutes until cooked through.
Stir in the prawns, tomatoes and chopped coriander, then squeeze in the lime juice and season with salt.
Cook for 3 minutes more until the prawns are cooked through, then set the filling aside.
Drain and rinse the black-eyed peas, then purée them with the garlic and chilli until smooth.
Scrape the purée into a bowl and mix in the onion, flour, salt, chilli powder and baking powder.
Roll the mixture into 16 balls.
Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan/gas 5).
Heat about 8cm of oil in a wok or small heavy pan until a piece of bread sizzles on contact.
Fry the balls in batches of 4-5 until golden and crisp, about 4-5 minutes.
Drain on kitchen paper, keeping the fried fritters warm in the oven while you finish the rest.
Slice each fritter open and spoon in the prawn filling.
Serve with hot sauce.

title: Brazilian carrot cake
instructions: Heat your oven to 180C (160C fan/gas 4), then oil and line the base of a 20cm springform tin.
Whisk together the flour, sugar and baking powder in a large bowl.
Blitz the oil, carrots and eggs in a blender until smooth, then stir this wet mix into the dry ingredients until you have a smooth batter.
Pour the batter into the tin, shake gently to knock out air bubbles, and bake for 45 minutes to 1 hour, until a skewer comes out clean (give it 5 more minutes if it's still sticky).
Let the cake cool fully in the tin.
For the drizzle, warm the milk, cocoa and sugar together in a saucepan over low heat, stirring for about 5 minutes until the sugar dissolves and it turns thick and shiny.
Turn the cooled cake out, level the top with a serrated knife, then flip it so the flat bottom faces up.
Pierce the cake all over with a skewer, set it on a wire rack over a tray, and pour the drizzle over in batches, letting it soak in — reuse any drizzle that drips through until it's mostly absorbed.

title: Easy White Bean Soup
instructions: Rinse the dried cannellini beans and place them in a large pot, covering generously with cold water.
Let them soak for at least 4 hours, or overnight for best results.
Drain and rinse the soaked beans before adding them to the soup.
Chop one onion finely and leave the other whole.
Chop the carrots into small pieces.
Warm the oil in a large pot over medium heat.
Add the chopped onion and carrots, and cook for about 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the soaked beans, water, whole red peppers, the whole onion, salt, pepper, summer savoury, mint, thyme, Vegeta seasoning, and tomato puree.
Stir well, bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and let it simmer gently for about 2 hours, or until the beans are tender.
Remove the whole peppers and onion from the pot.
Mash the softened onion and stir it back into the soup.
Peel the peppers, chop them, and return them to the pot as well.
Taste and adjust the seasoning with more salt and pepper if needed.
Garnish with freshly chopped parsley before serving.

title: Oyster Mushroom Soup
instructions: Give the oyster mushrooms a good wash under cold water, pat them dry and trim off the stems.

Melt the butter in a small pot, add the mushrooms and cook for about 10 minutes until they release their liquid.
Whisk the flour into the water until smooth, then stir it into the pot along with the salt and milk.
Let the soup simmer for another 10 minutes.
Meanwhile, mix the garlic and vinegar together in a small bowl.
Ladle the soup into bowls and finish each with a spoonful or two of the garlic-vinegar mix and a scattering of hot red pepper flakes.

title: Cabbage with Ground Beef
instructions: Heat a large skillet or braiser over medium heat and warm the olive oil.
Add the ground beef, breaking it up with a spoon or masher, and brown it for 3–4 minutes.
Stir in the onion, carrots, red pepper, garlic and tomato puree.
Season with salt, black pepper, smoked paprika and ground cumin, stirring for 2–3 minutes.
Add the chopped cabbage, apple cider vinegar and water, stirring to combine.
Cover, reduce heat to medium-low, and simmer for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally so the cabbage doesn't stick.
Uncover and check the cabbage's texture; if there's excess liquid, raise heat to medium-high and cook 5–7 minutes more to finish.
Sprinkle with chopped parsley before serving.

title: Potato Moussaka Recipe
instructions: Preheat the oven to 350°F (175°C).
Heat the oil in a skillet and add the ground beef, breaking it up with a wooden spoon and cooking for 6-7 minutes until browned.
Stir in the onion and garlic, and the carrots if using.
Season with salt, black pepper, dried mint and summer savoury, then stir in the tomato sauce and tomato paste and cook for 2-3 minutes, stirring frequently.
Grease a 9x9 or 9x13 baking dish with oil.
Layer the drained potatoes and the meat mixture in the dish, stirring gently to distribute the meat evenly through the potatoes.
Pour in cold water until it just covers the top of the potatoes.
Bake for 40-50 minutes, until the water is absorbed and the potatoes are fully cooked.
While the potatoes bake, make the bechamel: whisk the egg yolks in a bowl and set aside.
Melt the butter in a saucepan, whisk in the flour and cook for 3 minutes over medium heat, stirring frequently.
Slowly whisk in the milk, then season with salt and nutmeg, and cook for 3 minutes until thickened.
Let the sauce cool for 5 minutes, then whisk in the egg yolks and feta, and let it stand at room temperature until the potatoes are ready.
Pour the bechamel over the cooked potatoes, top with cheese, and bake for 15 minutes until golden, broiling briefly if you want more colour.

Spoon the cheese mixture into the pepper halves.
Sprinkle extra chopped chives and parsley on top.
Serve right away.

Top with sliced hard-boiled eggs, olives, dill and/or parsley if you like, and serve right away.

title: Bulgarian Honey Cookies
instructions: Preheat your oven to 350°F and line two or three baking sheets with parchment paper.
Whisk together the flour, salt, cinnamon and baking powder in a bowl.
In a stand mixer fitted with the paddle attachment, beat the eggs and powdered sugar together for three minutes.
Mix in the vanilla extract and honey, then add the oil.
Add the flour mixture in two batches, beating until just incorporated.
Let the dough rest for five to ten minutes.
Shape into two-tablespoon balls, place on the prepared sheets and flatten each one gently.
Bake for twelve to fifteen minutes, until golden, then let the cookies cool completely.
Melt the white and semi-sweet chocolate separately, stirring one tablespoon of vegetable oil into each.
Dip each cooled cookie halfway into the chocolate and let it set.
Store in an airtight container or wrapped in cellophane bags, up to two weeks at room temperature or refrigerated.

title: Keto Banitsa
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (360°F).
Whisk the eggs in a bowl until well beaten.
Slowly whisk in the melted butter until fully incorporated.

Add the baking powder and whisk again.
Grease an 8x8-inch baking dish with butter, pour in the mixture, and scatter sesame seeds on top if using.
Bake for 20 minutes until golden.
Let the banitsa cool in the pan for 5-10 minutes, then cut into pieces and serve.
Store in the fridge for up to 3 days.

title: Amok Trey – Cambodian Fish Curry
instructions: Whisk the coconut milk, red curry paste, fish sauce, brown sugar, and chicken stock together in a bowl until smooth.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large skillet over medium heat until shimmering.
Pour in the coconut milk mixture and bring to a gentle simmer, stirring occasionally, for 3-4 minutes.
Add the fish cubes and kaffir lime leaves, making sure they're fully submerged in the sauce.
Lower the heat, cover, and cook for 10 minutes until the fish is opaque and flakes easily.
Slowly stir in the beaten egg and cook for 2 more minutes until the sauce thickens slightly.
Pass the banana leaves briefly over a flame to soften them, or use parchment paper instead.
Spoon the curry into the centre of each banana leaf, folding the edges into a tight packet.
Steam the packets over boiling water for 10 minutes until heated through and fragrant.
Unwrap the packets and garnish with fresh cilantro and lime wedges before serving.

title: Bai Sach Chrouk – Grilled Pork with Rice
instructions: Whisk together the coconut milk, soy sauce, oyster sauce, honey, minced garlic, and black pepper in a large bowl to build the marinade.
Toss the thinly sliced pork shoulder through the marinade until every piece is coated, then cover and chill for at least 30 minutes.
Rinse the jasmine rice under cold water until it runs clear to wash off excess starch.
Tip the rinsed rice into a pot with the water, bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer, cover, and cook for about 15 minutes until the water is absorbed and the rice is tender.
Heat a grill pan or outdoor grill to medium-high (around 400°F) and brush lightly with vegetable oil.
Shake excess marinade off the pork and grill for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and cooked through to 145°F internally.
Work in batches so the grill isn't crowded, letting the marinade caramelize properly.
Rest the grilled pork for 5 minutes before slicing so the juices stay put.
If you'd like extra sauce, simmer the leftover marinade in a small pan for about 5 minutes until slightly thickened, then drizzle it over the sliced pork.
Fluff the cooked rice with a fork and serve warm alongside the pork slices.
Top with the sliced cucumber and fresh cilantro leaves for a fresh garnish.
Serve with pickled vegetables or a squeeze of lime on the side if you want extra brightness.

instructions: Warm the vegetable oil in a large pot over medium heat until it shimmers, about 2 minutes.

title: Samlar Kari – Khmer Red Curry
instructions: Warm the vegetable oil in a large pot over medium-high heat until it shimmers.
Brown the chicken thighs on all sides, then lift them out onto a plate, leaving the oil and juices behind.
Drop the heat to medium and soften the onion in the same pot until translucent.
Stir in the garlic and ginger and cook briefly until fragrant, watching that they don't catch.
Add the red curry paste and toast it for a minute, stirring so it doesn't stick.
Pour in the coconut milk and chicken stock, scraping up any browned bits from the base of the pot.
Stir in the fish sauce and brown sugar until dissolved, then return the browned chicken to the pot.
Bring to a gentle boil, then cover, lower the heat, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add the bamboo shoots and sliced red pepper, then simmer uncovered for another 5 minutes until just tender.
Take the pot off the heat and stir through the basil until wilted.
Serve the curry hot with lime wedges on the side.

title: Prahok Ktis – Pork and Coconut Dip
instructions: Heat the vegetable oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat until it shimmers.
Add the crushed garlic and chopped shallots, stirring constantly until fragrant and lightly golden, keeping the heat moderate so the garlic doesn't turn bitter.
Add the ground pork, breaking it up with a spoon, and cook until no pink remains.
Stir in the prahok, mashing it into the pork, and cook for a minute to blend the flavors.
Pour in the coconut milk, stir to combine, then lower the heat and simmer gently for about 10 minutes until slightly thickened, keeping the bubble steady so the coconut milk doesn't curdle.
Add the fish sauce, brown sugar, and chopped chillies, stirring until the sugar dissolves.
Take the skillet off the heat and stir in the lime juice so its fresh tang doesn't cook away.
Transfer the dip to a serving bowl and scatter over the chopped cilantro.
Let the dip rest for about 5 minutes before serving so the flavors settle.

title: Num Ansom – Sticky Rice Cake
instructions: Drain the soaked glutinous rice well in a fine-mesh strainer.
Stir the coconut milk, sugar, and salt together in a bowl until the sugar fully dissolves.
Fold the drained rice into the coconut milk mixture, coating every grain, then let it rest 15 minutes to soak up the liquid.
Set up a steamer with about 2 inches of water and bring it to a boil.
Lay a banana leaf square shiny-side up, spoon a quarter cup of the rice mixture into the center, and spread it into a 4-inch rectangle.
Lay 2 tablespoons of mung bean paste in a line down the middle of the rice.
Fold the leaf over the filling lengthwise, then fold in the sides to make a tight parcel, pressing firmly to compact the rice.
Tie each parcel securely with kitchen twine, leaving a little room for the rice to expand.
Repeat with the remaining rice, filling, and leaves to make 8 parcels total.
Arrange the parcels in a single layer in the steamer basket with small gaps between them.
Cover and steam over medium heat for 90 minutes, topping up with hot water if it runs low; they're done when the leaves turn deep green and the rice feels firm.
Lift the parcels out and let them cool for 20 minutes before unwrapping so the texture sets.
Unwrap the banana leaves and slice each parcel into 1-inch rounds to serve.

ingredients.note: 2 cups glutinous rice estimated at ~200 g/cup
1 cup ≈ 240 g/ml
listed as 'Brown Sugar' though instructions call for palm sugar; used brown sugar per ingredient line, ~220 g/cup halved
1/2 tsp ≈ 3 g
banana leaves are a non-food wrapping material, not in USDA table; missing ingredient, not counted toward nutrition
1 cup estimated at ~240 g for a thick paste

title: Num Pang – Cambodian Baguette Sandwich
instructions: Whisk the soy sauce and honey together in a bowl to make the marinade.
Toss in the chicken thigh strips, coat them well, and let them sit at room temperature for 20 minutes.
While the chicken marinates, stir the julienned carrots and daikon radish with rice vinegar and sugar in a separate bowl until the sugar dissolves, letting the vegetables quick-pickle.
Mix the mayonnaise and sriracha together in a small bowl to make the spicy aioli.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large skillet or wok over medium-high heat until shimmering, about 2 minutes.
Lay the marinated chicken strips in a single layer in the hot skillet and cook 4-5 minutes per side until golden and cooked through to 165°F.
Toast the split baguettes for 2-3 minutes until just crisp at the edges.
Spread the spicy aioli generously over the inside of each toasted baguette.
Drain the pickled carrots and daikon and layer them onto the bottom half of each baguette.
Lay the cooked chicken strips over the pickled vegetables.
Top with fresh cilantro leaves and thinly sliced cucumber.
Close each sandwich by pressing the top half of the baguette down gently.

ingredients.note: no per-piece weight given; assumed ~250g per baguette for 4 baguettes

title: Khmer Banh Xeo – Cambodian Savory Pancake
instructions: Whisk rice flour, cornstarch, coconut milk, water, and turmeric together until smooth, then let the batter rest for 15 minutes to hydrate.
Heat a 10-inch non-stick skillet over medium-high heat and add a splash of vegetable oil.
Sauté the pork belly slices for 3–4 minutes until lightly browned, then push them to one side of the pan.
Add the onion slices and cook for 1 minute until slightly softened.
Stir the batter, pour about half a cup into the skillet, and swirl immediately to coat the bottom in a thin layer.
Scatter bean sprouts over half the pancake, cover with a lid, and steam for 2 minutes.
Uncover and continue cooking for 3–4 minutes until the edges turn golden and crispy.
Fold the pancake in half over the bean sprouts with a spatula and transfer to a plate.
Repeat with the remaining batter and fillings, adding more oil as needed to stop sticking.
Serve immediately with fish sauce for dipping.

1/4 cup cornstarch converted at ~128 g/cup
